text: Chapter 5.1 introduces the full Transformer architecture from the original paper. The encoder stack processes inputs through embedding plus positional encoding, then repeats Nx blocks of multi-head attention, add & norm, point-wise FFN, and add & norm with residual connections around each sub-layer. The decoder stack mirrors this but adds a masked self-attention sub-layer and a cross-attention sub-layer that pulls K and V from the encoder's output. On top of the decoder sits the Linear & Softmax output head producing token probabilities. The chapter sets up later zoom-ins on embeddings, positional encoding, attention, add & norm, and FFN.

text: The complete Transformer architecture, matching the original 'Attention Is All You Need' diagram. Two stacks side by side: ENCODER on the left and DECODER on the right. Residual skips around all three sub-layers. The encoder's top output feeds into the decoder's cross-attention block as K and V (green arrow labeled 'K, V from encoder'). On top of the decoder sits the output head: Linear & SoftMax producing Output Probabilities.

text: Chapter 5.2 walks through how raw words get turned into the numerical vectors a Transformer can consume. Three stages: tokenization splits text into tokens (e.g., 'I love cats' becomes ['I','love','cats']; 'unbelievable' becomes ['un','believ','able']). Token-to-ID lookup maps each token to an arbitrary integer ID (e.g., 'I'->4, 'love'->6, 'cats'->2). The key step, ID-to-embedding, uses the ID to fetch a row from a learned embedding matrix - each row is 512 numbers representing meaning. Similar words end up with similar vectors. After this, every word is a 512-dim vector but still carries no position information, motivating positional encoding next.
summary: Three-stage pipeline (tokenize, ID lookup, embedding lookup) converts words into 512-dim learned vectors carrying meaning but not position.

text: Stage 3: ID to embedding (the KEY step). Each ID looks up a row in a learned matrix. Each row is 512 numbers representing that word's meaning. Examples: 'I' -> [0.12, -0.34, 0.78, ..., 0.91], 'love' -> [0.56, 0.23, -0.11, ..., 0.53], 'cats' -> [-0.45, 0.89, 0.33, ..., 0.67]. Similar words (cat/dog) get similar vectors. The embedding matrix is learned during training.

text: Chapter 5.3 - Positional Encoding, the Problem. Transformers process all tokens at once, so 'I love cats' and 'cats love I' produce the same set of embedding vectors and look identical to attention. Naive fixes fail: integer positions explode and overwhelm word meaning, pos/max_length depends on sentence length, and random vectors lose relative structure. The fix must give bounded values, a unique signature per position, and learnable relative distances - which sine and cosine waves provide.
summary: Chapter overview: transformers ignore word order, naive position schemes fail, and a good encoding must be bounded, unique per position, and preserve relative distance — motivating sine/cosine waves.

text:
The Transformer sees all words at the same time. It receives three embedding vectors simultaneously. Nothing tells it which is first, second, or third.

"I love cats" → [vec_I, vec_love, vec_cats]
"cats love I" → [vec_cats, vec_love, vec_I]

Same vectors, different order — but the model can't see order. Without a position signal, attention treats the input as an unordered set, so reversing or shuffling words gives the model the exact same view.
summary: Transformers process all tokens in parallel, so without an explicit position signal they cannot distinguish word order.

text:
Why simple solutions fail:

❌ Use [1,1,...], [2,2,...], [3,3,...]? Values grow forever. At position 1000, you add 1000 to every dimension - drowns out the word meaning.

❌ Use pos / max_length (0.0, 0.01, 0.02...)? Step size depends on sentence length. Model can't learn consistent patterns.

❌ Use random vectors? No relationship between positions. Can't learn 'pos 5 is near pos 6'.
summary: Three obvious position-encoding ideas — raw integers, normalized fractions, and random vectors — all fail because they explode, depend on sequence length, or destroy relative structure.

text:
We need: bounded values, unique per position, relative distances learnable. Solution: Sine and Cosine waves.

A good positional encoding must keep numbers in a fixed range so they don't drown out the embedding, give every position a distinct fingerprint, and let the model learn how far apart two positions are. Sine and cosine satisfy all three: outputs stay between -1 and +1, every position gets a unique pattern across many dimensions, and the smooth periodic structure makes relative distance learnable.
summary: A workable positional encoding must be bounded, unique per position, and preserve relative-distance structure — sine and cosine waves satisfy all three.

text: Chapter 5.4 introduces the sinusoidal positional encoding formula used in the Transformer. Even-indexed dimensions use sin(pos / 10000^(i/d_model)) and odd-indexed dimensions use cos(pos / 10000^(i/d_model)). pos is the position of the word, i is the dimension index, d_model is the embedding size (e.g. 512). The divisor 10000^(i/d_model) makes early dimensions oscillate fast and late dimensions oscillate slowly. Sin and cos keep values bounded in [-1, +1]. The clock analogy: fast seconds hand plus slow hours hand uniquely encodes time.
summary: Sinusoidal positional encoding formula with sin on even dims, cos on odd dims, frequency controlled by 10000^(i/d_model).

text: The Formula. Even dims (0,2,4...): sin( pos / 10000^(i/d_model) ). Odd dims (1,3,5...): cos( pos / 10000^(i/d_model) ).
summary: The sinusoidal positional encoding formula: sin for even dimensions, cos for odd dimensions, both divided by 10000^(i/d_model).

text: Each term in the formula explained: pos is the position of the word in the sentence (0, 1, 2, ...). i is which of the 512 dimensions we're computing. d_model is the size of the embedding (512). 10000^(i/d) controls wave speed - small i means fast, large i means slow. sin/cos output is always between -1 and +1, so it never explodes.
summary: Each variable in the positional encoding formula defined: pos, i, d_model, the 10000^(i/d) speed controller, and the bounded sin/cos output.

text: The KEY insight is the divisor 10000^(i/d_model). When i=0, divisor=1, so pos/1 = pos and the wave oscillates fast. When i=510, divisor is approximately 10000, so pos/10000 is tiny and the wave changes barely. This is like a clock: a fast seconds hand combined with a slow hours hand together give the exact time. Different dimensions oscillate at different speeds so that combining all 512 values produces a unique fingerprint per position.
summary: The 10000^(i/d_model) divisor makes early dimensions fast and late dimensions slow, like a clock combining seconds and hours hands.

text: Chapter 5.6 explains why positional encoding uses dimensions that oscillate at different speeds. Just like decimal counting uses ones, tens, and hundreds columns that change at different rates to give every number a unique fingerprint, positional encoding uses fast-changing early dimensions and slow-changing late dimensions. Using only the fast column repeats every 10 positions; using only the slow column makes 100 positions look identical. Combining all speeds gives every position a unique vector, and works for any sequence length.
summary: Why positional encoding mixes fast and slow oscillating dimensions, explained through the analogy of counting in ones, tens, and hundreds.

text: Think about how you count: ones, tens, hundreds. Each column changes at a different speed. The ones column changes every step, the tens column changes every 10 steps, the hundreds column changes every 100 steps. Counting through positions 0, 1, 2, 9, 10, 11, 99, 100, 101: the ones digit cycles fastest, tens cycles 10x slower, hundreds cycles 100x slower. Each column has its own job - ones distinguish neighbors, hundreds distinguish far-apart numbers.
summary: Decimal counting uses three columns that change at different speeds: ones change every step, tens every 10, hundreds every 100.

text: What if you ONLY had the ones column? Position 0 = 0, position 1 = 1, position 2 = 2, ..., position 9 = 9, position 10 = 0 (same as pos 0!), position 11 = 1 (same as pos 1!). After 10 positions the ones column repeats. Position 0 and position 10 look identical. You can only count to 9. Stuck. A single fast-changing dimension cannot distinguish positions that are 10 or more apart.
summary: Using only the fast ones column makes positions wrap around every 10 steps, so position 0 and position 10 are indistinguishable.

text: What if you ONLY had the hundreds column? Position 0 = 0, position 1 = 0 (same!), position 2 = 0 (same!), ..., position 99 = 0 (still same!), position 100 = 1 (finally changes). Positions 0 through 99 all look identical - the hundreds column just shows 0 for all of them. You cannot tell neighboring words apart at all. A single slow-changing dimension cannot distinguish nearby positions.
summary: Using only the slow hundreds column makes the first 100 positions all look identical, hiding nearby distinctions.

text: But COMBINE all three columns? Every position from 000 to 999 has a unique combination. That's 1000 unique positions using just 3 digits. Examples: pos 0 = 000, pos 1 = 001, pos 10 = 010, pos 11 = 011, pos 100 = 100, pos 101 = 101 - all unique. The fast column (ones) tells close positions apart (0 vs 1). The slow column (hundreds) tells distant positions apart (0 vs 100). Together, every position is unique.
summary: Combining fast, medium, and slow columns gives 1000 unique position fingerprints from just 3 digits, with fast columns separating neighbors and slow columns separating distant positions.
queries: why combining frequencies gives unique positions

text: This is EXACTLY what positional encoding does. Ones column (changes every step, repeats after 10) maps to fast dimensions (dim 0, 1) - sine wave changes fast, repeats quickly. Tens column (changes every 10 steps) maps to medium dimensions - sine wave at medium speed. Hundreds column (changes every 100 steps) maps to slow dimensions (dim 510, 511) - sine wave barely changes. Instead of 3 digits (0-9) giving 1000 unique positions, positional encoding uses 512 dimensions (sine waves -1 to +1), giving practically unlimited unique positions.
summary: The decimal counting analogy maps directly onto positional encoding: fast dimensions act like ones, slow dimensions like hundreds, and 512 sine waves give nearly unlimited unique positions.

text: That's the entire point of fast vs slow. Nothing more, nothing less. Different speeds exist so the combination is always unique, no matter how long the sentence is. Fast dimensions distinguish close-by positions, slow dimensions distinguish far-apart positions, and combining all 512 of them produces a unique vector for every position the model will ever see.
summary: Different oscillation speeds exist purely so that the combined positional encoding stays unique for any sentence length.

text: Chapter 5.7 (Positional Encoding - Final Addition): the embedding vector for each word is added element-wise to its sinusoidal positional encoding to produce the final input vector. A worked example for 'love' at position 1 shows embedding [0.56,0.23,-0.11,...] + PE [0.841,0.54,0.10,...] = final [1.40,0.77,-0.01,...]. The combined vector carries both meaning (embedding dominates) and position (small nudge). Four reasons this design is genius: bounded between -1 and +1, unique pattern per position across 512 dims, relative distances learnable via sin(a+b) identity, generalizes to longer sequences because sin/cos are smooth. The output is what enters the Transformer attention layers.
summary: Chapter-level summary of how embedding plus positional encoding produces the final transformer input and why the design works.

text: Four reasons sinusoidal positional encoding design is genius: 1) Bounded: sin/cos always between -1 and +1, so values never explode. 2) Unique: every position gets a unique pattern across 512 dimensions. 3) Relative distances: sin(a+b) is a linear combination of sin(a) and cos(a), so the model can learn relative positions. 4) Generalizes: works for longer sequences than seen during training because sin and cos are smooth and continuous.
summary: Lists the four key properties (bounded, unique, relative-distance-friendly, generalizable) that make sinusoidal positional encoding work.

text: Chapter 5.8 unveils the famous positional encoding heatmap for d_model = 128 across positions 0 to 39. The left side is a fast checkerboard, the right side calm vertical stripes. The frequency formula f_i = 1 / 10000^(2 floor(i/2) / d) explains why: small i gives f near 1.0 (sin/cos swing wildly each step), large i gives f near 0.0001 (40 positions only move the angle 0.004 radians). Each row is a unique 128-number fingerprint; nearby positions have similar vectors thanks to slow dimensions. Sin/cos PE is binary counting made smooth: fast dims act like low-order bits, slow dims like high-order bits, but values stay bounded in [-1, +1] and are differentiable.

text: Why the left side flips fast: each column uses its own frequency f_i = 1 / 10000^(2 · floor(i/2) / d). For small i (left side), the exponent is near 0, so f_i is close to 1.0. Example column i = 0, f = 1.0, sin(pos · 1.0) for pos 0..7: 0.00, 0.84, 0.91, 0.14, -0.76, -0.96, -0.28, 0.66. Every position is a very different number - that is the checkerboard on the left.

text: Why the right side barely changes: for large i (right side), the exponent approaches 1, so f_i approaches 1 / 10000 = 0.0001. Across 40 positions the angle only moves by 40 · 0.0001 = 0.004 radians (about 0.23 degrees). sin and cos barely move. Example column i = 127, f ≈ 1/10000, cos(pos · 0.0001) for pos 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 39 all stay near 1.0000. That is the solid blue stripe on the far right. These slow dimensions encode very long-range position info: tokens 1000 positions apart differ meaningfully in slow columns even when 40-position differences are invisible.

text: Binary counting: the same idea, one bit at a time. Counting in binary gives every integer a unique bit pattern; bits flip at different speeds: rightmost bit flips every step, next every 2, next every 4, next every 8. Mapping: Binary - bit 0 (rightmost, fast) flips 1,0,1,0; bit 3 (leftmost, slow) flips every 8 steps; values 0 or 1 (discrete); each number = unique bit pattern. Sin/cos - dim 0 (fast, f = 1.0) oscillates every step; dim 127 (slow, f = 0.0001) barely moves; values any number in [-1, +1] (smooth); each position = unique sin/cos signature. Sin/cos PE is essentially binary counting made smooth and continuous: fast dims play the role of low-order bits, slow dims play the role of high-order bits. The advantage of sin/cos over 0/1: dot products capture relative distance, values stay bounded, and the encoding is differentiable so gradients flow cleanly during training.
summary: Sinusoidal positional encoding is binary counting made smooth: fast dims act like low-order bits, slow dims like high-order bits, but values are continuous and differentiable.

text: RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) replaces additive sinusoidal position encoding by rotating each Q and K vector by an angle equal to position times frequency. Vectors are processed in 2D pairs, each pair rotating at its own frequency f_i = 1 / 10000^(2i/d) - fast pairs encode nearby distance, slow pairs encode long-range distance. Because rotation matrices are orthogonal, the absolute rotation cancels in the Q·K dot product, leaving only the relative position gap (n-m). This gives the model true relative position for free with zero parameters, preserves vector length, and supports extrapolation via NTK-aware frequency scaling. RoPE is used by LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, GPT-NeoX, and PaLM, and is the default position encoding in modern LLMs.
summary: Chapter overview: RoPE encodes position by rotating Q and K in pairs at multiple frequencies so attention sees only the relative gap, enabling extrapolation and zero extra parameters.
queries: Why use RoPE instead of sinusoidal

text: Recall chapters 5.3-5.7: sinusoidal positional encoding computes a position vector using sin/cos and ADDS it to each embedding, giving every token a unique position signal. But it has two fundamental problems. Problem 1: Absolute positioning. Position 5 always gets the same encoding vector regardless of context. 'Cats' at position 5 in a 10-word sentence and 'cats' at position 5 in a 1000-word sentence get identical position signals, even though relative context differs. Problem 2: Fixed length. If we train on sequences up to 2048 tokens, position 2049 has never been seen by the model. Performance degrades badly because the model cannot extrapolate to longer contexts. RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) solves both problems with a single elegant idea.
summary: Sinusoidal positional encoding fails because it is absolute and cannot extrapolate beyond training sequence length, motivating RoPE.

text: Picture every word's Query and Key vector as a clock hand. RoPE doesn't ADD anything to it - it TURNS the hand by an angle equal to position times frequency. Position 1 is a small turn, position 10 is bigger, position 100 is huge. No new numbers bolted on, no dimensions inflated - the hand just points somewhere else. Example with Q = [0.8, 0.6] and frequency f = 0.3 rad per step: pos 0 angle 0.0 gives [0.800, 0.600]; pos 1 angle 0.3 gives [0.587, 0.810]; pos 2 angle 0.6 gives [0.321, 0.947]; pos 3 angle 0.9 gives [0.027, 1.000]. Key property: turning preserves length. All four rotated vectors keep the same length (1.0 here). Rotation only changes direction, not length. Sinusoidal PE adds a position vector that changes both. The 2D rotation formula: R(t) · [q1, q2] = [q1 cos(t) - q2 sin(t), q1 sin(t) + q2 cos(t)] where t = position · frequency.
summary: RoPE rotates each Q and K vector by an angle proportional to its position, preserving length while encoding position as direction.
queries: What does RoPE do to Q and K

text: The attention score between two words is the dot product of their clock hands, and the dot product depends only on the angle between them. Same angle = same score, regardless of where the hands point absolutely. RoPE spins both Q and K by their own positions, so the common spin cancels and only the gap (position difference) survives. Example: Near scenario - Q at position 3, K at position 0, Q spun 0.9 rad, K spun 0 rad, gap = 0.9 rad, score = cos(0.9) ≈ 0.62. Far scenario - Q at position 10, K at position 7, Q spun 3.0 rad, K spun 2.1 rad, gap = 0.9 rad, score = cos(0.9) ≈ 0.62. Same answer. Turntable intuition: glue both Q and K to the same spinning turntable; the relative angle between them never changes regardless of absolute rotation. The model gets relative position for free, with zero extra parameters. Algebra: (R(m·f) Q) · (R(n·f) K) = Q · R((n-m)·f) K because rotation matrices are orthogonal so R(a)ᵀ R(b) = R(b-a). Only the gap (n-m) survives. For unit vectors at angular gap Δ, the dot product equals cos(Δ).
summary: Because rotation cancels in the dot product, RoPE makes attention scores depend only on the relative position gap between Q and K.
queries: Why does RoPE encode relative position
How does the absolute rotation cancel in RoPE

text: Real embeddings have 128 dimensions or more, not 2. RoPE handles this by processing dimensions in PAIRS, and each pair rotates at its own frequency - fast frequencies for early pairs, slow ones for late pairs. A 128-dim vector splits into 64 pairs: (d0, d1) fast, (d2, d3) fast, (d4, d5) medium, ..., (d124, d125) slow, (d126, d127) very slow. Each pair gets its own 2D rotation. Early pairs (d0, d1) rotate FAST: high frequency, sensitive to nearby position differences ('is the next word 1 or 2 positions away?'). Late pairs (d126, d127) rotate SLOW: low frequency, sensitive to distant position differences ('is this word 500 or 1000 positions away?'). The frequency formula matches sinusoidal PE: f_i = 1 / 10000^(2i/d), where i is pair index and d is total dimension. Pair 0 rotates fastest, pair 63 rotates slowest. After 10 position steps: pair 0 has spun 2.5 rad (143 deg), pair 10 spun 0.5 rad (29 deg), pair 60 spun 0.05 rad (3 deg).
summary: RoPE splits the embedding into 64 pairs and rotates each pair at a different frequency, fast pairs encode nearby positions and slow pairs encode distant ones.

text: Why RoPE won. Comparison table: Sinusoidal is absolute, applied to embedding by addition, fixed at training, fails beyond max length, 0 parameters. Learned PE is absolute, added to embedding, fixed at training, fails beyond max, has pos × dim parameters. RoPE is relative, applied to Q and K only by rotation, can extrapolate, works with scaling tricks, 0 parameters. NTK-aware scaling: a model trained on 4096 tokens can handle 16384 by scaling rotation frequencies. The NTK-aware variant adjusts different frequency bands differently - slowing down fast-rotating pairs while keeping slow ones nearly unchanged. Extends context length 4× or more with minimal quality loss. Models using RoPE: LLaMA, LLaMA 2, LLaMA 3, Mistral, Qwen, GPT-NeoX, PaLM. Nearly every modern open-source LLM uses RoPE. It became the default choice for position encoding since 2022.
summary: RoPE wins because it gives relative position with zero parameters, applies only to Q and K, and extrapolates to longer contexts via NTK-aware frequency scaling, making it the default for modern LLMs.

text: Chapter 6.1 sets up the core problem attention solves: word embeddings are static, so the same word gets the exact same vector regardless of surrounding words. The chapter uses 'bank' (river bank vs financial bank) and 'love' ('I love cats' vs 'love is blind') to show meaning depends on context. The goal of attention: take a fixed embedding like love = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1] and transform it into a NEW vector that represents the word in its specific context, by letting each word look around, find which other words are relevant, and absorb information from them.
summary: Why attention is needed: embeddings are context-free, but word meaning depends on neighbors.

text: This isn't just about 'bank' - it's about every word. The model has a big dictionary (learned during training) that maps every word to a fixed list of numbers. Take 'love': after embedding, 'love' always gets the same numbers, no matter what sentence it's in. But 'love' in 'I love cats' is different from 'love' in 'love is blind.' These numbers capture the word's meaning in isolation. It has no idea what words are around it. That's a problem - because we need context. The goal: take 'love' = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1] and transform it into a NEW list of numbers that represents 'love in the context of I and cats.'

text: This is what Attention solves. chapterTitle: How Does a Word Look At Others?
text: Chapter 6.2 — How Does a Word Look At Others? Attention mimics how humans read: when seeing 'it', your brain scans for what it refers to, focusing on nouns rather than every word equally. Attention computes a relevance score for every other word in the sentence. Worked example uses 'The cat sat because it was tired' — when 'it' is selected, 'cat' receives 55% attention (highest), so the model figures out 'it' = 'cat'. The chapter ends by motivating the dot product as the mathematical tool to measure relevance between two vectors.

text: Chapter 6.3 introduces the dot product as the mathematical tool that measures similarity between two vectors. Given vectors A and B, multiply matching pairs and sum: e.g. A=[1,2,3], B=[4,5,6] gives 1x4 + 2x5 + 3x6 = 4 + 10 + 18 = 32. The resulting single number reflects directional similarity: vectors pointing the same way produce a large positive number, opposite directions produce a large negative number, and perpendicular (unrelated) vectors produce a value near zero. Attention needs this tool to score relevance between words. However, the chapter ends by warning that we cannot dot-product raw word embeddings directly: words like 'I' (pronoun) and 'love' (verb) are related yet have dissimilar raw meanings, so we must compare them on a different basis - this motivates Query, Key, and Value.
summary: Dot product multiplies pairs and sums to get one number measuring directional similarity, the math tool attention uses for relevance scoring.

text: Given two vectors, multiply pairs and sum. A = [1, 2, 3], B = [4, 5, 6]. Multiply each pair: 1x4 = 4, 2x5 = 10, 3x6 = 18. Sum them up: 4 + 10 + 18 = 32. That's it - multiply pairs, add them up, get one number.

text: What does this number mean? It measures how similar two vectors are in direction. Think of you and a friend both pointing somewhere. Same direction (-> ->): large positive value, 'these are similar!' Opposite directions (-> <-): large negative value, 'these are opposites!' Perpendicular and unrelated (-> up): close to zero, 'nothing in common.' The dot product captures directional alignment between vectors.
summary: Explains the geometric meaning of the dot product as a directional alignment score: positive=same way, negative=opposite, ~zero=perpendicular.

We need to compare them on a different basis. This sets up why we transform embeddings into Query, Key, and Value before computing the dot product.

chapterTitle: Why Not Dot Product Embeddings Directly?
text: Chapter 6.4 explains why raw word embeddings cannot be dot-producted directly to measure attention relevance. Words like 'I' (pronoun) and 'love' (verb) have different raw meanings so a direct dot product is low, yet they are strongly related ('I' is the one loving). The fix: compare on a different basis - what each word is LOOKING FOR vs what each word OFFERS. 'love' looks for 'who is doing the loving?' (a subject, a person), and 'I' offers 'I am a subject, a person' - HIGH MATCH. This motivates needing three separate views of each word: Query, Key, Value.
summary: Why raw embedding dot products fail and why words need separate Query/Key/Value views.

chapterTitle: Why Not Dot Product Embeddings Directly?

chapterTitle: Q, K, V - The Classroom Analogy
text: Chapter 6.5 introduces the classroom analogy for Query, Key, and Value in attention. Imagine a classroom of 30 students. Student Riya has a question: 'who has yesterday's notes?' She looks around at everyone. Riya's question is the Query (what am I looking for?). Each other student's label or advertisement (e.g., Priya: 'I know the homework', Aman: 'I have yesterday's notes', Rahul: 'I know the canteen menu') is a Key (what can I be found for?). Aman matches, so he hands over the actual notes - physics equations, dates, diagrams - that real content is the Value (here's my actual info). Mapping back to attention: Query asks the question, Key advertises matchability, Value carries the real content delivered after a match.
summary: Classroom analogy maps Query to Riya's question, Key to each student's label, and Value to the actual notes handed over.

chapterTitle: Every Word is Both Asker and Answerer
text: Chapter 6.6 - Every Word is Both Asker and Answerer. Core idea: in self-attention, every token produces a Query, a Key, and a Value simultaneously. Using the classroom analogy, Riya, Aman, and Priya each have their own question (Query), their own label of what they offer (Key), and their own actual information (Value). The same is true for words in a Transformer: in "The cat sat because it was tired", "cat" asks its own question while also advertising itself as a noun-subject and carrying its own semantic content. This dual role is what makes self-attention symmetric across tokens, and motivates the next chapter's question of why Key and Value must remain separate.
summary: Chapter-level summary: in self-attention every token simultaneously plays asker (Q), advertiser (K), and information-carrier (V).

chapterTitle: Why Can't Key and Value Be the Same?
text: Chapter 6.7 explains why Key and Value must be separate vectors in attention. Uses a restaurant analogy: the menu description is the Key (what helps you match your craving to a dish), the actual food on the plate is the Value (what you actually consume). If Key and Value were the same, you would get a plate with the menu description printed on it - useless. Connecting back to the Transformer with 'The cat sat because it was tired': cat's Key encodes 'I'm a noun, subject, animate' which helps 'it' find cat; cat's Value carries the rich semantic content (small, furry, four-legged, alive, domesticated) that 'it' actually absorbs. Key is optimized for matching, Value is optimized for content delivery.
summary: Why Key and Value must be separate: Key is for matching (menu description), Value is the actual content (food on plate).

chapterTitle: Why Can't Key and Value Be the Same?

This helped you decide - yes, this matches what I'm craving. It's optimized for matching your preference (your Query) to the right dish. The actual food on your plate = Value: the smoky charred paneer cubes, the creamy gravy, the aroma, the nourishment. This is the actual content you receive. What you actually consume.

chapterTitle: Why Can't Key and Value Be the Same?
text: Now connect this back to the Transformer. Take the sentence: 'The cat sat because it was tired.' The word 'it' needs to find what it refers to. It finds 'cat'. cat's Key (the menu description) encodes: 'I'm a noun, I'm a subject, I'm animate' - this is what helped 'it' find 'cat' in the first place. Optimized for matching. cat's Value (the actual food) carries: rich semantic content - small, furry, four-legged, alive, domesticated - this is the actual information that 'it' absorbs after finding the match. Key helped 'it' FIND 'cat' (menu description). Value is what 'it' actually GOT from 'cat' (the actual food).

text: Another way to see Q, K, V is Google Search, broken into 4 steps. Step 1: Your search query, e.g. "best pizza near me" = Query. So the user's typed search string acts as the Query, page metadata/tags act as Keys, the matching score is computed by a dot product, and what you actually consume (page body content) is the Value.

chapterTitle: How Are Q, K, V Created?
text: Chapter 6.9 explains how Q, K, V vectors are created from a word's embedding. Each word starts as a single embedding vector (e.g., "love" = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1]). To get three different views (Query, Key, Value), the embedding is multiplied by three different W matrices: W_Q, W_K, W_V. Each W matrix is just a grid of numbers (a table/spreadsheet). Multiplying input by a table produces an output. Analogy: a photo with three filters - each filter highlights a different feature (eyes, nose, mouth). For real Transformers (GPT-style), embedding is 512 numbers, each W matrix is 512×64 = 32,768 numbers, producing 64-number Q/K/V vectors per head.
summary: How Q, K, V vectors are produced by multiplying a word's embedding through three learned weight matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V.

chapterTitle: How Are Q, K, V Created?
text: So far, Q, K, V are just concepts in our head. We know each word needs: Query - "what am I looking for?", Key - "what do I offer?", Value - "what info do I carry?". But each word starts as just one list of numbers - its embedding. For example: "love" = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1]. That's one list. We need three different lists from it (Query, Key, Value). How do you get three different things from one thing? You transform it three different ways. And the tool for each transformation is: a grid of numbers.

chapterTitle: How Are Q, K, V Created?
text: The grid of numbers is called a W matrix (W = "weights"). It's just a table of numbers. Like a spreadsheet. Rows and columns filled with numbers. Example: a tiny W grid: 0.02, -0.15, 0.31, 0.08, 0.41, 0.07, -0.22, -0.19, -0.09, 0.33, 0.14, 0.27, 0.18, -0.05, 0.42, 0.11. When you multiply a word's embedding by this grid, you get a new, smaller list of numbers - the Query (or Key, or Value, depending on which grid). Multiply input by a table → get an output. Nothing more complicated than that.
summary: Defines a W matrix as just a table of weights; multiplying an embedding by it produces a Q, K, or V vector.

chapterTitle: How Are Q, K, V Created?
text: Think of it like a photo with three filters. You have a photo of a person and want three different views. View 1: highlight only the eyes → captures where the person is looking. View 2: highlight only the nose → captures the nose shape. View 3: highlight only the mouth → captures the expression. How do you create these views? Apply three different filters. Each filter is a grid of numbers that, when multiplied with the photo, produces a different view. That's EXACTLY what W_Q, W_K, W_V are. Word embedding: [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] ← the "photo". Grid 1 (W_Q): a 4×2 grid - filter for "what am I looking for?". Grid 2 (W_K): a 4×2 grid - filter for "what do I offer?". Grid 3 (W_V): a 4×2 grid - filter for "what info do I carry?". Multiply the same embedding by Grid 1 → Query (2-number list). By Grid 2 → Key. By Grid 3 → Value.
summary: Photo-with-three-filters analogy: each W matrix is a filter highlighting a different aspect of the same embedding to produce Q, K, or V.

chapterTitle: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: Chapter 6.10 explains why Q, K, and V are smaller than the embedding. W_Q is rectangular (e.g. 4x2, not 4x4) because it projects the d_model embedding down to a smaller d_k query space. The embedding keeps its full size through the network; Q, K, V are temporary compressed lenses used only inside attention. The smaller shape exists because multi-head attention splits a fixed budget across heads: d_k = d_model / num_heads. Real GPT scale: d_model=512, num_heads=8, d_k=64, W_Q per head is 512x64. After all heads, outputs concat back to d_model and W_O blends them.
summary: Why Q/K/V dimension d_k is smaller than embedding d_model and how multi-head splits the budget.

chapterTitle: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: The puzzle: why is W_Q shaped 4x2, not 4x4? The embedding has 4 numbers, so it might seem the query should also have 4 numbers and W_Q should be 4x4. Short answer: no. Q (and K and V) are smaller than the embedding. W_Q's job is to project the 4-number embedding down to a 2-number query. The input and output sizes do not have to match. Visually: embedding (4 numbers) [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] times W_Q (4x2) equals query (2 numbers) [0.3, 0.7]. The matrix shape tells you exactly that: 4 rows = input size, 2 columns = output size.
summary: W_Q is rectangular (4x2) because it projects a 4-number embedding down to a smaller 2-number query.

chapterTitle: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: The embedding stays big; Q, K, V are small views. Think of the embedding as the word's full profile - it stays 4 numbers long for the entire journey through the Transformer. Q, K, V are three compressed lenses on that profile, built only for the attention step. The 4-number embedding [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] is multiplied by three different W matrices (W_Q, W_K, W_V) producing Query [0.3, 0.7], Key [0.9, 0.4], Value [0.6, -0.2] - each just 2 numbers. Three different W matrices give three different 2-number views. The 4-number embedding itself is untouched; it continues through the rest of the network in its full original size.
summary: Embedding keeps full size through the network while Q, K, V are three compressed 2-number lenses used only inside attention.

chapterTitle: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: Why smaller? Multi-head attention splits the budget. The Transformer runs several heads in parallel on the same embedding. Each head gets its own set of W_Q, W_K, W_V and works independently, looking at the embedding through its own 2-number lens. Two heads, each with its own W_Q (4x2), produce different 2-number Queries (e.g. Head 1 Q1 = [0.3, 0.7], Head 2 Q2 = [-0.4, 0.2]) because W_Q1 and W_Q2 learned different things. After both heads do their attention, their outputs are concatenated back to 4 numbers total (2 + 2) and fed forward. Budget intuition: fixed budget of 4 output numbers per token. Options: one big head with a 4-number Q, or two small heads each with a 2-number Q. Multi-head picks the second: more heads, each specialized. Formula: d_k = d_model / num_heads.
summary: Q is small because multi-head attention divides a fixed d_model budget across heads, giving each head its own d_k = d_model / num_heads lens.
queries: why multi-head attention uses smaller dimensions

chapterTitle: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: Real Transformer numbers (GPT-style). Scale everything up. The chapter's 4 becomes 512. The chapter's 2 becomes 64. The chapter's 2 heads become 8. This chapter (teaching): d_model = 4 (embedding size), num_heads = 2, d_k = 4 / 2 = 2 (Q/K/V per head), W_Q per head = 4 x 2. Real GPT (typical): d_model = 512 (embedding size), num_heads = 8, d_k = 512 / 8 = 64 (Q/K/V per head), W_Q per head = 512 x 64. 8 heads x 64 numbers each = 512 numbers total, which fits exactly back into the embedding size. The budget is preserved across heads.
summary: Real GPT uses d_model=512, 8 heads, d_k=64, W_Q per head 512x64; 8x64=512 fits back into the embedding.

chapterTitle: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: Shapes recap. Embedding: shape d_model (4 here, 512 in real), lives through the whole Transformer. Q, K, V (per head): shape d_k (2 here, 64 in real), temporary, only inside attention. W_Q, W_K, W_V (per head): shape d_model x d_k (4x2 here, 512x64 in real), projects embedding to Q / K / V. Multi-head concat + W_O: back to d_model (4 here, 512 in real), returns to the main residual stream. Bottom line: W_Q is not square. It's a rectangular projection from embedding space into a smaller query space. The embedding keeps its original size; the query is a compressed view used only for the attention computation.
summary: Recap table of shapes: embedding d_model, Q/K/V d_k per head, W matrices d_model x d_k, concat+W_O back to d_model.

text: Chapter 6.11 — W Matrices: Learned During Training. The Q/K/V projection matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V are not designed by humans. They start as random numbers on day 1. Training repeats billions of times: model predicts next word, computes error, error flows backward through every grid entry via backpropagation, each number gets nudged a tiny amount (e.g. -0.09 → -0.07). After billions of nudges, the random noise settles into meaningful values: W_Q produces Queries ("what am I looking for?"), W_K produces Keys ("what do I offer?"), W_V produces Values ("what info do I carry?"). Nobody programmed the entries; they emerged from gradient descent.
summary: W_Q, W_K, W_V start random and are nudged by backpropagation over billions of examples until they produce meaningful Q, K, V projections.

text: Day 1: Start with random garbage. Before training begins, every grid is filled with random numbers from a random number generator. Visual shows three 4×4 grids labeled W_Q (random), W_K (random), W_V (random) with values like 0.47, -0.82, 0.15, -0.63, 0.91, -0.24, 0.38, -0.71, 0.56, -0.09, 0.77, -0.45, -0.33, 0.62, -0.88, 0.19. With random grids the model computes: random W_Q × embedding = garbage Query; random W_K × embedding = garbage Key; random W_V × embedding = garbage Value. Garbage Q, K, V → garbage attention → garbage predictions. The model is terrible at this point. And that's completely fine.
summary: Before training, W_Q, W_K, W_V are filled with random numbers, so the resulting Q, K, V vectors and predictions are nonsense — and that's the expected starting point.

text: The training loop: predict, fail, nudge. Model reads millions of sentences and tries to predict the next word each time. Steps every attempt: 1) Model sees: "The cat sat on the". 2) Uses its current (bad) grids to compute attention. 3) Predicts next word: "elephant" (wrong! should be "mat"). 4) Calculates error: how wrong was that prediction? 5) Error flows backward through every grid number. 6) Each number gets nudged slightly to reduce the error. This loop repeats billions of times. Every repetition nudges the numbers a tiny bit closer to useful values.
summary: Six-step training loop where the model predicts, computes error, propagates it backward, and nudges every W matrix entry — repeated billions of times.

text: Zoom in: what does one nudge look like? Look at a single number in W_Q, row 3 column 2. Before: -0.09. After: -0.07. Change: +0.02. Reason: the error signal says "if this number were slightly larger, the Query for 'cat' would have paid more attention to 'sat' — and the prediction would have been a little better." That's it. Just +0.02. A tiny nudge. But multiply that by every number in every grid, across billions of training examples, and the grids slowly transform from random noise into something meaningful.
summary: Concrete example of one weight update: W_Q row 3 col 2 moves from -0.09 to -0.07 because the error signal says a slightly larger value would have improved the prediction.

Result: W_Q now produces Queries that correctly capture "what am I looking for?"; W_K produces Keys that capture "what do I offer?"; W_V produces Values that capture "what info do I carry?".

text: The key insight: nobody programmed these numbers. No human sat down and said "row 3, column 2 should be 0.14." The numbers emerged from the nudging process (backpropagation) over billions of examples. This is what "learned" means in machine learning. The model figured out, on its own, what numbers to put in each grid so that the resulting Q, K, V vectors produce useful attention patterns. Random noise in → billions of nudges → meaningful grids out.
summary: Defines what "learned" means: the W matrix entries emerge from backpropagation, not human design — random noise becomes meaningful through billions of small updates.

text: Chapter 6.12 traces a complete attention example end-to-end with concrete numbers. Sentence: "The cat sat". Embeddings: "The"=[0.1,0.8,-0.2,0.5], "cat"=[0.7,-0.3,0.6,0.1], "sat"=[-0.4,0.5,0.2,0.9]. For "cat", multiplying its embedding by W_Q, W_K, W_V produces Q_cat=[0.4,0.8] (looking for verbs/actions), K_cat=[0.9,0.3] (offering: noun, subject, animal), V_cat=[0.6,-0.2] (semantic content). For "sat", Q_sat=[0.2,0.7] (looking for a subject). Attention score = Q_sat dot K_cat = (0.2x0.9)+(0.7x0.3) = 0.18+0.21 = 0.39, a high match. "sat" absorbs cat's Value and learns "I'm an action performed by a cat."
summary: Walks through a complete attention computation between "sat" and "cat" using real numbers for embeddings, Q, K, V, and dot-product score.

chapterTitle: Step 1 - Compute Q, K, V for Every Word
text: Multiply "I"'s embedding [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] by all three matrices. Each embedding dim multiplies the corresponding row of W, then we sum. For W_Q: dim 1 = (1.0 x 0.15) + (0.5 x 0.12) + (-0.3 x 0.10) + (0.8 x 0.78) = 0.8; dim 2 = (1.0 x -0.03) + (0.5 x 0.78) + (-0.3 x 0.51) + (0.8 x -0.01) = 0.2; so Q_I = [0.8, 0.2]. For W_K: dim 1 = (1.0 x -0.12) + (0.5 x 0.51) + (-0.3 x 0.50) + (0.8 x 0.39) = 0.3; dim 2 = (1.0 x 0.16) + (0.5 x 0.37) + (-0.3 x 0.22) + (0.8 x 0.53) = 0.7; so K_I = [0.3, 0.7]. For W_V: dim 1 = (1.0 x 0.59) + (0.5 x 0.32) + (-0.3 x -0.21) + (0.8 x 0.24) = 1.0; dim 2 = (1.0 x -0.49) + (0.5 x 0.64) + (-0.3 x 0.77) + (0.8 x -0.13) = -0.5; so V_I = [1.0, -0.5]. "love" and "cats" go through the exact same three matrices - different embeddings in, different Q/K/V out.
summary: Worked example multiplying "I"'s embedding by each weight matrix to get Q_I = [0.8, 0.2], K_I = [0.3, 0.7], V_I = [1.0, -0.5].

text: Step 2 of attention computes scores by dot-producting each word's Query with every word's Key. Higher dot product means higher relevance. Doing this for all words produces a square score matrix (3x3 for 'I love cats') where each row shows how much one word attends to every other word. These raw scores feed into the next steps: scaling by sqrt(d_k) and softmax.
summary: Chapter 7.2 introduces attention scores as Q dot K dot products that produce a per-row relevance ranking and a square score matrix.

text: Step 2: Compute attention scores. Each word's Query asks every Key: 'how relevant are you to me?' Using 'I''s Query Q_I = [0.8, 0.2], take dot product with every word's Key. Q_I · K_I = (0.8×0.3) + (0.2×0.7) = 0.24 + 0.14 = 0.38 (lowest). Q_I · K_love = (0.8×0.6) + (0.2×0.4) = 0.48 + 0.08 = 0.56 (medium). Q_I · K_cats = (0.8×0.8) + (0.2×0.5) = 0.64 + 0.10 = 0.74 (highest). From 'I''s perspective: 'cats' most relevant, 'love' medium, 'I' itself lowest.
summary: Computes attention scores by dot-producting one word's Query with every Key, ranking 'I' against 'I/love/cats' to get [0.38, 0.56, 0.74].

text: Full score matrix: every word asks every word. Q_I row scores: [0.38, 0.56, 0.74] (max on K_cats). Q_love row: [0.66, 0.42, 0.53] (max on K_I). Q_cats row: [0.57, 0.54, 0.70] (max on K_cats). Each row = one word asking 'how relevant is each word to me?' Yellow highlights the max in each row. This 3x3 grid is the score matrix produced by Q · K^T.

chapterTitle: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit
text: Chapter 7.3 explains why the attention formula uses K transpose. The superscript T in Q · K^T means transpose - flipping rows into columns. Q is shape 3x2 and K is shape 3x2, so Q · K directly fails because matrix multiplication requires the inner dimensions to match (2 vs 3). Transposing K to 2x3 makes the inner dimensions both 2, producing a 3x3 score matrix where each cell is the dot product of one Query with one Key - the relevance score for every word pair. The transpose is purely a shape-fitting operation: same numbers, just rearranged so every Query can be compared with every Key in one matrix multiplication.
summary: Chapter overview of why K must be transposed in attention so Q and K shapes align for matrix multiplication and produce the full pairwise score matrix.

chapterTitle: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit
text: Transpose means: rows become columns, columns become rows. No math changes - just a flip. Original K is a 3 rows × 2 columns matrix where row 1 = K_I, row 2 = K_love, row 3 = K_cats. After transpose, K^T is 2 rows × 3 columns: column 1 = K_I, column 2 = K_love, column 3 = K_cats. Same numbers. Same order within each word's vector. Just rotated - rows became columns.
summary: Defines transpose as swapping rows and columns and shows how K becomes K^T while preserving all numbers.

chapterTitle: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit
text: The problem: shapes don't match. We want every Query to compute a dot product with every Key. Try Q · K directly. Q is 3 × 2 (3 words, 2 dims). K is 3 × 2 (3 words, 2 dims). 3×2 times 3×2 fails. Matrix multiplication rule: the inner dimensions must match. (3×2) × (3×2) → 2 ≠ 3. Broken. Can't multiply.
summary: Demonstrates that multiplying Q (3x2) by K (3x2) directly fails because inner dimensions 2 and 3 do not match.

chapterTitle: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit
text: The fix: transpose K, then multiply. Q is 3 × 2 (3 words, 2 dims). K^T is 2 × 3 (2 dims, 3 words). (3×2) × (2×3) → inner dims both 2. Works! The result is a 3×3 score matrix - one score for every pair of words. Exactly what we need: every word → every word.
summary: Shows that transposing K to 2x3 makes inner dimensions both 2 so Q dot K^T works and yields a 3x3 score matrix.

chapterTitle: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit
text: What does Q · K^T actually compute? Each cell in the 3×3 result = the dot product of one Query with one Key. That's the relevance score. For Q_I, Q_love, Q_cats against K_I, K_love, K_cats: Q_I row [0.38, 0.56, 0.74] (best on cats), Q_love row [0.66, 0.42, 0.53] (best on I), Q_cats row [0.57, 0.54, 0.70] (best on cats). Row = one word asking. Column = one word answering. Cell = how relevant the answer-word is to the asker.
summary: Each cell of the 3x3 Q dot K^T matrix is one Query dotted with one Key, giving a relevance score for every asker-answerer word pair.

text: Analogy: the classroom seating chart. Q (Queries) = each student's question list - 3 students, each with a question that has 2 traits (topic, urgency). K (Keys) = each student's expertise card - same 3 students, each with expertise described by 2 traits. Q · K^T = the compatibility chart - every student's question matched against every student's expertise → a 3×3 grid of 'how well can you help me?' The transpose just makes this matching possible. Without it, you can't compare every question to every expertise - the shapes won't fit.

text: Softmax formula rendered as mathematical notation: σ(z)_i with sigma symbol, equals e^(z_i) in numerator and Σ_{j=1}^{K} e^(z_j) in denominator. Labels indicate the numerator is 'this word's e^score' and the denominator is 'sum of all e^scores'. Visualizes how raw attention scores become probabilities.
summary: SVG rendering the softmax formula with labeled numerator (e^z_i) and denominator (sum of e^z_j).

text: Chapter 7.4 explains why attention needs softmax. After dot products produce raw scores like [0.38, 0.56, 0.74], we need to convert them into weights to blend Values. Simple division fails because dot products can be negative or exceed 100%. Softmax fixes this: apply e^score to make every value positive (e^x is always positive: e^-3=0.05, e^0=1, e^5=148.4), then divide each by the sum of all e^scores. Result: positive numbers summing to 1.0, ranking preserved, and the highest score is exponentially amplified. Formula: softmax(z_i) = e^z_i / Σ e^z_j. Worked example with [-3, 1, 5] yields [0.03%, 1.8%, 98.2%]. Amplification is good but excessive when scores are huge - motivates scaling by sqrt(d_k) in the next chapter.
summary: Softmax converts raw attention scores into positive probabilities that sum to 1, using e^x to handle negatives and amplify the winner.

text: Budget analogy: you have ₹100 to distribute among three friends based on helpfulness scores Riya=38, Aman=56, Priya=74. Can't give ₹38+₹56+₹74=₹168 total (only have ₹100). Convert to shares of ₹100. Total = 38+56+74 = 168. Riya: 38/168 = 23% → ₹23. Aman: 56/168 = 33% → ₹33. Priya: 74/168 = 44% → ₹44. Sums to ₹100. That's basically what softmax does: convert raw scores into shares that add up to 100%, but with one extra trick.
summary: Budget analogy showing how raw scores get converted into percentages summing to 100%.
queries: how to convert scores to percentages

text: The problem: dot products CAN be negative. Consider scores [-3, 1, 5]. Simple division: total = -3+1+5 = 3. Then -3/3 = -100% (NEGATIVE - can't pay minus attention!), 1/3 = 33%, 5/3 = 167% (more than 100%, nonsense). Simple division is broken. We need something better.
summary: Simple division breaks when scores are negative or the sum is small, producing nonsense percentages.
queries: why simple division fails for attention
why not just divide by sum

text: The Solution: Softmax. Before dividing, put each score through e^score (e ≈ 2.718). This magical function makes ANY number positive. The Softmax Formula: σ(z)_i = e^(z_i) / Σ_{j=1}^{K} e^(z_j). Numerator: this word's e^score. Denominator: sum of all e^scores. In plain English: take e to the power of this score, then divide by the sum of e to the power of every score.
summary: The softmax formula: e^score divided by the sum of all e^scores.
queries: e to the power of score divided by sum

text: Why e^x? It's ALWAYS positive. No matter what you feed it - negative, zero, huge - the output is always a positive number. e^(-3) = 0.05 (negative → small positive). e^(0) = 1.0 (zero → exactly 1). e^(1) = 2.72 (positive → bigger positive). e^(5) = 148.4 (large → much larger). Pattern: higher input → exponentially bigger output. This means softmax naturally amplifies the winner.
summary: e^x maps any real number to a positive value, with larger inputs producing exponentially larger outputs.
queries: e to the negative number positive

text: Full walkthrough scores [-3, 1, 5]. Step 1: Apply e^score to each. e^(-3) = 0.05, e^(1) = 2.72, e^(5) = 148.4. All negative numbers became positive. Step 2: Sum them all (the denominator): 0.05 + 2.72 + 148.4 = 151.17.
summary: Walkthrough steps 1-2: apply e^x to each score, then sum to get the denominator.
queries: compute e to the power of scores

text: Step 3: Divide each e^score by the sum 151.17. A (-3): 0.05/151.17 = 0.03%. B (1): 2.72/151.17 = 1.8%. C (5): 148.4/151.17 = 98.2%. Checks: all positive ✓, sum = 100% ✓, ranking preserved ✓.
summary: Walkthrough step 3: divide each exponentiated score by the sum to get probabilities.

text: Softmax amplifies differences by design. Raw scores -3, 1, 5: gap from 1 to 5 = just 4. After e^x: 0.05, 2.72, 148.4: gap from 2.72 to 148 = 145. The highest score doesn't just win - it dominates. This is good: the most relevant word SHOULD stand out. But if scores are too huge (like 12, 18, 25), softmax amplifies TOO aggressively → 99.99% on one word. That's why we scale first to keep scores in a useful range.
summary: Softmax exponentially amplifies the gap between scores; too-large scores cause one word to dominate at 99.99%.
queries: why divide by sqrt d_k motivation

text: Softmax complete picture. Step 1: e^score for each score - makes everything positive (even e^-1000 is positive). Step 2: divide each by the total sum - now everything adds up to exactly 1.0 (100%). Formula: softmax(z_i) = e^(z_i) / Σ e^(z_j). Output: valid percentages (all positive, sum to 100%) that respect the original ranking. But raw scores can be too big for softmax - that's the next problem to solve.
summary: Two-step softmax recap: exponentiate to make positive, then divide by sum to make probabilities.

text: Chapter 7.5 explains why attention scores must be divided by sqrt(d_k) before softmax. Dot products grow with dimensionality because they sum d_k product terms - 2 dims yield ~0.38, 64 dims yield ~25, 512 dims yield ~180. Large raw scores feed into softmax's e^x and produce near 100% on one token, killing the model's ability to blend information. Dividing by sqrt(d_k) (sqrt(64)=8) brings scores back to a manageable range like [1.51, 2.23, 3.20], yielding softmax weights like [14%, 28%, 58%] that focus on the top token while still attending to others. The square root specifically is chosen because the variance of the dot product of random Q and K grows linearly with d_k, so its standard deviation is sqrt(d_k); dividing normalizes variance back to 1. Applied to the running 2-dim 'I love cats' example, scaling barely changes scores, but in real 64-dim or 512-dim models scaling is the difference between a working model and a broken one.
summary: Chapter overview of scaling attention scores by sqrt(d_k) before softmax to prevent saturation.

text: The problem: dot products grow with dimensions. The dot product multiplies each pair of components and sums all of them, so more dimensions means more pairs being added up. With 2 dimensions, summing 2 terms like 0.24 + 0.14 gives 0.38. With 64 dimensions, summing 64 terms gives roughly 25 - 64 small numbers added up produce a much bigger total. With 512 dimensions, summing 512 terms gives roughly 180 - a huge total. Each individual term might be small (around 0.3 or 0.5), but adding 64 or 512 of them makes the total very large. The score grows just because of more dimensions - not because the words are more related.
summary: Dot products mechanically grow as dimensionality increases because more product terms are summed.

text: Why are big scores a problem? Softmax uses e^score. When scores are big, e^big_number becomes astronomically huge, and one word gets 99.99% of the attention - the model becomes blind to everything else. Compare small scores (2-dim) [0.38, 0.56, 0.74]: attention spreads as I=28%, love=33%, cats=39% - looks at all words, can blend info from multiple sources. Versus huge scores (64-dim) [12.1, 17.8, 25.6]: attention collapses to I=0.1%, love=0.4%, cats=99.6% - 99.6% on one word, others invisible, can't blend info.
summary: Big raw scores cause softmax to put nearly all weight on one token, destroying blending.

text: The fix: divide by sqrt(d_k). If d_k = 64, then sqrt(64) = 8. Dividing brings scores back to a manageable range. Before scaling: [12.1, 17.8, 25.6]. After dividing by sqrt(64) = 8: [1.51, 2.23, 3.20]. Softmax of [1.51, 2.23, 3.20] gives I = 14%, love = 28%, cats = 58%. Result: focuses on the most relevant word (58%) but still gathers info from others (14%, 28%). That's exactly what you want.
summary: Dividing scores by sqrt(d_k) yields a balanced softmax that focuses but still blends.

text: Why sqrt(d_k) specifically? Why not divide by 2 or 100? There's a mathematical reason. When Q and K have random values, the dot product's variance grows proportionally to d_k. The standard deviation = sqrt(d_k). So dividing by sqrt(d_k) brings the variance back to 1 - normalizing scores to a consistent range no matter the dimension. In simple terms: sqrt(d_k) is the exact right amount to undo the growth caused by summing d_k terms. Not too much (all scores become equal), not too little (problem remains).
summary: Sqrt(d_k) is chosen because dot product variance scales with d_k, so dividing by sqrt(d_k) normalizes variance to 1.

text: Chapter 7.6 - Step 4: Softmax to Probabilities. Apply softmax row-by-row to the scaled score matrix to convert raw similarity scores into attention weights that are positive and sum to 1.0 per row. The three-step recipe: exponentiate each score (e^z), sum the exponentials per row, divide each exponential by the row sum. The result is the attention weight matrix - each row tells one word how much of every other word's value to blend in. Next step uses these weights to compute the weighted sum of Values.
summary: Chapter-level summary: softmax turns scaled attention scores into the row-stochastic attention weight matrix used to blend Values in the next step.

text: Apply softmax step-by-step to scaled scores for 'I' looking at each word: [0.27, 0.40, 0.52]. Step 1 - e^score for each: e^0.27 = 1.31, e^0.40 = 1.49, e^0.52 = 1.68. Step 2 - sum all e^scores: 1.31 + 1.49 + 1.68 = 4.48. Step 3 - divide each by sum: I = 1.31 / 4.48 = 0.29, love = 1.49 / 4.48 = 0.33, cats = 1.68 / 4.48 = 0.37. Verification: 0.29 + 0.33 + 0.37 = 1.0.

text: Step 5 of attention: take softmax attention weights and use them to blend Value vectors into a context-aware output. For 'I' with weights 0.29, 0.33, 0.38: multiply each weight by the corresponding V vector (V_I=[1.0,-0.5], V_love=[0.3,0.8], V_cats=[-0.2,0.9]), then sum component-wise to get [0.313, 0.461]. This new vector is no longer just 'I' - it has absorbed 33% of 'love' and 38% of 'cats'. Same weighted sum runs for every word, producing context-aware representations.

text: Chapter 7.9 motivates multi-head attention. A single head must produce one set of attention weights for each word, so when a word like 'sat' has many relationships at once (subject 'cat', location 'mat', time 'last week'), the head is forced to compromise and weaker relations like time can collapse to ~5%. The fix is to run several attention heads in parallel, each with its own W_Q, W_K, W_V. Each head learns to ask a different question (subject-verb, verb-location, temporal, ...) and captures one relationship type fully, so the model preserves all the relationships at once.
summary: Chapter overview: one attention head must compromise across many relations, so the model uses multiple heads with separate W_Q, W_K, W_V to specialize.

text: Single-head attention has a problem. Our short example 'I love cats' has only 3 words and 2 main relationships (who loves? what's loved?). To really see why one head isn't enough, use a longer sentence with more relationships: 'The cat that I adopted last week sat on the mat.' The word 'sat' needs answers to MULTIPLE questions at once: Who sat? -> 'cat' (subject <-> verb relationship). Sat where? -> 'mat' (verb <-> location relationship). Sat when? -> 'last week' (verb <-> time relationship). One word, one position, but three different kinds of relationships it must encode simultaneously.
summary: A single attention head struggles because one word like 'sat' must encode multiple relationship types (subject, location, time) at once.

text: One head produces one set of attention weights, so it is forced to compromise across all relationships. Temporal info ('last week') got only 5% - it is almost lost. A single head cannot focus on all relationships simultaneously: spreading attention over subject, location, and time at once means none get full weight, and weak signals like temporal modifiers can vanish.

text: Solution: give attention MULTIPLE heads - like multiple ears. Each head has its own W_Q, W_K, W_V, so each asks a different question. Head 1 (subject-verb): 'sat' asks 'who is my subject?' -> finds cat (70%). Head 2 (verb-location): 'sat' asks 'where am I happening?' -> finds mat (65%). Head 3 (temporal): 'sat' asks 'when am I happening?' -> finds week (55%). Now NO information is lost. Each head specializes in one relationship and captures it fully, instead of one head trying to average across all of them.
summary: Multi-head attention gives each head its own W_Q, W_K, W_V so different heads specialize in different relationships and no info is lost.

text: Chapter 7.10 explains why Transformers split attention into multiple heads. A single 512-dim attention head produces only one Query arrow per word, forcing it to compromise across many relationship types. The fix: slice the 512-dim embedding into 8 chunks of 64 dims. Each head gets its own smaller W_Q, W_K, W_V (512x64) and runs attention independently. Cost stays the same as one big head (8 x 64 = 512 dims total) but you get 8 different perspectives. Each head specializes (subject-verb, verb-location, temporal, etc.) so no information is lost. Stacking layers (e.g. 96 layers x 8 heads = 768 attention patterns) lets early layers handle syntax and later layers handle reasoning - all self-organized through training.
summary: Splitting a single 512-dim attention head into 8 heads of 64 dims each gives 8 specialized perspectives at the same compute cost.

text: One head = one Query arrow = one direction. Each word's 512-dim embedding produces ONE Query vector through W_Q. That single arrow can only point one way, so it can only ask one type of question. 512-dim embedding -> W_Q (512x512) -> One 512-dim Query. One arrow. One question. Information lost. Solution: don't make one big arrow. Make 8 smaller arrows, each pointing in its own direction.
summary: A single attention head produces one Query arrow per word, limiting it to one question and losing information about other relationships.

text: Split the 512 dimensions into 8 chunks of 64. Imagine the embedding as a long bar sliced into 8 equal pieces (H1 through H8, each 64 dims wide). Total dimensions: 512. Number of heads: 8. Dims per head: 512 / 8 = 64. We don't run the full 512-dim attention 8 times. We split it - same total computation, but 8 independent perspectives.
summary: The 512-dim embedding is sliced into 8 equal chunks of 64 dims, one per head, keeping total compute the same as one big head.

text: Doesn't splitting lose precision? Compare two options. Option A: 8 heads x 512 dims each. Cost: 8 x 512 = 4,096 dims total (8x the compute). Quality per head very high, but diminishing returns past ~64 dims. Option B: 8 heads x 64 dims each (the split). Cost: 8 x 64 = 512 dims total (same budget as one head). Quality per head: 64 dims is plenty - 'is this the subject?' doesn't need 512 numbers to answer. Insight: more dims per head = diminishing returns. Going from 64 to 512 dims per head barely improves quality but costs 8x more.
summary: Giving each head only 64 dims (not 512) preserves quality due to diminishing returns while costing 8x less compute.

text: Each head gets its own W_Q, W_K, W_V. 8 heads = 8 independent sets of weight matrices. Each set is smaller (512x64 instead of 512x512). For each of the 8 heads: W_Q W_K W_V at 512x64 each. Same embedding goes into all 8 heads. Each head's different W matrices produce a different Query arrow - pointing in a different direction, asking a different question.
summary: Each of the 8 heads has its own W_Q, W_K, W_V (each 512x64), producing different Query arrows from the same input embedding.

Head 2 asks 'where?' -> mat 65%. Head 3 asks 'when?' -> week 55%. Head 4 asks 'what comes next?' -> on 60%. Heads 5-8 ask about grammar, coreference, proximity, etc. Each head specializes. Together they capture everything - no information lost.

text: Now zoom out: layers. A Transformer doesn't have just one set of 8 heads. It stacks multiple layers, each with its own 8 heads. Layer 1: word proximity, basic grammar. Layer 2: syntax, clause structure. Layer 3: meaning, sentiment. ... Layer 96: reasoning, generation. 96 layers x 8 heads = 768 attention patterns. Each layer reads the previous layer's output. Early layers see raw words. Later layers see increasingly abstract, context-rich representations. Nobody told the model which layer should handle grammar or reasoning. It organized itself this way during training.
summary: Transformers stack many layers, each with 8 heads; early layers handle grammar and later layers handle reasoning, all self-organized during training.

chapterTitle: Inside Each Head - Full Attention in 64 Dims
text: Chapter 7.11 zooms inside one attention head. Each of the 8 heads runs the full 5-step attention algorithm independently, but on a 64-dim slice instead of the full 512 dims. Because each head has its own learned W_Q, W_K, W_V matrices, the resulting attention weights differ between heads - one head specializes on subject-verb links, another on verb-location, another on temporal references, etc. This division lets the model capture multiple relationships simultaneously that a single 512-dim head would have to compromise on.
summary: Each of the 8 heads runs the full attention pipeline in 64 dims with its own W_Q, W_K, W_V, producing different attention patterns that specialize on different word relationships.

chapterTitle: Inside Each Head - Full Attention in 64 Dims
text:
Each attention head runs the FULL attention algorithm - exactly Steps 1 to 5 we learned, but in 64 dims instead of 512.

For Head 1:
  Q_1 = embedding x W_Q_1   [3 words x 64 dims]
  K_1 = embedding x W_K_1   [3 words x 64 dims]
  V_1 = embedding x W_V_1   [3 words x 64 dims]

  scores_1  = Q_1 . K_1^T / sqrt(64)   [3x3 score matrix]
  weights_1 = softmax(scores_1)        [3x3 attention weights]
  output_1  = weights_1 x V_1          [3 words x 64 dims]

The same computation runs for all 8 heads. Each head produces a [3 words x 64 dims] output.
summary: Each head executes the same 5-step attention pipeline (Q, K, V projection; scaled dot-product scores; softmax; weighted sum) but in 64 dims, producing a 3x64 output per head.

chapterTitle: Inside Each Head - Full Attention in 64 Dims
text:
The KEY insight: each head's attention weights are different, because their W_Q, W_K, W_V are different. Here is what the word 'sat' attends to in different heads:

Head 1 - subject-verb head, 'sat' attends to: cat 70%, mat 8%, week 5%, others 17%.
Head 2 - verb-location head, 'sat' attends to: cat 10%, mat 65%, week 8%, others 17%.
Head 3 - temporal head, 'sat' attends to: cat 8%, mat 7%, week 55%, others 30%.

Each head found what the single head missed. Head 3 captured 'last week' at 55%, while a single head only gave it 5%. Different W matrices produce different attention distributions, letting each head specialize on a different relationship.
summary: Different W_Q, W_K, W_V per head produce different attention distributions, so each head specializes on a different relationship (subject-verb, verb-location, temporal) and recovers info a single head loses.

text: Chapter 7.12 covers Concat + W_O, the final step of multi-head attention that blends the 8 separate head outputs into a single context-aware vector per word. After 8 heads each produce their own 64-dim result for each word, the outputs are concatenated end-to-end into a 512-dim vector - but each head's section is sealed, isolated from the others (like 8 sealed envelopes taped together). W_O is a learned 512x512 weight matrix that, when multiplied with the concatenated vector, mixes information across all heads so every output dimension carries a blend of all 8 heads' findings. W_O is randomly initialized, then nudged by backpropagation across billions of training examples until it discovers useful blending patterns. Different layers learn different W_O matrices specialized to their level of abstraction (syntax in early layers, reasoning in deeper ones). For the running example 'I love cats', after W_O 'love' becomes a unified vector encoding 'affection from I directed at cats with positive sentiment' - the context-aware representation we set out to build.
summary: How concatenation and the learned W_O matrix blend 8 separate head outputs into one rich context-aware vector per word.

text:
After the 8 heads finish, each word has 8 separate results. For 'love' in 'I love cats':
Head 1 gave: [0.3, 0.5] - figured out 'I' is the one who loves
Head 2 gave: [-0.2, 0.8] - figured out 'cats' is what's loved
Head 3 gave: [0.7, -0.1] - figured out the sentiment is positive
... 8 results total.
We stick them end-to-end (concatenate):
[0.3, 0.5, -0.2, 0.8, 0.7, -0.1, ..., 0.1, 0.4]
One long list - 512 numbers. But there's a problem.

text: The problem - these results are like 8 sealed envelopes. 8 detectives investigated the same crime. Each wrote findings and sealed them: Head 1 'suspect is male, 30s', Head 2 'happened at the park', Head 3 'Tuesday evening', Head 4 'weapon was a knife'. Tape them together (concatenation): a bar with thick black walls between each section - they can't see each other. They're physically side by side, but each section is still sealed. H1's numbers have no idea what H2 found. H3 can't see H4. They're taped together but not talking. Boss asks: 'one-line summary?' No envelope has the full picture. H1 only knows the suspect. H2 only knows the location. Nobody can combine them. We need someone to open all the envelopes, read everything, and write one combined summary.
summary: Concatenation alone leaves head outputs isolated - like 8 sealed envelopes taped together that cannot share information.

text:
W_O - the person who opens all the envelopes. W_O is just another weight matrix - a grid of numbers, same as W_Q, W_K, W_V. Multiply input by grid, get output. Nothing new in how it works. But what it does is special. When you multiply the concatenated list by W_O, every number in the output is computed from all numbers in the input.
Before W_O: Position 1 = 0.3 (only knows Head 1's finding).
After W_O: Position 1 = blend of 0.3 + (-0.2) + 0.7 + ... all positions = knows Head 1 + Head 2 + Head 3 + ... everything.
It's like the combined summary: 'A male suspect in his 30s used a knife at the park on Tuesday evening.' One sentence that contains information from all envelopes. After W_O, every single position in the output carries a mix of what all 8 heads found. The envelopes have been opened, read, and combined.
summary: W_O multiplies the concatenated vector so every output dimension becomes a learned mix of all 8 heads' findings.

text: Where did W_O's numbers come from? Same exact process as all other grids: 1) Start with random numbers. 2) Model tries to predict next word, gets it wrong. 3) Error flows backward, nudges each number in W_O. 4) 'If this number were 0.31 instead of 0.30, the blending would have been better → change to 0.31'. 5) Repeat billions of times. 6) W_O settles into numbers that produce good blends. The training discovers the best way to combine the 8 heads' findings into one useful output. Nobody programs this - it emerges from training.
summary: W_O starts random and is nudged by backpropagation across billions of examples until it learns optimal head-blending.

text: Back to 'I love cats' - what W_O does for 'love': After 8 heads run, 'love's concatenated vector has: Dims 1-64: Head 1 found 'I' is the subject who loves. Dims 65-128: Head 2 found 'cats' is the object being loved. Dims 129-192: Head 3 found this is a positive sentiment. Without W_O: each section is isolated. Dim 1 has no idea 'cats' is involved. After W_O: every dimension of 'love's output encodes the combined meaning: 'affection from I directed at cats, with positive sentiment.' One rich, integrated vector. This is what we set out to do in Chapter 1 - transform 'love' from an isolated word into a context-aware representation.

text: W_O is LEARNED - not hardcoded. The model discovers during training which combinations of head outputs are useful. Different layers learn different W_O matrices: Layer 1's W_O might focus on combining basic syntax patterns. Layer 3's W_O might focus on combining semantic relationships. Layer 6's W_O might focus on combining abstract reasoning. Nobody programs what to combine. W_O's 512×512 = 262,144 weights are all learned through backpropagation - the same process from chapters 1.3-1.9.

chapterTitle: Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: Chapter 7.13 explains why Transformers use 8 attention heads as the sweet spot, counts the parameters per attention layer, and shows the surprising result that multi-head attention has the same parameter count as single-head. Per layer: W_Q, W_K, W_V each contribute 8 heads x 32,768 = 262,144 parameters, plus W_O at 512 x 512 = 262,144, totaling 1,048,576 (~1 million) per layer. Multi-head reorganizes the same parameter budget as single-head into 8 independent groups. The chapter then scales this up: original Transformer (2017) had 6 layers (~6M attention params), GPT-2 (2019) 48 layers (~48M), GPT-3 (2020) 96 layers (175 billion total params). More layers build deeper, increasingly rich representations using the same mechanism stacked.
summary: Why 8 heads is the sweet spot, parameter counts per attention layer (~1M), and how stacking layers scales to billion-parameter models like GPT-3.

chapterTitle: Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: Why 8 heads? Why not 4 or 16? There is a tradeoff - the total dims (512) get divided equally among heads. With 16 heads: 512 / 16 = 32 dims each. Pro: more pattern types. Con: each head weaker. With 4 heads: 512 / 4 = 128 dims each. Pro: each head powerful. Con: fewer patterns. 8 heads x 64 dims = sweet spot. Enough capacity per head (64 dims) plus enough perspectives (8 types).
summary: Choosing 8 heads is a tradeoff between number of perspectives and capacity per head; 8 x 64 balances both.

chapterTitle: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: W_O (and every weight matrix W_Q, W_K, W_V, embeddings) changes during training as gradients nudge it across billions of examples, then becomes completely frozen once training ends and the model is saved. At inference, the same W_O is reused for every input, every user, every sentence. Different outputs come from different inputs flowing through the same constant machine - like a coffee machine producing different coffees from different beans. Activations (Q, K, V vectors, scores, softmax weights, blended outputs) are computed live per input and discarded.
summary: Chapter 7.14 summary: W_O is updated during training but frozen at inference, with input-dependent activations producing varied outputs from constant weights.

chapterTitle: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: During training: W_O changes. Every time the model sees a new batch of sentences and makes prediction errors, every number in W_O gets nudged slightly. Over billions of examples, W_O gradually improves. It changes millions of times during training, getting better at blending heads. After training is done: W_O is FROZEN, completely constant. The model is saved to a file, W_O's numbers are written to disk and they NEVER change again. When you use the model, W_O is loaded from disk and used as-is.
summary: Contrasts training (W_O nudged by gradients each batch) vs post-training (W_O frozen on disk and never updated).

chapterTitle: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: This is true for all grids, not just W_O. CONSTANT after training (stored on disk, never changes): Embedding dictionary, W_Q1 through W_Q8 (same grid for every input), W_K1 through W_K8 (same grid for every input), W_V1 through W_V8 (same grid for every input), W_O (same grid for every input), all other layer weights. Different for every input (computed live, then thrown away): Q, K, V vectors (different because the words are different), attention scores (different because Q, K are different), softmax weights (different because scores are different), blended outputs (different because weights and V are different), final output (different because everything above is different).
summary: Lists which transformer tensors are constant frozen weights versus which are recomputed per input.

chapterTitle: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: Why do different sentences get different results if the grids are constant? Because the grids are like a coffee machine. The machine (W_O) is always the same. But the coffee beans you put in (your sentence) are different each time. Same machine + different beans = different coffee. Same W_O × 'I love cats' embeddings = one output. Same W_O × 'dogs are great' embeddings = completely different output. The grids don't need to change. They've already learned the GENERAL SKILL of 'how to blend heads' or 'how to extract queries.' That skill works on ANY input. Just like a coffee machine doesn't need to be rebuilt for each type of bean - it already knows how to grind and brew.
summary: Uses coffee machine analogy to explain how constant weights still produce different outputs because the input embeddings differ.

chapterTitle: Attention Flow - Shapes at Every Step
text: Chapter 7.15 traces every matrix shape through one complete attention computation at teaching scale (N=3, d_model=4, d_k=2, 2 heads). Per head: X (3x4) is multiplied by three weight matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V (each 4x2) to produce Q, K, V (each 3x2). Scores Q dot K^T are 3x3, scaled by sqrt(d_k) and softmaxed row-wise into attention weights (3x3, rows sum to 1). Weights times V gives the head output (3x2). For multi-head, h head outputs are concatenated along columns into a 3x4 block, then multiplied by W_O (4x4) to give the final 3x4 attention output - same shape as X. The same shape rules hold in real Transformers (N=seq length, d_model=512, h=8, d_k=64).
summary: Chapter-level overview of how shapes flow through single-head and multi-head attention from X all the way to the final attention output.

chapterTitle: Attention Flow - Shapes at Every Step
text: Single-head attention flow at teaching scale: 3 tokens 'I love cats', d_model = 4, d_k = 2, 2 heads. Start with X (3x4 matrix, one row per word). Copy X and multiply by three different weight matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V (each 4x2) to get Q, K, V (each 3x2 - one row per word, 2-dim per head). Then compute Q dot K-transpose, divide by sqrt(d_k), apply softmax row-wise to get the attention weights matrix (3x3, one row per word, columns also per word). Finally multiply weights (3x3) by V (3x2) to produce the head output (3x2). That entire pipeline is ONE head. The model runs this in parallel for every head, each with its own W_Q, W_K, W_V. Each word keeps its row position (row 1 = I, row 2 = love, row 3 = cats) all the way through.
summary: Walks through every matrix shape in one full single-head attention pipeline from input X through Q, K, V, scores, softmax weights, and final head output.
