text: Layer-by-layer math: every decoder layer performs cross-attention with the exact same K and V but a different Q. Layer 1: Attn(Q_1, K, V) = softmax(Q_1 · K^T / sqrt(64)) · V. Layer 2: same with Q_2. Layer 6: same with Q_6. Q_n comes from decoder layer n's output. Dimensions (d_model=512, heads=8, d_k=64): Encoder output [3 x 512]; W_K projection [512 x 64] per head; K (cached) [3 x 64]; V (cached) [3 x 64]; Decoder layer n [4 x 512]; Q_n per layer [4 x 64]; Q_n · K^T scores [4 x 3]. Efficiency: encoder runs once, K and V computed once, only Q changes per decoder layer - for a 1000-token input this saves computing K and V six times over (6x reduction in encoder-side computation). Each decoder layer has 6 separate weight matrices: W_Q, W_K, W_V for masked self-attention plus W_Q, W_K, W_V for cross-attention - all learned independently.
summary: Each decoder layer reuses cached K and V but computes a fresh Q via softmax(Q_n·K^T/sqrt(64))·V, with dimensions and a 6-matrix-per-layer weight count showing why the design is efficient.

text: Where cross-attention lives. Decoder-Only models (GPT, Claude, LLaMA): no cross-attention at all - there's no encoder, so nothing to cross-attend to. The decoder handles everything with just self-attention; prompt and response are one continuous sequence. Encoder-Decoder models (Original Transformer, T5, BART): cross-attention in every decoder layer. Each decoder layer has three sub-layers in order: 1) Masked self-attention - Hindi looks at Hindi (causal); 2) Cross-attention - Hindi looks at English (full); 3) Feed-forward network - process each position independently. The green 'K, V from encoder' arrow in the architecture diagram connects to this cross-attention sub-layer. Cross-attention is elegant but optional - modern LLMs prove world-class results without it, but for tasks with clear input/output separation (translation, summarization) encoder-decoder with cross-attention remains powerful.
summary: Decoder-only LLMs skip cross-attention entirely while encoder-decoder models put cross-attention as the second of three sub-layers in every decoder block, and modern LLMs show it's powerful but optional.

text: Chapter 9.4 explains the Transformer's output head: the final stage that converts a 512-dim hidden state into a predicted token. It covers the Linear layer (a 512 x 50,257 weight matrix W producing logits via dot products with each vocabulary word), Softmax (exponentiating logits and normalizing so probabilities sum to 1), Temperature scaling (dividing logits by T to control how peaked the distribution is), Sampling strategies (Greedy, Top-k, Top-p / nucleus), Weight tying (reusing the embedding matrix transposed as W to save 25.7M parameters), and how the head behaves differently during training (every position predicts) vs inference (only the last position).
summary: Chapter-level overview of the Transformer output head: Linear, softmax, temperature, sampling, weight tying, and training vs inference behavior.

text: After all transformer layers, every position holds a rich, context-aware vector of 512 numbers. This vector encodes everything the model understood about that position. But we need an actual word, not 512 numbers. The model's vocab has 50,257 possible tokens. How does it choose one? Decoder output is [0.82, -0.31, 0.57, 0.14, ...] (512 numbers); we must map this to one of 50,257 words. That is exactly what the output head does. It has two parts: a Linear layer and Softmax. These are the two blocks sitting at the very top of the architecture diagram.
summary: Frames the gap: a 512-dim hidden state must be turned into one of 50,257 vocabulary words via the Linear + Softmax output head.

text: The Linear layer is a weight matrix W with 512 rows and 50,257 columns. Each column represents one word in the vocabulary. The matrix multiplication: hidden state [1 x 512] times W [512 x 50,257] equals logits [1 x 50,257]. One matrix multiply. That is it. No activation function, no non-linearity - just multiply the 512-dim hidden state by this giant matrix and you get 50,257 raw scores, one for every word in the vocabulary. This single matrix has 512 x 50,257 = 25.7 million parameters. It is the second largest weight matrix in the model (after the embedding table).
summary: The Linear layer is a single 512 x 50,257 matrix multiply with no activation, producing one raw logit per vocabulary word.

text: Each logit is a dot product between the hidden state and one column of W. A high dot product means the hidden state is pointing in the same direction as that word's template. Computing one logit: hidden state [0.82, -0.31, 0.57, 0.14, ...]; W column for 'cats' [0.71, -0.25, 0.63, 0.08, ...]; logit = (0.82)(0.71) + (-0.31)(-0.25) + (0.57)(0.63) + ... = 4.2. This happens for all 50,257 words simultaneously: cats +4.2, dogs +3.1, the +1.5, love -0.3, banana -2.1, ... and 50,252 more words. High logit = the model's hidden state is similar to that word's representation. Low or negative = dissimilar. But these raw numbers are hard to interpret - we need probabilities.
summary: Each logit is a dot product between the hidden state and a vocabulary column of W; worked example shows cats=4.2, dogs=3.1, banana=-2.1.

text: Raw logits can be any number: positive, negative, huge, tiny. Softmax converts them into probabilities that are all positive and sum to exactly 1. The softmax formula: P(word_i) = e^(logit_i) / sum over j of e^(logit_j). Exponentiate each logit, then divide by the sum of all exponentiated logits. Watch the transformation: raw logits cats +4.2, dogs +3.1, the +1.5, love -0.3, banana -2.1 become after softmax cats 70.8%, dogs 23.6%, the 4.8%, love 0.8%, banana 0.1%. Notice how softmax amplifies differences. A logit gap of just 1.1 between 'cats' (4.2) and 'dogs' (3.1) becomes a 3x probability difference (71% vs 24%). The exponential function makes the winner win big.
summary: Softmax formula P(i)=e^logit_i / sum e^logit_j turns raw logits into a probability distribution and amplifies gaps between top scores.

text: Before softmax, divide each logit by a number called temperature (T). This controls how peaked or spread out the resulting probabilities are. Formula: softmax( logit / temperature ). T = 0.2 (Focused, almost deterministic): cats 100%, dogs 0%, the 0%. T = 1.0 (Default, balanced): cats 71%, dogs 24%, the 5%. T = 2.0 (Creative, more spread out): cats 50%, dogs 29%, the 13%. Low temperature (0.2): divides logits by 0.2, making differences 5x larger before softmax. The top word dominates. High temperature (2.0): divides by 2.0, shrinking differences. More words get a fair chance. Temperature = 0 would be pure greedy (pick the top word every time).
summary: Temperature T divides logits before softmax; low T sharpens to greedy, high T flattens for creativity, T=0 is pure greedy.

Why does high temperature increase diversity?

text: After softmax gives us probabilities, we still need to decide which word to actually pick. Three main strategies. Greedy decoding: always pick the highest probability word. Simple but boring - same input always produces same output (e.g., always pick cats 71%). Top-k sampling (e.g., k = 3): keep only the k highest-probability words, re-normalize their probabilities, randomly sample from them. Balances quality with variety. Example with k=3: keep cats 71%, dogs 24%, the 5%; drop love 1%. Top-p sampling / nucleus (e.g., p = 0.9): keep adding words (highest probability first) until cumulative probability reaches p. More adaptive than Top-k - uses fewer words when model is confident, more when uncertain. Example: cats 71% (cum 71%), dogs 24% (cum 95%) - stop at 95%. In practice, most APIs combine temperature + Top-p. ChatGPT defaults: temperature = 1.0, Top-p = 1.0. For more focused output: temperature = 0.7, Top-p = 0.9.
summary: Three sampling strategies - Greedy, Top-k, Top-p (nucleus) - decide which word to pick from the probability distribution; APIs typically combine temperature with Top-p.
queries: How does nucleus sampling adapt to model confidence?
Sampling strategies for LLM next token

Picking the next token from softmax probabilities

text: Surprising fact: the output Linear layer's weight matrix W is the same matrix as the embedding table from the input - just transposed. Embedding (input): word -> vector, shape [50,257 x 512]. Linear (output): vector -> word scores, shape [512 x 50,257]. SAME MATRIX. Why does it work? The embedding table maps each word to a point in 512-dim space. The output layer asks: 'which word's point is closest to this hidden state?' That is the same operation in reverse. If embedding for 'cats' is [0.71, -0.25, 0.63, ...] and hidden state is [0.82, -0.31, 0.57, ...], the dot product is high because they point in similar directions. The model is literally asking: which embedding vector is this hidden state most similar to? Parameter savings: without tying, 50,257 x 512 = 25.7M extra parameters. With tying: zero extra parameters - the output layer reuses the embedding matrix. GPT-2 and most modern LLMs use this trick.
summary: Weight tying reuses the input embedding matrix (transposed) as the output Linear weights, saving 25.7M parameters because both ops measure similarity in the same space.

Embedding matrix transposed as output projection
How is the output layer related to the embedding table?
Weight sharing between input and output of LLM
Why is the output layer asking about similarity?
Same matrix used twice in Transformer

text: Complete output pipeline end to end: hidden state (512 dims) -> Linear (W transposed, 512 -> 50,257) -> logits (50,257 scores) -> divide by T (temperature) -> softmax (probabilities) -> sample (Top-k / Top-p) -> next token ('cats'). During training: the model computes this output head at every position simultaneously. At position 1 ('I'), it should predict 'love'. At position 2 ('love'), it should predict 'cats'. Cross-entropy loss measures how wrong each prediction was, and backprop updates the weights. During inference: only the last position's hidden state goes through the output head. That is the position where the model predicts the next token. This single prediction gets sampled, appended to the input, and the whole process repeats - one token at a time. Big picture: the entire transformer - embeddings, positional encoding, attention, FFN, layer stacking - exists to produce one good hidden state at the final position. The output head then converts that single 512-dim vector into a probability distribution over 50,257 words. One matrix multiply, one softmax.
summary: End-to-end output pipeline (Linear -> /T -> softmax -> sample) plus contrast between training (all positions, cross-entropy) and inference (only the last position, then loop).

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Chapter 9.5 walks through what a Transformer actually does end-to-end on the example sentence "I love cats". Six pipeline steps: (1) tokens become embedding vectors that capture meaning in isolation; (2) positional encoding is added so order matters; (3) self-attention via Q/K/V lets each word look at others and reshapes its vector by context; (4) Add and Norm plus a feed-forward network refine each word independently with residual connections; (5) the block repeats N times (GPT-2 has 12 layers, GPT-3 has 96, LLaMA 3 70B has 80) with early layers learning syntax and later layers learning semantics and reasoning; (6) the last position's vector goes through Linear plus Softmax to produce a probability distribution over the vocabulary, predicting the next token ("very" at 35%). The key insight: a generic embedding for "love" exits as a context-specific vector meaning "first-person affection directed at cats."
summary: End-to-end six-step Transformer pipeline from tokens to next-token prediction using "I love cats."

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: What is a Transformer actually doing? You give it a sentence: "I love cats". It needs to understand what each word means in context. The word "love" alone could mean many things - romantic love, love of food, a tennis score. But in "I love cats" it specifically means personal affection for cats. The Transformer figures this out through a pipeline of steps. Visual: three colored word boxes (I red, love purple, cats cyan) flow into a green "Contextual Understanding" box.
summary: The Transformer's job is to turn each word into a context-specific meaning, like resolving "love" to affection-for-cats.

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 1: Each word becomes a vector (embedding). The model's embedding table maps each token to a vector of numbers. These numbers capture meaning in isolation - "love" doesn't know about "I" or "cats" yet. Examples shown with 4 numbers each (real models use 512 dims): "I" -> [0.2, -0.5, 0.8, 0.1, ...]; "love" -> [0.9, 0.1, -0.3, 0.6, ...]; "cats" -> [0.4, 0.7, 0.2, -0.4, ...]. Similar words get similar vectors - "cat" and "dog" are nearby in the 512-dimensional space. But "love" here is generic - it could be any kind of love.
summary: Embeddings convert each token into a 512-dim vector that encodes meaning but not yet context.

what does an embedding table do
why do similar words have similar embeddings
why is love generic without context
do embeddings know about other words

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 2: Add position information. Without position, "I love cats" and "cats love I" look identical to the model - same words, same embeddings. Order matters. Positional encoding adds WHERE each word sits in the sentence. Example for "love" at position 1: embedding [0.9, 0.1, -0.3, ...] + position [0.84, 0.54, 0.00, ...] = positioned [1.74, 0.64, -0.30, ...]. Result: each word now knows WHAT it means AND WHERE it sits. "love" at position 1 is different from "love" at position 5.
summary: Adding a positional encoding vector to each embedding injects word order so identical words at different positions become distinguishable.
queries: why do transformers need positional encoding
I love cats vs cats love I
how is position added to embeddings
why same words different positions matter

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 3: Self-attention - words look at each other. This is the magic step. Each word sends a Query ("what am I looking for?"), every word offers a Key ("here is what I can offer"), and the model computes attention scores - how relevant is each word to every other? Attention scores for "I love cats" (rows = querying word, columns = attended-to word): I -> [I:0.60, love:0.25, cats:0.15]; love -> [I:0.15, love:0.30, cats:0.55]; cats -> [I:0.10, love:0.55, cats:0.35]. "love" attends strongly to "cats" (0.55) - loves WHAT? And "cats" attends to "love" (0.55) - cats in what context? After the weighted sum of Values: "love" is no longer generic. It now means "I-have-affection-for-cats" - encoded as a completely new vector shaped by its context.
summary: Self-attention uses Q/K/V to compute relevance scores between every pair of words and rewrites each word's vector as a weighted sum of context.

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 4: Add & Norm + FFN. Two important things happen after attention. Pipeline shown: Attention Output (context-aware vectors) + Original residual (pre-attention vectors) -> Add & Norm (stabilize values) -> FFN (two linear + GELU) -> Add & Norm (stabilize again). Residual connection: adds the ORIGINAL vector (before attention) back to the output. This prevents information loss and helps gradients flow during training. Without it, deep networks cannot learn. FFN (Feed-Forward Network): two linear layers with GELU activation. Processes each word INDEPENDENTLY. Attention mixed words together - the FFN thinks about each word alone, creating richer features.
summary: After attention, residual + LayerNorm stabilize values and an independent two-layer FFN with GELU enriches each word's representation.

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 5: Repeat N times. One attention + FFN block is ONE transformer layer. Real models repeat this block many times. Each layer refines understanding further. Layer 1-2 (~15%): Basic syntax, word boundaries. Layer 3-6 (~35%): Grammar, subject-verb agreement. Layer 7-10 (~65%): Semantic meaning, analogies. Layer 11-12 (~95%): Complex reasoning, long-range dependencies. GPT-2: 12 layers. GPT-3: 96 layers. LLaMA 3 70B: 80 layers. After all layers: "love" has been refined from "generic positive emotion" to "specific first-person affection directed at small furry animals in this particular sentence."
summary: Stacking transformer blocks N times progressively refines representations from syntax in early layers to semantics and reasoning in later layers.

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 6: Predict the next token. After all layers, each position holds a rich context-aware vector (512 numbers). The model takes the LAST position's vector and projects it through a Linear layer. Linear: 512 dims -> 50,257 (one score per word in vocabulary). Softmax: converts raw scores into probabilities that sum to 1. Top predictions after "I love cats": very 35%, so 22%, a 12%, the 8%, really 5%. The highest probability word becomes the prediction. The model picks "very" - "I love cats very..." and then repeats the whole process for the next token.
summary: The last position's hidden state goes through Linear (512 -> 50,257) and softmax to produce next-token probabilities, then the cycle repeats.

chapterTitle: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Step 7: The complete pipeline end to end. "I love cats" -> Tokenize -> Embed -> +Position -> Self-Attention -> Add & Norm -> FFN -> Add & Norm -> xN layers -> Linear -> Softmax -> "very" (35%). The key insight: the word "love" entered as a generic 512-number vector and exited as a completely different 512-number vector that encodes "first-person affection directed at cats in a casual sentence." This is what "understanding context" means - every word's vector is reshaped by all the other words around it. That reshaping is the pipeline you just saw.
summary: End-to-end the Transformer reshapes a generic word vector into a context-specific one through tokenize/embed/position/attention/FFN repeated N times then Linear+Softmax.

text: Chapter 9.6 walks through the complete encoder-decoder Transformer training (prefill) pipeline using 'The cat sat on the mat' as the encoder input and '<s> The cat' as the decoder input. All encoder tokens flow as a single 6x512 matrix through tokenization, embedding lookup, sinusoidal positional encoding, then encoder self-attention (Q=XW_Q, K=XW_K, V=XW_V, softmax(QK^T/sqrt(d_k))V, W_O), Add & LayerNorm, FFN (3->6->3 with ReLU), Add & LayerNorm. Encoder output (6 context-enriched vectors) is computed once and reused as K,V for cross-attention. The decoder runs all 3 target tokens in parallel via teacher forcing with a causal mask, applies masked self-attention, cross-attention to encoder K,V, FFN, and an output head that produces predictions at EVERY position simultaneously. Cross-entropy losses are averaged across positions (e.g. -log(0.78), -log(0.71), -log(0.72) -> 0.31). Self-attention mixes tokens; FFN and Add&Norm operate per-vector with no cross-token interaction.
summary: Full training-mode pipeline of an encoder-decoder Transformer with parallel processing, teacher forcing, causal mask, and per-position loss.

text: Step 0 - Raw Input. Training/Prefill Mode: all tokens flow as one matrix through every layer simultaneously. Raw input string: "The cat sat on the mat". This is the unprocessed text before any neural processing. Step 1 - Tokenization (lookup table, no neural network): split text into tokens, map each to an integer ID. The -> ID:1042, cat -> ID:5829, sat -> ID:3820, on -> ID:322, the -> ID:1042, mat -> ID:9382. Each word becomes one integer. Nothing neural yet. Note 'The' (capital, position 0) and 'the' (lowercase, position 4) here would actually map to different IDs in a real BPE; the diagram uses ID 1042 for both to illustrate that identical surface tokens map to the same ID.
summary: Raw text becomes integer token IDs through a non-neural lookup table.

text: Step 2 - Embedding Lookup (one matrix row per token). Each token ID selects a row from the embedding matrix (learned during training). For the 6 tokens we get 6 vectors (shown as 3-dim for clarity, really 512-dim): The=[0.21,-0.87,0.45], cat=[0.62,0.34,-0.18], sat=[-0.55,0.91,0.12], on=[0.08,-0.43,0.77], the=[0.21,-0.87,0.45], mat=[0.73,0.15,-0.61]. ALL 6 lookups happen at once - just row selection from a table. 'The' (ID 1042) and 'the' (ID 1042) get the SAME embedding - same word, same row. Position information is not yet encoded.
summary: Each token ID indexes a learned embedding matrix to produce a 512-dim vector, with identical IDs yielding identical embeddings.

text: Step 3 - Add Positional Encoding (pure math, not learned). sin/cos values based on position index, added element-wise to embeddings. Resulting position-aware vectors: 'The'(pos 0)=[0.21,-0.87,1.45], cat(pos 1)=[1.47,0.34,-0.17], sat(pos 2)=[-0.46,0.91,0.13], on(pos 3)=[0.16,-0.43,1.77], the(pos 4)=[0.30,-0.87,0.46], mat(pos 5)=[1.58,0.15,-0.60]. ALL 6 additions happen at once - element-wise, no dependencies. Now 'The'(pos 0)=[0.21,-0.87,1.45] vs 'the'(pos 4)=[0.30,-0.87,0.46] - now different! Position information is now baked into each vector so identical words at different positions become distinguishable.
summary: Sinusoidal positional encoding is added element-wise to embeddings so identical tokens at different positions become distinct.

text: Encoder Block (x6 in original Transformer, x96 in GPT-scale). Input: 6x512 matrix. Encoder Self-Attention is BIDIRECTIONAL - NO causal mask. Every token can attend to every other token (including future ones). Step A: Q = X * W_Q (one matrix multiply, 6x3 * 3x3 = 6x3) producing 6 Query vectors. Step B: K = X * W_K -> 6 Key vectors. Step C: V = X * W_V -> 6 Value vectors. Step D: Q . K^T -> Attention Scores (every token to every token). NO causal mask - 'The' can look at 'mat', 'sat' can look at 'cat'. Step E: Scale by 1/sqrt(d_k), Softmax, Weighted Sum of V. Formula: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V. Step F: x W_O (learned) -> Multi-Head Output. All operations on all 6 tokens happen simultaneously as batched matmuls.
summary: Encoder self-attention computes Q,K,V via learned projections, applies scaled dot-product softmax bidirectionally with no mask, then projects through W_O.

text: Add & LayerNorm 1: residual + normalize. Formula: LayerNorm(X + Attention(X)) - applied independently per vector, all at once. Then Feed-Forward Network (FFN): same weights applied to EACH vector independently. No cross-token mixing. All 6 go through simultaneously as a batched matrix multiply. FFN Layer 1: 3 -> 6 dims + ReLU (expand). FFN Layer 2: 6 -> 3 dims (compress back). Then Add & LayerNorm 2 (second residual + normalize). Encoder Output: 6 context-enriched vectors (6x512 matrix). These become K and V for the decoder cross-attention. Computed ONCE, reused every decoder step. Each vector now 'knows' about all other words thanks to bidirectional self-attention.
summary: After attention, residual+LayerNorm is added, then a per-token FFN expands and compresses, followed by another Add&Norm yielding context-enriched encoder output.

text: Decoder Block (x6 in original Transformer). During TRAINING: all decoder tokens processed in parallel (teacher forcing). Showing decoder input: <s> The cat - predicting next token at each position. Decoder Input is the target sequence shifted right + positional encoding. Tokens used: '<s>'=[0.5,-0.1,0.8], 'The'=[0.95,0.42,-0.31], 'cat'=[1.47,0.34,-0.17] (already embedded + positional encoding applied). Then Masked Self-Attention (causal - can only look backward): 'cat' can see '<s>' and 'The', but NOT future tokens. Prevents cheating during training. Compute decoder Q, K, V via x W_Q, x W_K, x W_V. Apply Q . K^T with CAUSAL MASK (lower triangle only): position 0 attends to {0}, position 1 to {0,1}, position 2 to {0,1,2}. Softmax + Weighted Sum of V -> Add & LayerNorm.
summary: Decoder runs target tokens in parallel via teacher forcing, applying causal-masked self-attention so each position only sees past tokens.

text: Cross-Attention: Decoder asks questions, Encoder provides answers. Q = from decoder. K, V = from encoder output (computed once, reused). This is how the decoder 'reads' the input sentence. Decoder x W_Q -> Cross-Attention Query (3 vectors from decoder). K and V come from ENCODER OUTPUT (the 6 context-enriched vectors). No mask - full access. Cross-Attention computation: decoder Q . encoder K^T, weighted sum of encoder V. Each decoder token can attend to ALL 6 encoder tokens (no causal mask here). 'cat' can now see encoder 'The', 'cat', 'sat', 'on', 'the', 'mat' - the full input. Then Add & LayerNorm.
summary: Cross-attention uses decoder Q against encoder K,V so each decoder position can attend to every encoder token without masking.

why no mask in cross attention

text: Decoder FFN (same structure as encoder FFN). Each vector processed independently. Same weights for all positions. FFN Layer 1: 3 -> 6 + ReLU. FFN Layer 2: 6 -> 3. Then Add & LayerNorm (final decoder layer output). The FFN does not mix tokens - it applies the same expand-then-compress nonlinear transformation to each vector independently. Followed by the third Add & LayerNorm in the decoder block, completing one decoder layer. In the original Transformer this whole decoder block (masked self-attention, cross-attention, FFN with three Add&Norms) repeats x6.
summary: Decoder FFN mirrors the encoder FFN, applying a per-vector expand-ReLU-compress transformation followed by a final Add&Norm.

text: Output Head - TRAINING: Every Position Predicts the Next Token. During training, we compute loss at ALL positions simultaneously (not just the last one). WHY USE ALL POSITIONS? Position 0 (<s>) predicts 'The', position 1 ('The') predicts 'cat', position 2 ('cat') predicts 'sat'. This gives 3 training signals from 1 forward pass. Causal mask ensures each position only sees past tokens, so every prediction is fair. Linear + Softmax at each position. Position 0: probabilities 'The'=78%, 'A'=12%, 'cat'=4%. Target='The'. Loss = -log(0.78) = 0.25. Position 1: 'cat'=71%, 'dog'=12%, 'sat'=8%. Target='cat'. Loss = -log(0.71) = 0.34. Position 2: 'sat'=72%, 'is'=5%, 'ran'=11%. Target='sat'. Loss = -log(0.72) = 0.33. Total Loss = avg(0.25 + 0.34 + 0.33) = 0.31 -> backprop updates ALL weights. During PRODUCTION (generation), only last position is used because previous tokens already known. 1000 tokens = 999 free training signals.
summary: At training the output head produces predictions and cross-entropy loss at every decoder position, averaging losses for backprop and yielding many training signals per pass.

text: Summary - Training Phase Flow. Encoder: ALL 6 input tokens flow as one 6x512 matrix through every layer. Bidirectional attention (no mask). Decoder: ALL 3 target tokens (<s>, 'The', 'cat') flow together (teacher forcing). Causal mask prevents cheating. Cross-Attention: Decoder Q attends to encoder K,V. Each decoder token can see ALL encoder tokens. Self-Attention: MIXES information between tokens. Q.K^T creates cross-token connections. FFN: Does NOT mix tokens. Same weights applied independently to each vector. No cross-talk. Add & Norm: Per-vector operation. Adds residual, normalizes. No cross-token interaction. Output Head (train): ALL positions produce predictions. Loss computed everywhere. 3 signals from 1 pass. Output Head (gen): Only LAST position predicts next token. Others already known. Encoder runs ONCE - its output is reused as K,V for every decoder step. Decoder runs once (train) or N times (gen).
summary: Recap contrasting which components mix tokens vs operate per-vector, and how training differs from generation in encoder-decoder Transformers.

text: Progressive encoder-decoder inference flow diagram showing autoregressive generation: encoder runs once, K V output is cached, decoder runs in a loop processing one new token at a time using KV cache for previously computed keys and values. Cached nodes drawn with dashed amber rings; new tokens drawn solid green. Includes phases for encoder output caching, masked self-attention with cached K,V, cross-attention reading 6 fixed encoder vectors, FFN on single new token, and output head producing next-token softmax.
summary: Diagram visualizes one decoder generation step: encoder cached output, KV cache reuse for past tokens, only the new token flowing through all layers.
queries: visual of kv cache in transformer
what does the inference flow diagram show

text: Encoder-Decoder Inference Flow: encoder runs once on the full input and its output is cached as cross-attention K,V. The decoder generates autoregressively, one new token per step. At each step, only the new token computes Q,K,V; previous tokens' K,V are read from a self-attention KV cache. Cross-attention uses cached encoder K,V (fixed) and decoder Q. FFN runs only on the new token because it has no cross-token mixing. Output head projects the last position to vocab and softmax picks the next token, which feeds back as input. KV cache turns O(N^2) attention into O(N), at the cost of large GPU memory (e.g. ~300 GB for 100K context on GPT-4 fp16).
summary: Inference is one-encoder-pass plus a decoder loop where only the new token runs while past K,V are reused from cache, trading memory for O(N) speed.
queries: overview of how transformers generate text
what is the inference flow in a transformer
kv cache role in inference summary
why is generation O(N) per step

text: PRODUCTION / INFERENCE MODE (autoregressive generation). Only ONE new token flows through the decoder at each step. Previous K,V are cached. HOW INFERENCE WORKS - THE BIG PICTURE: 1. ENCODER runs ONCE on the full input "The cat sat on the mat" (6 tokens, all in parallel). 2. Encoder output (6 vectors) becomes K,V for cross-attention. Cached permanently. 3. DECODER runs in a LOOP, one new token at a time: Step 1: feed <s> -> predict "The". Step 2: feed "The" -> predict "cat" (KV cache has <s>). Step 3: feed "cat" -> predict "sat" (KV cache has <s>, "The"). Step 4: feed "sat" -> predict "on" (KV cache has <s>, "The", "cat"). ...until <end> token is produced. 4. At each step, ONLY the new token runs through all layers. Previous tokens' K,V are read from cache.
summary: Inference is autoregressive: encoder runs once, decoder loops one token at a time using cached K,V from prior steps.

text: Decoder FFN (just 1 vector through - "cat" only). FFN processes each vector independently, so only the new token needs to go through. FFN Layer 1: 3 -> 6 + ReLU. FFN Layer 2: 6 -> 3. WHY ONLY RUN FFN ON THE NEW TOKEN? FFN does NOT mix information between tokens. It applies the same function to each vector independently. Previous tokens' FFN outputs are already "baked into" the K,V vectors stored in the cache. So running FFN on <s> or "The" again would produce the EXACT same output. No point recomputing. Add & LayerNorm (decoder block output for "cat").

text: Output Head ("cat" vector -> next word prediction). Linear Projection (3 -> vocab size). Vocab logits: "sat"=3.5, "is"=0.8, "ran"=1.2, "sees"=-0.3, "went"=0.5. Softmax -> Probabilities: "sat" 80.7%, "is" 5.4%, "ran" 8.1%, "sees" 1.8%, "went" 4.0%. Predicted next token: "sat". "sat" feeds back as decoder input for Step 4. WHY ONLY THE LAST POSITION? During generation, there IS only one position flowing through - the new token. <s> and "The" were processed in Steps 1-2. Their predictions are done. We already used them. The single vector for "cat" at the output is all we need to predict the next word "sat".

text: What Happens Next: Step 4: "sat" enters the decoder. KV cache now has <s>, "The", "cat". Predicts "on". Step 5: "on" enters. KV cache has <s>, "The", "cat", "sat". Predicts "the". Step 6: "the" enters. KV cache has <s>, "The", "cat", "sat", "on". Predicts "mat". Step 7: "mat" enters. Predicts <end>. Generation stops. Final output: "The cat sat on the mat" (7 decoder steps, encoder ran once). KV Cache: Memory vs Speed Tradeoff. What is stored: K and V vectors at every attention layer, for every past token. Self-attention cache: Grows by 1 entry per step. At step N, holds N-1 K,V pairs per layer. Cross-attention cache: Fixed size (6 vectors K + 6 vectors V). Set once from encoder output. Real scale (GPT-4): 96 layers, 128 heads, 128 dims/head. Each token = 96*128*128 = 1.57M floats for K+V. At 100K context: 100K * 1.57M * 2 bytes (fp16) = ~300 GB just for the KV cache. Without cache: Each step would recompute ALL tokens. Step N costs O(N). Total: O(N^2). Impossibly slow. With cache: Each step costs O(1) for FFN + O(N) for attention lookup. Total: O(N). Much faster. Tradeoff: KV cache uses lots of GPU memory but makes generation feasible. No cache = no fast LLMs.
summary: Generation continues step by step until <end>; KV cache trades large GPU memory (~300 GB at 100K context for GPT-4) for O(N) total cost vs O(N^2) without cache.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast. Naive autoregressive generation recomputes Q, K, V for every previous token at every step, wasting nearly all work at long contexts. But because token embeddings and weight matrices are frozen at inference, recomputing the same token always yields identical Q, K, V - so we cache them. Looking at the attention matmul Q · K^T = Scores and Scores · V = Output, only the last row is new each step; the rest were already produced. We save K and V (queries are never reused, so Q is discarded). The cache is two tables that grow by one row per step. With cache, per-step cost drops from O(N^2) to O(N), giving roughly 660x speedup at 1000 tokens. The cost is GB-scale memory: 10.7 GB per LLaMA-70B conversation at 4K context, 86 GB at 32K, multiplied by concurrent users - which is why grouped-query attention exists. The cache only exists at inference; training processes all tokens in parallel.
summary: Chapter 10.1 teaches how the KV cache exploits frozen weights to skip recomputing past tokens' keys and values, trading large memory for an O(N) per-step inference speedup.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: The Problem - Naive Generation Redoes History. To generate each new word, the naive approach re-processes every previous word from scratch. Watch the work pile up across three steps of generating 'I love cats': Step 1 ('I') = 1 new block. Step 2 ('love') = 1 redundant redo + 1 new block. Step 3 ('cats') = 2 redundant redos + 1 new block. Each block represents one token's Q, K, V computed. Counting waste across 3 steps: total work = 1+2+3 = 6 blocks; actually useful = 3 blocks; wasted = 3 blocks (50%). At 1000 tokens: 500,500 blocks of work, only 1000 useful - 99.8% wasted. Naive generation recomputes identical numbers every step.
summary: Without caching, each generation step recomputes every previous token's Q, K, V from scratch, wasting up to 99.8% of the work at long sequences.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: The Lightbulb - Same Input, Same Output, Always. When step 2 re-processes the token 'I', does it get the same numbers as step 1 did? Step 1 computed q_I = W_Q · x_I = [0.5, 0.2]. Step 2 re-computed q_I = W_Q · x_I = [0.5, 0.2]. Identical. Every. Single. Time. Why? x_I (embedding for 'I') is frozen - tokens don't change their embedding mid-generation. W_Q (weight matrix) is frozen - trained weights don't change during inference. Same input · same weights = same output, ALWAYS. So stop recomputing. Save the answer the first time. That's a cache.
summary: Because token embeddings and weight matrices are frozen at inference time, recomputing Q, K, V for an old token always returns the same numbers - so we should save them.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: The Matrix View - Only the Last Row Is New. At step 3 (generating 'cats'), draw the full matmul Q · K^T = Scores and Scores · V = Output. Q is 3xd with rows q_I, q_love, q_cats. K^T is dx3 with columns k_I, k_love, k_cats. Scores is 3x3 - but only the last row (q_cats · each key) is new; rows for q_I and q_love were already computed at earlier steps. Output = softmax(Scores) · V where V is 3xd with rows v_I, v_love, v_cats (all needed). Output is 3xd but only out_cats matters; out_I and out_love are old and discarded. Counting cells at step 3: without cache = 18 cells (9 score + 9 output); actually needed = 6 cells (3 score + 3 output); wasted = 12 cells (66%). Cost per step scales as N^2 without cache; with cache, cost per step stays O(N).
summary: Drawing the attention matmul shows that only the last row of the score and output matrices is new at each generation step - the older rows are dead weight.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: What to Save, What to Throw Away. We compute three things per token: Q, K, V. For Q: Don't cache. Only the new query q_cats appears in the new output row's formula. Old queries q_I and q_love are never used again - dead weight. For K: Cache it. The new row of Scores = q_new · every old K. Missing one key = missing one attention score; every old K needed. For V: Cache it. The new output row = weighted sum of every old V. Missing one value = losing that token's contribution; every old V needed. That is why it is called the KV cache. We save K and V. Never Q.
summary: Old keys and values are reused for every new query, but old queries are never used again - so the cache stores K and V only, never Q.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: The Cache Is a Growing Notebook. The KV cache is literally two tables that grow by exactly one row at every step. Frame 1 - After 'I': K cache = [[0.3, 0.4]]; V cache = [[0.8, 0.3]] (1 row each, freshly computed). Frame 2 - After 'love': K cache = [[0.3, 0.4], [0.7, 0.2]]; V cache = [[0.8, 0.3], [0.1, 0.9]] (2 rows; older row dimmed because it was not recomputed - just read). Frame 3 - After 'cats': K cache = [[0.3, 0.4], [0.7, 0.2], [0.5, 0.3]]; V cache = [[0.8, 0.3], [0.1, 0.9], [0.45, 0.6]] (3 rows; only the newest highlighted). One append per step. Existing rows don't move, don't change, don't get recomputed - they sit in memory ready to be read.
summary: The KV cache is just two tables that grow by appending one row of K and one row of V per generation step, with prior rows untouched.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: Before vs After - Step 3 Side by Side. Generating 'cats' with and without the cache. Without cache (recompute everything): q_I, q_love, q_cats; k_I, k_love, k_cats; v_I, v_love, v_cats - the I and love versions are wasted. Total: 9 projections + 9 score cells + 3 output rows = 15 operations wasted. With cache: read from cache k_I, k_love, v_I, v_love. Compute only for new token: q_cats = W_Q · x_cats; k_cats = W_K · x_cats; v_cats = W_V · x_cats. Append k_cats, v_cats to cache. Then scores = q_cats · K^T (last row only, 1xN); output_cats = softmax(scores) · V (1xd). Total: 3 projections + 3 score cells + 1 output row, 0 operations wasted. The output vector for 'cats' is identical in both - same numbers come out. The cache only changes where old K and V values come from (recomputed vs memory read).
summary: Side-by-side step 3: without cache 15 operations are wasted recomputing past tokens; with cache only the new token's projections and a single new attention row are computed, producing identical output.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: The Cost - 10.7 GB Per Conversation. Cache isn't free. Formula: cache_bytes = 2 (K+V) x layers x d_model x seq_len x bytes_per_param. Example LLaMA 70B: layers=80, d_model=8,192, seq_len max=4,096 tokens, precision=FP16 (2 bytes). 2 x 80 x 8,192 x 4,096 x 2 bytes = 10.7 GB per sequence. Memory meter ticks: 1K tokens = 2.7 GB; 4K tokens = 10.7 GB; 32K tokens = 86 GB. Every concurrent user needs their own cache: 1 user = 10.7 GB; 10 users = 107 GB; 100 users = 1,070 GB. Long-context models (128K tokens) consume enormous memory. This is why serving LLMs is expensive and why techniques like grouped-query attention exist to shrink the cache.
summary: KV cache memory equals 2 x layers x d_model x seq_len x bytes - reaching 10.7 GB per LLaMA 70B conversation at 4K tokens and scaling linearly with users and context length.

chapterTitle: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: The Deal - Memory for Speed. The KV cache is a trade. Without KV cache: speed slow, O(N^2) per step, O(N^3) total; memory low, nothing stored. With KV cache: speed fast, O(N) per step, O(N^2) total; memory GB-scale, grows per token. At 1000 tokens: without cache ~333 million ops; with cache ~500 thousand ops - approximately 660x faster. Every fast LLM you've ever used runs on this trick - without it, ChatGPT would take minutes per response instead of seconds. Scope note: the cache only exists at inference time. During training, all tokens are processed in parallel, so there's nothing to cache.
summary: The KV cache trades memory for speed, cutting per-step cost from O(N^2) to O(N) and total cost from O(N^3) to O(N^2), making real-time LLM serving possible.

chapterTitle: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) shrinks the KV cache by sharing K and V across groups of query heads. Standard Multi-Head Attention with 8 heads needs 24 weight matrices per layer (8 W_Q + 8 W_K + 8 W_V) and caches K,V for every head during inference. For LLaMA 2 70B with 64 heads, d_head=128, 4096 context, 80 layers, FP16, the KV cache costs ~10 GB per batch item. GQA gives every head its own Q but shares K,V across groups: 8 Q heads + 2 KV groups = 12 projections, 4x less KV cache. MQA shares one K,V across all heads (8x less). GQA matches MHA quality within 1% while saving 4x memory; MQA loses 2-5% quality. Used by LLaMA 2/3 70B, Mistral 7B, Gemma.
summary: Chapter overview: GQA shares K and V across query head groups to shrink the KV cache without losing quality.

chapterTitle: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: Recall: Multi-Head Attention. With 8 heads, the model maintains 8 independent sets of Q, K, V weight matrices: W_Q1..W_Q8, W_K1..W_K8, W_V1..W_V8. That is 8 W_Q + 8 W_K + 8 W_V = 24 weight matrices per attention layer. Each head has its own complete set. Great for quality but a hidden cost during inference.

chapterTitle: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: The inference problem: KV cache memory. During generation, we cache the K and V vectors for all previous tokens to avoid recomputing them every step. KV cache memory per layer = 2 x num_heads x seq_length x d_head x bytes_per_param. Concrete example LLaMA 2 70B: num_heads=64, d_head=128 (8192/64), seq_length=4096, layers=80, bytes=2 (float16). Per layer: 2 x 64 x 4096 x 128 x 2 = 134,217,728 bytes. Across 80 layers: 10,737,418,240 bytes ≈ 10 GB per batch item. Serving 8 users simultaneously = 80 GB just for KV caches - an entire A100 GPU before model weights.
summary: KV cache for LLaMA 2 70B costs ~10 GB per batch item using formula 2 x heads x seq x d_head x bytes per layer.

chapterTitle: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: GQA solution: share K and V across query groups. Instead of giving every head its own K,V, we share K,V across groups of query heads. Each head still has its own unique Q - queries stay personal. Example with 8 query heads and 2 KV groups: Group 1 has heads 1-4 sharing K_1, V_1. Group 2 has heads 5-8 sharing K_2, V_2. Weight matrices needed: 8 W_Q + 2 W_K + 2 W_V = 12 projections (down from 24). The K and V computations and their caches shrink by 4x.
summary: GQA gives every head its own Q but shares K,V across groups, cutting projections from 24 to 12 and KV cache by 4x.

chapterTitle: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: Visual comparison MHA vs GQA vs MQA. MHA (Multi-Head Attention): 8 Q heads, 8 K heads, 8 V heads, 24 projections, 8 K + 8 V cached per layer. GQA (Grouped-Query, 4 heads per group): 8 Q heads, 2 K heads, 2 V heads, 12 projections, 2 K + 2 V cached per layer (4x less). MQA (Multi-Query Attention): 8 Q heads, 1 K head, 1 V head, 10 projections, 1 K + 1 V cached per layer (8x less). KV cache savings on LLaMA 2 70B 4096 context: MHA (64 KV heads) ~10 GB, GQA-8 (8 KV groups) ~1.25 GB, MQA (1 KV shared) ~160 MB.
summary: Side-by-side: MHA has 8/8/8 heads for 10 GB cache, GQA-8 has 8/2/2 for 1.25 GB, MQA has 8/1/1 for 160 MB.

chapterTitle: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: Why does sharing K and V work? The query is the question each head asks - each head needs its own unique question to capture different patterns (syntax, semantics, coreference). But keys and values are the answers, and adjacent heads often attend to similar positions anyway. K and V vectors across heads are highly correlated, so sharing them loses very little information. Research result: GQA matches standard MHA quality within 1% on most benchmarks while using 4x less KV cache memory. Models using GQA: LLaMA 2 70B (8 KV groups), LLaMA 3 (8 KV groups), Mistral 7B (8 KV groups), Gemma (varies by size). Why not always use MQA? MQA saves the most memory but loses 2-5% on harder tasks. GQA is the sweet spot - most memory savings with almost no quality loss. Most modern large models use GQA with 8 groups, not full MQA.
summary: K and V are correlated across heads so sharing them barely hurts quality; GQA-8 is the sweet spot used by LLaMA 2/3, Mistral, Gemma.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: Mixture of Experts (MoE) replaces the single FFN in a transformer block with a router and N expert FFNs. The router picks the top-k experts per token (e.g., top-2 of 8) and produces a weighted sum of their outputs. This decouples total parameters from active parameters: Mixtral 8x7B holds 47B params in memory but uses only ~13B per token. An auxiliary load-balancing loss prevents one expert from hogging all tokens. Memory holds every expert (94 GB at fp16), but compute per token drops to about 26 GFLOPs. Production examples include Mixtral 8x7B, Mixtral 8x22B, DeepSeek-V3 (671B total / 37B active, top-8 of 256+1 shared), and Qwen3-Next (80B / 3B, top-10 of 512). MoE wins for data-center training and high-throughput serving but struggles on edge devices and latency-critical single-user inference.
summary: Chapter overview of Mixture of Experts: router plus N expert FFNs, top-k routing, load balancing, parameter math, memory-vs-compute tradeoff, and where MoE wins or loses.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: The problem - more brain, same effort per token. Training a bigger model means more knowledge, but every token pays the full compute cost. What if most of the brain could nap while the relevant part does the work? Dense 47B model: active params per token = 47B (all of it). Every FFN, every attention head, every layer fires for every token. MoE model (Mixtral 8x7B): active params per token = ~13B (only 2 of 8 experts). Same 47B total capacity loaded in memory, but only a slice runs per token.
summary: Motivates MoE by contrasting a dense 47B model that activates all params per token against Mixtral 8x7B which has 47B total but only ~13B active.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: Replace one FFN with a router + N expert FFNs. Before (standard transformer block): LayerNorm -> Attention -> Add; LayerNorm -> FFN -> Add. After (MoE block): LayerNorm -> Attention -> Add; LayerNorm -> [Router -> top-k experts -> weighted sum] -> Add. Each 'expert' is just a copy of the FFN architecture (two matrices, an activation). 8 experts means 8 independent sets of FFN weights. The router is a tiny linear layer that decides which experts to invoke.
summary: Shows how an MoE block swaps the standard FFN for a router that selects top-k expert FFNs and combines their outputs.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: Each token picks its top 2 experts. The router is a tiny linear layer. For each token, it produces one score per expert, applies softmax, and picks the top-2 highest. Token = 'cat' (from 'I love cats'). router_scores = softmax(cat . W_router) = [0.03, 0.02, 0.80, 0.01, 0.04, 0.05, 0.02, 0.03] over E1..E8. Top-2 picks: E3 (0.80) and E6 (0.05). Normalize: E3 = 0.80 / 0.85 = 0.94; E6 = 0.05 / 0.85 = 0.06. Output = 0.94 . E3('cat') + 0.06 . E6('cat'). The router matrix W_router has shape d_model x num_experts (e.g., 4096 x 8 = 32K params). Negligible cost compared to an expert's ~650M params.
summary: Worked top-2 routing example: softmax scores over 8 experts pick E3 and E6 for token 'cat', normalize their weights to 0.94 and 0.06, then weighted-sum.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: Without a fix, one expert hogs everything. Early in training the router has no reason to spread load. It often collapses to sending almost every token to one 'lucky' expert - the others never get gradient updates and go unused. Without load balancing example histogram: E3 = 92%, others ~1-2%. With auxiliary loss: E1..E8 roughly 12-13% each. Auxiliary loss formula: L_aux = alpha . N . sum_i (f_i . P_i). f_i = fraction of tokens routed to expert i; P_i = total router probability mass sent to expert i. Product is minimized when routing is balanced. The auxiliary loss is added to the main training loss.
summary: Explains routing collapse, why one expert dominates without intervention, and the auxiliary load-balancing loss L_aux = alpha * N * sum(f_i * P_i).

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: Why it is called 8x7B but totals 47B, not 56B. Attention is shared (not duplicated per expert). Only the FFN is replaced by experts. So 8 experts of ~7B FFN-size each do not multiply to 56B - most of each '7B' is the shared attention. Per layer: Attention (GQA) ~41M; 8 experts x ~176M = ~1.41B; Router + norms ~50K (negligible); per layer total ~1.45B. x 32 layers + shared embeddings = 46.7B total. Active per token: Attention 41M; 2 of 8 experts = 352M per layer; x 32 layers + embeddings = ~13B active. The '8x7B' name is marketing-ish. It hints at '8 experts, 7B-class model.' The literal total is 47B because attention is shared across all experts (not replicated eight times).
summary: Parameter math for Mixtral 8x7B explaining shared attention and per-expert FFN size to reach 46.7B total and ~13B active per token.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: Memory holds all experts. Compute only runs two. This is the core tradeoff. MoE does NOT reduce memory - every expert has to be loaded so the router can pick any of them. What it reduces is compute per token. GPU memory (fp16) = 94 GB (47B x 2 bytes per param). All 8 experts must be loaded - the router could pick any. Compute per token = 26 GFLOPs (13B x 2 fwd-pass cost per param). Only 2 of 8 experts actually multiply anything for this token. Takeaway: MoE is great for data-center serving where memory is abundant but compute is precious. It struggles on edge devices where memory is the bottleneck.
summary: Quantifies the MoE tradeoff: 94 GB GPU memory holds every expert while only ~26 GFLOPs of compute fires per token.

chapterTitle: Mixture of Experts - Bigger Model, Same Compute
text: MoE is not free lunch. Different deployments have different bottlenecks. MoE trades memory for compute - that wins in some contexts and loses in others. Wins: large-scale data-center training; high-throughput serving (many users at once); compute-constrained + memory-rich settings; when model capacity matters more than latency. Struggles: edge and on-device deployment; small-batch single-user inference; memory-constrained environments; latency-critical apps (the router adds hops). If you can afford the memory to hold a huge model but not the compute to run it, MoE is the answer.
summary: Honest tradeoff list: MoE wins for data-center training and high-throughput serving but loses on edge devices and latency-sensitive workloads.

text: Reasoning models like o1, o3, and DeepSeek-R1 are the same transformer architecture as regular LLMs - tokenizer, attention, FFN, KV cache, autoregressive loop all unchanged. What differs is training: stage 1 standard pre-training, stage 2 SFT on ~50K reasoning traces with <think>/</think> special tokens, stage 3 RL with a verifier over millions of rollouts using PPO or GRPO. The model learns to emit <think> and </think> as ordinary vocabulary tokens; thinking is just the same autoregressive loop running 120+ iterations instead of 3. More thinking tokens means more compute means better accuracy on checkable problems - test-time compute scaling. Training data comes from existing CoT, synthetic generation, and rejection sampling - no humans label reasoning steps. Self-correction, verification, and confidence calibration emerge purely from outcome rewards. Reasoning lifts math, code, logic, and proofs; it barely helps creative writing, empathy, or casual conversation.
summary: Reasoning LLMs are vanilla transformers trained with SFT plus outcome-reward RL so the autoregressive loop runs longer inside <think> tags - test-time compute scaling that helps verifiable tasks only.

text: Not a new loop. The same loop, running longer. Autoregressive generation always loops - the transformer can only produce one token per forward pass. Regular LLM example: FWD -> 10, FWD -> 81, FWD -> <EOS>, about 3 loop iterations. Reasoning LLM example: FWD -> <think>, FWD -> Let, FWD -> me, ... FWD -> </think>, FWD -> 1081, about 120 loop iterations. Same mechanism either way.
summary: Reasoning models run the standard autoregressive loop many more times - 120 iterations vs 3 - rather than introducing any new generation mechanism.

text: How the model switches between thinking and answering. There is no thinking mode switch in the code. The vocabulary has two special tokens: <think> and </think>. The model learned to emit them at the right moments, the same way it learned any other word. The softmax output is a probability distribution over all tokens; the special tokens simply win at the right moments. Examples: after 'User: What is 23 x 47? Assistant:' the next-token distribution is <think> 0.87 (winner), 1081 0.03, The 0.02. Inside thinking after '... = 1081 OK' the distribution is </think> 0.62 (winner), Let 0.09, Also 0.07.
summary: <think> and </think> are ordinary vocabulary tokens; the model learned when to emit them via softmax probabilities, no architectural mode switch exists.

text: More thinking tokens means better answers on hard problems. This is the new scaling axis: instead of training a bigger model, you let the same model think longer. More thinking tokens means more forward passes, which means more compute spent on the problem. Representative accuracy on hard math vs thinking budget: 100 tokens 30%, 1,000 tokens 55%, 10,000 tokens 78%, 100,000 tokens 89%. For decades, better AI meant a bigger model. Reasoning models added a second knob: spend more compute at inference time instead - this is called test-time compute scaling. (Numbers represent trends across o1/o3/DeepSeek-R1, not a single published result.)
summary: Test-time compute scaling: accuracy on hard math climbs from 30% at 100 tokens to 89% at 100,000 thinking tokens, a new axis beyond model size.

text: What is actually different is the training, not the model. Reasoning models go through three training stages. Stage 1 Pre-training: predict the next token on trillions of internet tokens (trillions of tokens scale). Stage 2 SFT: fine-tune on curated reasoning traces with <think> tags (~50K curated examples). Stage 3 RL with verifier: generate rollouts, reward correct final answers, PPO/GRPO update (millions of rollouts). Stage 1 is identical to a regular LLM. The magic is entirely in stages 2 and 3.
summary: Reasoning capability comes from a three-stage pipeline: standard pre-training, SFT on ~50K reasoning traces, then RL with a verifier over millions of rollouts.

what makes a reasoning model different in training
how does reasoning emerge from training

text: How reasoning improves, one reward at a time. The model generates many rollouts per problem. A verifier checks each final answer. PPO or GRPO nudges weights: tokens in winning rollouts become more likely, tokens in losing rollouts less likely. Example - Problem '23 x 47 = ?' (ground truth 1081), generate 64 rollouts. #1 '... 23 x 50 - 23 x 3 = 1081' answer 1081 OK r=+1. #2 '... (20 + 3) x 47 = 940 + 141 = 1081' answer 1081 OK r=+1. #3 '... estimate: roughly 1000' answer 1000 X r=-1. #4 '... 23 x 47 = 1081' answer 1081 OK r=+1. #5 '... quick mental math: 1104?' answer 1104 X r=-1. PPO/GRPO update: winning rollouts -> token probabilities UP, losing rollouts -> token probabilities DOWN. The reward only checks the FINAL answer. The model figures out on its own what kinds of reasoning lead to correct answers. Nobody hand-labels reasoning steps.
summary: RL with verifier: 64 rollouts per problem, +1 for correct final answer, -1 otherwise; PPO/GRPO upweights winning chains, no per-step labels needed.
queries: how does RL with verifier work
rollouts per problem in RL training
how does verifier check LLM answer
what is a rollout in LLM training

text: You do not need humans to write millions of reasoning traces. The only thing you need is problems with checkable answers. Three data sources: 1. Existing chain-of-thought - already in pre-training data: math textbooks, Stack Overflow answers, competitive programming editorials, tutorial posts. 2. Synthetic generation - a big LLM generates 64 reasoning attempts per problem, a verifier checks each final answer, keep the ones that got it right and use them as training data. 3. Rejection sampling - have the base model itself try each problem 64 times, keep the attempts that got the right answer, model learns from its own best work. Domains with checkable answers are abundant: GSM8K, MATH, AIME, olympiad problems, LeetCode, Codeforces, scientific multiple-choice.
summary: Reasoning training data comes from existing CoT, synthetic generation filtered by a verifier, and rejection sampling - no humans writing millions of traces.

text: The tricks nobody programmed. Reasoning chains that include self-correction and verification tend to produce correct answers more often. The reward signal pushes those patterns up over millions of rollouts. Nobody writes code that says 'self-correct' - it emerges from the rewards. Example trace: '23 x 47 = 23 x 50 - 23 x 3 = 1150 - 69 = 1081. Wait, let me double-check. (<-- emerged: self-doubt) Try another way: 20 x 47 + 3 x 47 = 940 + 141 = 1081. (<-- emerged: verification) Both methods give 1081. Confident. (<-- emerged: confidence calibration)'. The DeepSeek-R1 papers documented an 'aha moment' during training where thinking length spontaneously grew and phrases like 'wait' and 'let me reconsider' appeared - purely from the reward signal.
summary: Self-doubt, verification, and confidence calibration emerge purely from outcome-based rewards - the DeepSeek-R1 'aha moment' phenomenon.

text: Reasoning models are not universally better. Thinking helps when there is a right answer to check. It barely helps when there is not. Big lift domains: Math, arithmetic, algebra; Competitive programming; Logic puzzles; Scientific Q&A with verifiable answers; Constraint-satisfaction problems; Formal proofs. No real gain domains: Creative writing; Poetry, storytelling; Open-ended opinions; Empathy, emotional support; Summarization; Casual conversation. Reasoning helps when there is a right answer to check. It barely helps when there is not.
summary: Reasoning lifts performance only on tasks with verifiable answers (math, code, logic, proofs); creative, empathic, and open-ended tasks see no real gain.

text: Chapter 11.1 introduces the retrieval problem at the heart of every vector database: given N pre-computed embedding vectors and one query vector, return the top-k most similar. It establishes the running cat corpus (10 documents, 4-dim illustrative vectors, query 'Information about cats'), shows that brute-force scan costs N times d multiplications per query (about 1.5 trillion at N = 1 billion, d = 1536), surveys where vector search shows up (semantic search, recommendation, image retrieval, duplicate detection, anomaly detection, RAG), and reframes Section 11 as a systems problem - storage, indexing, retrieval - rather than a training problem. The embeddings already exist; the work now is the index.
summary: Overview of chapter 11.1: the top-k nearest-neighbor retrieval problem, the cat corpus, scaling pain, where it appears, and the systems-not-training framing.

text:
From embeddings to a retrieval problem. Back in chapter 5.2 the transformer turned every word into an embedding - a dense vector capturing meaning. Now picture a library: 10 documents, each pre-encoded into its own vector. A user types a query. How do we find documents whose meaning matches?

Query: 'Information about cats', vector [0.85, 0.14, 0.44, 0.21].

Stored database: 10 documents, each with a 4-dim embedding:
#1 Cats are small domesticated carnivores [0.81, 0.12, 0.45, 0.22]
#2 Dogs are loyal pets [0.33, 0.68, 0.29, 0.41]
#3 Lions are big cats that live in Africa [0.76, 0.18, 0.52, 0.31]
#4 My cat sat on the mat [0.72, 0.22, 0.48, 0.26]
#5 Tigers are striped cats [0.79, 0.15, 0.41, 0.28]
#6 Python is a programming language [0.09, 0.88, 0.12, 0.74]
#7 Kittens grow up to be cats [0.83, 0.10, 0.47, 0.19]
#8 The dog chased the cat [0.55, 0.44, 0.36, 0.39]
#9 Birds can fly [0.18, 0.31, 0.74, 0.62]
#10 Fish live underwater [0.21, 0.29, 0.68, 0.81]

Real embeddings have 768 or 1536 dimensions instead of 4, and production databases hold billions of rows instead of 10. The shape of the problem is the same.
summary: Sets up retrieval as finding documents whose embedding vectors match a query vector, using a 10-doc cat corpus with 4-dim vectors as the running example.

text:
Find the 10 most similar documents. The core retrieval task: one query vector, N stored vectors, return the top-10 nearest. For the tiny 10-doc corpus, top-10 is everything, so the diagram highlights top-3 (docs 1, 3, 7 - all cat-related) to make 'closest' visible. The real problem scales: hundreds of millions of vectors, top-10 closest in milliseconds.

Formal statement:
  Given: N stored vectors V = {v_1, v_2, ..., v_N}
  Given: one query vector q
  Return: the k vectors in V most similar to q

This is the retrieval problem at the heart of every vector database. k is usually 10 or 20 - we want a shortlist, not the whole library.
summary: Defines the formal top-k retrieval task: given N stored vectors V and a query q, return the k most similar; k is typically 10 or 20.

Given N vectors return k closest

Why highlight only top-3 in 10 doc demo

text:
Now make it one billion. With 10 docs the naive approach - compare the query against every vector - is instant. Real systems have millions or billions of vectors and the cost of 'check every vector' explodes.

Toy corpus: N = 10, scan ~0.01 ms (this chapter)
Wikipedia-scale: N = 1,000,000 (all English Wikipedia chunked), scan ~500 ms
Internet-scale: N = 1,000,000,000 (web-scale search index), scan ~500,000 ms (~8 min)

Naive cost = N times d multiplications per query
At N = 1 billion, d = 1536: ~1.5 trillion multiplies per query

Checking every vector works for 10 docs. At 1B it is an 8-minute wait per search. Users expect answers in tens of milliseconds.
summary: Scales the brute-force scan cost from 10 docs (0.01 ms) through Wikipedia (500 ms) to 1 billion (8 minutes), giving the formula N times d multiplications per query.
queries: How long does brute force search take at 1 billion vectors
Naive cost formula N times d
Why does scanning every vector explode at scale
Internet scale 1 billion vectors search time
How many ms to scan 1 million vectors
Why is brute force 8 minutes at 1B
Toy corpus vs Wikipedia vs internet

text:
Where vector search shows up. The same retrieval setup - query vector, big pile of stored vectors, top-k - powers dozens of production systems.

Semantic search: User types 'how to fix leaky faucet'. System returns pages about plumbing repair even if those words never appear verbatim.

Recommendation: Spotify embeds every song. Given your listening history vector, find songs with nearby vectors. Netflix does it for shows, Amazon for products.

Image retrieval: Upload a photo of a red sneaker. CLIP encodes it, finds catalog images with similar vectors. Reverse image search, visual shopping.

Duplicate detection: Near-identical images on social platforms, plagiarized paragraphs in documents, duplicate bug reports. Vectors collapse minor differences.

Anomaly detection: Embed every credit-card transaction. Points far from any cluster are flagged as fraud. Works for network intrusions and medical outliers too.

RAG retrieval: An LLM is asked a question. Before answering, embed the question, find the top-5 most relevant documents, paste them into context. Grounded answers.

Different surfaces, identical plumbing. Every one of these systems runs a top-k nearest-neighbor query over a giant bag of vectors.
summary: Lists six production applications - semantic search, recommendation, image retrieval, duplicate detection, anomaly detection, RAG - all powered by the same top-k vector retrieval.
queries: What is vector search used for
How does CLIP do image retrieval
How does fraud detection use embeddings
Reverse image search how it works

text:
A systems problem, not a model problem. The earlier sections were about learning - teaching a model to produce good embeddings. That work is already done. Now the embeddings exist. The job is storage, indexing, and retrieval. No gradients, no loss functions, no backprop. Just systems design.

Training time (done): The model learned an embedding space.
  Forward pass
  loss = cross_entropy(...)
  Backward pass
  Weights -= lr times grad
Output: a function that maps text to vectors.

Query time (what this section is about): We search the space the model built.
  Encode query -> q
  index.search(q, k=10)
  return top-10 docs
  < 50 ms per query
The embeddings are fixed inputs. The work is the index.

This is a systems problem. Not a training problem. The rest of Section 11 is about storage layouts, index data structures, approximate algorithms, and the latency/recall/memory tradeoffs they force.
summary: Reframes Section 11: training built the embedding space; query time is a systems job - storage, indexing, retrieval - with latency/recall/memory tradeoffs, no gradients.

text: Chef and warehouse diagram. A super-fast chef on the left (labeled chef = chip) stands next to a cutting board with chopping marks, connected by a long dashed red arrow labeled 'slow delivery' to a distant warehouse on the right (labeled warehouse = memory). The chef can chop in 1 second but has to wait 10 minutes for each delivery. Two cards underneath: Chop speed ~10^12/sec (FLOPS - how fast math happens), Delivery speed ~50 GB/sec (memory bandwidth - how fast data arrives). The illustration shows fetching ingredients is the real bottleneck, not chopping speed. This is the analogy for why memory bandwidth, not FLOPS, limits brute-force search at billion-vector scale.

Left card: Exact brute force - Recall@k 1.00, Latency at 1B ~60 sec, perfect answers but unusable latency. Right card: ANN approximate - Recall@k 0.99, Latency at 1B ~10 ms, misses 1 neighbor in 100 but runs ~6000x faster. The arrow illustrates the tradeoff move from exact slow search to approximate fast search that production systems make.

text: Chapter 11.2 - Brute-Force kNN. Brute-force kNN is the correctness-at-any-cost baseline: compute similarity from the query to every stored vector, sort, return top-k. At N=10 it is instant (10 dot products, sub-millisecond, exact result docs 1, 3, 7). At N=1M with d=768 it costs ~770M multiply-adds and ~500 ms - slow but feasible. FLOPS (floating-point operations per second, ~10^12 on a modern GPU) is not the bottleneck; memory bandwidth (~50 GB/sec on DDR5) is. The chef-and-warehouse analogy: chip chops in 1 second but waits 10 minutes for each delivery. At N=1B, d=768 float32, every query must stream 3.072 TB of vectors past the CPU - ~60 seconds at 50 GB/sec, infeasible. The solution is to give up exactness: Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms return a top-k that is mostly correct. Recall@k = |approx_topk intersect true_topk| / k measures the loss. Production ANN hits 99%+ recall while running 100-1000x faster than brute force. The chapter sets up why every later vector-database technique exists.
summary: Brute-force kNN is exact but memory-bound; at billion scale it needs 3 TB/query - infeasible - so production trades 1% recall for 6000x speed via ANN.
queries: why brute force fails at scale
FLOPS vs memory bandwidth ANN motivation
exact vs approximate top k tradeoff

text: The naive approach: compare to everything. Before getting clever, start with the baseline algorithm. Given N stored vectors and one query, the dumbest thing that works is to compare the query against each stored vector, rank them, and return the best k. This is called brute-force k-nearest-neighbors, or brute-force kNN. Three steps: (1) Compute similarity - for every stored vector v_i, compute sim(q, v_i). With cosine similarity that is one dot product plus two norms per doc. (2) Sort - arrange all N scores from highest to lowest. Every document gets a rank from 1 (most similar) to N (least similar). (3) Return top-k - slice off the first k entries of the sorted list. Pseudo-code: scores = [cosine(q, v_i) for v_i in V]; ranked = sort(scores, descending=True); return ranked[:k]. No shortcuts, no index, no approximation. We literally touch every vector in the corpus. That is where the word brute-force comes from - it is the correctness-at-any-cost baseline.
summary: Brute-force kNN baseline: compute similarity to every vector, sort, return top-k.
queries: why is it called brute force

text: On 10 docs, brute force is instant. Run the algorithm on the cat corpus. For each doc, compute cosine similarity against the query 'information about cats', sort, take top-3. Cat-related docs float to top, off-topic ones sink. Total work: 10 dot products. Ranked results: #1 doc 1 'Cats are small domesticated carnivores' cos=0.97 (top-3), #2 doc 3 'Lions are big cats that live in Africa' cos=0.94 (top-3), #3 doc 7 'Kittens grow up to be cats' cos=0.92 (top-3), #4 doc 5 'Tigers are striped cats' cos=0.89, #5 doc 4 'My cat sat on the mat' cos=0.87, #6 doc 8 'The dog chased the cat' cos=0.68, #7 doc 2 'Dogs are loyal pets' cos=0.48, #8 doc 6 'Python is a programming language' cos=0.35, #9 doc 9 'Birds can fly' cos=0.30, #10 doc 10 'Fish live underwater' cos=0.27. This result is exact: we compared to every doc, no doc skipped. The top-3 returned (1, 3, 7) are guaranteed to be the true three most similar. That is the promise of brute force: perfect answers. Wall-clock time on a laptop: well under one millisecond. At N = 10, brute force is the right answer.
summary: Worked example: brute-force kNN on 10 cat docs produces exact top-3 (docs 1, 3, 7) in under 1 ms.
queries: cosine similarity ranked results cat corpus
wall clock time brute force search

text: At N = 1,000,000 it is slow but possible. Crank N up to one million vectors. The algorithm does not change - we still compare the query to every vector. But each comparison is no longer trivial because real embeddings are 768-dimensional, not 4-dim. Every dot product is 768 multiplies plus 768 adds. Ops per query = N * d = 1,000,000 * 768 = ~770 million multiply-add operations. Latency comparison: N = 10 (toy corpus) ~0.01 ms; N = 1,000,000 (Wikipedia-scale) ~500 ms. Half a second is slow for an interactive search but still technically possible. You could run this brute force on a single modern CPU and ship it for low-traffic workloads. The algorithm starts to creak, but does not break.
summary: At N=1M with d=768, brute force needs ~770M multiply-adds and ~500ms per query - slow but feasible.
queries: brute force kNN at 1 million vectors
ops per query N times d
how many multiplies per dot product 768 dim
is brute force feasible at million scale

text: First, what is FLOPS? FLOPS means FLoating-point OPerations per Second. In plain words: how many math steps (an add, a multiply, a compare) a chip can do in one second. It is the standard way people brag about chip speed. 1 FLOP = one floating-point math step. The expression a*b + c counts as 2 FLOPs (one multiply, one add). A modern GPU does about 10^12 FLOPS/sec - a trillion math steps a second. The chef and the warehouse analogy: chef = chip with chop speed ~10^12/sec (FLOPS, how fast math happens). Warehouse = memory with delivery speed ~50 GB/sec (memory bandwidth, how fast data arrives). Chopping is lightning-fast. Fetching the next vegetable from the warehouse is the slow part. So when we say 'the real killer is not FLOPS, it is memory,' we mean: the chip is waiting on deliveries, not on math.
summary: FLOPS = floating-point operations per second; chef-and-warehouse analogy shows memory bandwidth, not compute, is the real bottleneck.
queries: floating point operations per second meaning
how many FLOPs in a multiply add
chef and warehouse analogy memory bandwidth
why memory is the bottleneck not compute
chip speed vs data delivery speed
why FLOPS doesn't matter for vector search

text: At N = 1 billion it is hopeless. Push N to one billion. Compute cost scales linearly - a thousand times more work than a million. But the real killer is not FLOPS, it is memory. To touch every vector, we have to READ every vector from RAM. Every. Single. Query. Bytes of vector data to read per query: bytes = N * d * sizeof(float32) = 1,000,000,000 * 768 * 4 = 3,072,000,000,000 bytes = 3.072 TB per query. What we need: 3.072 TB read from memory per single user query. What RAM can deliver: ~50 GB/sec, a single DDR5 channel at peak bandwidth. NOT FEASIBLE: 3.072 TB / 50 GB/sec = ~60 seconds per query, just to stream the data past the CPU. Memory bandwidth is the bottleneck, not FLOPS. Even with infinite compute, brute force at 1 billion is hopeless for interactive search. The data does not even fit in a single machine. Google, Meta, and OpenAI all run indexes at billions of vectors. Brute force is off the table. Something has to give.
summary: At N=1B, d=768 float32, brute force must stream 3.072 TB per query; ~60 sec at 50 GB/sec - infeasible.
queries: why brute force fails at billion scale
bytes per query formula N d float32
Google Meta OpenAI vector index scale
why we need ANN at billion scale

text: Give up exactness for speed. The way out is to stop insisting on the EXACT top-k. Instead, return an approximate top-k: a list that is mostly the true neighbors, with the occasional miss. This is the family of algorithms called Approximate Nearest Neighbor, or ANN. How do we measure what we give up? Recall@k = |approx_topk intersect true_topk| / k. Of the k neighbors the approximate method returned, what fraction were in the true top-k? 1.0 = perfect (same as brute force), 0.0 = completely wrong. Comparison: Exact brute force - Recall@k 1.00, latency at 1B ~60 sec (perfect answers, unusable latency). ANN approximate - Recall@k 0.99, latency at 1B ~10 ms (misses 1 neighbor in 100, runs ~6000x faster). The tradeoff is wildly favorable: production ANN algorithms hit 99%+ recall while running 100 to 1000 times faster than brute force. You give up one neighbor in a hundred and gain orders of magnitude of speed. For search, recommendations, and RAG, that is an obvious win - the user never notices the missed result, but they notice the wait.
summary: Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) trades exactness for speed; Recall@k measures the loss; 99% recall at 6000x speedup vs brute force.
queries: what is ANN approximate nearest neighbor
how is recall measured for ANN
tradeoff exactness vs speed vector search
what does recall at k mean

text: Annotated tradeoff triangle: equilateral triangle with Recall, Latency, and Memory at the vertices and interior labels mapping each family of techniques to its direction of movement - Indexing trades latency for memory (orange), Quantization trades memory for recall (yellow), Filtering and sharding push on all three corners (purple).

text: Chapter 11.3 introduces the three-way tradeoff triangle for vector databases: recall (fraction of true top-k neighbors returned), latency (wall-clock time per query), and memory (bytes to hold the index). Every algorithmic choice - indexing structure, similarity metric, compression, sharding, replication - moves along an edge of this triangle, improving one corner at the cost of another. Recall@k = |ANN_results ∩ true_top_k| / k. Production targets 0.95-0.99 recall, P50/P95/P99 latencies in tens of ms, and bytes-per-vector around 3 KB at d=768 plus ~100 B HNSW graph overhead. Concrete tradeoffs: raising ef_search lifts recall but adds latency; PQ compression cuts memory 32x but drops recall; replicas cut tail latency but multiply RAM. Indexing buys latency with memory, quantization buys memory with recall, sharding/filtering touches all three.
summary: Chapter overview: vector-database design balances recall, latency, and memory on a tradeoff triangle where every technique pushes one corner at another's cost.

text: Every decision a vector database makes - which index, which similarity function, how aggressively to compress, how many machines - bends on three axes: Recall (top vertex, of true nearest neighbors how many we returned, 1.00 perfect 0.80 missed a fifth), Latency (bottom-left, wall-clock time per query, users feel 10ms vs 500ms as snappy versus laggy), Memory (bottom-right, bytes to hold the index, RAM is the single biggest cost in a vector DB bill). Pick any corner and the other two push back. Every tradeoff in the section is a move along one edge of the triangle.
summary: Introduces the three axes - recall, latency, memory - that every vector-DB design decision bends on.

what does memory mean in vector index
