intro to tradeoff triangle for ANN
three corners of vector database design
why every choice trades one axis for another

text: Recall@k is the formal score for how good an approximate search is. Run brute force to get the ground truth - the k documents actually closest to the query. Then run the fast method and ask: of the k it returned, how many matched the true set? Formula: Recall@k = |ANN_results ∩ true_top_k| / k. Size of the intersection divided by k. 1.0 means the ANN result is identical to brute force. Concrete example with query 'information about cats' and k=10: true top-10 are docs 1,3,7,5,4,2,8,9,6,10. ANN returns docs 1,3,7,5,4,2,8,9,6, and one impostor (doc 11). Nine of ten slots match. Recall@10 = 9/10 = 0.9 (90%). Production targets are usually 0.95 to 0.99. A recall of 0.90 means one neighbor in ten is wrong - often acceptable for search, rarely acceptable for fraud detection.
summary: Defines Recall@k as the intersection of ANN results with the true top-k divided by k, with a worked 9/10 example.
queries: how do you measure recall in ANN
how is approximate search accuracy measured
how to compute recall of vector search
why brute force gives ground truth
how to evaluate vector index quality
what is impostor in top-k results

text: Latency is the wall-clock time from query in to top-k out. On 1 million vectors, brute force takes about 100 ms - slow enough to feel. A tuned HNSW index on the same data answers in about 1 ms. Two orders of magnitude for the cost of a recall sliver. Query latency at N=1,000,000 (log scale): HNSW tuned ANN ~1 ms, brute force ~100 ms, disk-backed scan ~1000 ms (1 sec). Production cares about the tail not the mean. Engineers say P50, P95, P99 - the 50th, 95th, 99th percentiles. P50 is the typical query, P99 is the slowest 1%. Example latencies: P50 = 8 ms (typical), P95 = 25 ms (5% are slower), P99 = 80 ms (the tail). For a search API handling thousands of queries per second, P99 is what users notice when the page stalls. A good median with a bad P99 still feels broken.
summary: Latency is wall-clock query time; HNSW hits 1 ms vs brute force 100 ms on 1M vectors, and production tracks P50/P95/P99 percentiles, not means.
queries: what is query latency in vector search
why use percentile latency instead of average
how slow is brute force at 1 million vectors
what does the tail of latency look like

text: Memory is the cost that scales brutally. A single embedding at d=768 stored as float32 is 768·4 = 3,072 bytes = 3 KB per vector. bytes per vector = d · sizeof(float32). Multiplied by N the index can be bigger than the dataset it came from. Memory grows linearly with N: N=10 vectors 30 KB + HNSW graph 1 KB = 31 KB total; N=1,000,000 vectors 3 GB + graph 100 MB = 3.1 GB; N=100,000,000 vectors 300 GB + graph 10 GB = 310 GB; N=1,000,000,000 vectors 3 TB + graph 100 GB = 3.1 TB. HNSW overhead column matters because a vector is not the only thing the index stores - HNSW keeps a graph of edges between vectors, roughly 32 neighbor pointers per node, about 100 bytes of graph overhead per vector. At 1 billion that overhead alone is 100 GB. Indexing algorithms and compression schemes live or die on this number. 3 KB sounds tiny until you multiply by a billion.
summary: Memory at d=768 float32 is 3 KB per vector plus ~100 B HNSW graph overhead, scaling linearly to TBs at billion-vector scale.
queries: how many bytes per vector at 768 dimensions
how big is a vector index at 1 billion
why is memory the costly axis

text: The three axes are linked - tightening one corner loosens another. Three concrete production levers: (1) Raise ef_search to chase recall - HNSW knob controls how many candidate nodes to explore per query. Bigger number = more exploration = higher recall, but linearly more work so latency rises. ef_search=200 gives 0.99 recall, ef_search=50 gives 0.95 recall at 4x speed. Recall up → Latency up. (2) Add PQ compression to shrink memory - Product Quantization replaces each 3 KB vector with ~96 bytes of codebook indices (small integer codes that look up an approximate version of the real vector), 32x memory win. Stored vectors are approximate, distances are approximate, recall drops. Typical cost: 0.98 recall becomes 0.94. Memory down → Recall down. (3) Add replicas to cut tail latency - run three copies of the index on three machines, route each query to the least-loaded one. P99 drops because no single node gets a long queue, but you pay 3x the RAM. Caching frequent queries is a variant. Latency down → Memory cost up. Three different knobs, three different axes, same shape of answer. Nothing is free.
summary: Three production examples - ef_search, PQ compression, replicas - each push one triangle corner at the documented cost of another.
queries: how does ef_search trade recall for latency
what does PQ compression cost in recall
how do replicas reduce tail latency
why nothing is free in vector index tradeoffs
how to lower P99 with replicas
examples of axis tradeoffs in ANN
what knob to turn for higher recall

text: Every technique in a vector database is a labeled arrow on the tradeoff triangle. Indexing algorithms (Flat / IVF / HNSW) are different shapes of graph or partition - they buy latency at the cost of memory (the graph edges) and a small recall discount. Quantization (Scalar / Product / Binary) shrinks bytes per vector by 4x to 32x, buying memory at the cost of recall. Production concerns (filtering, sharding, replicas, cache) push on memory or latency depending on stacking, sometimes at the cost of recall when filters hide neighbors. The triangle is the whole architecture: every algorithm, every knob, every deployment choice is a move along one edge. When an engineer says 'HNSW with PQ32 behind two replicas', they are naming three specific moves on this triangle - one on each axis.
summary: Recap that every indexing, quantization, and production technique is a labeled move on the triangle, with the architecture's choices reading off one edge at a time.

text: Chapter 11.4 covers the three similarity metrics that dominate vector databases: cosine similarity (angle between vectors, range -1 to 1), L2 / Euclidean distance (straight-line distance, smaller is closer), and inner product (raw dot product, fastest to compute). Each measures something different and ranks documents differently. For text embeddings (SBERT, OpenAI, Cohere), cosine is default because the model encodes meaning in direction. For vision/face embeddings (FaceNet), L2 keeps magnitude signal. The key production identity: when vectors are L2-normalized to unit length (||q|| = ||d|| = 1), cosine, inner product, and L2 produce identical rankings. So normalize once at ingest, store unit vectors, and use inner product forever - the fastest possible similarity, no sqrt or divide in the hot loop.
summary: The three production similarity metrics, when each is appropriate, and the normalization trick that lets inner product replace cosine.

text: Three ways to measure similarity. So far everything has used cosine similarity. In production, three metrics dominate: cosine, L2 (Euclidean distance), and inner product (dot product). Each measures a different thing, and picking the right one is the first decision a vector database forces on you. Cosine: (q · d) / (||q|| ||d||) - angle between the vectors, ignores magnitude. L2 (Euclidean): sqrt(Σ (q_i - d_i)^2) - straight-line distance, smaller is closer. Inner product: q · d = Σ q_i · d_i - raw dot product, no normalization, no sqrt. Same corpus, same query - different metrics can rank documents differently. Which metric you pick is a statement about what 'similar' means for your embeddings.
summary: Overview of the three dominant similarity metrics in vector databases and what each one measures.
queries: what similarity metrics do vector databases support
difference between cosine L2 and dot product
which metric measures angle vs distance
does the metric choice change ranking
what is the simplest dot product
picking a similarity function for embeddings
what does similar mean for vectors

text: Cosine: the angle between vectors. Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors, ignoring how long they are. Two vectors that point the same direction score 1.0 regardless of magnitudes. Vectors at 90 degrees score 0. Pointing opposite scores -1. Formula: cos(q, d) = (q · d) / (||q|| · ||d||). Range: [-1, 1]. Concrete values on the cat corpus with query q = [0.85, 0.14, 0.44, 0.21]: Doc 1 (Cats are small...) cos = 0.9993; Doc 7 (Kittens grow up to be cats) cos = 0.9984; Doc 10 (Fish live underwater) cos = 0.6229; Doc 6 (Python is a programming language) cos = 0.3554. Cat docs hug 1.0, random docs slide toward 0. For text embeddings - SBERT, OpenAI, Cohere - cosine is the default. These models are trained so that semantic similarity shows up in the angle, not the length.
summary: Cosine similarity formula, range, geometric meaning, and concrete scores on the cat corpus showing why text embeddings default to cosine.
queries: what is the cosine similarity formula
what does the angle between vectors measure
semantic similarity in angle not length

text: L2: straight-line distance. L2 distance (also called Euclidean) is the straight-line distance between two points - Pythagoras, but in d dimensions. Unlike cosine, L2 cares about magnitude: two vectors with the same direction but different lengths are far apart. Formula: L2(q, d) = sqrt(Σ_i (q_i - d_i)^2). Smaller is closer (it is a distance, not a similarity). Concrete values on the cat corpus, query q = [0.85, 0.14, 0.44, 0.21]: Doc 1 L2 = 0.047; Doc 7 L2 = 0.057; Doc 10 L2 = 0.922; Doc 6 L2 = 1.228. Cat docs are closest (smallest L2), random docs are far. Magnitude matters - some vision embeddings encode confidence or saliency in the vector's length, and L2 keeps that signal. Face embeddings and image retrieval systems often live in L2 land.
summary: L2 / Euclidean distance formula, example values on the cat corpus, and why vision/face embeddings prefer L2 over cosine.
queries: when to use L2 instead of cosine

text: Inner product: the fastest of all. Inner product is the raw dot product - just multiply component-wise and add. No normalization, no sqrt, no division. A CPU can do hundreds of dot products per microsecond using SIMD instructions that fuse multiply-and-add into a single op. Formula: q · d = Σ_i q_i · d_i. Range: unbounded (depends on magnitudes). Inner product at d = 768: 768 multiplies + 767 adds = ~1535 FLOPs. One fused multiply-add per pair; SIMD does 8 at a time. Cosine at d = 768: 768 multiplies (q · d) + 1536 mults (||q||, ||d||) + 2 sqrt calls + 1 divide = ~4600 FLOPs + 2 sqrt + 1 div. 3x more math, plus expensive sqrt and divide. At billions of vectors, that 3x difference is the difference between 100 ms and 300 ms per query. Inner product wins by being stripped to the bone - just a tight loop of multiply-add that GPU and CPU SIMD units love.
summary: Why inner product is the fastest similarity - a FLOP-by-FLOP comparison versus cosine showing the 3x compute gap.
queries: why is inner product the fastest similarity
inner product vs cosine compute cost
FLOPs for cosine vs dot product
how many FLOPs for cosine at d=768
dot product no sqrt no divide
why no normalization in inner product
cost of sqrt and divide in cosine

text: When vectors are L2-normalized, all three are equivalent. The identity production systems exploit every day: if you divide every vector by its own length so ||q|| = ||d|| = 1, then cosine, inner product, and L2 all rank documents in the same order. Normalize once at ingest, then use the fastest metric - inner product - forever after. Cosine: (q · d) / (||q|| ||d||) with ||q|| = ||d|| = 1 → q · d. Inner product: q · d, already bounded in [-1, 1]. L2 squared: ||q||^2 + ||d||^2 - 2(q · d) = 1 + 1 - 2(q · d) = 2 - 2(q · d). All three collapse to the same ranking: max cos(q, d) = max (q · d) = min L2(q, d). Larger inner product = closer in L2 = higher cosine. Concrete check: q = [0.85, 0.14, 0.44, 0.21], d = [0.81, 0.12, 0.45, 0.22]. Before normalization: ||q|| = 0.9898, ||d|| = 0.9599, q · d = 0.9495, cos = 0.9993. After normalization: q' = [0.8587, 0.1414, 0.4445, 0.2122], d' = [0.8438, 0.1250, 0.4688, 0.2292], q' · d' = 0.9993 = cosine. Normalize once at ingest. Store unit vectors. Use inner product forever. FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate all let you do exactly this.
summary: Algebraic proof and concrete check that L2-normalization makes cosine, inner product, and L2 produce identical rankings - the production trick.
queries: are cosine and inner product the same after normalization
unit vector trick for vector search
L2 squared identity 2 minus 2 dot product
prove cosine equals dot product unit vectors
how to use inner product instead of cosine
do all three give same top-k

text: Which metric for which workload. Pick the metric to match how the embedding model was trained. Text embeddings (SBERT, OpenAI text-embedding-3, Cohere) → cosine. Trained with cosine-similarity loss; magnitude carries no signal, only direction. Vision / face embeddings (FaceNet, some CNN retrieval heads) → L2. Length can encode confidence, saliency, or image quality, and L2 preserves those magnitude differences. Pre-normalized anything (any model after ||v|| = 1 at ingest) → inner product. Fastest possible similarity, same ranking as cosine, no sqrt or divide in the hot loop. The production default for text embeddings from SBERT, OpenAI, or Cohere: normalize at ingest, store unit vectors, use inner product at query time. Same recall as cosine, but the ranking loop is the fastest code your hardware can run.
summary: Decision table mapping embedding family (text vs vision vs pre-normalized) to the right metric and the production default for text retrieval.
queries: what is the production default metric

Each split halves the search region, illustrating clean logarithmic partitioning at low dimensions before the curse of dimensionality kicks in.

Recall@10 is on the x-axis (log) and QPS on the y-axis (log).

text: Chapter 11.5 The ANN Family Tree surveys the four algorithm families that tried to deliver sub-linear nearest-neighbor search at billion scale. Brute force is O(N) and melts down at 1B vectors; the goal is O(log N). Trees (KD-tree, ball tree, M-tree, VP-tree) work at d=2 but collapse under the curse of dimensionality at d=768. LSH (Locality-Sensitive Hashing) hashes vectors with random hyperplanes into bit codes; needs 50-100 hash tables for 95% recall on high-d data, so it loses on memory. IVF (Inverted File Index) clusters into Voronoi cells with k-means and probes nearest cells; cheap to build, weak at cell boundaries. Graphs (HNSW 2016, Vamana 2019/DiskANN) build proximity graphs and greedy-hop to the query, reaching top-k in roughly log N hops. Graph indexes sit on the Pareto frontier of ann-benchmarks.com, beating trees, LSH, and pure IVF at every recall target. HNSW ships in FAISS, Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, pgvector, Pinecone, Chroma, Redis. Vamana ships in Azure AI Search and Milvus disk mode for graphs that don't fit in RAM.
summary: Overview of the four ANN algorithm families - trees, LSH, clustering/IVF, graphs - and why graph indexes (HNSW, Vamana) won production.

why did graph indexes win in vector databases
KD-tree vs LSH vs IVF vs HNSW

text: Brute force at 1 billion vectors melts down because it touches every single stored vector for every query - a linear cost in N. To make queries fast at billion scale, the algorithm has to skip most of the corpus. The target is sub-linear: growing with something much slower than N, like log N or sqrt(N). brute force: cost proportional to N times d (linear). Goal: cost proportional to log(N) times d (sub-linear, often O(log N)). At N = 1,000,000,000: linear is a billion ops, log N is about 30 ops. Four families of algorithms have tried to deliver this. Three of them had their moment and lost. One family won.
summary: Why brute-force linear search fails at billion scale and why the goal is sub-linear cost like O(log N).
queries: why is brute force search slow at billion scale
why do we need O(log N) search
cost of brute-force kNN at 1 billion vectors
what is the target complexity for ANN
how many ops at log N for 1 billion vectors
why skip most of the corpus
why we need approximate nearest neighbor
comparing N vs log N at 1B
cost proportional to N times d
how fast is log of one billion

text: Trees: beautiful at d = 2, broken at d = 768. The KD-tree partitions space by alternating axis splits - split on x at the median, then y at the median of each half, then x again, recursively. At d = 2 with 10 docs, it gives a clean log N search. At high dimensions it collapses: the curse of dimensionality erases the partitioning's advantage. d = 2 (drawable): each split halves the search region; log(10) steps to find the neighbor. d = 768 (real): in high dim, almost every pair sits at a similar distance, so partitions lose meaning and KD-tree scans almost all nodes. At d = 768 typical for BERT/OpenAI embeddings, KD-trees degrade to brute force. Ball trees, M-trees, cover trees, VP-trees all share the same fate. Space-partitioning trees are the wrong tool for high-dimensional dense vectors.
summary: KD-trees and other space-partitioning trees give log N search at low dimensions but degrade to brute force at d=768 due to the curse of dimensionality.
queries: why do KD-trees fail in high dimensions
why are space-partitioning trees bad for vectors
what happens to KD-tree at d=768
why does KD-tree degrade to brute force

text: LSH: hash similar vectors to the same bucket. Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH) picks a few random hyperplanes through the embedding space. Each vector gets a bit for each hyperplane: 1 if it falls on the positive side, 0 if on the negative. The resulting bit string is a hash code, and vectors close in space tend to share most bits. Three random hyperplanes cut the space; each region is one hash bucket; similar vectors land in the same bucket. The catch: recall sags at high d. To hit 95% recall on 768-dim data, LSH needs many hash tables (often 50-100) and each table holds long hash codes. That pushes memory back up and erodes the speed advantage. Graph indexes beat LSH on the same recall target with much less memory. LSH had its moment in the 2000s. Modern production systems rarely reach for it - the follow-up work on graphs turned out to be simpler and more accurate.
summary: Locality-Sensitive Hashing uses random hyperplanes to bucket similar vectors but needs many hash tables for high-d recall and lost to graph indexes.
queries: how does LSH work for vectors
why LSH needs many hash tables
is LSH still used in production

text: Clustering (IVF): partition the space, probe the nearest cell. IVF (Inverted File Index) does partition-and-probe: cluster the corpus into nlist Voronoi cells ahead of time, and at query time scan only the nprobe nearest cells. Cheap to build, easy to update, decent recall at nprobe = 8 to 32. Its weakness is neighbors that sit just across a cell boundary - the partition hides them from the probe. Strengths: low memory overhead, fast to rebuild, trivial to add or remove docs; production default for huge static corpora when paired with PQ compression. Weaknesses: recall cliffs at cell boundaries, needs nprobe tuning per workload, struggles with mixed distributions where k-means produces unbalanced cells.
summary: IVF clusters vectors into Voronoi cells with k-means and probes only the nearest nprobe cells - cheap and easy but prone to recall cliffs at boundaries.
queries: what is IVF in vector search

text: Graphs: navigate the metric space directly. A graph index stores the corpus as a proximity graph: every vector is a node, every edge connects a vector to one of its nearest neighbors. At query time, we start somewhere in the graph and greedily hop to whichever neighbor is closer to the query. Each hop drops the distance. A well-built graph reaches the true top-k in roughly log N hops. HNSW (2016) - Hierarchical Navigable Small World: in-memory graph with a multi-layer hierarchy. Production default for FAISS, Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, Elasticsearch, pgvector, Pinecone. Vamana (2019, DiskANN) - Microsoft Research: single-layer graph with alpha-pruning, designed so the graph can live on SSD and still answer queries in milliseconds. Used by Azure AI Search and Milvus disk mode.
summary: Graph-based ANN indexes (HNSW, Vamana/DiskANN) build proximity graphs and greedy-hop to the query in log N steps - the production default.
queries: log N hops in graph index
how do graph indexes find top-k
why graphs are fast for ANN

text: Why graphs won in production. Every year a volunteer benchmark called ann-benchmarks.com measures the best ANN algorithms on standard datasets, plotting recall against queries-per-second. HNSW and Vamana sit on the Pareto frontier: at every recall target they run faster than trees, LSH, and pure IVF. Graph indexes are now the default in every major vector database. Where you see HNSW: FAISS (Meta), Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, pgvector, Pinecone, Chroma, Redis-Search - effectively every vector DB ships an HNSW index. Where you see Vamana: Azure AI Search, Milvus disk mode, some self-hosted DiskANN deployments - the go-to when the graph cannot fit in RAM.
summary: Graph indexes dominate the ann-benchmarks Pareto frontier and ship as default in nearly every production vector database.
queries: why did HNSW win in production
when to use Vamana over HNSW

text: IVF (Inverted File Index) speeds up nearest-neighbor search by partitioning the corpus into nlist clusters via k-means and storing each cluster as a posting list. At query time, distance is computed only to the nlist centroids, then only the top-nprobe nearest cells are scanned. The Cell(p) = argmin formula assigns docs to centroids; only centroid list + posting lists are stored on disk. Four interchangeable names: cluster, Voronoi cell, partition, posting list. nprobe is the recall-latency knob: more cells scanned = higher recall, higher latency. Production rule of thumb: nlist ~= sqrt(N), nprobe = 8 default. Trades small recall (boundary misses) and one-time k-means cost for a 100x-1000x per-query speedup.
summary: IVF partitions vectors by k-means, stores posting lists per centroid, and scans only nprobe nearest cells per query - trading small recall for huge speedup.

IVF speedup vs brute force overview

text: Brute force is correct but slow because it reads every stored vector for every query. In a 2D scatter of 10 cat-corpus docs, the query sits in the middle and an arrow goes from the query to every vector - nothing is skipped. Every line is a distance computation. At N = 10 this is nothing; at N = 1 billion it balloons to the 3 TB of scans per query that brute force cannot afford. Cost = N times d per query. The IVF trick: group vectors ahead of time so at query time only the group the query landed in is scanned.
summary: Brute-force kNN computes a distance from the query to every stored vector, costing N*d per query and motivating IVF's grouping trick.

text: Before accepting any queries, run k-means on the stored vectors. Pick the number of clusters nlist = 3 and let the algorithm find three centroids that minimize the average distance from each doc to its closest centroid. Each doc gets assigned to exactly one cluster. In the cat corpus: cluster A (purple centroid) holds the 5 cat docs {1,3,4,5,7}; cluster B (yellow) holds the 2 dog docs {2,8}; cluster C (cyan) holds {6,9,10}. nlist is a tuning knob - small nlist means few large clusters, large nlist means many tiny clusters. 3 is drawable; production picks roughly sqrt(N).
summary: IVF preprocesses the corpus by running k-means with nlist centroids, assigning every doc to exactly one cluster.
queries: how does IVF cluster the corpus
production rule sqrt N for nlist
what does k-means do for IVF

text: Draw the region of space closer to centroid A than to any other centroid - that is centroid A's Voronoi cell. Repeat for B and C and the whole plane is divided into three cells. Every stored doc sits in exactly one cell, and the cell it sits in is its cluster assignment. Four names for one thing: Cluster (k-means view: docs landed near same centroid), Cell or Voronoi cell (geometric view: region of space closest to centroid A), Partition (set-theory view: dataset split into nlist disjoint groups), Posting list or inverted list (storage view: on-disk array of doc IDs assigned to centroid A - this is why it's called Inverted File index). One centroid owns one cell owns one cluster owns one posting list. Centroid = representative. Cell = territory. Cluster = group. Posting list = stored IDs.
summary: Voronoi cells partition space by nearest centroid, and the same group of docs is called a cluster, cell, partition, or posting list depending on viewpoint.
queries: what is a Voronoi cell IVF
why is it called inverted file
cluster vs cell vs partition vs posting list
what is a posting list in IVF

text: Cell(p) = argmin over i of ||p - centroid_i||. In words: cell of point p is the index of the closest centroid. ||p - centroid_i|| is the distance from p to centroid i; argmin picks the i that makes that distance smallest. Every doc gets its cluster assignment fixed after training (the k-means step that picked centroids). New queries do not move docs around; assignments only change if you retrain centroids. What IVF actually stores on disk: only two tiny things - (1) Centroid list: A: [1.3, 1.1], B: [3.1, 1.0], C: [3.5, 2.3]; (2) Inverted file (posting lists): A -> [1,3,4,5,7], B -> [2,8], C -> [6,9,10]. No polygons, no boundary geometry. Boundaries are implicit - run the argmin formula on the fly. Centroids alone fully define the partition.
summary: The Cell(p) = argmin formula assigns each point to its nearest centroid, and IVF stores only the centroid list plus posting lists - boundaries are implicit.
queries: what does IVF store on disk
posting list example A B C

text: A query arrives. Compute distance from query to each of the nlist centroids - just 3 distances, not 10. Pick the nearest centroid (A) and scan only docs inside its cell. The other 2 cells are skipped entirely. Work with nprobe = 1: 3 + 5 = 8 dot products (3 to pick the nearest centroid, 5 to scan cluster A). Brute force: 10 dot products (scan every doc every query). At N = 10, 8 vs 10 looks tiny. At N = 1M with nlist = 1000, IVF at nprobe = 1 scans about N/1000 = 1000 docs instead of a million - a 1000x speedup, where IVF earns its keep.
summary: With nprobe = 1 IVF probes only the nearest centroid's cell, costing nlist + N/nlist comparisons - about a 1000x speedup at million scale.
queries: IVF query algorithm step by step

text: nprobe = 1 is fastest but fragile: if the true neighbor sits across a cell boundary, the probe misses it. Raising nprobe tells IVF to scan the top-nprobe closest cells. More cells scanned = higher recall + higher latency - a direct move on the tradeoff triangle. Measured at N = 1M, nlist = 1000: nprobe=1 -> Recall@10 = 0.80, 1000 docs scanned, 1000x vs brute; nprobe=4 -> 0.93, 4000 scanned, 250x; nprobe=8 -> 0.97, 8000 scanned, 125x; nprobe=32 -> 0.995, 32000 scanned, 31x. nprobe is the single most important IVF knob.
summary: nprobe controls how many of the closest cells IVF scans, directly trading recall against latency.

text: Two rules of thumb cover 90% of IVF deployments. Pick nlist to keep each cell small enough that scanning is fast, and pick nprobe from a recall curve measured on your own data. nlist ~= sqrt(N) (FAISS default). N = 1,000,000 -> sqrt(N) ~= 1000 -> FAISS rounds up to 4096; N = 100,000,000 -> sqrt(N) ~= 10,000 -> 32,768. nprobe = 8 is a sensible starting point that typically reaches 97% recall on text embeddings; raise to 16 or 32 for recall-critical workloads, drop to 4 or less for tight latency. Memory overhead: each centroid is d=768 float32 = 3 KB, so nlist*3KB. At nlist=4096 that is 12 MB - negligible next to the dataset. IVF trades a tiny bit of recall (boundary misses) and a one-time clustering cost for a massive per-query speedup. This is why IVF is the FAISS default for million-scale static corpora.
summary: Production IVF sizing uses nlist ~= sqrt(N), nprobe = 8 as a starting default, with negligible centroid memory overhead.
queries: how to size IVF in production
nlist sqrt N rule of thumb

This is the starting point before any hierarchy is added - shows that every doc is reachable but only via local short edges, with no long-range jumps across the dataset.

text: Two-layer HNSW illustration: bottom cyan layer is the flat proximity graph of all 10 docs; top yellow layer holds three hub nodes (docs 1, 6, 10) with yellow long-range edges between them spanning the space. Shows how a query can jump across the dataset on the hub layer before dropping down to layer 0.

text: Three-layer HNSW illustration: bottom cyan all-nodes layer, middle yellow hub layer with long-range edges, top red hub-of-hubs layer with a single global entry-point node. Shows how stacking layers exponentially produces a global entry that descends through tiers to the target.
summary: Diagram of a three-layer HNSW with a top red hub-of-hubs entry node, a middle yellow hub layer, and the bottom cyan flat layer holding every doc.

text: Chapter 11.7 introduces HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) by building intuition from a flat proximity graph up to a multi-layer hierarchy. A flat graph connects each of the 10 cat-corpus docs to its M nearest neighbors (M = 3 for the visual, M = 16 in production), but greedy search from a random start takes too many hops because there are only local edges. Adding a sparse hub layer with long-range edges lets queries jump across the dataset in one hop, then drop down for the local fine-grained search. Stacking layers exponentially - each holding roughly 1/M of the nodes below - turns search from O(N) hops on a flat graph into O(log N) hops total. The airport analogy makes it concrete: local airstrips (layer 0), regional airports (layer 1, ~6% of nodes), international hubs (layer 2+, <1%) - same hierarchical strategy planes use to cross continents.
summary: HNSW turns slow O(N) flat-graph search into O(log N) by stacking sparse upper layers of long-range hub edges over a dense bottom proximity graph.
queries: how does HNSW achieve log N search
why hierarchical graph beats flat graph

text: HNSW starts with one simple idea: build a graph over the docs where each node links to its M nearest neighbors. M is a tuning knob; the visual uses M = 3 so the picture stays readable, while M = 16 is the production standard. One nuance: because the graph is undirected, a node ends up with at least M edges, not exactly M. If another node picks this one among its M nearest, the link is added both ways - so some nodes have 4 edges instead of 3. Real HNSW caps this at a hard upper bound called M_max (usually 2M), pruning older neighbors if a node gets too crowded. Pseudocode: for each doc d, neighbors(d) = M nearest docs by distance. Every doc is reachable from every other doc by hopping along edges. The question is how fast we can reach the right one.
summary: A flat HNSW proximity graph connects each node to its M nearest neighbors, with bidirectional edges and a M_max cap (usually 2M) to prevent overcrowding.
queries: how does HNSW build a flat graph
what does M_max mean in HNSW

text: Greedy search starts somewhere and moves to whichever neighbor is closer to the query. Simple and correct - but if the starting node is far from the query, the walk across the flat graph takes many short hops. On N = 1,000,000 with a random start, greedy-on-flat averages about 1,000 hops. Slow. Concrete example: start at doc 10 (birds/fish), target doc 1 (cats). Hops on this flat graph: ~8 hops for 10 docs. Extrapolated to N = 1,000,000: many hundreds of hops. The reason: the flat graph has only local edges. There is no way to jump across the space in one step - we have to trudge through every intermediate doc.
summary: Greedy search on a flat proximity graph is correct but slow because only local edges exist, forcing many short hops from a random start - about 1000 hops at N = 1M.
queries: why is greedy search slow on flat graph
how many hops does greedy search take
what is greedy nearest neighbor search
why does flat graph need many hops

text: Add a sparse second layer above the flat graph. Only a handful of nodes exist on it - we call them hubs. Hubs are connected to each other by long-range edges that span the whole dataset. A query enters the graph at the hub layer, jumps across the space in one or two long hops, then drops down to the flat layer for the final fine-grained search. Long-range edges: hub-to-hub edges reach across the dataset in one hop. Enter at a hub, pay one long-haul, and we are already near the target's neighborhood. Drop down once close: when no hub is closer to the query, drop to layer 0 and do the last mile on the flat graph's short edges. One sparse hub layer already drops search from N-scale hops to a handful. Stacking one more layer on top takes it to log N.
summary: Adding a sparse hub layer with long-range edges lets queries jump across the dataset in one hop, then drop down to the flat layer for final local search.
queries: what is a hub layer in HNSW
why add a second layer to HNSW
hub to hub edges span dataset
drop down to lower layer HNSW
long haul hops in graph search
what are hubs in vector search

text: Stack a third layer on top, holding only a hub-of-hubs node or two. Searches enter at the top layer, descend through each tier with a constant number of hops per layer, and land at layer 0 already right next to the target. Because each layer holds roughly 1/M of the nodes beneath it, the number of layers grows with log(N) and so does the total hop count. Formula: layers = ceil(log_M(N)). At M = 16, N = 1,000,000: log_16(1,000,000) ~ 5 layers. Hops per layer ~ constant (~6 with ef_search = 50). ef_search = how many candidates the search keeps in its shortlist at each step (bigger = better recall, slower). Total hops ~ 30 to find the top-k vs ~1000 on flat. Flat graph: O(N) hops. Hierarchical graph: O(log N) hops. Exponential speedup for a small memory bump from the upper layers.
summary: Stacking layers exponentially gives layers = ceil(log_M(N)) and total hops ~ 30 at N = 1M, M = 16, turning O(N) flat search into O(log N) hierarchical search.

text: Airport analogy: getting from a small town in India to a small town in Portugal is not one direct flight. You take a local cab to the regional airport, a regional flight to an international hub, a long-haul flight across continents to another international hub, a regional flight down, and a local cab in. HNSW search does the same thing for vectors. Three tiers: Local (layer 0) - every city has an airstrip, many connections to nearby towns, 100% of nodes; on a flat graph you are stuck using only these. Regional (layer 1) - a handful of regional airports reachable from any local airstrip and connected to each other by medium-range flights, ~6% of nodes (1/M = 1/16). International (layer 2+) - a few global hubs with long-haul flights between continents, search enters here and drops down as it gets closer to the target, <1% of nodes. The route a query takes: long-haul at international layer -> regional hops once you are over the right continent -> short local hops to the final neighbor. Same strategy planes use. Same strategy HNSW uses.
summary: The airport analogy maps HNSW's three tiers to local airstrips (layer 0, 100%), regional airports (layer 1, ~6%), and international hubs (layer 2+, <1%) for long-haul-first search.
queries: real world analogy for hierarchical search

how does a query route through HNSW layers
long haul flight analogy vector search

text: HNSW Construction: build the hierarchical graph by inserting vectors one at a time. Each insert (1) rolls a layer L from L = floor(-ln(u)*mL) where mL = 1/ln(M) ≈ 0.36, (2) greedy-descends from the top entry node down to layer L+1 with one candidate per layer, (3) runs beam search at layers L..0 with ef_construction candidates (default 200), (4) connects the new node bidirectionally to its M nearest from the pool (default M=16). The exponential layer distribution puts ~94% of nodes at L=0, ~5.7% at L=1, decaying by 1/M per layer. M sets edge density and memory: ~70 bytes/vector graph overhead at M=16. ef_construction is a one-time build cost; M is fixed at build time and requires a rebuild to change.
summary: How HNSW builds its graph: layer roll, greedy descent, beam search with ef_construction, and bidirectional M-nearest connection per insert.

text: Start with an empty graph and insert one vector at a time. The very first doc has nothing to connect to, so it becomes the graph's entry point at whatever layer the layer formula assigns it. The entry point matters: every future query starts from whichever doc currently sits in the top layer. As more docs are inserted, upper-layer residents can get replaced.
summary: HNSW build is incremental: insert vectors one at a time; the first vector becomes the top-layer entry point.

text: Every inserted vector is placed at a random layer L drawn from an exponential distribution so upper layers stay sparse. Formula from the original HNSW paper: u = uniform(0, 1); L = floor(-ln(u) * mL), where mL = 1/ln(M) ≈ 0.36 and M = 16. Example u = 0.7: -ln(0.7) = 0.357, 0.357 * 0.36 = 0.128, floor = L = 0. Example u = 0.01: -ln(0.01) = 4.605, 4.605 * 0.36 = 1.658, floor = L = 1. Only rare extreme u values get promoted; this asymmetric distribution is the only reason upper layers stay small. No tuning needed beyond M.
summary: Layer L is sampled by L = floor(-ln(u) * mL) with mL = 1/ln(M); the exponential distribution keeps upper layers sparse.

text: Simulate the layer formula a million times at M = 16. Expected node counts per layer at N = 1,000,000: L=0 ≈ 93.75% (937,500), L=1 ≈ 5.86% (58,600), L=2 ≈ 0.37% (3,700), L=3 ≈ 0.023% (230), L=4 ≈ 0.001% (14). Exponential decay: P(L >= k+1) / P(L >= k) = 1/M, so each layer is 1/16 the size of the one below. Upper layers hold a handful of hub nodes - cheap in storage, expensive in importance because every query enters through them.
summary: ~94% of HNSW nodes land at L=0 and counts shrink by factor 1/M per layer, leaving tiny but critical upper tiers.

text: Insert one new doc into a graph holding docs 1-10, with M = 3 and ef_construction = 4. Frame 1: roll layer. u = 0.45 -> L = floor(-ln(0.45) * 0.36) = floor(0.288) = 0. Frame 2: greedy descend from top entry doc 1 down to layer L+1, keeping just one candidate per layer above target (cheap). Frame 3: at layer L = 0, run beam search with ef_construction = 4 candidate pool. Distances from new node at (260, 110): doc 7 = 58.3, doc 4 = 94.9, doc 3 = 111.8, doc 2 = 117.0. Frame 4: pick M = 3 nearest from pool (docs 7, 4, 3) and add bidirectional edges N<->7, N<->4, N<->3. Doc 2 stays in the pool but is not selected. HNSW Algorithm 1: roll L -> greedy descend top to L+1 -> beam search at L..0 with ef_construction -> connect M nearest. Production uses ef_construction = 200 for better neighbor choice.

text: M is the cap on neighbors per node at layer 0 and is the most important construction parameter. Larger M = more edges per node = more starting points for search = better recall, but more memory and slightly slower per-hop queries. Visualizing M = 4 (sparse, degree 4), M = 8 (mid, degree 8), M = 16 (production, degree 10 capped at N-1 = 11) on a 12-node ring. M = 16 production rules: edges/node at L0 <= M = 16 bidirectional; at upper layers <= M/2 = 8; memory ≈ M * 4 bytes + upper layer overhead ≈ 70 bytes per vector. Graph overhead: 70 MB at N = 1M (vs 3 GB vectors), 7 GB at N = 100M (vs 300 GB vectors). Profiles: M = 8 memory-tight, M = 16 typical default, M = 48 recall-critical. M is the one knob you set up front and leave alone - changing it requires a graph rebuild.
summary: M caps L0 neighbors per node; M=16 default gives ~70 bytes/vec graph overhead and is fixed at build time.

text: Chapter 11.9 walks through HNSW search end-to-end on the 10-doc cat corpus. Step 0: query enters at the fixed top-layer entry node (doc 1 at L2). Steps 1-2: greedy descent through upper layers - at each layer hop to a closer neighbor or drop down. L2 has no neighbors, so drop. L1's only neighbor (doc 6 at distance 267.1) is farther than doc 1 (87.1), so drop again. Step 3: at layer 0, switch from pure greedy to beam search with a priority queue of capacity ef_search (production default 50, visualized at 3). Step 4: repeatedly pop the closest unexplored candidate, evaluate its neighbors, insert any closer than worst-in-queue, evict the worst; converge when the beam top stops changing. Three frames trace popping doc 1 then doc 3 then doc 7. Step 5: return the top-k from the beam - docs 3, 7, 1 at distances 40.0, 86.4, 87.1. Same algorithm scales to a billion vectors; only layer count (~log N) and ef_search change.
summary: End-to-end HNSW search: enter at top-layer entry, greedy-descend to layer 0, run beam search of capacity ef_search until the top of the priority queue stabilizes, then return top-k.

text: Step 1: greedy descent at L2 - no neighbors, drop. Greedy rule: at each layer, look at every neighbor of the current node; if any neighbor is closer to the query than the current node, hop there; otherwise drop one layer down. At L2, doc 1 has zero neighbors, so we drop straight down to L1 doc 1. Distance from current doc 1 to query: 87.1. L2 neighbors of doc 1: none. Greedy step in code: while any neighbor n has dist(n, q) < dist(current, q): current = n.
summary: L2 holds only doc 1 with no neighbors at that layer, so greedy descent drops vertically to L1 doc 1.

text: Step 2: greedy descent at L1 - neighbors farther, drop again. At L1 doc 1 has one neighbor: doc 6. Compare distances to the query. Doc 1: 87.1. Doc 6: 267.1. Doc 6 is far. Stay at doc 1 and drop to L0. Drops are free - the same node id sits in every layer it was assigned to; only the adjacency list changes. Decision: doc 6 farther → stay at doc 1 → drop to L0.
summary: At L1, doc 1's only neighbor doc 6 sits at distance 267.1 versus doc 1's 87.1, so the search stays put and drops to L0.

text: Step 3: at L0, switch to beam search with a priority queue. Layer 0 is dense - pure greedy gets stuck. Instead, keep a priority queue of the closest ef_search candidates seen so far. ef_search defaults to 50 in production. The visualization uses ef_search = 3 so the queue fits on screen. Search starts at doc 1 in the layer-0 graph, with the beam initialized to [doc 1, distance 87.1].
summary: On the dense layer 0, HNSW switches from pure greedy to a beam search backed by a priority queue of size ef_search (default 50, illustrated with 3).

text: Step 4: expand the beam - pop, evaluate neighbors, update queue. Loop: pop the unexplored candidate with smallest distance, look at every neighbor, insert any neighbor closer than worst-in-queue, evict the worst. Stop when a full pass fails to update the top of the queue. Frame A: pop doc 1 (87.1), expand neighbors {3, 5, 7}; insert doc 3 (40.0) and doc 7 (86.4); reject doc 5 (135.4) - farther than worst-in-queue (87.1). Frame B: pop doc 3 (smallest, 40.0); expand {1, 4, 5, 7}; reject doc 4 (136.6); docs 1 and 7 already in queue. Frame C: pop doc 7 (86.4); expand {1, 2, 3, 4}; none beat worst-in-queue, beam top unchanged - converged. Beam has capacity ef_search; expand until best in queue stops changing.
summary: Beam expansion repeatedly pops the closest unexplored candidate, inserts neighbors closer than worst-in-queue, evicts worst, and converges when the queue top stabilizes - illustrated by popping docs 1, 3, 7.

text: Step 5: return top-k. Take the top k entries of the converged beam. For query 'information about cats' with k = 3, the returned docs are 3, 7, 1 - exactly the cat docs. Distances: doc 3 = 40.0, doc 7 = 86.4, doc 1 = 87.1. Doc 3 text: 'Lions are big cats that live in Africa'. Doc 7: 'Kittens grow up to be cats'. Doc 1: 'Cats are small domesticated carnivores'. End-to-end path: L2 doc 1 → L1 doc 1 → L0 beam → top-3. Same algorithm scales to a billion docs; only layer count (~log N) and ef_search change.

All curves rise from low recall at ef_search = 10 toward 1.0 as ef_search grows; higher M curves converge closer to 1.0 but require more memory. M = 16 is the standard compromise. text: HNSW exposes exactly three tuning parameters: M (graph shape, build-time, default 16), ef_construction (insertion candidate pool size, build-time, default 200), and ef_search (query-time beam width, default 50). M trades recall for memory and is fixed at build; ef_construction trades recall for build time as a one-off cost; ef_search is the only knob you can change without rebuilding and is the production dial for recall vs latency. Pick M first, then tune ef_search per workload. Memory at production scale is dominated by vectors (N x d x 4 bytes) plus a smaller graph overhead (N x M x 8 bytes). Playbook: low recall -> raise ef_search; high memory -> lower M + apply PQ; slow build -> lower ef_construction; slow queries -> lower ef_search.
summary: Chapter 11.10 covers the three HNSW tuning knobs (M, ef_construction, ef_search), their defaults, tradeoffs, and a production playbook for which to adjust when.

text: HNSW exposes exactly three parameters. M controls the graph shape and is a build-time structural choice. ef_construction controls how hard we work to pick good neighbors during insert (build-time effort, one-off). ef_search controls how wide a net we cast at query time (per-query recall dial). Defaults: M = 16, ef_construction = 200, ef_search = 50. M trades recall vs memory. ef_construction trades recall vs build time. ef_search trades recall vs latency. Change one knob at a time and measure - changing two at once makes it impossible to tell which one mattered.

text: M is the cap on neighbors per node at layer 0. Doubling M roughly doubles the per-vector graph memory and gives a small but measurable recall bump; returns flatten at high M. Measured at ef_search = 50, N = 1M, d = 768: M=8 gives recall@10 = 0.93 with ~35 bytes/vec graph overhead = 35 MB total; M=16 gives 0.97 at ~70 bytes/vec = 70 MB; M=32 gives 0.99 at ~140 bytes/vec = 140 MB; M=48 gives 0.995 at ~210 bytes/vec = 210 MB. M = 16 is the sweet spot for text. Drop to 8 when RAM is tight; raise to 32 or 48 when recall is critical and memory is available.
summary: Concrete table of how M affects recall@10 and per-vector graph memory, with M=16 as the sweet spot for text embeddings.

text: ef_construction is the candidate pool size during insertion. Larger pool finds better neighbors for every new node, yielding a better graph and better recall at query time, at the cost of longer builds. Once the index is built, ef_construction no longer matters - it is a one-time up-front cost. Measured at N = 1M, d = 768, M = 16: ef_construction=100 builds in ~5 min with recall ceiling 0.95; 200 in ~9 min for 0.97; 500 in ~20 min for 0.98; 1000 in ~35 min for 0.985. Recommended starting point: ef_construction = 200. Returns diminish sharply past 500 on most text datasets.
summary: Shows how ef_construction trades build time for a recall ceiling on the resulting graph; recommends starting at 200 with diminishing returns past 500.

text: ef_search is the only knob you change after deployment. Raising it expands the beam at layer 0 - more candidate nodes explored per query, higher recall, higher latency. Lowering it does the opposite. This is the knob production teams reach for when they need a quick recall bump. Query-time sweep at M = 16, N = 1M, d = 768, single CPU core: ef_search=10 gives recall 0.85 at 0.5 ms; 50 gives 0.97 at 1.2 ms; 200 gives 0.995 at 3.0 ms; 500 gives 0.999 at 6.0 ms. Start at ef_search = 50. Bump to 100 or 200 if recall is low. Drop to 10-20 only when sub-millisecond latency matters more than a 1-2% recall drop.
summary: Demonstrates the ef_search dial: a per-query knob that trades latency for recall and is the only HNSW parameter changeable without rebuilding.
queries: can I change ef_search without rebuild

text: Each M value traces its own recall@10 curve as ef_search varies on a log x-axis. Three curves shown: M=8 (cyan) plateaus lowest, M=16 (yellow) is the standard compromise, M=32 (green) converges closest to 1.0. Higher M lets the curve flatten closer to 1.0 before latency gets painful. Numbers are empirical, measured on a laptop with SIFT-1M. Moving along one curve is cheap (change ef_search). Switching curves is expensive (rebuild the graph with new M). That asymmetry is why M is a fixed architectural choice while ef_search is the runtime tuning dial.
summary: Recall@10 vs ef_search curves for M = 8, 16, 32 illustrate why M is fixed at build time while ef_search is the runtime dial.
queries: HNSW M=8 vs M=16 vs M=32 curve
why is ef_search the tuning dial

text: At production scale, total HNSW memory is dominated by vectors plus a smaller graph overhead. Memory ~= N * (d * 4 + M * 8) bytes. At N = 100M, d = 768, M = 16: 100M * (3072 + 128) ~= 320 GB total (300 GB vectors + 20 GB graph). Playbook: Recall too low -> raise ef_search first, then raise M (rebuild required). Memory too high -> lower M (rebuild) then apply PQ compression. Build time too slow -> lower ef_construction first, then lower M. Query latency too high -> lower ef_search (recall drops, queries get faster). ef_search is the only knob changeable without a rebuild, making it the default tool for incident response. Everything else requires planning ahead.
summary: Memory formula and a four-row symptom-to-action playbook for which HNSW knob to raise or lower in production.

text: HNSW hierarchical graph with three stacked layers: the top layer (L2) has a single hub node, the middle layer (L1) has three hub nodes connected by long orange arcs, and the bottom layer (L0) has eight nodes connected by short green edges. Dashed vertical lines link the same node across layers, showing how upper-layer hubs sit above their L0 copies. Caption: HNSW: 3 layers, long jumps live on top.
summary: Diagram of HNSW's 3-layer pyramid showing top hub, middle hub row, and dense bottom layer with cross-layer links.

text: Single flat Vamana graph with eight nodes on one plane connected by a mix of short green edges (local precision) and long orange edges stretching across the plane (cross-space jumps), doing the work HNSW's upper layers used to do. Caption: Vamana: 1 layer, every edge lives here.
summary: Diagram of Vamana's single flat layer with mixed short and long edges replacing HNSW's hierarchy.

text: Focal Vamana node p at the center surrounded by a mix of short green edges fanning to close neighbors and long orange edges shooting across the plane to distant nodes, showing how one node's 64 edges do double duty as both local-precision and long-range hub edges. Legend: short edges = local precision; long edges = cross-space jumps. The short greens do what HNSW's bottom layer did; the long oranges do what its top layer did - both baked into one node on one flat graph.
summary: Diagram of one Vamana node p whose 64 edges mix short-range precision with long-range cross-space jumps.

text: Storage hierarchy diagram: the top layer shows a RAM cache (green) holding a small subset of high-degree entry nodes labeled '~1-5% of nodes, kept hot for every query'. The bottom layer shows the SSD (orange, NVMe) storing every graph node as 4 KB blocks aligned to NVMe pages, labeled '4 KB blocks, one per node (vector + adjacency list)'. Side annotation: NVMe random read latency ~10 us per block.
summary: Diagram showing DiskANN's two-tier storage: small RAM cache of entry hubs over NVMe SSD with 4 KB per-node blocks.
queries: RAM cache and SSD layout Vamana

text: Chapter 11.11 introduces Vamana / DiskANN, Microsoft Research's 2019 graph-index algorithm built for billion-scale vector search where the graph cannot fit in RAM. HNSW assumes everything lives in RAM and breaks past ~100M vectors (320 GB at d=768, M=16). Vamana drops HNSW's hierarchical pyramid and uses one flat graph because every SSD hop costs the same ~10 microseconds, so layered shortcuts no longer pay. Each node has R=64 edges chosen by alpha-pruning to mix short-range edges (local precision) and long-range edges (cross-space jumps). Adjacency lists plus the vector itself are stored as 4 KB blocks aligned to NVMe pages; a small RAM cache holds the high-degree entry-layer nodes. A query greedy-walks the cache for free then issues 40-80 NVMe reads (~10 us each) for ~1 ms total. One 128 GB + 10 TB NVMe server can hold 100 billion vectors at ~5 ms latency. Used in Azure AI Search, Milvus disk mode, Weaviate, with FreshDiskANN (2021) adding inserts/deletes.
summary: Vamana/DiskANN: flat-graph SSD-resident ANN index for billion-scale search beyond HNSW's RAM wall.

text: HNSW assumes the entire graph plus every vector lives in RAM. That assumption breaks at production scale. A single machine with 384 GB of RAM can just barely hold 100 million 768-dim float32 vectors plus their HNSW graph. At N = 100M, d = 768, M = 16: vectors = 300 GB, HNSW graph = 20 GB, total = 320 GB (barely fits one server). At N = 1,000,000,000 the total is 3.2 TB and needs multi-node sharding. Microsoft hit this wall on Bing. Their answer, published in 2019, is Vamana - a graph index designed from the ground up to work when the graph lives on SSD and only a cache slice lives in RAM.
summary: HNSW's all-in-RAM design breaks past ~100M vectors, motivating Vamana/DiskANN.

text: HNSW builds a pyramid of layers so it can make fast long-range jumps through the upper hubs. Vamana throws the pyramid away and uses a single flat graph - every node sits on one plane with no layer stack above it. HNSW: 3 layers, long jumps live on top. Vamana: 1 layer, every edge lives here.
summary: Vamana replaces HNSW's hierarchical pyramid with one flat graph layer.

text: HNSW's pyramid only earns its keep because top-layer hops in RAM are essentially free. Once the graph lives on SSD, every hop - whether through a top-layer hub or a bottom-layer neighbor - costs the same 10 microseconds. The hierarchy stops paying for itself, so Vamana skips it. HNSW in RAM: top-layer jump ~0.1 us, bottom hop ~0.1 us. Vamana on SSD: any hop ~10 us, every layer same cost. A 100x speed difference per hop is why HNSW wins in RAM and Vamana wins on SSD.
summary: On SSD every hop costs ~10 us so layering yields no benefit; in RAM HNSW's free top hops do.
queries: Why drop the pyramid for SSD-resident graph
SSD hop latency vs RAM hop latency
Why does hierarchy not help on SSD
Cost of one hop in DiskANN
When does graph hierarchy stop paying off

text: Without a pyramid to provide long-range jumps, each Vamana node's 64 neighbors have to cover both jobs at once: some are short-range (for final local precision near the target) and some are long-range (for fast cross-space jumps that HNSW used to do on the top layer). R = 64 (default max neighbors per node), single flat layer, no hierarchy, each node's edges span short + long ranges. Vamana's build algorithm, called alpha-pruning, picks these 64 edges to be diverse - a mix of short for precision and long for cross-space jumps - instead of the 64 closest neighbors which would all clump together.
summary: Each Vamana node's R=64 edges, picked by alpha-pruning, mix short- and long-range to replace HNSW's layers.
queries: Short-range vs long-range edges in Vamana
What does alpha do in DiskANN

text: NVMe stands for Non-Volatile Memory Express. It is the modern protocol that plugs an SSD directly into the PCIe bus, skipping the slow older SATA path. Inside the drive, flash is organised into fixed-size pages - almost always 4 KB each. One SSD read returns one page; asking for fewer bytes still costs a whole page. Older SATA SSD: ~500 MB/s, ~100 us per random read. NVMe SSD (modern): ~3-7 GB/s, ~10 us per random read. ~10x faster than SATA. Vamana only works as an online search engine because of NVMe speeds.
summary: NVMe is the PCIe SSD protocol with 4 KB pages and ~10 us random reads that makes DiskANN viable.
queries: How fast is an NVMe SSD
How fast is NVMe random read
Random read latency NVMe vs SATA

text: Vamana stores each node's adjacency list on SSD as a 4 KB block aligned to the NVMe page. Every block holds one node's neighbor list AND the vector itself, so a single 4 KB read delivers both the edges and the data needed to compute distance - one IO per hop, no waste. A small subset of the graph (typically the central high-degree entry-layer nodes, ~1-5% of nodes) is kept in RAM as a cache. Disk block = 4 KB = 1 vector + neighbor ids; NVMe random read latency ~10 us per block; memory cache holds entry-layer nodes (most-traversed). The 4 KB choice is deliberate - it matches NVMe page granularity so we pay for one IO per hop, no more.
summary: Vamana packs each node's vector + adjacency into one 4 KB SSD block, with a RAM cache of entry-layer hubs.
queries: How does Vamana store data on SSD

Why store vector with adjacency list

text: A Vamana search starts in the RAM cache and greedy-descends there for free (no disk IO). When the greedy walk drops off the cached subgraph, every hop costs one SSD read. With a good graph and a well-chosen cache, total SSD reads per query stay under 80 - about 800 microseconds of IO plus compute. Per-query budget at N = 100M, d = 768: RAM hops ~20 (free, nanoseconds each); SSD reads 40-80 (10 us each on NVMe); distance ops ~80 (~60 ns each). Total latency = 80 * 10 us + compute = ~1 ms per query. A single-digit-millisecond vector search over a graph that never fits in RAM - that is the whole point of DiskANN.
summary: Vamana queries take ~20 free RAM hops then 40-80 NVMe reads at 10 us each for ~1 ms total.
queries: How fast is a Vamana query

How many hops in a DiskANN query
How long does one DiskANN search take

text: With R = 64, d = 768, and 1.2 alpha-pruning, a single server with about 128 GB of RAM and 10 TB of NVMe holds 100 billion vectors and still answers queries in a few milliseconds. That is more vectors than Google indexes for web search. 1 server: 128 GB RAM + 10 TB NVMe holds 100,000,000,000 vectors; query latency ~5 ms at recall@10 = 0.95; cost ~1/10 of HNSW multi-node cluster at the same scale. Where DiskANN is deployed: Azure AI Search (core engine for Bing), Milvus disk mode, Weaviate with Vamana backend, self-hosted DiskANN at Microsoft, Snowflake, Databricks. FreshDiskANN (2021 follow-up) adds incremental inserts and deletes on top of Vamana, handling the one thing the original couldn't, so production avoids periodic full rebuilds. Vamana is the answer when the graph does not fit in RAM; HNSW is still the production default under 100M vectors.
summary: One server with 128 GB RAM + 10 TB NVMe runs DiskANN over 100 billion vectors at ~5 ms; FreshDiskANN adds updates.

text: The Memory Wall: a single 768-dim float32 embedding is 3 KB, so 1M vectors is 3 GB, 100M is 300 GB, and 1B is 3 TB. Adding an HNSW graph (M=16 edges, 8 bytes each) adds ~100 bytes per vector. One AWS r7i.24xlarge with 768 GB RAM holds 100M comfortably but cannot hold 1B without sharding 4-8 machines and tripling cost. Four compression techniques attack the wall: scalar quantization (4x, 1-3% recall loss), product quantization (32x, 3-8%), binary quantization (32x, 5-10%), and Matryoshka truncation (6x, minimal). They stack and the framework for picking is recall cost versus memory win.
summary: Chapter 11.12 establishes that float32 embeddings hit a memory wall at scale and motivates four compression techniques.

text: A single embedding is 3 KB at d = 768. Every document in the corpus becomes a dense vector. SBERT, OpenAI text-embedding-3-small, and most production encoders produce 768-dimensional float32 vectors. Each dimension is a 32-bit float - 4 bytes on disk and in RAM. Multiply it out: one single 'Cats are small domesticated carnivores' vector costs 3,072 bytes. That is 3 KB for a single sentence. Formula: bytes per vector = d times sizeof(float32) = 768 times 4 bytes = 3,072 bytes = 3 KB. Example dimension values shown: [0.81, 0.12, 0.45, 0.22, 0.63, 0.07, 0.38, 0.91] (first 8 of 768). 3 KB looks small until you realize this is the cost of every single document you plan to search.
summary: One 768-dim float32 embedding occupies 3,072 bytes (3 KB) because each of 768 dimensions is a 4-byte float.

text: From 3 GB to 3 TB as N grows. The 3 KB per vector is a constant. Scale is what turns it into a problem. A startup indexing help-center docs at N = 1M is already burning 3 GB of pure float32 before a single index, graph, or copy is added. A mid-size SaaS with N = 100M product listings needs 300 GB of RAM per replica. Google-scale search at N = 1B hits 3 TB. Table: N = 10 (cat corpus) -> 30 KB, fits in L1 cache. N = 1,000,000 -> 3 GB, fits on a laptop. N = 100,000,000 -> 300 GB, needs a real server. N = 1,000,000,000 -> 3 TB, needs multi-node sharding. The 1 billion line is where production web search, e-commerce catalogs, and internal knowledge bases tend to land.
summary: Multiplying 3 KB per vector by corpus size N yields 3 GB at 1M, 300 GB at 100M, and 3 TB at 1B vectors.
queries: How much RAM does 1 billion vectors need?

text: Add the HNSW graph: another 100 bytes per vector. The raw vectors are only half the story. An HNSW graph keeps M = 16 edges per node at layer 0 plus about M more total across the higher layers. At 8 bytes per edge (a 64-bit node id), the graph adds roughly 100 bytes of overhead per vector, or about 3% on top of 3 KB. Formula: HNSW overhead per vector approx (M + M/2 + M/4 + ...) times 8 bytes approx 2M times 8 bytes = 32M bytes approx 100 bytes per vector. Total RAM table: N = 1M -> 3 GB vectors + 100 MB graph = 3.1 GB. N = 100M -> 300 GB + 10 GB = 310 GB. N = 1B -> 3 TB + 100 GB = 3.1 TB. Everything assumes you have not added metadata, filter index, or replica copy.
summary: An HNSW graph with M=16 edges adds ~100 bytes per vector (about 3% overhead) bringing 1B-scale total to 3.1 TB.
queries: How much memory does HNSW graph add?

text: Does the math still work on one server? AWS's largest memory-optimized instance for vector workloads is the r7i.24xlarge: 768 GB of DDR5 RAM for around $5,000 per month on-demand. That single box can hold a 100M vector index comfortably. It cannot hold 1 billion. At that point you need sharding across 4-8 machines, which doubles or triples cost and adds query-fan-out latency. One server fits: r7i.24xlarge 768 GB RAM, 100M vectors = 310 GB, headroom 458 GB for cache + copies, cost ~$5,000/month. One server cannot fit: r7i.24xlarge 768 GB RAM, 1B vectors = 3.1 TB, needs 4+ shards, cost ~$20,000+/month. At 1B scale, raw vectors drive 80% of the bill. Shrinking them is the single biggest cost lever.
summary: One AWS r7i.24xlarge (768 GB, ~$5K/mo) holds 100M vectors but a 1B index requires 4+ shards at ~$20K+/mo.

text: Four ways to shrink a vector. Production systems use four main techniques. Each targets a different point on the accuracy-compression curve. They stack: a real system often uses scalar quantization plus PQ, or binary plus Matryoshka truncation. The framework for picking is recall cost versus memory win. (1) Scalar quantization: 4x compress, 1-3% recall drop, float32 to int8 one byte per dimension. (2) Product quantization (PQ): 32x compress, 3-8% recall drop, split into subvectors and replace each with a 1-byte codebook id. (3) Binary quantization: 32x compress, 5-10% recall drop, one bit per dimension, XOR and popcount for distance. (4) Matryoshka embeddings: 6x compress, minimal recall loss if trained for it, truncate the vector dimension itself at query time. Memory budget is almost always the forcing function in production vector search.
summary: Production teams compress vectors with scalar quantization (4x), PQ (32x), binary quantization (32x), or Matryoshka truncation (6x), trading recall for memory.
queries: What are the main vector compression techniques?
Which compression has lowest recall loss?

text: Number line diagram for SQ drift: calibrated cyan band from -1.2 to 1.4 with a 0-to-255 bucket scale beneath. Green training point at 0.5 maps cleanly to bucket 167. Red new-vector point at 1.8 sits outside the band; a bent arrow shows it clipped back to 1.4 = bucket 255, with a red gap bar labelled error = 0.4. A faded tombstone marker shows a deleted entry whose int8 row still lives.
summary: Visual of how an out-of-range insert gets clipped to the calibrated max, producing a 0.4 error and leaving a tombstone behind.

text: Two stacked number lines comparing min/max calibration to p1/p99 + 10% headroom. Top band spans -1.2 to 1.4; new value 1.8 clips with a red error bar of 0.4. Bottom band spans -1.4 to 1.7 (percentile-based with headroom); the same new value clips with a much smaller error bar of only 0.1. Visualizes the industry fix: widening calibration with quantile-based bounds dramatically shrinks clip error.
summary: Compares min/max vs p1/p99 + headroom calibration, showing the percentile approach reduces clip error from 0.4 to 0.1 on the same out-of-range value.
queries: min max vs p1 p99 calibration diagram

text: Scalar Quantization (SQ) compresses each float32 dimension to one int8 byte by linearly mapping per-dim [min, max] to [0, 255]. Build-time: one calibration pass over the corpus. Encode: q = round((v - min)/(max - min) * 255). At d = 768, vectors shrink from 3,072 bytes to 768 bytes (4x smaller). Distances run on int8 SIMD instructions like AVX-512 VPDPBUSD, 3-4x faster than float32. Recall loss is just 1-3% on typical embeddings. Drawbacks: out-of-range new values get clipped (mitigated with p1/p99 + headroom and recalibration jobs); tombstones handled by vacuum/compaction. SQ slots into any index unchanged - HNSW, IVF, or flat - because only the per-vector payload changes, not the distance algorithm. It is the first compression step nearly every production vector DB enables: FAISS, Qdrant, pgvector, Milvus, Vespa, Pinecone all support it natively.
summary: Chapter overview: scalar quantization gives 4x memory and 3-4x speed wins for ~1-3% recall loss by mapping each dim to one int8 byte, dropping into any index unchanged.
queries: why use SQ in vector search
SQ in HNSW IVF flat indexes

text: A float32 vector uses 4 bytes per dimension. A 768-dim embedding is 3,072 bytes (3 KB). Most embedding coordinates sit in a narrow range like [0.05, 0.95], so spending a full float32 per dim is wasteful. An 8-bit integer carries enough precision for search. Doc 1 first 8 dims: [0.81, 0.12, 0.45, 0.22, 0.63, 0.07, 0.38, 0.91] = 32 bytes. Full vector: 768 dims x 4 bytes = 3,072 bytes. Question: what do we lose if we replace each 4-byte float with one byte?
summary: Establishes float32 baseline cost of 4 bytes per dim and 3 KB per 768-dim vector before scalar quantization.

text: Calibration is a one-time pass at index build. Walk the entire corpus and for each of the 768 dimensions find the smallest and largest value seen. Per-dim ranges differ - dim 0 might be [0.09, 0.83], dim 5 [0.05, 0.72] - so one global range wastes precision. Per-dimension calibration is the norm. Example calibration table for first 8 dims: min = [0.09, 0.10, 0.12, 0.19, 0.11, 0.05, 0.10, 0.62], max = [0.83, 0.88, 0.74, 0.81, 0.95, 0.72, 0.70, 0.93]. One scan, one pair of numbers per dimension - tiny metadata cost, foundational for every quantize/dequantize op.
summary: Explains scanning the corpus to record per-dimension min and max values used to calibrate the int8 mapping.

text: Each dimension's float range is stretched linearly onto integers 0 to 255. An 8-bit unsigned integer gives 256 buckets; the smallest value lands at 0, the largest at 255. Bucket size for dim i = (max_i - min_i) / 255. Formulas: quantize(v, min, max) = round((v - min) / (max - min) * 255). dequantize(q, min, max) = min + (q / 255) * (max - min). Worked example on dim 0: v = 0.81, min = 0.09, max = 0.83. q = round((0.81 - 0.09) / (0.83 - 0.09) * 255) = round(0.9730 * 255) = round(248.1) = 248. Round-to-nearest is most common; some implementations use floor or stochastic rounding. 248 of 255 buckets means we lose about 0.2% of representable precision.
summary: Gives the linear quantize/dequantize formulas and walks a worked example mapping 0.81 to bucket 248.
queries: how to quantize float to int8
how to convert float to byte for vector
how is precision lost in SQ

text: Apply the linear map to every dimension. Float32 array becomes int8 byte array of same length. Before float32: [0.81, 0.12, 0.45, 0.22, 0.63, 0.07, 0.38, 0.91] = 8 x 4 = 32 bytes. After int8 (per-dim min/max applied): centroid IDs like [248, 9, 130, 11, 154, 8, 124, 244] in 8 x 1 = 8 bytes (4x smaller). At d = 768 the row becomes 768 bytes instead of 3,072. Same semantic content, a quarter of the footprint.
summary: Shows the same 8-dim slice before and after scalar quantization, dropping from 32 to 8 bytes (4x smaller).

text: Modern CPUs have dedicated int8 dot-product instructions. AVX-512 VPDPBUSD processes 64 int8 multiply-accumulates per op; AVX-512 F float32 does only 16 floats per op. Float32 path: vfmadd132ps, ~48 ops per 768-dim pair. Int8 path: VPDPBUSD, ~12 ops per 768-dim pair. Net: int8 distance is 3-4x faster than float32 per vector, on top of the 4x memory win. Stacked: same hardware serves 3-4x more QPS. FAISS, Qdrant, pgvector, and Milvus all use int8 SIMD kernels when scalar-quantized.
summary: Describes how int8 SIMD instructions like VPDPBUSD make scalar-quantized distance computation 3-4x faster than float32.
queries: why is int8 faster than float32

text: The tradeoff is lopsided: memory shrinks 4x, distance computation speeds up because operations are cheaper, recall degrades only 1-3% on typical embedding benchmarks. Essentially free across production workloads. Virtually every vector DB enables it by default or as a one-line toggle. d = 768 vector. Before: 3,072 bytes (float32). After: 768 bytes (int8), bytes per vector = d. Compression: 4x. Recall loss: 1-3%. What you get: 4x less RAM, 3-4x faster distance, simple to implement. What you pay: 1-3% recall loss on BERT-style embeddings, requires a calibration pass at build time.
summary: Sums up scalar quantization as a 4x memory win and 3-4x speed win for only 1-3% recall loss, the easy-win first compression step.

how much recall does scalar quantization cost
first compression step in vector DB

text: New vectors arrive after calibration: insert/update/delete drift the calibrated range. INSERT: new value 1.8 outside [-1.2, 1.4]. UPDATE: replaced value 1.8 still outside band, gets clipped to 1.4 = bucket 255 with red error bar of 0.4. DELETE: stale int8 row stays as a tombstone; live min/max drift over time. Number line shows calibrated cyan band -1.2 to 1.4 with 0-to-255 bucket scale, green training point at 0.5 mapping to bucket 167, red new-vector point at 1.8 sitting outside band with bent arrow clipping back to the boundary, and a faded tombstone marker for a deleted entry whose int8 row still lives.
summary: Diagram showing how inserts, updates, and deletes drift the calibrated min/max range and force clipping, with a 0.4 error bar.
queries: what happens when new value is outside SQ range

text: Industry fix: percentile bounds (p1/p99) plus 10% headroom, tombstone vacuum, and recalibration job. Two stacked number lines: top min/max band [-1.2, 1.4] with new value 1.8 clipping at error 0.4; bottom p1/p99 + 10% headroom band [-1.4, 1.7] with same new value clipping at error only 0.1. Per-op behaviour: INSERT/UPDATE rare clip, drift counter increments. DELETE tombstone bit set, vacuum job rebuilds row store. DRIFT: more than 0.5% clipped triggers scheduled recalibration. Real systems: FAISS IndexScalarQuantizer(d, QT_8bit_uniform) + custom range; Qdrant quantization_config.scalar.quantile = 0.99 + optimizers_config.deleted_threshold; Pinecone managed re-quantization on shard rebalance; Vespa tensor int8 cell-type with explicit bounds + auto-compact.
summary: Industry mitigations for SQ drift: percentile bounds with headroom, tombstone vacuum, and triggered recalibration, illustrated by reduced clip error from 0.4 to 0.1.

text: Scalar quantization is a drop-in replacement for the vector payload. Index structure - HNSW graph, IVF posting lists, or flat scan - is unchanged; only per-vector storage swaps from float32 to int8. No new distance algorithm, no rescore stage, no codebook training. HNSW + SQ: same multi-layer graph, node payload 768 int8 bytes, edges/traversal unchanged, distance via int8 dot product. IVF + SQ: same nlist centroids float32, posting list payload int8, nprobe scan unchanged. Flat + SQ: brute-force linear scan, row storage int8, 4x more vectors per cache line. APIs: pgvector halfvec / vector(int8); Qdrant quantization_config.scalar.type = int8; FAISS IndexHNSWSQ, IndexIVFScalarQuantizer; Milvus IVF_SQ8, HNSW_SQ8. PQ has dedicated combo chapters because its asymmetric LUT changes how distance is computed. SQ does not change distance, only its precision.
summary: Shows that scalar quantization slots transparently into HNSW, IVF, or flat indexes by swapping only the per-vector payload from float32 to int8.

text: Product Quantization (PQ) compresses high-dim vectors by chopping each 768-dim vector into m=96 slots of 8 dims, training a 256-entry k-means codebook per slot, and replacing each sub-vector with a 1-byte centroid id. Result: 3,072 bytes shrinks to 96 bytes (32x compression). Search uses Asymmetric Distance Computation (ADC): build a per-query 96x256 lookup table once, then score each doc with 96 lookups + 96 adds (no multiplies). OPQ adds a learned orthonormal rotation R before splitting, decorrelating dims so per-slot k-means clusters tighten and recall@10 lifts from 0.89 to 0.94. Drift from inserts/updates/deletes triggers retraining when 95p reconstruction error breaches threshold. The m knob trades bytes-per-vector against recall: m=96 is the production sweet spot (32x compression, 0.96 recall).
summary: Chapter overview: PQ compresses 768-dim vectors to 96 bytes via per-slot codebooks, ADC search uses lookup tables, OPQ rotation boosts recall, m=96 is the sweet spot.

text: Cut one fat vector into 96 small ones. A 768-dim embedding is too fat to compress as a single thing. PQ's first move is to chop it into 96 small pieces, 8 dims each. Each piece is called a slot. Why slots matter: every slot gets its own dictionary. Slot 0's dictionary only has to describe patterns appearing in dims 0-7 across the corpus - much easier than describing all 768 dims at once. Formula: d = 768, m = 96, d/m = 8 dims per slot. One vector becomes 96 mini-problems, each small enough to compress hard.
summary: Step 1 of PQ: split a 768-dim vector into m=96 slots of 8 dims each so each slot can be compressed independently.

text: Each slot learns its own 256-word dictionary. Pick one slot - say slot 0. Look at slot 0's sub-vector across every document in the corpus - billions of 8-D points. Run k-means on them with k = 256. k-means finds 256 prototype points called centroids that summarize this cloud. These 256 centroids are slot 0's codebook. Every future slot-0 sub-vector will be replaced by whichever of these 256 it is closest to. Why exactly 256? Because 2^8 = 256, and that fits in a single byte. The whole PQ design is built around squeezing one slot into one byte. Total codebook size: 96 slots x 256 centroids x 8 dims x 4 bytes = 786 KB total codebook (one-time cost, fits in L2 cache). 256 prototypes per slot, 96 codebooks, the whole dictionary is L2-resident.
summary: Step 2 of PQ: per-slot k-means with k=256 builds a codebook so each sub-vector can be encoded as a single byte (2^8=256).

text: Encode = snap each slice to its nearest prototype. After all 96 codebooks exist, encoding a new document vector is just lookup. For each of its 96 slots, find the closest centroid in that slot's codebook. Write down that centroid's index - a number 0 to 255. That index fits in one byte. Repeat 96 times. The whole 768-dim vector is now 96 bytes. The original floats are thrown away. The index stores only the byte codes. Example: Doc 1 code = [17, 203, 89, 142, ..., 71] - 96 centroid ids, 1 byte each, 96 bytes total. A vector becomes 96 byte-pointers into 96 tiny codebooks.
summary: Step 3 of PQ: encoding finds the nearest centroid per slot and stores its 1-byte index, turning a 768-dim vector into 96 bytes of centroid ids.

text: 3,072 bytes shrinks to 96 bytes per vector - 32x smaller. One float32 vector at d = 768 takes 3,072 bytes. The PQ code at m = 96 takes 96 bytes. Compression ratio = 32x per vector. At billion-vector scale the economics change: storage stops being the bottleneck. The whole corpus fits on one server with room for the graph index and a cache. float32: 768 x 4 = 3,072 bytes. PQ (m=96): 96 x 1 = 96 bytes. Storage table: N=1M float=3 GB PQ=96 MB; N=100M float=300 GB PQ=9.6 GB; N=1B float=3 TB PQ=96 GB. A billion vectors in 96 GB on one server.
summary: Step 4 of PQ: float32 to PQ-code yields 32x compression - 3 TB at 1B vectors becomes 96 GB.

text: Search at query time uses Asymmetric Distance Computation (ADC) in a 4-frame storyboard. Frame A (setup): query arrives as a float vector with 96 sub-queries; docs stored only as 96-byte PQ codes - no float doc vectors on disk. Frame B (build LUT once per query): each sub-query q_s feeds into one column of the lookup table; LUT shape 96 cols x 256 rows = 24,576 floats ~96 KB, fits L2, computed once per query. Frame C (score one doc): 96 lookups + 96 adds - each PQ code byte indexes its slot's LUT column, the cell value is read, all 96 cells summed for d(q, doc). No multiplies during scan - just byte reads, table lookups, adds. Frame D (rank all docs): repeat for every doc, sort ascending, return top-k. Cost comparison: float32 brute = 768 mul + 768 add per doc; SQ int8 = 768 byte ops, ~3-4x faster; PQ + ADC = 96 lookups + 96 adds, no multiplies, ~10x faster. Asymmetric: query stays precise, docs stay approximate.
summary: Step 5 of PQ: ADC search builds a per-query 96x256 LUT once, then scores each doc as 96 lookups + 96 adds with no multiplies.

text: OPQ: rotate first, so the slots line up with the data. Real embedding dimensions are not independent - dim 0 and dim 200 might be highly correlated. When PQ chops the vector by raw position, correlated information gets split across slots and each slot's k-means sees a stretched, awkward cloud. OPQ (Optimized Product Quantization) fixes this: learn an orthonormal rotation matrix R alongside the codebooks. Apply Rv to every vector before splitting. The rotation decorrelates the dimensions, the per-slot clusters become tight and round, k-means fits them better, and recall goes up. Pipeline: v -> Rv -> split -> encode. Recall@10 at m = 96: plain PQ 0.89, OPQ + PQ 0.94. R is 768 x 768 orthonormal, learned alongside codebooks. Free recall bump for one extra matrix multiply. FAISS, ScaNN, and Qdrant all default to OPQ.
summary: Step 6: OPQ learns an orthonormal rotation R applied before splitting, decorrelating dims so per-slot k-means tightens and recall@10 lifts from 0.89 to 0.94.
