text: Writes go to a single leader (primary). The leader applies the write and ships it asynchronously to followers, who apply it and become eventually consistent. Replication lag is the gap between leader-applied and follower-applied. Typical lag: steady state 50 ms (P50 normal load), moderate spike 200 ms (write burst, healthy pipeline), heavy spike or rebuild 2 s (followers fall behind). Leader accepts at t=0; followers apply at t=50 ms to 2 s. Queries in the lag window see stale results. "I just added a document but search doesn't find it" is almost always lag, not a bug. Either wait out the window, or route the read-after-write to the leader.
summary: Leader-follower writes with async replication produce 50 ms to 2 s lag, causing stale reads inside the lag window.
queries: why do my new docs not show up immediately
read after write consistency vector search
how long until follower catches up
fix stale read in vector DB
route reads to leader for consistency

text: If the leader fails, a follower is promoted to primary via a consensus algorithm (Raft in most modern vector DBs). Failover timeline: t=0 ms leader processes write W_{n+1}; t=10 ms leader crashes - W_{n+1} never replicated; t=50 ms followers notice, Raft election starts; t=500 ms new leader promoted, cluster accepts writes again. Lost window: writes in-flight at t=0 to t=10 ms. Async replication (default) gives fast writes (ack at leader), at-most-50-ms lag, but possible data loss on failover. Sync replication gives slower writes (ack only after one follower applies), zero data loss on failover, but write latency ≈ 2x async. Most vector DBs default to async; pick sync only when a few lost writes in a rare failover would cost more than steady-state latency.
summary: On leader crash, Raft promotes a follower in ~500 ms; async replication risks losing in-flight writes while sync replication trades 2x write latency for zero data loss.
queries: what happens when leader crashes vector DB
data loss on failover vector database
when should I use sync replication
ack on leader vs ack on follower

text: Vector indexes live in RAM for speed. RAM is volatile - if every node restarts at once, durability depends on the persistence model. Three patterns, usually layered: WAL on disk - every write appended to a log before ack, RPO = 0 (synchronous) or 1 batch. Periodic snapshots - dump the index to disk every N minutes, RPO = interval, faster startup. Rehydrate from source - re-read every doc from the app DB and re-embed, RPO = 0 but slow. If every RAM copy dies, the index is only as durable as its WAL + snapshot. The source-of-truth DB is the ultimate recovery path. Many teams never test full cluster loss; the first time it happens they discover snapshot cadence is 24 hours and WAL retention is 1 hour, leaving them stuck re-embedding for days.
summary: Durability comes from layering WAL, periodic snapshots, and source-of-truth rehydration, each with different RPO and recovery characteristics.
queries: how is vector index made durable
WAL write ahead log vector database
RPO recovery point objective vector DB
what if every RAM copy dies
source of truth database for vectors
test full cluster loss vector DB

text: Worst case: entire cluster dies. Recovery time depends on what is cached. For a 100M-vector corpus: snapshot + WAL replay takes 10 min to 2 hours (disk I/O bound, graph pages stream in); WAL-only replay with no snapshot takes ~12 hours (every insert replayed from scratch); re-embed from source DB takes 1 to 14 days (embedding API throughput plus dollars billed per token). RTO budget = snapshot cadence + WAL replay rate. Pick snapshot interval that matches your RTO target. Re-embed is the fallback of last resort, measured in days. Put this math into the runbook before a real outage; the cost of a one-day outage at 10K QPS usually dwarfs the cost of snapshots every 30 minutes.
summary: Recovery from total cluster loss for 100M vectors ranges from 10 minutes (snapshot+WAL) to 14 days (full re-embed); pick snapshot cadence to hit your RTO.
queries: how long to recover 100M vector index
recovery time after total cluster loss
cost of one day vector search outage

text: Hybrid-search flow diagram with a green query icon on the left that splits into two parallel lanes. The upper cyan lane flows to a Vector ANN box emitting a top-N ranked list. The lower yellow lane flows to a BM25 box emitting another top-N ranked list. Both lists arrive at a green merge node on the right labeled RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion). Illustrates the parallel-ranker architecture of hybrid search: two independent retrievers run side by side and a score-free rank fusion step merges their outputs into a single final ranking.
summary: Diagram of hybrid-search architecture: query fans out to parallel Vector ANN and BM25 lanes, each producing top-N, merged at an RRF node.
queries: diagram of vector and BM25 in parallel
fan out to vector and lexical diagram

text: Hybrid search recovers the literal-token signal that pure vector embeddings smooth away by running BM25 keyword search and vector ANN in parallel and merging their ranked lists. BM25 scores docs by IDF times saturating term-frequency with Lucene defaults k1=1.2, b=0.75. Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with k=60 combines the two ranked lists score-free as sum of 1/(k+rank). Hybrid wins when queries mix exact terms (SKUs, proper nouns, rare tokens like 'tabby') with semantic intent ('cat genetics'); pure vector misses literals, pure BM25 returns off-topic exact matches. Production deployments use weighted RRF score = alpha*RRF_vec + beta*RRF_bm25, default alpha=0.7 beta=0.3 for natural language, flipped for code/catalog. Worth it for mixed queries, multi-lingual, RAG; skip for pure chat or strict <10ms latency.
summary: Hybrid search merges vector ANN with BM25 via RRF to recover exact-token retrieval that pure embeddings lose.
queries: when to use hybrid search recap
hybrid search architecture in one place
BM25 RRF weighted alpha beta summary

text: Semantic embeddings reward meaning - two docs saying the same thing with different words end up close in embedding space. The flip side: docs containing unique identifiers like SKU-A4291, proper nouns like 'Dr. Whiskers', or rare tokens like 'kernel panic 0x7B' get no special treatment - the exact token is smoothed into its neighborhood. Semantic search silently misses lookups where the word itself is the signal. What vectors lose: SKUs and IDs (SKU-A4291, ORD-88213) need exact match; proper nouns (Rajul, Mumbai, AlphaFold) are rare in training; rare terms (Tabby, kernel panic, RoPE) get blurred into neighbors. Vector search excels at meaning, misses literal tokens. Hybrid search combines vectors with keyword search to recover both. The rescue is classic BM25 lexical search running alongside the vector index - merge the two ranked lists and you keep the best of each.
summary: Why pure vector search fails on exact-token lookups like SKUs, proper nouns, and rare technical terms.
queries: why does vector search miss exact terms
why semantic search fails on SKUs
why dense vectors lose rare tokens
why I need keyword search alongside vectors
why pure vector retrieval misses identifiers
embeddings vs keyword search exact match
what is hybrid search and why use it
why we need BM25 plus vectors

text: BM25 is the lexical-search workhorse, shipped in every production search engine since Lucene. For a query of terms t, a document d scores by summing the IDF of each term (rare terms count more) times a saturating term-frequency contribution (more appearances help, with diminishing returns). Formula: BM25(q, d) = sum over t in q of IDF(t) * TF_norm(t, d). IDF(t) = log(N / df(t)) - inverse document frequency. TF_norm(t, d) = tf(t, d) * (k1 + 1) / (tf(t, d) + k1 * length-adjustment). Standard Lucene defaults: k1 ~ 1.2, b ~ 0.75. Worked example on cat corpus, query 'cats domesticated': Doc 1 'Cats are small domesticated carnivores' BM25=4.82 rank 1; Doc 5 'Tigers are striped cats' BM25=2.30 rank 2; Doc 3 'Lions are big cats that live in Africa' BM25=2.10 rank 3; Doc 8 'The dog chased the cat' BM25=1.90 rank 4; Doc 7 'Kittens grow up to be cats' BM25=1.70 rank 5. Doc 1 wins because it contains both query terms and is the shortest match. BM25's length-normalization keeps it fair across docs of different sizes.
summary: BM25 lexical scoring formula combines IDF and saturating term frequency with length normalization.

text: Given a query, fire both retrieval systems simultaneously. The vector index returns its top-N by embedding similarity; the BM25 index returns its top-N by lexical score. Neither list needs to know the other exists at this stage - they each do what they are good at. Merging is a separate step. Vector returns top-N by similarity, BM25 returns top-N by lexical score, then merge them. The two systems can even live on different nodes. The only shared state is the query itself and the final merge point. Architecture: query splits into two parallel lanes - upper lane to Vector ANN producing top-N ranked list, lower lane to BM25 producing top-N ranked list, both feeding into an RRF merge node. This is the parallel-ranker architecture of hybrid search.
summary: Hybrid search runs vector ANN and BM25 in parallel as independent rankers, each producing its own top-N list.
queries: how does hybrid search architecture work
vector ANN and BM25 in parallel
how do you combine vector and keyword search
can vector and BM25 run on separate nodes
fan out to vector and BM25
how to merge two ranked lists

text: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) combines two ranked lists without needing their raw scores - BM25 and cosine live on different scales. For each doc, compute 1 / (k + rank) on every ranker that returned it, then sum. The constant k dampens the contribution of low-ranking docs; the Lucene-standard value is k = 60 from the 2009 TREC paper. Formula: RRF(d) = sum over r in rankers of 1 / (k + rank_r(d)). RRF on cat corpus example: Doc 1 vec rank 2, BM25 rank 1, calc 1/62 + 1/61 = 0.0326 (winner); Doc 3 vec rank 1, BM25 rank 3, 1/61 + 1/63 = 0.0323; Doc 5 vec rank 4, BM25 rank 2, 1/64 + 1/62 = 0.0317; Doc 7 vec rank 3, BM25 rank 5, 1/63 + 1/65 = 0.0313; Doc 8 vec rank 5, BM25 rank 4, 1/65 + 1/64 = 0.0310. Doc 1 wins because it ranked well on both. RRF rewards doc consensus; docs only one ranker likes fall below the consensus winners.
summary: Reciprocal Rank Fusion merges ranked lists by summing 1/(k+rank) with k=60 to reward documents both rankers agree on.
queries: how to merge ranked lists without raw scores
how to combine different score scales

text: Concrete side-by-side for query 'tabby cat genetics'. Pure vector finds semantically cat-ish docs and misses 'tabby': 1) Lions are big cats (near miss), 2) Tigers are striped cats (near miss), 3) Cats are small carnivores, 4) Kittens grow up to be cats, 5) My cat sat on the mat. No hit on tabby - the actual topic. Pure BM25 finds the exact 'tabby' hit but ranks unrelated fluff with the word in it: 1) Tabby cat coat color genetics, 2) Abyssinian cat tabby pattern, 3) Genetics 101 textbook (unrelated), 4) Random tabby mention in a tweet, 5) Tabby bar and grill menu. Off-topic exact matches rank high. Hybrid (RRF) merges: 1) Tabby cat coat color genetics, 2) Abyssinian cat tabby pattern, 3) Tigers are striped cats, 4) Cats are small carnivores, 5) Kittens grow up to be cats. Tabby + genetics concentrated at the top. Hybrid wins when the query has both exact and semantic intent. Pure vector loses 'tabby', pure BM25 loses 'cat genetics'. If product queries ever include a specific term that must match exactly, hybrid is almost always worth enabling.
summary: Worked comparison showing pure vector misses 'tabby', pure BM25 returns off-topic exact matches, hybrid RRF concentrates relevant results at top.
queries: hybrid search vs vector search example
when does hybrid search beat vector
side by side hybrid vs pure vector
why hybrid wins on mixed queries
comparison hybrid pure vector pure BM25

text: Production deployments often weight the two rankers. Standard form: score(d) = alpha * RRF_vec(d) + beta * RRF_bm25(d). Typical defaults: alpha = 0.7, beta = 0.3 when corpus is mostly natural language; flip to 0.3/0.7 for code or catalog data where literal tokens dominate. Tune via held-out recall on your own data via A/B tests. When hybrid wins: mixed natural-language plus SKU queries, multi-lingual or technical corpora, long-tail identifier lookups, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). When hybrid is not worth it: pure chat or FAQ where vectors are already great, very short queries with no identifiers, strict latency budgets under 10 ms, BM25 index is itself unmaintained. Weighted hybrid adds operational complexity - two indexes, two query paths, one merge. Turn it on only after you measure a specific quality gap vectors alone cannot close.
summary: Weighted RRF tunes alpha/beta per data type and hybrid is only worth its operational complexity for specific workload patterns.
queries: when is hybrid search worth it
when not to use hybrid search

Arrows go up from each token into a single wide yellow rounded rectangle labeled 'Transformer (full attention across all tokens)' that spans all tokens. A green Score circle sits at the top connected by an arrow from the transformer output. Illustrates that cross-encoder rerankers run full attention over the concatenated query and doc tokens in one forward pass, emitting a single scalar score.

text: Chapter 11.25 covers rerankers in vector search. Two-stage retrieval: stage 1 ANN pulls top-100 candidates fast (~5 ms over 1B vectors with HNSW); stage 2 cross-encoder rescorer reranks those 100 to top-10 (~100 ms on A10 GPU). Cross-encoder concatenates query + doc tokens ([CLS] query [SEP] doc [SEP]) and runs one transformer pass with full attention, emitting a single relevance score. Beats bi-encoder cosine because token-level interactions are preserved instead of pooled. Quality jump: +10 to +20 MRR@10 over bi-encoder. Latency depends on GPU: T4 5 ms/pair, A10 1 ms, A100 0.5 ms, H100 0.3 ms. Production models: Cohere Rerank 3 (managed API, ~400M params), BGE-reranker v2 (open weights, 268M base / 560M large from BAAI), MS-MARCO cross-encoders (MiniLM 33M, BERT-base 110M, classic baseline), RankGPT/LLM-as-judge (highest quality, 10x latency). Reranker cannot fix bad stage-1 recall - it can only reorder existing candidates.
summary: Chapter overview of two-stage retrieval with cross-encoder rerankers, costs, models, and limits.
queries: what is a reranker in vector search
why use a reranker after ANN
how much does reranking add to latency

text: Two-stage retrieval: ANN returns top-100, rerank returns top-10. Accurate retrieval does not need to be accurate everywhere - only at the top. Stage 1 uses ANN to quickly pull 100 roughly-relevant candidates from a billion-vector index. Stage 2 is a slower but smarter model that re-scores those 100 candidates. Total cost: fast over the billion, slow over the hundred. Stage-2 accuracy matters only on k=100, not on N=1B. Stage 1 retrieval: corpus N = 1B vectors, ANN latency ~5 ms per query (HNSW), output top-100 candidates, may include some near-misses. Candidate quality is fine at k=100, not at k=10. Stages: Stage 1 retrieval - 5 ms - HNSW/IVF-PQ - 1B to 100. Stage 2 rerank - 100 ms - cross-encoder - 100 to 10. Final - ~105 ms - combined - 10. The two-stage pattern is standard in search engines, ads systems, and RAG pipelines.
summary: Two-stage retrieval splits work: fast ANN narrows 1B to 100, slow reranker picks final 10.
queries: what does ANN return before rerank
how many candidates does a reranker score
why not run reranker on whole corpus
how does retrieval scale to 1 billion vectors

text: Cross-encoder: query and doc concatenated into one transformer pass. A cross-encoder takes the query and the candidate doc, concatenates their tokens into one input stream (often [CLS] query [SEP] doc [SEP]), runs the whole thing through a transformer, and emits a single relevance score. Because query and doc tokens share attention inside the stack, the model can condition on exactly which part of the doc matches which part of the query. Input = [CLS] query [SEP] doc [SEP]. One transformer pass, one scalar output. Score = P(doc is relevant to query). The architecture is dead simple. The expense is running it per candidate: each doc is a full forward pass through the model.
summary: Cross-encoder concatenates query and doc tokens through one transformer to emit a relevance score.
queries: how does a cross encoder work
what is the input format for cross encoder
why one forward pass per document
full attention query and doc tokens
what is the output of a cross encoder

text: Bi-encoder cosine cannot see query-doc token interactions. A bi-encoder produces one vector per query and one per doc, then measures cosine similarity. All token-level information is collapsed into those two vectors before they meet. The cross-encoder keeps the tokens separate and lets full attention decide which interactions matter. Bi-encoder (stage 1): Query -> encoder -> q_vec; Doc -> encoder -> d_vec; Score = cosine(q_vec, d_vec). No token-level interaction - everything pooled into a single vector first. Cross-encoder (stage 2): Concat(query, doc) -> transformer; full attention across all tokens; Score = one scalar. Query tokens attend to doc tokens and vice versa - maximally informed score. Typical quality jump: +10 to +20 MRR@10 over bi-encoder alone. MS-MARCO leaderboards: cross-encoders win on almost every benchmark. Price: cannot pre-compute cross-encoder scores like embeddings - every (query, doc) pair is a fresh forward pass.
summary: Cross-encoder beats bi-encoder by preserving token-level interactions instead of pooling, gaining +10 to +20 MRR@10.
queries: why is cross encoder more accurate
why cant you pre-compute cross encoder scores
quality gap bi encoder cross encoder
why rerank instead of just embeddings

text: Rerank: score each candidate, sort, return top-10. Once stage 1 has supplied 100 candidates, run the cross-encoder once per candidate with the same query. Sort by score, return top-10 to user. The rerank does not change which candidates exist - only their ordering. A bad stage-1 candidate set cannot be fixed at stage 2. Algorithm: for candidate d in top-100: score(d) = cross_encoder(query, d); sort candidates by score desc; return top-10. Example before rerank (stage 1 ANN rank) vs after rerank (stage 2): Doc 1 'Cats are small domesticated carnivores' ANN rank 4 -> rerank 1. Doc 7 'Kittens grow up to be cats' ANN 2 -> rerank 2. Doc 3 'Lions are big cats that live in Africa' ANN 1 -> rerank 3. Doc 4 'My cat sat on the mat' ANN 7 -> rerank 4. Doc 5 'Tigers are striped cats' ANN 3 -> rerank 5. The reranker is not psychic - stage 1 must include the right doc in its top-100. Recall at stage 1 is the upstream bottleneck.
summary: Reranker scores each of the 100 candidates with the cross-encoder and reorders them; bad stage-1 recall caps the result.
queries: how does rerank actually work step by step
rerank score sort top k algorithm
example of rerank changing rank order
ANN rank vs reranker rank example
why doc 1 jumps from rank 4 to rank 1
rerank does not add new candidates
if ANN misses can rerank recover

text: Latency cost: 100 rerank runs is about 100 ms on an A10 GPU. Cross-encoders are small transformers - BERT-base or BERT-large sized. On a modern GPU, one (query, doc) forward pass is ~1 ms. 100 candidates = ~100 ms latency added on top of stage 1. Big enough to notice, small enough to justify for quality-critical paths. Reranker latency on production GPUs: T4 (older) 5 ms per-pair, 500 ms for 100 candidates, 2/sec QPS cap. A10: 1 ms per-pair, 100 ms for 100, 10/sec. A100: 0.5 ms per-pair, 50 ms for 100, 20/sec. H100: 0.3 ms per-pair, 30 ms for 100, 33/sec. Reranker latency = candidates · per-pair cost. Batch all 100 pairs on one GPU to hit these numbers. Dropping to top-50 halves latency. In RAG, this extra 100 ms usually trades against an LLM call that already costs 500 ms - rerankers fit comfortably in that budget.
summary: Reranking 100 candidates costs ~100 ms on A10, scaling linearly with candidates and per-pair GPU time.

text: Production models: Cohere Rerank, BGE-reranker, MS-MARCO cross-encoders. Three families dominate production. Cohere's managed API ships general-purpose rerankers via simple REST call. BGE and similar open-weight models from BAAI run locally on GPU. MS-MARCO cross-encoders are the classic open-source baseline and still competitive. Production models: Cohere Rerank 3 - managed API, ~400M params, multi-lingual, context 4K tokens, simple REST. BGE-reranker v2 - open weights, base 268M / large 560M, BAAI release, self-host on A10/A100, BGE-reranker-m3 multilingual. MS-MARCO cross-encoder - open weights, MiniLM ~33M, BERT-base 110M, Sentence-Transformers, classic baseline, smallest latency. RankGPT / LLM-as-judge - managed LLM, listwise LLM-driven, highest quality, 10x latency, expensive. Strategy: start with MS-MARCO MiniLM as cheap baseline, move to BGE-reranker or Cohere when quality matters, reserve LLM-as-judge for offline evals. Swap reranker without touching stage 1 - interface is always (query, candidates) -> reordered candidates, making A/B tests straightforward.
summary: Cohere Rerank, BGE-reranker, MS-MARCO MiniLM, and RankGPT are the four production reranker families with different cost/quality trades.
queries: what reranker should I use in production

text: Diagram: 3x5 token-level similarity grid for ColBERT max-sim. Rows are query tokens (Cats, Live, Outside); columns are doc tokens (Cats, Are, Small, Carnivores, That). Each cell shows the cosine similarity between that query token and doc token: row 1 [0.94, 0.22, 0.18, 0.35, 0.21] max 0.94; row 2 [0.11, 0.17, 0.15, 0.25, 0.28] max 0.28; row 3 [0.19, 0.14, 0.21, 0.17, 0.26] max 0.26. The max in each row is highlighted in green. The right-most 'Max' column accumulates row maxes; their sum (0.94 + 0.28 + 0.26 = 1.48) is the ColBERT max-sim score for this (query, doc) pair. The visual makes clear that each query token picks its single best doc-token match independently.
summary: Visualization of the 3x5 ColBERT similarity grid - query tokens on rows, doc tokens on columns, row maxes highlighted, summed to score 1.48.
queries: how to draw a ColBERT score table
highlighted row max in similarity matrix
what does the ColBERT scoring matrix look like

text: Multi-vector retrieval (ColBERT-style) keeps one contextual embedding per token instead of pooling each document into a single dense vector. ColBERT (Stanford 2020, Contextualized Late Interaction over BERT) encodes a ~200-token doc as ~200 vectors and a query as ~5 token vectors, then scores via late interaction max-sim: for each query token, take the maximum cosine/dot similarity against any doc token, sum those maxes. This rewards docs that match every query token somewhere, recovering rare-term and long-document fidelity that single-vector cosine averages away. Cost: storage balloons 20-100x (e.g. 1M docs at d=768, 200 tokens/doc => 600 GB float32, ~60 GB with PQ/ColBERTv2 residual compression vs 3 GB single-vector). Production support: Vespa native tensor max-sim, Qdrant native multi-vector since v1.10 (Jul 2024), Elasticsearch nested dense_vector 8.13+, Weaviate preview. Pinecone and pgvector do not support it natively. Use for RAG over long docs and dense technical corpora.
summary: Chapter 11.26 overview of ColBERT-style multi-vector retrieval: per-token embeddings, max-sim late interaction, storage tradeoffs, and production support.
queries: why per-token embeddings beat pooled embeddings on long docs
how much memory does multi-vector retrieval need
when to use multi-vector retrieval over single vector
RAG over long documents retrieval quality

text: One vector per doc blurs token-level signal. A single dense embedding per document is the workhorse of vector search: compact, fast to compare, great on short text. For long docs (paragraphs, articles, chapters) the single-vector representation loses fidelity because every token is averaged into one 768-dim summary. Rare terms and specific phrasing get blurred into bulk meaning. Example: 'Cats are small domesticated carnivores that originated in the fertile crescent...' encoded into [0.81, 0.12, 0.45, ..., 0.22] (768 dims, one per doc) - every token's contribution is averaged into that one vector. Long doc + single vector = lossy compression. Multi-vector retrieval keeps one vector per token instead. This motivates ColBERT and the whole multi-vector family: keep more information, rank better, pay in storage.
summary: Single-vector embeddings average all tokens into one 768-dim vector, blurring rare terms in long docs - motivation for multi-vector retrieval.
queries: why does single vector embedding lose information on long documents
why average pooling hurts long doc retrieval
how do dense vectors blur rare terms
tradeoff between compactness and fidelity in embeddings
why one vector per document is not enough
what does mean pooling lose in BERT embeddings
single vector blur problem long passages
why rare terms disappear in pooled embeddings

text: ColBERT idea: one vector per token. ColBERT (Contextualized Late Interaction over BERT, Stanford 2020) keeps every token's contextual embedding instead of pooling to a single doc vector. A 200-token document becomes 200 vectors. Queries are also expanded to per-token embeddings (~5 query tokens for 'where do cats live'). Scoring happens at token granularity - the 'late interaction' - rather than after pooling. Single-vector retrieval: 1 vector per doc, cosine similarity, scalar output, fast and compact. Multi-vector ColBERT: ~200 vectors per doc (per token), max-sim aggregation across tokens, more accurate on long text. ColBERT is still a dense-embedding model; only the pooling step is dropped. The pipeline otherwise looks like any other BERT encoder.
summary: ColBERT keeps every BERT token's contextual embedding (~200 per doc, ~5 per query) instead of pooling, enabling token-granularity late interaction.
queries: how many vectors per document in ColBERT
what is late interaction in retrieval
how does ColBERT differ from a bi-encoder
BERT token embeddings retained for retrieval
what makes ColBERT a multi-vector model

text:
Max-sim: for each query token, find the best matching doc token, then sum. Given Q query vectors and D doc vectors, compute pairwise cosine (or dot) similarities - a Q x D matrix. For each query token, take the maximum similarity across all doc tokens. Sum those maxes over the query tokens. This is 'max-sim over late interaction'. It rewards a doc that matches each query token somewhere, even if no single doc token is a perfect match for everything. Formula:

score(q, d) = sum over i in query tokens of  max over j in doc tokens of  q_i · d_j

Sum of per-query-token max similarities against all doc tokens. Max-sim keeps the best signal per query token. A doc that matches 'cats' perfectly but does not mention 'genetics' at all scores lower than one that hits both, even if neither match is perfect.
summary: Max-sim score = sum over query tokens of the maximum similarity to any doc token; rewards docs that match each query token somewhere.
queries: what is the late interaction scoring formula
sum of max similarities over query tokens
why use max not average across doc tokens
Q x D similarity matrix scoring
how does multi-vector retrieval rank documents
max over j of q_i dot d_j
advantages of max aggregation over mean pooling for retrieval

text:
Max-sim walkthrough on the cat corpus. Query 'cats live outside' (3 tokens). Doc 'Cats are small carnivores that' (5 tokens). Compute the 3x5 similarity matrix, highlight the max in each row, sum them.

Grid (rows = query tokens, cols = doc tokens):
              Cats   Are   Small  Carnivores  That    Max
Cats          0.94   0.22  0.18   0.35        0.21    0.94
Live          0.11   0.17  0.15   0.25        0.28    0.28
Outside       0.19   0.14  0.21   0.17        0.26    0.26

Score = 0.94 + 0.28 + 0.26 = 1.48. Strong pick on 'cats' drags the overall score up. No doc token is perfect for 'live' or 'outside', but each query token is still scored against its nearest neighbor. This robustness is what single-vector cosine throws away.
summary: Worked example: query 'cats live outside' vs doc 'Cats are small carnivores that' yields max-sim score 0.94 + 0.28 + 0.26 = 1.48.
queries: how to calculate max-sim score by hand

max per row sum scoring example

text: Storage cost: N x tokens x d bytes (20 to 100x larger). The tradeoff is memory. Single-vector retrieval stores one 768-dim vector per doc. Multi-vector stores one per token. At ~200 tokens/doc that is 200x more vectors. Compression (PQ on the token vectors) and shorter vector widths reduce overhead to 20-50x in practice, but ColBERT-style indexes are always much bigger. Memory at N = 1M, d = 768, 200 tokens/doc: Single-vector float32 = 3 GB (1x baseline). ColBERT raw float32 = 600 GB (~200x larger). ColBERT compressed via PQ = ~60 GB (~20x larger). Multi-vector storage = N · tokens · d bytes, typical 20-100x single-vector footprint. PQ and ColBERTv2's residual compression take it to ~20x at good quality. Multi-vector is a great pick when retrieval quality matters more than cost; for 1B-scale workloads expect to pay for extra nodes.
summary: Multi-vector indexes cost N·tokens·d bytes - 200x raw, ~20x with PQ/ColBERTv2 residual compression - so 1M docs goes from 3 GB single-vector to 60-600 GB.
queries: how much memory does ColBERT use
N times tokens times d storage formula
is ColBERT worth the storage cost
storage tradeoff ColBERT vs single vector

text: Production support: Vespa, Qdrant native multi-vector, Elasticsearch. A few production systems handle multi-vector natively. Vespa: native tensor fields with max-sim as a built-in ranking expression, since v7 in Yahoo production. Qdrant multi-vector collections since v1.10 (July 2024), per-vector payload. Elasticsearch nested dense_vector fields with script-score max-sim, 8.13+. Weaviate multi-vector preview via RAG module experiments since 1.25. Multi-vector is table stakes in most production systems now; Vespa and Qdrant are common production picks. Pinecone and pgvector currently do not support it natively. Worth it for RAG on long documents, search over dense technical corpora, and any case where single-vector retrieval has been quality-limited. Swap is straightforward where supported.
summary: Production multi-vector support: Vespa tensor fields, Qdrant v1.10+, Elasticsearch 8.13 nested dense_vector, Weaviate preview - Pinecone and pgvector lack it.
queries: which vector databases support ColBERT natively

text: Embedding Lifecycle & Re-embedding covers what to do when the embedding model that produced your indexed vectors becomes outdated. Example: 500M docs indexed with OpenAI ada-002 (1536 dims) two years ago, now text-embedding-3-large (3072 dims) is the default. Vector DB collections are dimension-pinned so you cannot mix dims in one index. Three migration options: (1) re-embed everything and swap (clean cut-over, ~$32,500 one-time at 500M docs * 500 tokens * $0.00013/1K, ~1 week, requires retained source text); (2) parallel indexes with dual-write, backfill, shadow-query, cutover (low-risk, double storage during overlap, can flip back); (3) pin the old model forever (short-term cheap, fails on provider deprecation, silent drift, new domains, missed capability). Drift monitoring is the prerequisite for all three: keep a 1K-10K (query, relevant-doc) ground-truth set, run weekly recall@10, monitor embedding distribution per ingest, lock model version.
summary: Migration strategies and drift monitoring for production vector indexes when embedding models go stale.
queries: how do I handle outdated embedding models in production
what happens when OpenAI deprecates ada-002
vector database migration to new embedding model
how to switch embedding models without downtime

ground truth eval set for embeddings

text: You indexed 500M vectors two years ago. The model has moved on. Two years ago the team shipped a retrieval system with 500M documents indexed via OpenAI's text-embedding-ada-002 (d = 1536). Today that model is deprecated and text-embedding-3-large (d = 3072) is the new default. Every vector in the existing index is wrong in the sense that it was produced by an encoder that is no longer the state of the art. Timeline: 2024 - 500M docs embedded with ada-002 (d=1536). 2025 - OpenAI upgrades to text-embedding-3-large (d=3072). 2026 - ada-002 announced deprecated. Production system runs on two-year-old encoders. This is the most common silent pain in production vector search: models change, and what used to be a state-of-the-art embedding decays into yesterday's encoder.
summary: Sets up the lifecycle problem: an indexed corpus locked to a now-deprecated encoder.
queries: why does my embedding model become outdated
what is the embedding lifecycle problem

model went out of date production retrieval

what happens when embedding model is deprecated

when do I need to re-embed my corpus

text: Dimension mismatch is a hard migration. The new model outputs a different vector dimension. ada-002 emits 1536 dims; text-embedding-3-large emits 3072. Vector DBs pin the collection to one dimension at creation time - you cannot mix 1536-dim vectors with 3072-dim vectors in the same index. Old index: 1536 dims (ada-002). New index: 3072 dims (text-embedding-3-large). Vector DB collections are dimension-pinned. No mixing 1536-dim and 3072-dim in the same index. Any migration starts with creating a new collection. Matryoshka embeddings (chapter 11.16) partly ease this: truncating a 3072-dim Matryoshka to 1536 is still a sensible embedding, though not from the older model.
summary: Vector DB collections are dimension-pinned, so swapping models with different dims forces a new index.
queries: why can't I mix different embedding dimensions in one index
how to migrate when embedding dimension changes
dimension mismatch between old and new embeddings
do I need a new collection for new model
why is dimension change a hard migration
creating new collection for upgraded encoder

text: Option 1: re-embed everything, swap indexes. The conceptually clean option. Keep the source text around (if you still have it), run every doc through the new model, build a fresh index, atomically swap. You pay the full re-embed cost one time. Re-embed cost estimate at 500M docs, 500 tokens each: Tokens: 500M * 500 = 250 billion. Price: $0.00013 per 1K tokens (text-embedding-3-large). Total: ~$32,500 one-time bill. Time: ~1 week at API rate limits, parallelized. Requires retained source text (huge gotcha if you dropped it). Cost scales linearly with N and token budget. Clean cut-over, no two versions to maintain. Re-embedding is often the cheapest option measured over a year. The upfront bill is what blocks it - engineering teams are reluctant to spend five figures on something that looks like cleanup.
summary: Re-embed all docs through the new model, build fresh index, atomic swap; ~$32,500 for 500M docs * 500 tokens.
queries: how much does it cost to re-embed 500 million documents
how long does re-embedding 500M docs take
do I need to keep source text for re-embedding
is re-embedding the cheapest migration option
swap old index for new index strategy

text: Option 2: parallel indexes during migration, serve from old, populate new. Stand up the new index alongside the old. Dual-write every new or updated doc to both. Backfill the old corpus to the new index in the background. Compare quality on real traffic by shadow-querying. When the new index is caught up and its quality is verified, flip traffic. Then deprecate the old one. Parallel migration timeline: Week 0 - create new collection, start dual-write on new docs. Week 1-3 - backfill old corpus into new index in background. Week 4 - shadow-query both, measure recall delta. Week 5 - cutover traffic to new index. Week 6 - retire old index. Traffic stays on the old index the whole time. Double storage cost during the overlap window. Quality can be flipped back if the new index underperforms. This is the standard low-risk path. It costs more - you run two indexes for weeks - but regressions never reach production traffic.
summary: Run new index in parallel with old; dual-write, backfill, shadow-query, cutover - low risk but double storage during overlap.
queries: how to migrate vector index without downtime
backfill old corpus into new index
rollback embedding migration if quality drops
how long does parallel index migration take

text: Option 3: pin the old model forever (pin and pray). The do-nothing option. Pin the embedding model version, keep using it, never migrate. Works until the provider deprecates the model or drift quietly erodes quality. Many teams end up here because upgrading feels optional; a year later they discover retrieval quality has decayed and they now face a forced migration under deadline pressure. Failure modes of pinning: Provider deprecation - vendor sunsets the old model, forced migration under a deadline. Silent drift - model did not change but the domain did, recall decays over time. New language / domain - content types the old model never saw are served poorly. Missed capability - competitors ship features (longer context, better multilingual) that the pinned model cannot match. Pinning is short-term cheap, long-term expensive. Works fine for 6-12 months, pain compounds after. Drift is invisible unless you monitor it. Pinning is a valid choice as long as you plan the exit.
summary: Pinning the old model is cheap short-term but fails to provider deprecation, silent drift, and missed capabilities.
queries: should I pin my embedding model version
what happens if I never upgrade my embedding model
provider sunsets embedding model what to do
risks of not migrating embedding model
when does drift start hurting retrieval

text: Drift monitoring: periodic ground-truth evals. The cure for silent quality decay is explicit monitoring. Keep a held-out set of (query, relevant-doc) pairs. Every week or month, run the current index against the eval set and record recall@k. Any sudden drop signals either a model change, a content change, or an index regression - each demands a different response. Drift monitoring stack: Ground-truth eval set - 1K to 10K (query, relevant-doc) pairs, refreshed quarterly. Weekly recall@10 run - run the eval, record the curve, alert on regression. Content-change monitor - track the embedding distribution (mean, variance) per ingest batch. Model-change signal - lock embedding model version, surface drift when you bump it. Monitor recall, drift, eval - or discover regression after users notice. Ground-truth sampling is cheap; a quality regression is not. Whatever migration option you pick, drift monitoring is the common prerequisite. Without it, none of the other three can be operated safely.
summary: Held-out eval set + weekly recall@10 + distribution monitoring detects model, content, or index regressions before users do.
queries: how to monitor embedding drift in production
ground truth eval set for vector search
what is drift monitoring for embeddings
how to detect retrieval quality regression
size of held out eval set for vectors
should I refresh my eval set quarterly
how to catch silent quality decay
what to do when recall drops

text: Chapter 11.28 covers production observability for vector search systems: tracking percentile-based latency (P50/P95/P99/P99.9) instead of mean, periodic recall@k sampling against brute-force ground truth to catch ANN regressions, per-query cost telemetry (L3 cache hit rate, memory pages read, distance computations, CPU cycles), the ANN-Benchmarks QPS-vs-recall methodology for comparing index configs, dashboard design separating alert lines (P99 latency >100ms, recall@10 <0.95) from watch lines (cache hit rate ~0.85, memory usage), and the hidden-metric checklist (per-tenant latency, filtered-query recall, cold-start latency, fan-out tail, write-to-read lag, index build time, tombstone percent).
summary: Production observability stack for vector DBs: tail latencies, recall sampling, per-query cost signals, ANN-Benchmarks, dashboards, and hidden metrics.

text: Mean latency tells you almost nothing in production vector search. Queries are cheap most of the time; the slow tail ruins the user's day. Track percentile-based latency: P50 (median), P95 (worst that most users see), P99 (worst 1%). Alert budgets are written against the tail, not the average. Typical production targets for HNSW: P50 = 10 ms (median query), P95 = 30 ms (95% of queries faster), P99 = 80 ms (99% of queries faster), P99.9 = 200 ms (tail blows up here). Mean masks the tail. Alert on P99, watch P99.9, write the SLA against the tail. The P99 is usually 5-10x the P50. Investments in caching, SIMD, and index tuning show up in the tail, not the mean.
summary: Track P50/P95/P99/P99.9 latency percentiles for HNSW search; mean hides the tail and the tail is what hurts users.

text: ANN indexes are approximate; without active monitoring a recall regression goes unnoticed for months. The cure is periodic evaluation: sample K queries, compute the exact top-k via brute force on the full corpus, compare against what the ANN index returned. Recall@10 is the most common single number to track. Protocol: Step 1 - sample 1,000 queries from production traffic. Step 2 - for each, compute exact top-10 via brute force. Step 3 - compare against ANN top-10 (intersection / 10). Step 4 - average over the 1,000 queries = recall@10. Step 5 - repeat weekly, plot the trend. The sample is cheap compared to the damage of not knowing; even a slow brute-force run at 1K queries is minutes, not hours.
summary: Sample 1K production queries weekly, compute exact top-10 via brute force, compare to ANN top-10, average to track recall@10 trend.

text: Beyond top-level latency, modern vector DBs emit per-query cost signals you watch when latency regressions appear without any code change. Key signals: L3 cache hit rate (fraction of hot graph pages served from CPU cache), memory pages read (how many 4 KB pages touched per query), distance computations (how many candidate comparisons), CPU cycles per query (wall-clock profile of a typical query). A sudden dip in cache hit rate is the most common early warning - it usually means the working set grew or the graph layout changed after a rebuild.
summary: Per-query cost telemetry: cache hit rate, memory pages, distance comps, CPU cycles - cache-hit drop is earliest regression signal.

text: The open-source ANN-Benchmarks (github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks) is the standard way to compare index configurations. It plots queries per second (QPS) on the horizontal axis and recall on the vertical. Every point is a parameter combination; the Pareto frontier tells you what tradeoffs are even possible. X-axis: recall (0.90 to 1.00). Y-axis: QPS (log scale, queries per second). Each curve = one index family (HNSW, IVF-PQ, Vamana). Each point = one parameter combination. Winners sit on the Pareto frontier (upper-right). Methodology: same dataset, same hardware, same recall target. Fair comparison forces every system to trade QPS for recall. Run it on your data, not just the public datasets. ANN-Benchmarks shows up in almost every paper, blog, and vendor comparison.
summary: ANN-Benchmarks plots QPS vs recall across parameter combos; Pareto frontier shows tradeoffs across HNSW, IVF-PQ, Vamana.

text: A good production dashboard is the difference between catching a regression in 5 minutes vs a customer email 3 days later. Core panels split into ALERT lines (page on breach) and WATCH lines (track trend). ALERT panels: P99 latency threshold 100 ms (SLA violation if above for 5 minutes), Recall@10 threshold <0.95 (quality regression - investigate model or index). WATCH panels: cache hit rate ~0.85 (degrades before latency shows symptoms), index memory usage 60-70% RAM (growth predicts scale-up or shard). Keep the number of alerts small. Alerts on everything train the team to ignore them - two alerts you trust beats ten you mute.
summary: Dashboard separates ALERT panels (P99 latency, recall@10) from WATCH panels (cache hit rate, memory usage); few trusted alerts beat many ignored ones.

text: Latency and recall are headline metrics; the quiet ones that trip production: per-tenant latency (is one customer degrading the P99 for everyone?), filtered-query recall (does recall tank when a predicate is tight?), cold-start latency (first query after a new replica is up - how long?), fan-out tail (slowest shard on a multi-shard query?), write-to-read lag (time from upsert to queryable?), index build time (duration of the last full rebuild?), tombstone percent (delete pressure on the current graph?). Every production incident has a postmortem that ends in 'we were not capturing X'. Build the checklist before you need it. Rule of thumb: for every new feature (filtering, sharding, replication) add a monitor before you ship - the feature is cheap, the blind spot it creates is not.
summary: Hidden metrics that trip production: per-tenant latency, filtered recall, cold-start, fan-out tail, replication lag, build time, tombstone percent - add a monitor with every feature.

text: Chapter 11.29 Capacity Planning & Cost Models walks through how to size a production vector search system. Starts with six sizing inputs (N vectors, d dimensions, QPS target, P99 target, filter selectivity, availability). Derives RAM via the formula RAM = N x (vec_bytes + graph_bytes) x cache_factor (~1.2) x frag_factor (~1.3) where vec_bytes = d x 4 for float32 and graph_bytes ~100 for HNSW or ~20 for IVF-PQ. CPU sized as cores = per-query-cost x QPS x headroom; HNSW typical = 30 hops x 10 us = 300 us per query. Worked example: 500M vectors at d=768, 200 QPS, P99 100 ms needs ~1.5 TB raw + 50 GB graph, ~2.4 TB with overhead, 6 r7i.24xlarge nodes (768 GB each), 2x replication = 12 nodes / 3 TB / ~96 cores. Monthly cost order-of-magnitude: Pinecone ~$30K, self-host Qdrant ~$8K, pgvector ~$5K. Decision lens: $ per million vectors per month (storage) and $ per million queries (compute), adjusted for ops capacity and feature needs.
summary: Sizing inputs, RAM and CPU formulas, worked 500M-vector example, vendor cost comparison, and per-million decision ratios.
