text: Domain adaptation fine-tunes a pretrained embedding model on your own corpus to close the semantic gap that off-the-shelf models leave on specialized vocabularies. Method: contrastive learning with triplet loss L = max(0, d(A,P) - d(A,N) + margin) using anchor/positive/negative triples - hard negatives mined from top-20 retrievals teach the most. Use it when domain vocabulary is specialized, recall on a golden set is below 80%, and you have 5k+ labeled pairs. A 50k-pair fine-tune on BGE-large costs ~$200 and typically lifts Recall@5 from ~0.78 to ~0.91 with unchanged latency. Re-evaluate every 3-6 months for drift.
summary: Chapter overview of fine-tuning embeddings with triplet loss on domain triples to lift recall ~10-20 points when off-the-shelf models miss specialized vocabulary.
queries: how to fine-tune embeddings for RAG
when to fine-tune embedding model RAG

text: Off-the-shelf general-purpose embedding models are trained on web text. They know cat and dog are similar but they have barely seen MI (myocardial infarction) or tPA (tissue plasminogen activator). On a medical corpus this shows up as low similarity between an abbreviation and its full term, which wrecks retrieval. Cosine similarity on a medical corpus: 'MI' vs 'Myocardial infarction' = 0.42 off-the-shelf, 0.95 fine-tuned. 'tPA contraindicated' vs 'Tissue plasminogen activator should not be given' = 0.51 off-the-shelf, 0.90 fine-tuned. Recall@10 on a held-out medical query set: off-the-shelf embedder = 0.62, fine-tuned on medical corpus = 0.88. That is a 26-point recall lift - the value of teaching the embedder your domain.
summary: Off-the-shelf embeddings miss specialized vocabulary like MI and tPA, scoring abbreviations far from full terms and tanking recall on medical corpora.

text: Contrastive fine-tuning: pull positives close, push negatives away. We do not retrain the embedder from scratch. We take the existing model and nudge its vector space using triples: an anchor query, a positive document that should match it, and a negative document that should not. Each training step pulls the anchor closer to the positive and pushes it farther from the negative. Triplet loss formula: L_triplet = max(0, d(A, P) - d(A, N) + margin) where d = distance, A = anchor, P = positive, N = negative, margin = 0.2 typical. The loss is zero only when the negative is at least 'margin' farther from the anchor than the positive. If the negative is too close, the gradient pushes it away. The margin prevents the loss from going to zero just because positives are slightly closer than negatives.
summary: Contrastive fine-tuning uses triplet loss to pull positive documents close to a query anchor and push negatives away with a fixed margin.
queries: why use a margin in triplet loss
pull positives close push negatives away
what is the margin parameter in triplet loss
anchor query positive document negative document

text: Pair construction decides quality. The training data IS the model. Each row is one triple: an anchor (real user query), a positive (the chunk that should match), and a negative (a chunk that should not). Examples from the support corpus: Anchor 'How do I reset my password?' / Positive [doc-1, chunk 1] 'To reset your password, visit the account settings page and click Forgot Password' / Negative [doc-3] 'Refunds are processed within 5-7 business days of approval.' Anchor 'I can't sign in' / Positive [doc-25] 'Login Troubleshooting: clear cookies, verify caps lock, and check for typos in your email' / Negative [doc-7] 'Dashboard tour'. Anchor 'Cancel my subscription' / Positive [doc-15] 'Account Deletion Flow' / Negative [doc-2] 'Email Change'. Hard negatives teach the most: a hard negative is a doc that sits NEAR the positive in vector space but is irrelevant - same vocabulary, wrong topic. Mining hard negatives (top-20 retrieval results minus the true positive) teaches the actual semantic boundary. Easy negatives (random unrelated docs) waste compute because the gap is already obvious to the base model.
summary: Training triples of anchor/positive/negative drive fine-tuning quality, and hard negatives mined from top-20 retrieval results teach the semantic boundary far better than random easy negatives.
queries: how to build training data for embedding fine-tuning
what is a triple in contrastive learning
how to label data for embedding training
what makes a good negative example

text: When is fine-tuning worth the cost? Fine-tuning takes labeled data, compute time, and a recurring re-training budget as your corpus drifts. Fine-tune when: highly specialized vocabulary (medical, legal, code); off-the-shelf recall below 80% on your golden set; you have 5k+ query-positive pairs labeled; budget for periodic re-training as embeddings drift. Skip and use off-the-shelf when: general English domain; common domain (e-commerce, support, news); less than 5k labeled pairs available; recall already above 80% on the golden set. The cleanest signal: build a small golden set (200-500 query-positive pairs) and measure recall with the off-the-shelf model. Below 80%, fine-tuning is usually worth it. Above 80%, the marginal gain rarely pays for the recurring training cost.
summary: Fine-tune embeddings only for specialized vocabularies with 5k+ labeled pairs and sub-80% off-the-shelf recall; skip for general domains where off-the-shelf already clears the bar.

text: Before vs after on the support corpus. Fine-tuning on 50k support-ticket triples produces these results: Recall@5 0.78 -> 0.91, MRR 0.62 -> 0.84, Latency 30 ms -> 30 ms (unchanged). Recall and MRR jump; latency does not - fine-tuning changes the vector geometry, not the index or the query path. Training loss over 3 epochs on 50k triples: epoch 0 loss = 0.50, epoch 1 = 0.22, epoch 2 = 0.10, epoch 3 = 0.05. Cost: ~$200 for a 50k-pair fine-tune on BGE-large via a managed service. Quality delta: usually 10-20 points on recall. Re-evaluate every 3-6 months as your corpus drifts and new query patterns appear.

text: Chapter 12.16 covers hybrid retrieval tuned for RAG. Dense retrieval misses exact terms (acronyms, IDs); BM25 misses synonyms and paraphrases. Two fusion strategies dominate: Reciprocal Rank Fusion RRF(d) = sum 1/(k + rank), k=60 (parameter-free, robust default) and weighted score fusion Score = alpha * bm25 + (1-alpha) * dense (tunable per query type, needs score normalization). On the support corpus, recall@5 jumps from 0.68 (BM25) and 0.74 (dense) to 0.86 with hybrid RRF for only +30 ms latency. Per-query-type alpha: 0.7 for factual, 0.3 for conceptual, 0.5 default, chosen by a small LLM classifier (~$0.0002, ~150 ms). Production rule: ship RRF first; upgrade to weighted alpha only when metrics demand it; re-evaluate quarterly.
summary: Chapter-level summary of hybrid retrieval for RAG: fusion formulas, recall benefits, alpha tuning by query type, and production defaults.
queries: summary of hybrid retrieval for RAG
alpha tuning by query type summary

text: Dense misses exact terms; BM25 misses synonyms. Hybrid search (covered in Section 11.24) ships dense vectors and BM25 side by side and fuses their rankings. Two failure examples on the support corpus: Query "How do I get an API key?" - truth is doc-13 (API Keys). Dense top-3: [doc-7, doc-12, doc-5] - missed doc-13 because the embedding of "API key" sat closer to generic dev-tools docs than the actual API-Keys page. BM25 top-3: [doc-13, doc-7, doc-12] - found it via exact token match. Query "Subscription cancel" - truth is doc-15 (Account Deletion). Dense top-3: [doc-15, doc-19, doc-3] - found it via semantic similarity. BM25 top-3: [doc-3, doc-19, doc-25] - missed doc-15 because the cancel page is titled "Account Deletion" and does not contain the token "cancel". Hybrid keeps both retrievers and fuses their rankings; each catches what the other misses.
summary: Two concrete queries showing dense retrieval misses exact-token matches and BM25 misses synonym matches, motivating hybrid fusion.

text: Two fusion strategies dominate production RAG: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF, parameter-free) and weighted score fusion (tunable). RRF formula: RRF(d) = sum over retrievers r of: 1 / (k + rank_r(d)), where k = 60 (standard). Robust default. No weights to tune. Each retriever contributes a rank-reciprocal, so doc-1 always outweighs doc-2 regardless of underlying score scales. Weighted score formula: Score(d) = alpha * bm25_score(d) + (1 - alpha) * dense_score(d), where alpha is in [0, 1]. Tunable per query type. Requires score normalization first - BM25 scores are unbounded positive while dense cosine sits in [-1, 1], so min-max scaling each list to [0, 1] is the standard prep.
summary: Defines the two hybrid fusion formulas - RRF with k=60 and weighted score with tunable alpha - and notes when each is appropriate.
queries: how to combine BM25 and dense scores
BM25 score range vs cosine range
two ways to fuse rankings RAG

text: RRF worked example for query "I can't sign in" with k = 60. BM25 top-3: 1. doc-25, 2. doc-7, 3. doc-13. Dense top-3: 1. doc-12, 2. doc-25, 3. doc-7. Compute RRF(d) with k = 60: doc-25: BM25 term 1/(60+1) = 0.01639, Dense term 1/(60+2) = 0.01613, sum = 0.03252. doc-7: BM25 term 1/(60+2) = 0.01613, Dense term 1/(60+3) = 0.01587, sum = 0.03200. doc-12: BM25 term 0 (not in BM25 top-3), Dense term 1/(60+1) = 0.01639, sum = 0.01639. doc-13: BM25 term 1/(60+3) = 0.01587, Dense term 0 (not in dense top-3), sum = 0.01587. Final ranking: doc-25 (0.03252) > doc-7 (0.03200) > doc-12 (0.01639) > doc-13 (0.01587). doc-25 wins because both retrievers ranked it in their top-2. doc-7 is second because both kept it in the top-3. Singletons trail behind.
summary: Step-by-step numeric RRF computation showing how docs that appear in both retriever lists outrank singletons.
queries: why do overlap docs win in RRF
RRF reciprocal rank fusion numeric example

text: Complementary recall: hybrid catches what either misses. Recall@5 measured on a 50-query held-out set against the support corpus. BM25 only: 0.68. Dense only: 0.74. Hybrid (RRF): 0.86. Hybrid is not just an average of the two retrievers - the failures of BM25 and dense are largely uncorrelated, so fusion picks up the union of their wins. Hybrid is rarely worse than the best single method, often noticeably better. The complementary recall gain here is +12 points over dense alone. Latency cost: +30 ms for the BM25 lookup on top of the dense search. For most RAG systems that is well below the LLM generation tail, so the recall lift is almost always worth it.
summary: Empirical recall numbers showing hybrid RRF beats either retriever alone by +12 points with only +30ms latency overhead.
queries: BM25 vs dense vs hybrid recall
how much does hybrid beat dense

text: Tune alpha by query type. One alpha for everything leaves recall on the table. Factual lookups (account IDs, error codes) lean BM25. Conceptual questions lean dense. A small classifier picks the right alpha per query before retrieval. Factual / Lookup: example "What is my account ID?", alpha = 0.7, lean BM25 - exact tokens like account IDs, error codes, and SKUs dominate the answer. Conceptual / How-Does-X-Work: example "How does our pricing model work?", alpha = 0.3, lean Dense - synonyms and paraphrase dominate, the answer rarely uses the same words as the query. Mixed / Ambiguous (default): example "Why is my charge wrong?", alpha = 0.5, balanced - mix of keywords (charge, refund) and intent (something is wrong), hedge with 0.5. A simple LLM-based classifier (one call with a 3-class prompt) can label each query at runtime before retrieval. Cost: 1 extra LLM call (~$0.0002 with a small model). Adds ~150 ms but unlocks 4-8 points of recall on mixed query workloads.
summary: Per-query-type alpha tuning recipe: 0.7 for factual lookups, 0.3 for conceptual, 0.5 default - chosen by a small LLM classifier.
queries: tune alpha hybrid search by query type
when to lean BM25 vs dense

text: Hybrid defaults for RAG. Hybrid is the production default for RAG. Start simple with RRF and only add complexity if your evaluation metrics demand it. Four defaults: 1. Always start with RRF (no tuning needed). 2. Move to weighted-alpha only if you have query-type labels. 3. Add a classifier when your queries naturally split into factual vs conceptual. 4. Re-evaluate alpha quarterly as your corpus and query mix drift. The cheapest big win in RAG retrieval: if you only do one thing after picking an embedding model, ship hybrid with RRF first - it almost always beats either retriever alone with a tiny latency cost.
summary: Production decision rule for hybrid in RAG: start with RRF, upgrade to weighted alpha only when query-type labels and metrics justify it.

text: Chapter 12.17 The Reranker Cascade builds a 3-stage RAG funnel that narrows candidates while spending more compute per surviving item: cheap vector retrieval (HNSW or IVF-PQ) cuts 1M chunks to top-50 in ~30ms/$0.0001, a cross-encoder reranker reads query+candidate jointly to reduce 50 to top-10 in ~80ms/$0.0003, then the LLM reads top-3 to generate the answer in ~800ms/$0.0120. End-to-end p50 latency ~910ms and cost ~$0.0124 per query, with LLM generation dominating both budgets. The two tuning knobs are top-k retrieved and top-k reranked; the sweet spot is 50->10->3 (recall 0.89). 20->5 saves a touch but recall slips to 0.82; 100->20 buys +3 points recall for +80ms (diminishing returns). At 1M queries/day daily cost is $12,400, cacheable 50-90%.
summary: Three-stage RAG cascade (vector top-50, cross-encoder reranker top-10, LLM top-3) trading compute for quality at each stage, with latency, cost, and tuning analysis.

text: Each stage cuts the candidate set and pays for better quality. Rerankers (covered in Section 11.25) re-score a candidate list using a cross-encoder that reads the query and the doc together. The 3-stage cascade: Vector top-50 (cheap) -> Cross-encoder rerank top-10 (medium) -> LLM reads top-3 (expensive). Vector retriever is cheap and lossy. Reranker is expensive and precise. LLM is most expensive and uses the smallest set. Each stage filters the work for the next one.

text: Stage 1 Vector Top-50 -> Stage 2 Reranker Top-10 -> Stage 3 LLM Top-3. The candidate pool shrinks by 5x at each stage. Each shrink lets us spend more compute per remaining item. That is the whole game. Stage details: Vector Retrieval (Cheap And Lossy) - input 1M chunks, output top-50, ~30 ms / $0.0001, HNSW or IVF-PQ scan, approximate but blazing fast. Cross-Encoder Reranker (Medium Cost, Sharp Scores) - input 50 candidates, output top-10, ~80 ms / $0.0003, reads query + each candidate jointly, 50 forward passes through a small transformer. LLM Reads Top-3 + Generates (Expensive, Final Answer) - input 10 reranked, output answer, ~800 ms / $0.0120, top-3 chunks stuffed into the prompt; the LLM does the reasoning and writes the answer.

text: Chapter 12.18 motivates query rewriting in RAG. User-typed questions are rarely the optimal retrieval query: the right doc exists in the corpus but the retriever misses it because the user's wording and the doc's wording diverge. Three failure shapes ruin naive retrieval - (1) lexical mismatch (sign-in vs log-in, cancel vs delete), (2) ambiguity (one short query has multiple plausible intents), and (3) multi-intent (one sentence packs two distinct asks). Four query-transformation strategies map onto these failures: HyDE (12.19) fixes lexical mismatch by embedding a hypothetical answer, multi-query expansion (12.20) fixes ambiguity via N paraphrases plus RRF, query decomposition (12.21) splits multi-intent queries, and query routing (12.21) classifies queries to the right index.
summary: Chapter overview: why user queries fail at retrieval and the four query-transformation strategies that fix the three failure shapes.

text: User queries are rarely optimal for retrieval. The user types a sentence in their own words; the retriever embeds it and finds the nearest chunks. But the user's wording is rarely the wording that lives in the index, so the right doc is in the corpus and the retriever still misses it. Concrete example: User query = "I can't sign in". Best doc in corpus = "Login Troubleshooting". Cosine(user, best_doc) = 0.61. Top-3 misses it; the correct doc lands at rank 7. Three failure shapes ruin naive retrieval and four query-transformation strategies fix them.

text: Failure 1: Lexical mismatch. Users and docs share an idea but not vocabulary. The retriever sees two strings of tokens that look unrelated and the right chunk falls out of the top-k. Examples on the support corpus: (a) User "I can't sign in" vs doc "Login Troubleshooting" - mismatch: sign in vs log in. (b) User "How do I cancel?" vs doc "Account Deletion Flow" - mismatch: cancel vs delete. (c) User "My screen is frozen" vs doc "Browser Performance Issues" - mismatch: screen frozen vs browser performance. Dense embeddings help a bit (sign-in and log-in sit close in vector space) but only partially. HyDE (12.19) and multi-query (12.20) close the gap by rewriting the user query into wording that matches the doc.
summary: Lexical mismatch: user words and doc titles use different vocabulary (sign in vs log in, cancel vs delete, screen frozen vs browser performance) and dense embeddings only partially bridge it.

text: Failure 2: Ambiguity. One short question has multiple plausible intents. A naive retriever picks one direction and quietly drops the other two. Worked diagram: the query "How do I export?" fans out into three branches: (1) Export data to CSV -> doc-9 (Export Formats), (2) Export user list for admin -> doc-26 (Bulk Operations), (3) Cancel an export job that failed -> doc-29 (Export Failures). A naive retriever picks one branch and misses the other two. Multi-query (12.20) generates paraphrases that cover each branch, and routing (12.21) sends each branch to the index that fits best.
summary: Ambiguity: the query 'How do I export?' has three plausible interpretations mapping to three different docs; naive retrieval picks one and silently misses the others.

text: Failure 3: Multi-intent. The user packs two asks into one sentence. The retriever embeds the whole thing and lands on docs that cover one ask, missing the other entirely. Worked example: User query = "Cancel my subscription and get a refund". Sub-intent 1: How to cancel subscription -> doc-15 (Account Deletion Flow). Sub-intent 2: How to get a refund -> doc-4 (Refunds). Naive retrieval finds docs for one intent and misses the other. Decomposition (12.21) splits the query into two sub-queries, retrieves for each, then combines the results.
summary: Multi-intent: a single sentence like 'Cancel my subscription and get a refund' carries two distinct asks; naive retrieval covers one and misses the other, motivating query decomposition.

text: Four strategies map to three failures. Each transformation tackles a different failure shape. (1) HyDE (12.19) fixes lexical mismatch: ask an LLM to draft a hypothetical answer, then embed the answer instead of the question. (2) Multi-Query Expansion (12.20) fixes ambiguity: generate N paraphrases of the query, retrieve for each, then merge results. (3) Query Decomposition (12.21) fixes multi-intent: split a compound query into independent sub-queries, retrieve for each, then combine. (4) Query Routing (12.21) picks the right index: classify the query and send it to the index, tool, or filter that best matches. Picking the right transformation is itself a routing decision - in production, a small classifier or LLM call inspects the query and chooses which strategy (or combination) to run.
summary: Preview grid: HyDE fixes lexical mismatch, multi-query expansion fixes ambiguity, decomposition fixes multi-intent, routing picks the right index; choice itself is made by a classifier or LLM call.

text: Five-box horizontal flow diagram showing the HyDE pipeline: User Query -> LLM Generates Hypothetical Answer -> Embed Hypothetical Answer -> Vector Retrieval -> Top-K Real Docs. Each stage flows into the next via an arrow. The hypothetical answer is throwaway; only its embedding goes to the index. The user receives the real top-K docs, never seeing the LLM's draft answer.

text: HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) is a query-transformation technique that improves dense retrieval by asking an LLM to draft a plausible hypothetical answer to the user's question, then embedding that answer instead of the question, then retrieving against the index. The hypothetical answer shares vocabulary and shape with real docs, so cosine similarity scores higher. The 5-stage flow: user query, LLM generates hypothetical answer, embed the answer, vector retrieval, return top-K real docs. Helps with long descriptive queries, lexical mismatch, and conceptual 'why' questions. Hurts on short factual queries and adds 200-400ms latency plus one LLM call (~$0.0005). Includes a prompt template with a {query} placeholder.
summary: Chapter overview of HyDE: embed an LLM-drafted hypothetical answer instead of the user question to improve dense retrieval recall.
queries: Why embed the answer instead of the question?
How much does HyDE cost per query?
How to fix lexical mismatch in RAG retrieval?
Why do dense embeddings miss questions?

text: Don't embed the question - embed the hypothetical answer. A user's question and the doc that answers it often live far apart in vector space because they use different words and have different shapes. HyDE flips the order: have an LLM draft a plausible answer first, then embed THAT, then retrieve. Normal RAG: Embed("Why is my dashboard slow?") -> retrieve. HyDE: LLM("Why is my dashboard slow?") = "The dashboard may be slow due to large data volumes, browser extensions, or network latency." -> Embed that ANSWER -> retrieve. The hypothetical answer is text-shape-closer to actual docs than the question is, so cosine similarity wins.
summary: Core HyDE insight: questions and answers sit far apart in vector space, so embed an LLM-drafted answer instead of the raw question.
queries: Question vs answer shape in vector space
Why are questions and docs far apart in vector space?

text: The HyDE flow: Query -> LLM -> Hypothetical Answer -> Embed -> Retrieve. HyDE adds one extra step before retrieval: an LLM call that writes a hypothetical answer. That answer is what gets embedded and sent to the vector index. The user never sees it directly. The five stages are User Query, LLM Generates Hypothetical Answer, Embed Hypothetical Answer, Vector Retrieval, Top-K Real Docs. The hypothetical answer is throwaway - only its embedding goes to the index. The user gets the real top-K docs back, never the LLM's draft.
summary: HyDE pipeline walkthrough: an extra LLM step drafts a throwaway answer whose embedding (not the raw query) feeds the vector retriever.
queries: What are the steps in HyDE?
How does HyDE work end to end
Does the user see the hypothetical answer?
Where does the LLM call happen in HyDE?

text: Worked example: "Why is my dashboard slow?". Three panels show what the user typed, what the LLM drafted, and what the retriever returned. LLM hypothetical answer: "The dashboard may be slow due to large data volumes, browser extensions interfering with rendering, network latency, or background sync jobs consuming resources. Try refreshing, disabling extensions, or checking the network panel." Top-3 retrieved docs: doc-22 Slow Page Load Troubleshooting, doc-23 500 Errors, doc-27 Browser Compatibility Issues. The LLM's draft pulled in vocabulary like "page load", "browser extensions", and "network latency" - exactly the words in the docs that actually help. Without HyDE, retrieval returned doc-7 (Dashboard Tour), a generic doc that doesn't help. The question "why slow" embedded poorly; the answer embedded well.
summary: Concrete dashboard-slow query example showing the LLM's hypothetical answer pulling in doc vocabulary to retrieve doc-22, doc-23, doc-27.
queries: How HyDE retrieves the right docs
Why does HyDE find better docs?

text: Why HyDE works - three reasons the hypothetical-answer trick beats embedding the raw question. (1) Different shapes in vector space: questions look different from answers in vector space. (2) Borrows doc vocabulary: hypothetical answers borrow doc vocabulary even if the LLM doesn't know the actual answer. (3) Robust to lexical mismatch: robust to lexical mismatch because the LLM does the rewriting for you. Even if the LLM's draft is factually wrong, the SHAPE of the answer matches the SHAPE of real docs - and that is what cosine similarity scores.
summary: Three reasons HyDE outperforms raw-query embedding: matching shapes, borrowed doc vocabulary, and robustness to lexical mismatch.
queries: Does HyDE need a correct answer?
Why factually wrong LLM draft still helps
Why questions and answers differ in embeddings

text: When HyDE helps vs when it hurts. HyDE is not free and is not universal. Helps: long descriptive queries; lexical mismatch (sign-in vs log-in); conceptual / 'why does X happen' questions; queries that don't share vocabulary with docs. Hurts: short factual queries with good embeddings (e.g., 'API key'); queries where the LLM might hallucinate the wrong answer shape; latency-sensitive pipelines (HyDE adds an LLM call: +200-400 ms). If recall on your golden set is already above 0.85, HyDE likely won't help and will add latency.
summary: Decision guide for HyDE: helps on long, conceptual, lexical-mismatch queries; hurts on short factual lookups and high-recall systems.
queries: Should I use HyDE for short queries

text: The HyDE prompt template. The exact prompt fired at the LLM: "You are a helpful assistant. Write a single concise paragraph that answers the user's question as if you knew the answer. The paragraph will be used to search a documentation index, so include domain terms and likely doc vocabulary. Do not say 'I don't know' - guess plausibly. Question: {query} Hypothetical Answer:" The {query} placeholder gets filled with the user's question. The LLM's response is the hypothetical answer that gets embedded. Cost: +1 LLM call per user query (~$0.0005 + ~250ms). Cache the hypothetical answer by query hash to amortize. Caching is covered in chapter 12.36.
summary: Concrete HyDE prompt template with {query} placeholder, plus cost and latency overhead (~$0.0005 / ~250ms) and caching guidance.

text: Three-box horizontal pipeline diagram for multi-query expansion. Box 1 (orange): LLM Generates 3-5 Query Variants. Box 2 (yellow): Run Vector Retrieval For Each Variant. Box 3 (green): Fuse Rankings With RRF. Arrows connect each box left to right, showing the sequential pipeline flow.
summary: Three-stage pipeline diagram: generate variants, retrieve each, fuse with RRF.

text: Multi-Query Expansion (also called RAG-Fusion) turns one user query into 3-5 LLM-generated variant phrasings, runs vector retrieval for each variant in parallel, and fuses the rankings with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) to produce one final top-K. RRF formula: RRF(d) = sum over variants v of 1 / (k + rank_v(d)), with k = 60 standard. Helps on ambiguous, multi-intent, or multi-clause queries. Skip on short factual lookups. Adds ~300 ms (one LLM call) plus N parallel retrievals. Step-back prompting is a niche variant that adds a more general rewording. Pairs well with HyDE.
summary: Chapter overview: expand one query into N LLM-rewritten variants, retrieve per variant, fuse with RRF.

text: One query becomes many; fuse the results. If the user's exact wording missed the right doc, one of the variant wordings might catch it. RAG-Fusion is the same idea under a different name; this chapter absorbs it.

text: Three steps: generate variants -> retrieve each -> fuse. Stage 1: LLM generates 3-5 query variants. Stage 2: run vector retrieval for each variant. Stage 3: fuse rankings with RRF. Stage 2 runs in parallel - N retrievals fire at once, not in sequence. Latency is dominated by stage 1 (the LLM call), not stage 2. A three-box horizontal pipeline diagram shows the LLM generating 3-5 query variants, vector retrieval running for each variant, and rankings fused with RRF into one final list.

Variant 3: [doc-5, doc-15, doc-21]. Final fused top-3 after RRF: Rank 1: doc-15 (covers cancellation). Rank 2: doc-4 (covers refund). Rank 3: doc-3 (subscription tiers). text: Fuse with RRF (same formula as chapter 12.16). Reciprocal Rank Fusion takes N rankings and produces one. Each doc's score is the sum of 1 / (k + its rank) across every ranking it appears in. The constant k = 60 dampens the influence of any single list so agreement across variants matters more than a top-1 appearance. Formula: RRF(d) = sum over variants v of: 1 / (k + rank_v(d)) where k = 60 (standard). Same fusion math as hybrid search (chapter 12.16); different inputs - here the rankings come from N variants of the same query rather than from BM25 + dense.
summary: RRF formula: sum of 1/(k+rank) across variants, k=60, rewards agreement across rankings.

text: Step-back prompting: add a more general variant. Step-back is a specific variant style: ask a broader, less specific version of the question. The intuition: a doc might not match the user's exact narrow wording, but it almost certainly matches a more general phrasing of the same problem. Example - Original (very specific): 'Why is the user role page failing on Chrome v124 with error E_PERM?' Step-back (more general): 'Why does the user role page fail?' The original is too specific - the doc might not mention Chrome v124. The step-back catches more general docs. Reasoning models reduce the need for explicit step-back; include it when your queries are unusually specific. This is one of many possible variant prompts; the chapter's main approach covers the common case; step-back is a niche addition.
summary: Step-back prompting generates a more general variant of a too-specific query to catch broader docs.

text: When multi-query helps. Multi-query expansion is not a default - it earns its keep on hard, ambiguous, or multi-intent queries; on short factual lookups it just burns budget. Helps: ambiguous queries with multiple plausible intents; complex multi-clause queries; recall is the priority over latency; reranker can absorb the wider candidate set. Skip: short factual lookups ('What is my account ID?'); latency-critical paths (adds 1 LLM call + N parallel retrievals); when HyDE already solves the same failure. Adds ~300 ms (1 LLM call) + N parallel retrievals (cheap). Often pairs well with HyDE for hard cases.
summary: Helps vs skip table: use multi-query on ambiguous/multi-intent queries, skip on short factual lookups.

text: Chapter 12.21 covers two related query-transformation strategies in RAG: routing (which index or tool should this query hit) and decomposition (does this query need to be split into sub-queries). A router classifies each query and sends it to the matching index - or skips retrieval entirely for chitchat. Decomposition splits complex queries like comparisons, multi-hop questions, and disjunctions into 2-3 sub-queries, retrieves for each, then assembles. Routers can be implemented as fast embedding-based semantic routers (~10 ms, 85-90% accuracy) or as slower LLM classifiers (~150 ms, 92-97% accuracy). The four production combinations are route only, decompose only, both, or neither - pick by query complexity. Combined overhead: ~300-450 ms per query.
summary: Query routing picks the right index/tool while decomposition splits complex queries into sub-queries; choose route, decompose, both, or neither based on query complexity.

text: Routing decides where; decomposition decides how many. Two related query-transformation strategies share this chapter. Routing answers a where question - which index or tool should the query hit. Decomposition answers a how-many question - does the query need to be split into smaller sub-queries first. Routing: pick one target. Skip retrieval entirely for off-topic chitchat. Decomposition: split complex queries into 2-3 sub-queries; retrieve and assemble. Both rely on an LLM (or a cheaper classifier) to inspect the query and decide. They compose - a router can send a query to a decomposition step, or a decomposed sub-query can itself be routed to a different index.
summary: Routing chooses which index a query hits while decomposition decides whether to split it into sub-queries; the two strategies compose.
queries: what is the where vs how-many framing
what does an LLM router do
when do you split a query in RAG
can you route a decomposed sub-query

text: Routing sends each query to the right target. On the running support corpus, queries split cleanly into four buckets. A router classifies each query into one of them, then sends it to the matching index - or skips retrieval entirely for chitchat. The four leaves: Account & Billing -> Account-Billing Index, Top-K=5; Product Features -> Product-Features Index, Top-K=5; Troubleshooting -> Troubleshooting Index, Top-K=8; Chitchat / Off-Topic -> Skip Retrieval, Reply Directly. Routing avoids retrieving from indexes that can't help. Skipping retrieval on chitchat saves latency and cost - and prevents the LLM from hallucinating about a doc it doesn't need to read.
summary: A router classifies each support query into one of four buckets (billing, features, troubleshooting, chitchat) and sends it to the matching index or skips retrieval.
queries: what indexes does a router target
route a query to billing index

text: Two router implementations: embedding-based vs LLM classifier. The routing decision can be made by a tiny embedding lookup or by a small LLM call. Semantic Router (embedding-based): embed the query, compare to embeddings of route prototypes ('Billing question', 'Troubleshooting question'). Pick highest cosine. Cost: ~$0.0001/query. Latency: ~10 ms. Accuracy: 85-90%. LLM Classifier: pass the query + route labels to a small LLM ('Classify this query: billing / feature / troubleshoot / chitchat'). Pick the label. Cost: ~$0.0002/query. Latency: ~150 ms. Accuracy: 92-97%. Semantic router scales cheaper; LLM classifier handles edge cases better. Start with semantic; switch to LLM if routing accuracy stalls below your bar.
summary: Routers can be implemented as fast embedding-based semantic routers (~10 ms, 85-90% accuracy) or slower LLM classifiers (~150 ms, 92-97% accuracy).

text: Decomposition splits 'Compare X and Y' into two sub-queries. User query: 'Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.' One query, two subjects. A single vector lookup gets pulled toward whichever subject has stronger phrasing and misses the other. LLM splits into sub-queries: Sub-Query 1: 'What's in the Pro plan?' retrieves doc-3 (Subscription Tiers), doc-10 (Team Seat Management). Sub-Query 2: 'What's in the Enterprise plan?' retrieves doc-3 (Subscription Tiers), doc-10 (Team Seat Management), doc-21 (Quota Exceeded - Enterprise-Only Feature). LLM Assembly Step: union of retrieved docs (doc-3, doc-10, doc-21) is passed to a single LLM call that writes the side-by-side comparison. Without decomposition, retrieval finds one tier's docs and misses the other.
summary: Worked example: 'Compare Pro and Enterprise plans' splits into two sub-queries; each retrieves its own docs, and the union (doc-3, doc-10, doc-21) feeds one LLM assembly call.

text: When to decompose: multi-hop, comparison, disjunction. Three query shapes consistently benefit from being split before retrieval. Comparison: 'Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.' Split: Sub-1: What's in the Pro plan? + Sub-2: What's in the Enterprise plan? Multi-Hop: 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' Split: Sub-1: How to recover email? + Sub-2: How to reset password? Disjunction (Mixed Types): 'Why is the dashboard slow and showing 500 errors?' Split: Sub-1: Dashboard slow troubleshooting + Sub-2: 500 error troubleshooting. If the query has 'and' / 'compare' / 'or' between two distinct subjects, decomposition usually helps. Single-subject queries don't benefit and just pay the extra LLM call for nothing.
summary: Decomposition helps for comparison, multi-hop, and disjunction query shapes; single-subject queries don't benefit and just pay the extra LLM cost.
queries: three shapes that benefit from decomposition
how to spot a decomposable query

text: The decision: route, decompose, both, or neither. Four combinations cover every production case. Route Only: most production RAG systems start here. Cheap, easy, high-impact. Decompose Only: use when queries are consistently complex (multi-hop, comparisons). Adds 1 LLM call + N parallel retrievals. Both: route first to pick the right index, then decompose if the query is complex. They compose - they don't interfere. Neither: default for simple lookup queries. Don't add complexity you don't need. Cost summary: Routing +10-150 ms / +$0.0001-0.0002 per query. Decomposition +200-300 ms / +$0.0005 per query. Combined: ~+300-450 ms total query-transformation overhead.
summary: Four combinations - route only, decompose only, both, or neither - cover every production case; combined overhead is roughly 300-450 ms and ~$0.0007 per query.

text: Chapter 12.22 Context Packing covers how to fit retrieval into an LLM context window. Every prompt has a token budget split across system prompt, retrieved context, user question, reserved completion, and headroom (8000-token example: 200 + 5000 + 50 + 1500 + 1250). Three ordering strategies: relevance-first (sort by score), chronological (sort by date/position), and MMR (deduplicate with MMR = lambda x sim(chunk, query) - (1 - lambda) x max(sim(chunk, already_picked))). The final packed prompt combines system instructions, [doc-N, chunk-M] citation markers, the user question, and an authorized refusal phrase, all within the budget (~60% of 8k).
summary: Chapter 12.22 teaches token budget allocation, three chunk-ordering strategies (relevance-first, chronological, MMR), and how to assemble a complete packed prompt within budget.

text: Every prompt has a token budget. An 8000-token context window splits across four jobs: the system prompt that frames the model (200 tokens, 2.5%), the retrieved context with the answer (5000 tokens, 62.5%), the user question (50 tokens, 0.6%), and the reserved completion tokens the model will use to write its answer (1500 tokens, 18.8%). Headroom (1250 tokens, 15.6%) is the slack you keep so a long answer never overflows. Slot / Tokens / Share Of Budget: System Prompt 200 (2.5%); Retrieved Context 5,000 (62.5%); User Question 50 (0.6%); Reserved Completion 1,500 (18.8%); Headroom 1,250 (15.6%). Total = 8,000.

text: Pack top-3 chunks for the password reset query. Running query: 'How do I reset my password?' Returned chunks from doc-1 with cosine scores. [doc-1, chunk 1] - Score 0.91: 'To reset your password, go to Settings then Security.' [doc-1, chunk 2] - Score 0.84: 'An email with a reset link will be sent to your registered address.' [doc-1, chunk 3] - Score 0.77: 'Click the link within 24 hours to set a new password.' Scores tell us how to order; chunks tell us what to pack.
summary: Top-3 retrieved chunks for the password reset query with cosine scores 0.91, 0.84, 0.77 illustrate what gets packed into the prompt.

text: Ordering Option 1: Relevance-First. Sort by retrieval score, highest first. The most-relevant chunk lands at position 1 where the model attends best. Default for single-best-answer queries. Packed order: 1. [doc-1, chunk 1] (Score 0.91); 2. [doc-1, chunk 2] (Score 0.84); 3. [doc-1, chunk 3] (Score 0.77). Most relevant first. Cheap to do (just sort by score). Risk: tail-position chunks lose attention weight.
summary: Relevance-first ordering sorts chunks by descending retrieval score so the highest-scored chunk sits in position 1 where model attention is strongest.
queries: How should I order chunks in a RAG prompt?
Does chunk position affect LLM attention?
Trade-offs of sorting context by score
How does the LLM attend to position 1?
Why do tail chunks lose attention weight?
Default chunk ordering strategy for RAG
Sort chunks by retrieval score descending

text: Ordering Option 2: Chronological. Sort by publication date or document position. Useful for time-sensitive docs - changelogs, policy histories, incident timelines - where the natural reading order helps the model follow cause-effect. Packed order: 1. [doc-1, chunk 1] - Document Position 1; 2. [doc-1, chunk 2] - Document Position 2; 3. [doc-1, chunk 3] - Document Position 3. Time-sensitive ordering preserves the natural sequence so the model reads cause then effect.
summary: Chronological ordering sorts chunks by date or document position so the model reads cause then effect, useful for changelogs and incident timelines.
queries: When should chunks be sorted by date?
How to pack changelogs into a prompt?
How do I preserve document position when packing?
Sort chunks by date in RAG prompt

text: Ordering Option 3: Deduplicate with MMR (Maximal Marginal Relevance). Keeps high-score chunks while dropping near-duplicates. Same budget, more unique information. Before MMR (5 chunks, redundant): chunk 1 'reset password go to Settings then Security'; chunk 2 'email with reset link will be sent'; chunk 3 'click link within 24 hours'; chunk 4 'reset email is sent with a clickable link' (duplicate of 2, wastes budget); chunk 5 'set a strong new password to finish'. After MMR (4 chunks, diverse): chunks 1, 2, 3 plus doc-9 chunk 1 'MFA must be re-verified after a reset (different but related)'. The MMR formula: MMR = lambda x sim(chunk, query) - (1 - lambda) x max(sim(chunk, already_picked)). Lambda = 0.7 weights relevance over diversity. Lambda = 0.3 weights diversity over relevance. Tune per workload.
summary: MMR deduplicates retrieved chunks using MMR = lambda x sim(chunk, query) - (1 - lambda) x max(sim(chunk, already_picked)), trading relevance against diversity.
queries: How does MMR work in retrieval?
What does lambda do in MMR?
How to avoid redundant chunks in a prompt?
When to use lambda = 0.7 vs 0.3 in MMR?
How does MMR pick the next chunk?

text:
The final packed prompt - all pieces in one prompt: system instruction, retrieved chunks with citation markers, user question, refusal phrase the model is allowed to use. Prompt template:

You are a helpful support assistant. Use the documentation below to answer.
If the docs don't contain the answer, say "I don't have enough information".

Documentation:
[doc-1, chunk 1] To reset your password, go to Settings then Security.
[doc-1, chunk 2] An email with a reset link will be sent to your address.
[doc-1, chunk 3] Click the link within 24 hours to set a new password.

Question: How do I reset my password?
Answer:

Within budget: ~4,800 / 8,000 tokens (60%).
summary: Final packed prompt combines system instruction, retrieved chunks with [doc-N, chunk-M] citation markers, user question, and refusal phrase, fitting within ~4800 of 8000 tokens.

text: Chapter 12.23 explains the lost-in-the-middle problem from Liu et al. 2023: LLMs attend best to the start and end of a long context window and dip in accuracy when the answer-bearing chunk sits in the middle. The chapter shows a U-shaped accuracy curve over 20 positions, demonstrates how the same retrieved chunk produces different answers depending on its position, and presents two reordering strategies (front-load and sandwich) that move top-scored chunks into the high-attention regions. It closes with failure modes where reordering alone is not enough: truly long multi-fact queries, tail-buried critical details, and model-specific curve shapes.
summary: Overview of the lost-in-the-middle effect and the front-load and sandwich reordering strategies that mitigate it.

text: Liu et al. (2023) showed that LLMs attend best to the start and end of a long context window. The middle gets ignored. Plot 20 packed chunks: answer accuracy is highest when the relevant chunk is at position 1 or position 20, and lowest in the middle. The curve is U-shaped. Approximate accuracy values across 20 positions: pos 1 = 85%, pos 2 = 80%, pos 3 = 75%, pos 4 = 70%, pos 5 = 65%, pos 6 = 60%, pos 7 = 56%, pos 8 = 55%, pos 9 = 55%, pos 10 = 56%, pos 11 = 56%, pos 12 = 58%, pos 13 = 60%, pos 14 = 64%, pos 15 = 68%, pos 16 = 72%, pos 17 = 75%, pos 18 = 78%, pos 19 = 80%, pos 20 = 82%. The middle dip sits around 55%.
summary: U-shaped accuracy curve showing LLM answer correctness peaks at start and end positions and dips to about 55% in the middle.

text: Query: 'Does the Pro plan include SSO?' The answer-bearing chunk is 'SSO is Enterprise-only' (doc-15, chunk 3). With 10 packed chunks, the position of that chunk decides whether the model finds the fact or not. Row A: 10 packed chunks; the SSO chunk lands at position 5 (middle). Model output: 'I don't see SSO mentioned for the Pro plan.' Wrong - the model ignored the middle chunk. Row B: Same 10 chunks reordered; the SSO chunk lands at position 1 (front). Model output: 'No, SSO is Enterprise-only.' Correct - same chunk, better position.
summary: Concrete example where the same SSO chunk yields a wrong answer at position 5 but a correct answer at position 1.

text: Strategy 1: Front-load the most relevant chunk. Relevance-first ordering: sort by retrieval score descending. The top-scored chunks land in positions 1, 2, 3 - the high-attention region. Tail positions hold the lowest-scored chunks. Scores by position: pos 1 = 0.91 (Top-1), pos 2 = 0.88 (Top-2), pos 3 = 0.84 (Top-3), pos 4 = 0.78, pos 5 = 0.71, pos 6 = 0.66, pos 7 = 0.59, pos 8 = 0.52, pos 9 = 0.45, pos 10 = 0.38. Cheap. Helps single-best-answer queries. Risk: tail context still ignored, but the top-scored chunks land in the attention sweet spot.
summary: Front-load strategy sorts chunks by descending retrieval score so top-3 land in positions 1 to 3 of the prompt.
queries: how to place best chunk at position 1
is front-load enough for multi-fact queries
how front-load helps lost in the middle

text: Strategy 2: Sandwich the best chunks at start and end. Put the top-2 chunks at the front and the next-best two at the back. The middle dip on the U-curve becomes the least-relevant region. Same total tokens; reordering is negligible compute. Slot layout: pos 1 = 0.91 (Top-1), pos 2 = 0.88 (Top-2), pos 3 = 0.66, pos 4 = 0.59, pos 5 = 0.52, pos 6 = 0.45, pos 7 = 0.38, pos 8 = 0.31, pos 9 = 0.84 (Top-3), pos 10 = 0.78 (Top-4). Best chunks bookend start and end. More robust for multi-fact queries. Same total tokens. Costs reordering compute (negligible).
summary: Sandwich strategy places top-2 chunks at the front and next-best 2 chunks at the back so low-relevance chunks fall in the middle dip.
queries: bookend chunks at start and end
top-2 chunks at front next 2 at back
is sandwich more robust than front-load
how to exploit U-shaped attention curve
what fills the middle in sandwich strategy

text: When reordering isn't enough. Three failure modes need different fixes than position tricks. (1) Truly long multi-fact queries: 30 chunks all needed; even sandwich leaves middle facts ignored. Fix: hierarchical summarization or multi-hop retrieval (chapter 12.25). (2) Tail-buried critical detail: the killer fact lives at chunk 18; both strategies miss it. Fix: rerank harder, fetch fewer. (3) Model-specific curve shape: some models have flatter curves than others; benchmark on your own model. Lost-in-middle is a model fingerprint, not a universal law. Always benchmark on your own model and workload.
summary: Three failure modes where front-load and sandwich are insufficient, and the alternative fixes for each.
queries: model-specific lost in the middle curve
is lost in the middle universal
benchmark attention curve on your own model
when reordering chunks is not enough
lost in middle as model fingerprint
what to do when position tricks fail

text: Chapter 12.24 covers Citations, Refusal & Groundedness for RAG. Without citations a user cannot verify answers and reviewers cannot audit them, so hallucinations slip through. Inline [doc-N] markers make every claim traceable. The chapter shows: a citation prompt template (literal [doc-N] markers tied to the Documentation block), a structured JSON output schema pairing answer with citations and confidence, an explicit refusal instruction with the exact sentence 'I don't have enough information to answer that.', a 3-step faithfulness audit (parse claims, locate cited chunk, score via LLM judge), a regex parser (\[doc-\d+\]) that resolves markers to a citation table with doc, chunk, quoted span, and a production template combining numbered RULES for citations + refusal because structured constraints survive long context better than prose.
summary: Chapter 12.24 teaches inline [doc-N] citations, refusal instructions, faithfulness auditing, and a production prompt template that combines both for grounded RAG answers.
queries: how to make RAG cite sources
how to prevent hallucination in RAG
production RAG prompt with citations and refusal
I don't have enough information sentence

text: Why citations are a production requirement. Without citations the user cannot verify the answer and a reviewer cannot audit it; hallucinations slip through silently. With inline [doc-N] markers every claim is traceable. Same answer with citations becomes an artifact you can review, replay, and trust. Comparison on query 'Why is my account locked?'. No-citations answer: 'Your account may be locked due to too many failed logins or a billing issue.' - user cannot verify, reviewer cannot audit, hallucination has nowhere to land. With-citations answer: 'Your account locks after 5 failed logins [doc-12]. Billing issues trigger a 24-hour hold [doc-7].' - every claim traces to a specific chunk, audit-friendly, hallucinations exposed.
summary: Citations turn an opaque LLM answer into an auditable artifact where every claim ties back to a retrieved chunk.
queries: why are citations important in RAG

text:
Tell the model to cite inline. The citation instruction lives in the system prompt. Use a literal [doc-N] marker the model can emit inline. Specify that N must come from the Documentation block, not be invented. Prompt template:

You are a helpful support assistant. Use the documentation below
to answer the user's question.

For every claim you make, cite the source as [doc-N], where N is
the doc number from the Documentation section. Do NOT cite a doc
you did not use.

Documentation:
{context}

Question: {query}
Answer:
summary: A citation-instruction prompt template tells the model to emit literal [doc-N] markers tied to doc numbers from the Documentation block.
queries: how to instruct LLM to cite

summary: Structured JSON output pairs the answer with doc_id, chunk_id, quote, and a confidence score so audit pipelines can parse citations without regex.

text:
Tell the model to refuse when context is missing. Without an explicit refusal instruction, models tend to invent. With one, the model has an authorized exit for 'I don't know'. Exact wording matters - models follow the most precise instructions. Refusal template:

If the documentation does not contain enough information to answer,
respond with EXACTLY this sentence and nothing else:
"I don't have enough information to answer that."

Do not invent facts. Do not guess. Do not cite a doc you didn't use.

Example on query 'What's the refund window for monthly plans?' with no matching doc. Without refusal instruction: 'Monthly plans have a 7-day refund window.' - hallucinated, model guessed because it had no exit. With refusal instruction: 'I don't have enough information to answer that.' - safe, user knows the system was honest about its limit.
summary: An explicit refusal instruction gives the model an authorized exit phrase so it stops inventing answers when retrieved docs don't cover the question.
queries: I don't have enough information sentence

text: Faithfulness: every claim must trace to a chunk. Faithfulness is the share of claims in an answer that are supported by the retrieved chunks. A 3-step audit converts citations into a measurable score. Chapters 12.31-12.35 cover RAGAS faithfulness in depth. Step 1: Parse answer into atomic claims (example: '5 failed logins triggers a lock' / 'Billing issues hold for 24 hours'). Step 2: Locate the cited chunk for each claim by following the [doc-N] marker back to retrieved chunk text. Step 3: Score claim_supported = LLMJudge(claim, chunk); aggregate per-claim scores into a faithfulness number from 0 to 1. Output is a single number from 0 (none supported) to 1 (every claim has evidence in the retrieved chunks).
summary: Faithfulness is a 0-1 score measuring how many atomic claims in an answer are actually supported by the retrieved chunks, computed via parse-locate-LLMJudge.

text:
Parse citations back out of the answer. After generation, parse [doc-N] markers with regex \[doc-\d+\]. Map each marker to retrieved chunk and render as footnotes or hover tooltips. Model output highlighted: 'Your account locks after 5 failed logins [doc-12]. Billing issues trigger a 24-hour hold [doc-7].' Resolved citation table:

[doc-12] | doc-12 (Account Lockouts) | chunk 3 | "5 failed login attempts"
[doc-7]  | doc-7 (Billing Holds)     | chunk 1 | "24-hour hold after a failed charge"

Render as footnotes or inline tooltips. Now the user can click a citation and see exactly what the model used to write that sentence.
summary: A regex \[doc-\d+\] extracts citation markers from the answer and resolves each to a table of doc, chunk, and quoted span for UI footnotes or tooltips.

text:
Production template: citations plus refusal. One combined template that does both. Numbered RULES make the contract explicit so the model treats citations and refusal as non-optional. Template:

You are a helpful support assistant. Use ONLY the documentation
below to answer the user's question.

RULES:
1. Cite every claim inline as [doc-N], where N is the doc number.
2. Quote 3-7 words from the cited chunk after each [doc-N].
3. If the docs do not answer the question, respond with EXACTLY:
   "I don't have enough information to answer that."
4. Do not invent facts. Do not paraphrase what isn't in the docs.

Documentation:
{context}

Question: {query}
Answer:

Numbered rules survive long context better than prose instructions. The model attends to short structured constraints more reliably than a paragraph.
summary: A production prompt template combines citations and refusal into 4 numbered RULES, which the model follows more reliably than prose instructions.

text: Multi-hop retrieval control-loop flowchart: starts with User Query, enters Retrieve Top-K For Current Query, then a sufficiency diamond 'Does Context Answer The Question?' Three branches: YES leads right to Generate Answer (green); NO + Hops < max_hops leads left to Reformulate Query (orange) then loops back up to the Retrieve box; NO + Hops >= max_hops leads down to Refuse: 'I Don't Have Enough Information' (red). Arrow markers color-coded to indicate each path.
summary: Diagram of the multi-hop control loop with retrieve, sufficiency diamond, and three branches (generate, reformulate-and-loop, refuse) for hitting max_hops.

text: Chapter 12.25 - Multi-Hop Retrieval. Some queries depend on chained facts that live across multiple docs; one retrieval misses half. Multi-hop retrieval is a control loop: retrieve top-k, run a sufficiency check, then either generate, reformulate-and-loop, or refuse when max_hops is hit. The sufficiency check is a structured prompt forcing SUFFICIENT or INSUFFICIENT+follow-up. Worth it for compound, cross-doc, step-dependent queries; not for single-doc FAQ lookups. Cost scales linearly with hops, so cap max_hops (typically 3-5), cache reformulations, and use a smaller judge. Watch three failure modes: infinite loops (cap + log), divergence (anchor original query), stuck sufficiency judges (separate calibrated model).
summary: Multi-hop retrieval iterates retrieve-check-reformulate with a hard max_hops cap to handle compound queries, trading cost and latency for accuracy while guarding against loops, drift, and overconfident judges.
queries: how to chain retrievals in RAG
when to use multi-hop in RAG

text:
Some queries need more than one retrieval. Single-hop retrieval works when the answer fits in one chunk. Multi-hop retrieval kicks in when the question depends on a chain of facts that live in different docs. Signal you need it: even a perfect retrieval returns only half of what is needed.

Single-hop works example: Query 'How do I reset my password?' - 1 retrieval finds doc-1 (password reset). Answer: 'Go to Settings then Security, click Reset Password.'

Multi-hop needed example: Query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' - 1 retrieval finds doc-1 (password reset) but the question also needs doc-3 (email change / recovery). One retrieval misses half; multiple retrievals needed.
summary: Multi-hop retrieval applies when answering a query requires chaining facts from multiple docs that one retrieval cannot fetch together.
queries: when do I need multi-hop retrieval
question needs facts from multiple documents
password reset if forgot email RAG example
signal that you need multiple hops

text:
Two hops on the running query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?'

Hop 1: Query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' Retrieved: doc-1 (password reset). Reasoning: Reset requires email access. The user says they forgot email - need email recovery info before answering. Decision: INSUFFICIENT. Reformulate as: 'How do I recover access if I forgot my email?'

Hop 2: Query 'How do I recover access if I forgot my email?' Retrieved: doc-3 (email change / recovery). Reasoning: Now both pieces are in hand: the reset flow (doc-1) and the email-recovery path (doc-3). Decision: SUFFICIENT.

Final answer combines doc-1 + doc-3 with citations. Each hop reformulates the query to fetch the missing piece.
summary: Worked two-hop trace showing the LLM marking hop 1 INSUFFICIENT, reformulating, then marking hop 2 SUFFICIENT and combining doc-1 with doc-3.
queries: how does LLM decide to do another hop
combining doc-1 and doc-3 with citations

text:
The multi-hop control loop flowchart. Loop structure: retrieve top-k for current query -> check if context answers the question (diamond) -> if YES generate answer; if NO and hops < max_hops, reformulate query and loop back to retrieve; if NO and hops >= max_hops, refuse with 'I don't have enough information'.

max_hops typically 3-5. Each hop adds 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call latency. Tune based on workload and budget. A hard cap on max_hops prevents infinite loops. If the cap is hit without sufficiency, the system refuses.
summary: Control-loop flowchart: retrieve, check sufficiency, then either generate, reformulate-and-loop, or refuse when max_hops cap is hit.
queries: loop back to retrieve after reformulation

text:
Asking the model: do you have enough? The sufficiency check is a small structured task. The model decides whether the retrieved text answers the question. SUFFICIENT means 'I can answer now'; INSUFFICIENT means 'fetch this follow-up'.

Prompt template:
You are an assistant deciding whether you have enough information.
Question: {query}
Retrieved so far: {context}
Respond with EXACTLY one of:
- SUFFICIENT (you can answer the question from the retrieved text)
- INSUFFICIENT: <one short follow-up query to retrieve more>
Do not answer the question yet.

Example trace:
Hop 1: 'INSUFFICIENT: How do I recover access if I forgot my email?'
Hop 2: 'SUFFICIENT'
summary: Sufficiency-check prompt forces the model to emit either SUFFICIENT or INSUFFICIENT with a follow-up query, without prematurely answering.
queries: how to ask LLM if it has enough info
prompt to decide more retrieval needed

text:
When to reach for multi-hop. Multi-hop is not free. Each hop multiplies latency and cost. Use only when query genuinely needs chained facts, always with a hard cap.

Worth It: Compound questions (password reset if you forgot your email); cross-doc reasoning (compare Pro vs Enterprise plans); step-dependent flows (cancel subscription then refund).

Not Worth It: Single-doc lookups; simple FAQ-style queries; cost outweighs benefit when one hop already answers.

Cost Multiplier: Each hop = 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call; 3 hops = ~3x latency, ~3x cost vs single-hop; budget per query becomes a hard constraint.

Mitigations: Cap max_hops at 3; cache reformulated queries; use a smaller model for the sufficiency check.
summary: Multi-hop is worth it for compound, cross-doc, and step-dependent queries but multiplies cost ~Nx, so cap hops, cache reformulations, and use a cheap sufficiency judge.

text:
Failure modes: infinite loops and divergence. Three known failure modes show up in production multi-hop systems; each has a known mitigation that costs either an extra check or stricter caps.

Infinite Loop: Model never says SUFFICIENT. Each hop reformulates without making progress. Mitigation: hard cap on max_hops + log every reformulation.

Divergence: Each reformulation drifts farther from the original intent. 'How do I reset my password?' becomes 'How do I configure SSO?' by hop 3. Mitigation: keep the ORIGINAL query in the reformulation prompt as an anchor.

Stuck Sufficiency Judge: Model always says SUFFICIENT after hop 1 even when context is incomplete (overconfident). Mitigation: separate judge model, calibrate via a golden dataset.
summary: Three multi-hop failure modes - infinite loops, divergence, and stuck sufficiency judges - each fixed with caps, anchoring the original query, or a calibrated judge model.
queries: model drifts off topic across hops

text: Self-RAG token-emission timeline diagram: horizontal line with six markers showing the model alternating between special control tokens and answer text across the generation sequence. Markers in order: <retrieve> (model gates retrieval), Reformulate (tokens 2-3 produce search query), doc-1 In (retriever returns top doc), <isrel> (model judges doc-1 relevant), Answer (tokens 6-20 generate answer text), <issup> (model judges answer supported). Generation order left to right.
summary: Diagram showing the left-to-right order of Self-RAG control tokens interleaved with answer generation.
queries: what does Self-RAG timeline look like

text: Retrieve / no-retrieve decision diamond diagram: User Query box at top flows into yellow diamond labeled "Need external knowledge?". YES branch on right exits to green box "<retrieve> -> <isrel> -> <issup>". NO branch on left exits to red box "<no-retrieve> -> Answer". Below: "Trained via instruction tuning + RL. Reduces unnecessary retrievals ~40% (Asai et al. 2023)".
summary: Diagram of the Self-RAG gate diamond routing queries to either retrieve-plus-critique or direct-answer.

text: Self-RAG (Asai et al. 2023) trains the LLM to decide per-query whether to retrieve, then self-critique what comes back. Model emits four special control tokens inline: <retrieve>, <no-retrieve>, <isrel> (judges each retrieved doc's relevance), and <issup> (judges whether final answer is supported by kept docs). Runtime intercepts tokens and acts. Cuts unnecessary retrievals ~40%. Wins on mixed-knowledge corpora, latency-sensitive workloads, adaptive depth. Limits: needs a fine-tuned model, larger vocab, classifier calibration is the bottleneck.
summary: Self-RAG teaches the LLM to gate retrieval and self-critique results using four special control tokens.
queries: special tokens RAG retrieve isrel issup

text: Should the model retrieve, or just answer? Always-retrieve wastes tokens and latency. Never-retrieve risks hallucination. Self-RAG decides per-query. Example queries: "What is 2 + 2?" -> <no-retrieve> (factoid already known from pre-training). "How do I reset my password?" -> <retrieve> (custom-corpus knowledge model cannot answer without retrieval). "Summarize what we just discussed." -> <no-retrieve> (context already in conversation; no external lookup needed).
summary: Self-RAG gates retrieval per-query so the model only fetches when external knowledge is genuinely needed.

text: Self-RAG extends model vocabulary with four control tokens emitted inline during generation; runtime intercepts each. <retrieve>: retrieve a document now. <no-retrieve>: do not retrieve, answer from model's own knowledge. <isrel>: is this retrieved doc relevant? Emitted after each retrieved doc. <issup>: is the answer supported by the retrieved doc? Emitted with the answer.
summary: Four special tokens (<retrieve>, <no-retrieve>, <isrel>, <issup>) extend the model vocabulary to control retrieval and grade results.

text: Token emission timeline for query "How do I reset my password?": <retrieve> (model gates retrieval) -> Reformulate (tokens 2-3 produce a search query) -> doc-1 In (retriever returns top doc) -> <isrel> (model judges doc-1 relevant) -> Answer (tokens 6-20 generate answer text) -> <issup> (model judges answer supported). Each marker is a decision point the runtime watches. Generation order left to right.
summary: Walk through a real query showing the sequence of control tokens and answer text Self-RAG emits.

text: Retrieve / no-retrieve gate sits at top of every generation. User query enters decision diamond "Need external knowledge?" YES branch -> <retrieve> -> <isrel> -> <issup>. NO branch -> <no-retrieve> -> Answer. Trained via instruction tuning + RL on retrieval decisions. Reduces unnecessary retrievals ~40% in original Asai et al. 2023 paper.
summary: The gate diamond routes queries to retrieval-plus-critique or direct-answer paths, trained with instruction tuning plus RL.

text: Self-critique re-scores every retrieved doc. After retrieval, model emits <isrel> per doc to grade relevance. Per-doc critique example: doc-1 (Password Reset) RELEVANT -> Keep; doc-9 (Login Troubleshooting) PARTIALLY RELEVANT -> Keep but de-weight; doc-22 (Rate Limits) IRRELEVANT -> Drop. After answer, model emits <issup> final verdict: FULLY-SUPPORTED | PARTIALLY-SUPPORTED | NO-SUPPORT. Labels how grounded the answer is in the kept docs.
summary: After retrieval Self-RAG grades each doc with <isrel> and grades the final answer's groundedness with <issup>.

text: Self-RAG wins: mixed-knowledge corpora (some queries need retrieval, some don't); latency-sensitive workloads (skips retrieval for ~40% of queries); adaptive depth (decides how many docs to fetch). Limits: requires a model trained for Self-RAG (not plug-and-play); token vocabulary increases serialization cost; calibration of the special-token classifier is the bottleneck. Pick Self-RAG when fine-tuning is on the table and the workload mixes retrieval-needing and already-knew-it queries.
summary: Self-RAG wins on adaptive mixed-knowledge workloads but requires fine-tuning and classifier calibration.

text: CRAG three-branch decision tree: an evaluator score routes retrieved docs to a CORRECT path (use directly), an AMBIGUOUS path (combine internal + web), or an INCORRECT path (web-search fallback), all converging into knowledge refinement before generation.
summary: Diagram showing the CRAG evaluator routing docs into three branches that converge into knowledge refinement and generation.
queries: Visual showing CORRECT AMBIGUOUS INCORRECT branches

text: CRAG (Corrective RAG, Yan et al. 2024) inserts a retrieval evaluator between retrieval and generation. Each retrieved doc is scored and classified as CORRECT (use directly), AMBIGUOUS (combine internal docs with external web search), or INCORRECT (fall back to web search). All branches converge into a knowledge refinement step that decomposes docs into 100-150 token strips, drops off-topic strips, and packs only on-topic ones before generation. Threshold guidance: below 0.5 = INCORRECT, above 0.8 = CORRECT, between = AMBIGUOUS. Demonstrated on query 'What is our cancellation policy for monthly plans?' where doc-7 scored 0.92 (CORRECT), doc-22 scored 0.31 (INCORRECT), doc-15 scored 0.62 (AMBIGUOUS).
summary: CRAG adds an evaluator that routes retrieved docs into CORRECT, AMBIGUOUS, or INCORRECT branches and refines surviving docs into strips before generation.
queries: When does CRAG fall back to web search
How to handle bad retrieval in RAG
How to detect irrelevant retrieved docs
What threshold makes a doc CORRECT in CRAG

text: CRAG (Yan et al. 2024) adds a retrieval evaluator between retrieval and generation. Each doc is scored and classified: CORRECT (use it), AMBIGUOUS (combine with external sources), or INCORRECT (fall back to web). Running query: 'What is our cancellation policy for monthly plans?' Scored docs: doc-7 (Refunds + Cancellation) score 0.92 verdict CORRECT - snippet 'Customers can cancel monthly plans any time before the next billing cycle.' doc-22 (API Rate Limits) score 0.31 verdict INCORRECT - snippet 'Free tier allows 100 requests per minute; Pro tier 1000 per minute.' doc-15 (Subscription Tiers) score 0.62 verdict AMBIGUOUS - snippet 'Monthly and annual plans differ in pricing and feature access.' The evaluator can be a small classifier model or an LLM judge. Threshold below 0.5 = INCORRECT, above 0.8 = CORRECT, between = AMBIGUOUS.
summary: The CRAG retrieval evaluator scores each retrieved doc and labels it CORRECT, AMBIGUOUS, or INCORRECT using thresholds 0.5 and 0.8.

text: CORRECT branch: highest-confidence path. Doc passes the evaluator; CRAG routes it through knowledge refinement and then to the LLM with citations. Flow: Retrieved Doc -> Knowledge Refinement -> Generate Answer With Citations. Same as baseline RAG once the docs pass the evaluator.
summary: On the CORRECT branch, passing docs go through knowledge refinement and into generation just like baseline RAG.

text: INCORRECT branch: doc fails the evaluator. CRAG rewrites the query for web search, calls an external search API, and uses the web results in place of the internal doc. Flow: Failed Doc -> Rewrite Query -> External Web Search -> Use Web Results -> Generate Answer. Web fallback costs latency (300-800 ms) and requires a search API. Use when refusal is unacceptable. Trade-off versus admitting 'I don't know'.
summary: On the INCORRECT branch, CRAG rewrites the query and falls back to web search instead of using the failed internal doc.

text: AMBIGUOUS branch: doc partially answers. CRAG keeps it AND also fetches external web results, then merges both into the generation prompt. Flow: Partial Doc + Web Results -> Merge Into Context -> Generate Answer. The hedge bet: highest cost path, lowest miss rate. Use when refusal is unacceptable but confidence in internal docs is low.
summary: On the AMBIGUOUS branch, CRAG hedges by combining the partial internal doc with web results before generating.

text: CRAG knowledge refinement splits each retrieved doc into 100-150 token strips and scores each strip individually. Off-topic strips are dropped; on-topic strips are packed. Doc-7 (Refunds) starts at 800 tokens; after refinement, only 3 of 6 strips are kept (~50% reduction). Strip breakdown: Strip 1 Refund Eligibility KEEP. Strip 2 Annual Plans Prorating KEEP. Strip 3 Refund Processing Time KEEP. Strip 4 Tax Handling DROP. Strip 5 Legal Disclaimers DROP. Strip 6 Chargeback Policy DROP. Final: 3 kept strips, ~50% of original tokens, all on-topic. Packed into the prompt for generation.
summary: Knowledge refinement splits each doc into 100-150 token strips, drops off-topic ones, and keeps only the on-topic strips for the prompt.
queries: How many strips are kept after CRAG refinement

text: The full CRAG decision tree: Query + Retrieved Docs feed into an Evaluator Score diamond, which branches three ways - CORRECT -> Use Docs (green), AMBIGUOUS -> Combine (yellow), INCORRECT -> Web (red). All three branches converge into a Knowledge Refinement (Strips) step, which feeds Generate Answer. One evaluator. Three branches. All converge into knowledge refinement and generation (Yan et al. 2024).
summary: Full CRAG pipeline diagram: one evaluator routes to three branches that all converge into knowledge refinement and final generation.

text: GraphRAG (Microsoft Edge et al. 2024) builds a knowledge graph from a corpus at index time, then retrieves subgraphs for local queries and community summaries for global queries. Chapter uses a 15-node legal-citation network (Civil Rights, Contract Law, IP Law communities) instead of the customer-support corpus because relationships are sparse there. Steps: extract entities + relations with an LLM per doc (~$0.02/doc), build a directed citation graph, run community detection (Leiden/Louvain), generate per-community summaries. Local queries fetch a subgraph (5-node precedent chain); global queries map-reduce over community summaries. Worth it for rich entity-relationship corpora and frequent global queries; not worth it for self-contained FAQ-style docs.
summary: End-to-end overview of GraphRAG: offline entity/relation extraction, citation graph, community detection, and local-vs-global query routing.
queries: What are community summaries in GraphRAG?
How to build a knowledge graph for RAG

text: The customer-support corpus is mostly self-contained how-to pages, so relationships are sparse and a graph built over it would be thin. This chapter switches to a 15-node legal-citation network where each case decision cites 2-5 prior cases. Customer Support Corpus - Won't Fit: 30 docs, mostly self-contained how-to pages; relationships sparse; a graph here would have few edges and demonstrate nothing. Legal Citation Network - Fits: 15 case decisions where each cites 2-5 prior cases; rich entities (Plaintiff, Defendant, Court, Statute) and a real graph topology. The customer-support corpus returns in chapter 12.29; this chapter only uses the legal-citation secondary corpus.

text: Offline at index time, each case runs through an LLM extraction prompt once. The LLM emits entities (people, organizations, statutes) and relations (sued, decided-in, applies, holding). Cost is ~$0.02 per doc. Example: Smith v Jones (2018). Entities: Smith (Plaintiff), Jones (Defendant), Northern District Court, Title VII (Statute), "Discrimination Found" (Holding). Relations: Smith -- sued --> Jones; Smith v Jones -- decided-in --> Northern District Court; Smith v Jones -- applies --> Title VII; Smith v Jones -- holding --> Discrimination Found.

text: The 15-Node Citation Graph: built once offline. Each node is a case; each directed edge is a citation from the citing case to the cited case. 15 cases, 27 citation edges. Three communities are present: Civil Rights (nodes 1-6: Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State, Miller v EEOC), Contract Law (nodes 7-11: Apex v Beta, Cosmos v Stellar, Delta v Epsilon, Falcon v GoTech, Hera v Olympus), and IP Law (nodes 12-15: Aurora v Beacon, Cipher v Decode, Eagle v Falcon IP, Sigma v Tau). Updated incrementally as new cases arrive.

text: Query Time: Retrieve A Subgraph. Query: "What is the precedent chain for workplace discrimination cases?" The retriever finds a 5-node subgraph (Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State) and sends only those nodes + their edges to the LLM. Entity-aware retrieval; the graph topology is the signal. 5 nodes highlighted in orange. The LLM reads only the highlighted subgraph - not the whole 15-node corpus.
summary: Shows how a local query triggers subgraph retrieval, fetching only a connected 5-node precedent chain instead of the full corpus.
queries: What is subgraph retrieval in GraphRAG?
