chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Capstone Use Case: Q&A Over Case Law. Customer: a legal research firm. Design brief: Corpus = 200,000 published court cases across 50 US jurisdictions. Metadata = jurisdiction tag, year, citation tree. Quality bar = partner-lawyer rating >= 4/5 on legal research rubric. Latency budget = 5-10 seconds (legal research is patient). Cost budget = $0.05/query. Audit requirements = every answer cited, jurisdiction filter mandatory. Example queries: 'What's the precedent for tortious interference in California state court?'; 'Cases citing Smith v Jones about negligence in medical contexts'; 'How has the standard for qualified immunity evolved 2010-2025?'. Domain is fully different from the customer support corpus used earlier.
summary: Defines the capstone problem: a legal case-law RAG system with 200k cases, $0.05/query budget, 5-10s latency, mandatory citations and jurisdiction filtering.
queries: How big is the legal RAG corpus?
What's the cost budget for legal RAG?
What latency does legal RAG allow?

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 1: Chunking Strategy (chapters 12.8, 12.9). Choice: Hierarchical Chunking (12.8) + Contextual Retrieval (12.9) - per-paragraph chunks with parent (case-level) metadata and LLM-injected 'this paragraph is from case X about Y' preamble. Why: case opinions have natural hierarchy (intro, facts, reasoning, holding); hierarchical preserves parent-child relationships; contextual retrieval injects the per-chunk preamble that disambiguates citation-level retrieval; plain fixed-size chunking would mid-sentence-split a court's analysis. Tradeoff: more storage (each chunk now has parent metadata); higher index build cost (contextual augmentation is an LLM call per chunk); worth it because case-law precision demands citation-level retrieval.
summary: Capstone chunking decision: hierarchical + contextual chunking preserves case structure and citation precision despite higher storage and build cost.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 2: Embedding + Index (chapters 12.15, 12.16, 12.17). Choice: Domain-Adapted Embedding (12.15) on top of a base Legal-BERT or Cohere Embed Multilingual. HNSW index with hybrid (BM25 + dense, 12.16). Jurisdiction stored as filter metadata. Cross-encoder cascade rerank (12.17). Why: general-purpose embeddings underweight legal jargon ('tortious', 'qualified immunity'); hybrid catches both exact-citation lookups ('Smith v Jones') and semantic queries ('medical negligence cases'); jurisdiction filter eliminates 96% of irrelevant cases before reranking; cross-encoder lifts precision on legal-language matching. Tradeoff: domain-adapt requires labeled training pairs (expensive but one-time); cross-encoder adds 50-100ms per query; latency budget allows it.
summary: Capstone embedding/index decision: domain-adapted legal embeddings with HNSW + BM25 hybrid, jurisdiction filter, and cross-encoder rerank.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 3: Query Transformation (chapters 12.18, 12.19, 12.20, 12.21). Choice: Multi-Query Expansion (12.20) + Decomposition for complex multi-hop queries (12.21). HyDE (12.19) SKIPPED. Why: a query like 'cases citing X about Y' naturally decomposes into 'find X' -> 'find cases citing X' -> 'filter for cases about Y'; multi-query rewriting (legal synonyms: 'negligence' / 'duty of care' / 'breach of duty') improves recall on lexically-narrow legal language. Tradeoff: more retrievals per query (3-5x); cost goes up linearly; mitigated by aggressive deduplication after RRF merge.
summary: Capstone query transformation: multi-query expansion + decomposition; HyDE skipped because precise legal language makes fake docs hurt more than help.
queries: When to use HyDE vs skip

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
summary: Capstone context+generation: 16-30k token budget with sandwich packing, mandatory [Case Name, Year] citations, and explicit refusal clause via the legal prompt template.
queries: How big should the context window be?

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 5: Advanced Retrieval Patterns (chapters 12.25, 12.27, 12.28, 12.30). Choice: GraphRAG (12.28) for the case-citation graph (cases citing X / cases cited by X). Multi-Hop Retrieval (12.25) for 'cases citing X about Y' decomposition. Long-Context (12.30) SKIPPED. Why: legal citation IS a graph; GraphRAG indexes the relationships; without it, 'cases citing X' requires querying every case in the corpus; with it, follow the citation edges; multi-hop chains the steps for complex precedent queries. Long-context skipped because 200k cases > any context window so RAG is mandatory. Tradeoff: GraphRAG indexing is 10x slower than vector-only; re-indexing the citation graph on corpus update is a nightly batch; multi-hop is slower per query but precision gain on multi-hop queries is large.
summary: Capstone advanced retrieval: GraphRAG over case-citation graph + multi-hop retrieval; long-context skipped because corpus exceeds any window.
queries: Why skip long context for 200k cases?

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 6: Evaluation (chapters 12.32, 12.33, 12.34, 12.35). Choice: LLM-as-Judge (12.32) with a legal-specific rubric. Golden Dataset (12.34) of 500 partner-curated query/expected-answer pairs. RAGAS Metrics (12.33): faithfulness, citation precision, jurisdiction match. Online A/B (12.35) on partner-rated 4/5 score. Why: general-purpose RAG eval misses legal correctness; partner-curated golden set is the ground truth because no automated metric reaches partner-quality; RAGAS faithfulness catches hallucinations; citation precision (% of cited cases that are real and on-point) is critical. Tradeoff: golden dataset is expensive to build (partner hours); 500 examples covers ~80% of query distribution; remaining 20% caught by online feedback; rerun golden eval weekly on prod.
summary: Capstone eval: 500-pair partner-curated golden set + LLM-as-judge with legal rubric + RAGAS faithfulness/citation precision + online A/B on 4/5 partner score.
queries: What metrics for legal RAG quality

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 7: Production Operations (chapters 12.36, 12.38, 12.39). Choice: Semantic Cache DISABLED. Prompt Cache ENABLED for system prompt + few-shot examples (12.36). Full tracing per query (12.38). Hallucination detection with legal-fact-check post-process (12.39). Drift monitoring on case-law updates. Why: semantic cache fails when 'negligence in CA' and 'negligence in NY' cosine to 0.96 - the answers differ entirely; prompt cache safe because system + few-shot are constant; tracing every query is non-negotiable for audit. Tradeoff: no semantic cache = higher per-query cost; mitigated by prompt cache; drift monitoring catches when judicial reasoning shifts (rare but high-impact).
summary: Capstone prod ops: prompt cache on, semantic cache off (false-hit risk), full per-query tracing, legal-fact-check hallucination detection, and drift monitoring.
queries: Why disable semantic cache for legal?
Negligence CA vs NY cache problem
Prompt cache system prompt few shot

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Putting It All Together: The Capstone Stack. Five layers: 1. Ingest - Hierarchical + Contextual chunking; domain-adapted embedding; HNSW + BM25 hybrid index; jurisdiction metadata; case-citation graph index. 2. Retrieve - Multi-query expansion; jurisdiction filter; hybrid retrieval; cross-encoder rerank; GraphRAG for citation queries. 3. Generate - 16-30k token budget; sandwich ordering; mandatory citations; I-don't-know refusal if no jurisdiction-matching cases. 4. Eval - Partner-curated golden set (500 pairs); LLM-as-judge with legal rubric; RAGAS faithfulness; online A/B. 5. Ops - Prompt cache (semantic cache disabled); full tracing per query; hallucination detection with legal-fact-check post-process; drift monitoring on case-law updates. Framework Choice (12.40): No Framework + LlamaIndex retriever for RAG core + raw Anthropic SDK. Cost ~$0.04/query (under $0.05 budget). Latency ~6 seconds. Every answer carries [Case Name, Year] citations. You now have everything to design production-grade RAG for any new domain.
summary: Final capstone stack: 5 layers (ingest, retrieve, generate, eval, ops) + No Framework + LlamaIndex + Anthropic SDK, hitting ~$0.04/query, ~6s latency, every answer cited.
queries: Cost per query legal RAG capstone
How to design RAG for any domain

text: Anatomy of an LLM Call: every call is an ordered list of role-tagged messages (system, user, assistant); the model processes tokens, not words, against a fixed context window (e.g. 128k or 200k); three sampling knobs - temperature, top-p, top-k - shape next-token selection; generation halts on one of four stop conditions (end_turn, length, stop_sequence, tool_use) exposed via finish_reason; the response object returns content, stop_reason, and usage (input_tokens + output_tokens). The full cycle is six steps: build messages, set parameters, send request, stream tokens, hit stop reason, deliver final response.
summary: Chapter-level overview of the LLM call: messages with roles, token-based context, sampling knobs, stop conditions, response shape, and the six-step lifecycle.

text: Every LLM call is a list of messages, not a chat. You send an ordered list of messages, each with a role; the model reads the whole list and writes the next assistant turn. Three roles: System sets the role, rules, and tone of the assistant (example: 'You are Alice's customer-support assistant. Be concise. Cite docs when possible.'). User is the end-user's turn, the thing the model must respond to (example: 'I can't log in - reset my password for alice@example.com.'). Assistant is the model's reply, which goes back into the messages list for the next turn (example: 'I'll help you reset it. I just sent a one-time link to alice@example.com.'). Every later request includes prior assistant replies so the model has the full conversation.
summary: An LLM call is an ordered messages list with three roles - system, user, assistant - and prior assistant replies are re-sent each turn.

text: The model doesn't see characters or words - it sees tokens, sub-word chunks from the tokenizer. Both input and output are billed and limited in tokens. Example user message 'I can't log in - reset my password' is 9 tokens: I, can, 't, _log, _in, _-, _reset, _my, _password. Example assistant reply 'I'll help you reset it.' is 7 tokens: I, 'll, _help, _you, _reset, _it, .. The context window is the total tokens across all messages: system + user + assistant history + new reply. A 200k context window holds roughly 150k English words. Cross the window and the oldest messages get dropped or the call fails.
summary: Models process tokens (sub-word chunks), not words, and the context window caps the total tokens across all messages.

text: Three sampling knobs shape how the model picks the next token from its probability distribution. Lower = safer and more repeatable, higher = more varied. Temperature controls sharpness of the distribution; common stops: 0.0 deterministic, 0.3 focused, 0.7 default, 1.0 creative, 2.0 wild. At T=0 the model always picks the most likely next token; at T=2 unlikely tokens get a real chance. Top-P (nucleus) keeps tokens until cumulative probability hits P. Example with top_p=0.9 on candidates: reset p=0.42 cum=0.42 kept; change p=0.21 cum=0.63 kept; update p=0.16 cum=0.79 kept; fix p=0.09 cum=0.88 kept; recover p=0.05 cum=0.93 cut; send p=0.04 cum=0.97 cut; create p=0.03 cum=1.00 cut - 4 tokens stay, 3 are cut. Top-K keeps the K most likely tokens. With top_k=5 the same list keeps reset, change, update, fix, recover and discards send, create before sampling.
summary: Temperature, top-p (nucleus), and top-k are sampling knobs that control how deterministic or varied the next-token choice is.
queries: what is temperature in LLM sampling
how does the model pick the next token
how do I make LLM output more deterministic
how to make LLM output more creative
how to control randomness in LLM
what sampling knob for code generation

text: One LLM call doesn't 'just stop' - it stops for a specific reason and the response tells you which. Four stop conditions, four finish reasons. Max Tokens Reached (max_tokens: 1024) - hard ceiling on generated tokens, finish_reason: length. Stop Sequence Hit (stop: ['</answer>']) - model produced a configured stop string, useful for structured output, finish_reason: stop_sequence. End-Of-Turn Token (model decides on its own) - model emitted its special end-of-turn token, the natural end of a reply, finish_reason: end_turn. Tool Call Requested (only with tool use enabled) - model paused to ask for a tool, generation halts so your code can run the tool, finish_reason: tool_use. Always check finish_reason. A length stop means the answer got cut off mid-thought - retry with a higher max_tokens.
summary: Generation ends for one of four reasons - end_turn, length, stop_sequence, or tool_use - exposed via finish_reason.
queries: how to detect truncated LLM output
when does the model emit end of turn token
how do I know if response was cut off

text: The response is a small structured object. Three fields matter for almost every call: the generated text, why the model stopped, and the token usage you pay for. Response shape: { 'content': 'I'll help you reset it...', 'stop_reason': 'end_turn', 'usage': { 'input_tokens': 9, 'output_tokens': 7 } }. content is the generated assistant text - feed this back into the messages list for the next turn. stop_reason (some providers call this finish_reason, same idea: end_turn, length, stop_sequence, tool_use). usage = input_tokens + output_tokens - this is what gets billed and what counts against your context window.
summary: An LLM response object carries content (generated text), stop_reason (why it ended), and usage (input/output token counts for billing).
queries: what does an LLM API response look like
what fields are in the response object
what is the usage field in LLM response

what to feed back for next turn
where is the generated text in response

text: Every LLM call follows a six-step cycle. 1. Build Messages - combine system + history + new user turn into the messages list (roles assigned). 2. Set Parameters - pick temperature, top-p, top-k, max_tokens, stop (sampling locked). 3. Send Request - POST messages + params to the model endpoint (HTTPS POST). 4. Tokens Stream Back - model emits one token at a time over the wire (SSE chunks). 5. Stop Reason Reached - one of: end_turn, length, stop_sequence, tool_use (end_turn fired). 6. Final Response - content + stop_reason + usage delivered to your app. Typical latency: ~1.5s median for a 7-token response. Long replies stream tokens as they're generated. Messages in, parameters in, request out; tokens stream back until a stop reason fires; response object lands with content, stop_reason, and usage.
summary: Diagram of the full six-step LLM call cycle from building messages to receiving the final response with content, stop_reason, and usage.

text: Chapter 13.2 frames the system prompt as a contract the model re-reads every turn, built from four parts: Persona (who the agent is, its voice and role - name the user, the role, one never), Capabilities (the tool inventory - search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human - declared in the prompt and wired via API schema), Constraints (explicit 'never' rules like the $200 refund cap and the no-sharing-customer-data rule that catch the failure modes code cannot), and Output Rules (greeting, citations, <thinking> wrapping of tool calls, and a follow-up or escalation offer to close each reply). Skip a part and the agent goes off-script. The chapter ends with a full ~10-line system prompt assembling all four parts into one artifact.
summary: Chapter overview: system prompt = persona + capabilities + constraints + output rules, demonstrated in one ten-line artifact.
queries: four parts of system prompt summary
what does chapter on system prompt cover
persona capabilities constraints output rules summary
four building blocks of system prompt

text: The system prompt is the role contract. It is the constitution every later message is interpreted under. The model re-reads it on every single turn, so anything not in it does not exist for the agent. Four moving parts: Persona (who am I, what is my voice and role), Capabilities (what tools and data am I allowed to use), Constraints (what must I never say, promise, or do), Output Rules (how must my reply be formatted, cited, and structured). Get all four right and the agent behaves predictably. Skip one and it goes off-script.
summary: System prompt is contract re-read every turn with four parts: persona, capabilities, constraints, output rules.
queries: why does model re-read system prompt every turn
what fields go in system prompt

text: Persona answers 'who am I, what is my voice, and what is my role?' BEFORE (vague): 'You are a chatbot.' No role, no voice, no domain - model fills the gap with a generic helpful tone. AFTER (persona-specific): 'You are Alice's customer-support assistant for the customer support knowledge base. You are friendly, concise, and you never invent policies.' Names the user (Alice), names the role (customer-support assistant), names the voice (friendly, concise) and one guardrail (no invented policies). Three rules of thumb: name the user, name the role, name one thing the assistant must never do.
summary: Persona names the user, the role, the voice, and one guardrail - vague chatbot prompts become specific assistants.
queries: what is persona in system prompt
how to write persona for LLM
you are a chatbot bad prompt
how to give LLM a voice

text: Capabilities tell the model which tools are available, what each one does, and when to use it. Without this section the model either refuses or hallucinates tool calls. Example tools: search_kb (searches customer support knowledge base at docs.example.com), lookup_customer (fetches profile for customer id like c-9924), process_refund (issues refund up to configured dollar limit), escalate_human (hands conversation to human agent). Prompt line: 'Tools available to you: search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human.' The system prompt declares the tools. The API schema (chapter 13.8) is what actually wires them up. Both sides have to agree.
summary: Capabilities section lists available tools and when to use them; declared in prompt and wired via API schema.
queries: what are capabilities in system prompt
what tools does the agent have
prompt declares tools API wires them

text: Constraints are the negative-space rules - the 'never's. Every refund-gone-wrong, every leaked customer record, every invented policy traces back to a missing line here. Be explicit. Example constraints for the support agent: Never promise refunds over $200 without escalation. Never share another customer's data. Never claim policies not in the KB. Never call tools without first looking up the customer. The $200 line and the escalation line live here, not in code. The model checks them on every reply, so a policy change is a one-line edit, not a deploy.
summary: Constraints are explicit 'never' rules that prevent leaks, bad refunds, and invented policies - edited in prompt not code.
queries: what are constraints in system prompt

text: Output rules shape the surface of every reply: tone, structure, citations, and what each turn must end with. They make the assistant feel consistent across thousands of conversations. Example output rules: 1) Always greet the customer by name. 2) Cite the KB doc when quoting policy. 3) Wrap tool calls in <thinking> tags first. 4) End with a follow-up question or escalation offer. Citations and follow-up offers are not polish - they are how you keep the agent honest and the conversation moving. Without them, replies dead-end and trust erodes.
summary: Output rules dictate reply tone, structure, citations, and closing, keeping agent consistent and trustworthy.
queries: what are output rules in system prompt

text:
Full system prompt weaving all four parts into one artifact, sent as the system message on every turn:

You are Alice's customer-support assistant for the customer support knowledge base at docs.example.com. You are friendly, concise, and you never invent policies.

Tools available to you: search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human. Always call lookup_customer (e.g. c-9924) before any other tool.

Never promise refunds over $200 without escalating to a human. Never share another customer's data. Never claim a policy that is not in the KB.

Always greet the customer by name. Cite the KB doc when quoting policy. Wrap any tool call in a <thinking> block first. End every reply with a follow-up question or an escalation offer.

Ten lines, four parts, zero ambiguity. The agent's behavior in production is mostly the quality of this one block of text.
summary: Complete system prompt example weaving persona, capabilities, constraints, output rules into one production artifact.

text: Chapter 13.3 covers few-shot prompting and structured output for LLMs. Zero-shot describes the task in English; few-shot demonstrates it with 3-5 worked input-output pairs and reliably produces correctly-shaped output. JSON schemas with enums, required fields, and numeric ranges enforce structure that examples alone cannot guarantee. Together, few-shot teaches values and style while the schema locks structure - zero drift across calls. Production rules: 3-5 examples, diverse not duplicate, edge-case-heavy, span all enum values, hardest case last. The full ticket-classifier template (system + three examples + schema + ticket slot) is reusable, auditable, and tunable without retraining.
summary: Few-shot examples teach values and style; JSON schemas lock structure; together they produce drift-free classification.
queries: How to design a production LLM classifier
Best techniques for reliable LLM output

text: Zero-shot prompts describe the task. Few-shot prompts demonstrate the task with 3 to 5 worked examples first. The model is far better at pattern-matching examples than parsing English instructions. Zero-shot example: 'Classify this ticket as billing, product, or troubleshooting: "Charged twice for last month."' Output is unpredictable prose like 'This looks like a billing issue, possibly a duplicate charge...' - wrong shape. Few-shot example: three labeled pairs (Input: 'I can't log in.' -> billing, Input: 'Charged twice.' -> billing, Input: '500 errors.' -> troubleshooting) then the query. Output snaps to one token: 'billing'. Same model, same task, but examples raise classification accuracy from wobbly to reliable. Showing beats telling.
summary: Few-shot prompting beats zero-shot because the model pattern-matches examples better than it parses instructions.
queries: Why does few-shot work better than zero-shot
How to make LLM output a single label
Why does the model output prose instead of a label
How to constrain LLM output to one word

text: The anatomy of a few-shot block: a list of input-output pairs in identical format. The format is the lesson - the model learns the shape from these three, then applies it to the real query. Three examples, all identical format: Example 1 - Input: 'I can't log in - reset my password.' Output: Category: billing | Urgency: low. Example 2 - Input: 'Charged twice for last month.' Output: Category: billing | Urgency: high. Example 3 - Input: 'Dashboard shows 500 errors.' Output: Category: troubleshooting | Urgency: medium. Three categories (billing, product, troubleshooting) and three urgency levels (low, medium, high). Every example uses the same labels in the same slots. Consistency is the whole point.
summary: A few-shot block is an identically-formatted list of input-output pairs that teaches the model the response shape.
queries: What does a few-shot example look like
Why use the same format for all examples
Categories and urgency labels in ticket classification
Should examples follow the same format
How does the model learn the shape from examples

summary: A JSON schema constrains LLM output to a fixed shape via enums, required fields, and numeric ranges.
queries: How to force JSON output from an LLM
What does enum do in a schema
Why use required fields in JSON schema
How to constrain confidence to 0 to 1
Locking LLM responses to a shape

text: Few-shot and structured output do different jobs. Examples teach VALUES and STYLE. Schemas enforce STRUCTURE. Run them together and the model produces the same shape with the same vocabulary every single call. Few-shot teaches values + style: which categories exist, what urgency means here, how a typical ticket maps to a label. Schema enforces structure: field names, allowed enum values, types, and required keys; API rejects anything that doesn't match. Together: zero drift - same shape, consistent labels, every single call. Safe to feed straight into a database or downstream tool. Example output: { "category": "billing", "urgency": "high", "confidence": 0.85 }. Belt and suspenders - each technique catches a different failure mode, so together they leave almost nothing to chance.
summary: Combining few-shot examples with a JSON schema gives zero drift: examples teach the vocabulary, schema enforces the structure.
queries: What does few-shot teach vs schema
Why use both examples and schemas
How to make LLM output reliable for downstream tools
Style vs structure in LLM output
Examples teach values schema enforces structure

text: Five production rules for few-shot. Tip 1: Use 3 to 5 examples - more than 5 often hurts more than it helps, diminishing returns set in fast. Tip 2: Diverse examples beat similar ones - three near-duplicates teach the model one pattern, three different cases teach three. Tip 3: Edge cases improve robustness - a tricky example moves the needle more than a typical one, include the cases that break naive rules. Tip 4: Span all enum values - every category and urgency level should appear at least once across your examples. Tip 5: Put the hardest case last - the last example sets the strongest pattern, end on the case you most want the model to remember. Diverse, edge-case-heavy, enum-spanning, hardest-last. The ordering rule is the one teams forget most - and it matters more than the count of examples.
summary: Five production rules: 3-5 examples, diverse, edge-case-heavy, span all enums, put the hardest case last.
queries: How many few-shot examples should I use
Should examples cover all enum values
Why put the hardest example last
How does example order affect LLM accuracy

text:
All the pieces stitched into one prompt - system line on top, three few-shot examples in the middle, output schema, then the ticket slot. Template:
SYSTEM:
You classify support tickets for Alice's customer-support team. Return JSON only, matching the provided schema.

EXAMPLES:
Input:  "I can't log in - reset my password."
Output: { "category": "billing",         "urgency": "low",    "confidence": 0.91 }

Input:  "Charged twice for last month."
Output: { "category": "billing",         "urgency": "high",   "confidence": 0.95 }

Input:  "Dashboard shows 500 errors."
Output: { "category": "troubleshooting", "urgency": "medium", "confidence": 0.88 }

OUTPUT SCHEMA:
{
  "category":   enum["billing", "product", "troubleshooting"],
  "urgency":    enum["low", "medium", "high"],
  "confidence": number in [0, 1]
}

TICKET TO CLASSIFY:
"{{ ticket_text }}"
Three properties: Reusable - one template, thousands of tickets per day, identical output shape. Auditable - every field is logged, you can review wrong calls and tune examples. Tunable - swap one example, redeploy, measure, no model retraining required.
summary: Complete ticket-classifier template with system, three few-shot examples, output schema, and ticket slot - reusable, auditable, tunable.
queries: System prompt plus few-shot plus schema
How to assemble a production prompt
Prompt slot for variable ticket text
Combining system examples schema in one prompt

text: Chapter 13.4 teaches Chain of Thought (CoT) prompting and Self-Consistency. CoT forces the model to show step-by-step reasoning instead of jumping to a final answer, exposing math and recovering from slips. Three flavors: zero-shot CoT (append 'Let's think step by step' or 'Think carefully before answering. Show your work.'), few-shot CoT (examples include the reasoning trace, not just the answer), and self-consistency (sample N runs at temperature 0.7, take majority vote). Tradeoffs: direct = 1x tokens, CoT alone = 5x tokens and +15% on math, self-consistency N=5 = 25x tokens for +20% on math. Use CoT for multi-step arithmetic, multi-hop reasoning, ambiguous questions. Skip CoT for simple classification, lookups, single-fact Q&A. Reasoning models (Section 10.4) do CoT internally so the trigger phrase becomes redundant.
summary: Overview of CoT and Self-Consistency: techniques, tradeoffs, and when to use each.
queries: what is chain of thought prompting
when to use chain of thought

text: Reasoning In The Open. Some questions look simple but hide multi-step math. Asking for the final number alone gives the model nowhere to recover when it slips. Chain of thought forces the work onto the page - and the work is what catches the mistake. Worked example: Ticket T4: customer on annual plan cancels in month 7. Annual plan is $120/year. Cancellation fee is 10%. What is the refund? Direct Answer (WRONG): $45, no reasoning shown, the model guessed and stopped. Chain Of Thought (CORRECT): 1. Annual plan = $120 for 12 months. 2. 5 months remain (12 minus 7 used). 3. Prorated refund = 120 x 5 / 12 = $50. 4. Cancellation fee = 10% of $50 = $5. 5. Final refund = $50 minus $5 = $45. Exposes the math so the answer can be checked and corrected. Same model, same question - the only difference is whether the model shows its work. CoT exposed the prorating and the fee; the direct call had nothing to anchor to.
summary: Direct-answer vs CoT compared on a prorated refund problem with 10% cancellation fee.
queries: direct answer vs chain of thought
example of chain of thought reasoning

text: The "Let's Think Step By Step" Trick. Zero-shot CoT is the cheapest reasoning upgrade you can buy: one extra sentence on the user turn. No examples, no system-prompt changes. Two phrases work reliably. Classic Trigger: 'User: Ticket T4 refund - annual plan cancelled month 7. What is the refund? User: Let's think step by step.' Stronger Trigger: 'User: Ticket T4 refund - annual plan cancelled month 7. What is the refund? User: Think carefully before answering. Show your work.' Zero examples, just a trigger phrase. The model has seen so much 'step by step' reasoning in training that this single phrase flips it into reasoning mode for free.
summary: Zero-shot CoT: append a trigger phrase like 'Let's think step by step' to unlock reasoning.
queries: how to enable CoT without examples
does step by step actually work

text: Show The Reasoning In Examples. Few-shot CoT raises the bar. Each example includes the reasoning trace, not just the answer. This transfers the reasoning STYLE, not just the answer format - so the model imitates how you think, not just what you output. Example 1: Question: Ticket T1: annual plan $120, cancelled month 3. No fee. What is the refund? Reasoning: 1. 9 months remain out of 12. 2. Prorated refund = 120 x 9 / 12 = $90. 3. No fee applied. Answer: $90. Example 2: Question: Ticket T4: annual plan $120, cancelled month 7. 10% cancellation fee. What is the refund? Reasoning: 1. 5 months remain out of 12. 2. Prorated refund = 120 x 5 / 12 = $50. 3. Fee = 10% of $50 = $5. Answer: $45. The example does the work. The model now knows: list months remaining, prorate, apply the fee, then state the answer. Few-shot CoT teaches that exact chain.
summary: Few-shot CoT: examples include the full reasoning trace so the model imitates the thinking style.
queries: examples that teach chain of thought

text: Run N Times, Vote. Self-consistency is a simple trick: ask the same CoT question N times with temperature 0.7, collect the answers, and take the majority vote. Variance shrinks. Wrong runs get out-voted. Question: Ticket T4 refund - annual plan $120, cancelled month 7, 10% fee. Sample 5 times with temperature = 0.7. Run 1: Prorated $50 minus 10% fee = $45 (correct). Run 2: 5/12 x 120 = $50. Fee $5. Final $45 (correct). Run 3: Refund $50 prorated. Forgot the fee. Answer $50 (wrong). Run 4: $50 prorated, $5 fee, $45 refund (correct). Run 5: Months left 5. Refund $50. Fee 10%. Answer $45 (correct). Majority Vote: $45 x 4 vs $50 x 1. Winner: $45. One run can slip and forget the fee. Five runs disagree, the correct answer wins by majority. Variance collapses without retraining anything.
summary: Self-consistency samples N CoT runs at temperature 0.7 and uses majority vote to outvote slips.

text: What CoT Buys And What It Costs. Reasoning isn't free. CoT generates extra tokens. Self-consistency multiplies that by N. The accuracy gain is real but the bill is real too. Tradeoff table: Direct Answer - Tokens: 1x, Latency: 1x, Accuracy: Baseline. CoT Alone - Tokens: 5x, Latency: 5x, Accuracy: +15% on math. Self-Consistency (N=5) - Tokens: 25x, Latency: 5x (parallel) to 25x (serial), Accuracy: +20% on math. Self-consistency tradeoff: 5x to 25x the cost for ~5% extra accuracy over CoT alone. Run samples in parallel and latency drops back to 5x. Cost stays at 25x. Only worth it when accuracy is critical - billing, refunds, dosage, legal answers.
summary: Cost / latency / accuracy tradeoffs across direct answers, CoT alone, and self-consistency N=5.
queries: how expensive is chain of thought

text: When Chain-Of-Thought Wins. CoT is a Decision Matrix problem. The technique helps when there is real reasoning to do. It hurts when the task is a lookup or a one-step classification - extra tokens, extra latency, no payoff. USE COT (Reasoning Required): Multi-Step Arithmetic - refunds, prorating, tax breakdowns, discount stacking - any math with two or more steps. Multi-Hop Reasoning - questions that chain facts: who did X for the customer that signed up in 2023 and is in tier B? Ambiguous Questions - cases where the user's intent isn't obvious; reasoning surfaces the assumption. SKIP COT (Direct Answer Is Better): Simple Classification - map ticket text to one of three labels. Few-shot already nails it. CoT just adds latency. Lookup Tasks - fetch the customer's email or plan. No reasoning needed - it's a retrieval. Single-Fact Q&A - what time does support open? CoT will hallucinate a derivation where a direct answer suffices. Reasoning Models Change The Calculus: reasoning models like Section 10.4 Thinking do CoT internally before replying. You no longer prompt for 'step by step' - the model thinks first, then emits the answer. CoT prompts still help on non-reasoning models, but with a reasoning model the technique often becomes redundant. Match the technique to the task.
summary: Decision matrix for when to use CoT vs skip it, plus how reasoning models change the calculus.
queries: when to use chain of thought

Markers place each approach by how much new capability it unlocks: Prompting sits near the bottom (trigger existing skill), RAG sits middle-low (knows the skill, missing the facts), Agent sits middle-high (knows the skill, missing the actions), Fine-Tuning sits at the top (skill never seen in training).

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Chapter 13.5 frames Prompting, Fine-Tuning, RAG, and Agent as four distinct tools chosen by four axes: data freshness (does the data change?), capability gap (is the skill missing?), latency budget, and cost budget. A decision tree walks the axes top-down: model knows it -> Prompt, knowledge must stay current -> RAG, specialized skill missing -> Fine-Tune, actions via tools needed -> Agent. Production systems usually stack all four, and four anti-patterns recur when teams pick a favorite tool instead of walking the axes (fine-tuning for fresh facts, RAG for missing behavior, agent loop for simple lookup, massive prompts instead of small fine-tuned models).
summary: Chapter-level overview of how to choose between prompting, fine-tuning, RAG, and agents using four axes, a decision tree, stacking, and known anti-patterns.

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Four tools in the toolbox for shipping any customer-support feature: Prompting (changes nothing in model or data - write better messages, 1 LLM call), Fine-Tuning (model weights change, data baked in via training on new behavior examples, 1 LLM call to specialized model), RAG (data retrieved at call-time via vector search pulling fresh chunks into prompt, 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call), and Agent (model uses tools in a loop, decides what to do, calls tool, reads result, loops until done, multiple LLM calls + tool calls). They aren't competitors - they solve different problems.
summary: Introduces the four approaches (Prompting, Fine-Tuning, RAG, Agent) by what each changes and what each costs at inference time.
queries: what are the four ways to customize an LLM
prompting vs fine-tuning vs RAG vs agent difference

how many LLM calls does an agent make
what is RAG at inference time

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Data freshness is the first filter for choosing an approach. If the answer depends on facts that move - prices, inventory, the ticket the user just filed - those facts cannot live inside model weights. Freshness axis from Never to Real-Time: Fine-Tuning = Never to weekly (retraining is slow and expensive), Prompting = Never (whatever you wrote into the prompt is frozen), RAG = Hourly (re-index docs and the model sees them next call), Agent = Real-time per query (tools hit live APIs at the moment of the question). Rule of thumb: data that moves fast belongs OUTSIDE the model. Prompt and weights are frozen between calls; retrieval and tools are not.
summary: Compares the four approaches on data freshness, showing fine-tune and prompt are frozen while RAG and agents stay current.
queries: RAG vs fine tuning for current data
why fine tuning is bad for changing data
when does data move outside model
freshness axis prompt RAG fine tune agent

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Capability gap is the second axis: does the model already know how to do this, or is the skill itself missing? Prompting works at the bottom - already in base model, trigger skill that's already there, cheapest path. RAG sits middle-low - knows the skill, missing the facts (reading-comprehension is in the model, your private docs are not). Agent sits middle-high - knows the skill, missing the actions (reasoning is in the model, side-effects need tools). Fine-Tuning sits at the top - specialized skill never seen (style, domain jargon, structured output the base model can't imitate). RAG fills factual gaps. Agent fills action gaps. Fine-tuning fills skill gaps. Prompting works only when the skill is already inside the model.
summary: Maps the four approaches to the type of gap they fill: facts (RAG), actions (Agent), skills (Fine-Tune), or none (Prompt).
queries: what gap does each approach fill
when do I need fine tuning vs RAG
does the base model know the skill
reading comprehension is in the model
when to fine tune a model
action gap vs fact gap vs skill gap

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Latency and cost are the third and fourth axes - real-time chat has a latency budget, batch jobs have a cost budget. Four quadrants: Low latency + Low cost = Prompting (1 call, small prompt, cheapest and fastest). Low latency + Higher upfront cost = Fine-Tuning (training expensive, inference after is short prompt + 1 call). Higher latency + Low cost = RAG (vector search before the call, tokens stay small, freshness is free). Highest latency + Variable cost = Agent (each loop is another call, cost scales with tool steps). Latency order: Prompting ~ Fine-Tune < RAG << Agent. Cost per call: same order, but Agent loops can balloon. Budgets break ties - if two approaches both work technically, the one fitting latency floor and cost ceiling wins.
summary: Frames latency and cost as the tie-breaking axes, placing each approach in a latency/cost quadrant.

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Decision tree to combine the axes: walk top to bottom, first Yes is the answer (or starting point if you need to stack). Question 1: Does the model already know it well enough? Yes -> Prompting alone. Question 2: Knowledge needs to stay current? Yes -> RAG. Question 3: Need a specialized skill the model doesn't have? Yes -> Fine-Tuning (plus RAG if data also moves). Question 4: Need to take actions in the world via tools? Yes -> Agent. Need to take actions via tools always points at an Agent. It's a decision tree not a flowchart - each leaf is a single technique. If two questions answer Yes, combining them is the next step.
summary: Provides a four-question decision tree that maps to the right technique starting with prompting and ending with agents.

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Most production systems stack the techniques rather than picking one - each layer covers what the layer below cannot. Real customer-support assistant stack: Layer 1 - Fine-Tuned Base Model (teaches the support voice and refund-policy JSON shape). Layer 2 - + RAG Layer (pulls today's order history, today's billing record, today's playbook). Layer 3 - + Agent Loop (reads tickets, issues refunds, sends emails - real side-effects). Layer 4 - + Prompting / Few-Shot Per Task (system prompt + examples shape each individual call). Stack pattern: fine-tune + RAG + agent loop + prompting combine into one system. Knowing each layer's job is how you debug the stack when something breaks.
summary: Shows the production stacking pattern where fine-tune, RAG, agent loop, and prompting layer together in one system.
queries: do production systems use one technique

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Four common anti-patterns where teams pick the wrong tool for an axis they didn't think about. Anti-pattern 1: Fine-Tuning To Add Fresh Facts - retraining every time the pricing page changes. Fix: Use RAG; facts that move belong outside the weights. Anti-pattern 2: RAG For Behavior The Model Doesn't Know - stuffing style guides into the prompt and hoping the tone sticks. Fix: Fine-tune; behavior lives in weights, not retrieved chunks. Anti-pattern 3: Agent Loop For A Simple Lookup - multi-step planning to answer 'what's the refund window?'. Fix: Direct call + 1 tool; loops are for tasks that branch. Anti-pattern 4: Massive Prompt Instead Of Fine-Tuning A Small Model - sending 30 examples on every call to a frontier model. Fix: Fine-tune a small model once; cost runaway stops, latency drops. Pattern under every misuse: someone picked their favorite tool instead of walking the four axes.
summary: Lists four anti-patterns where the wrong technique is chosen and gives the correct technique for each.
queries: anti patterns RAG fine tuning agent

text: Context engineering is the discipline of packing the right tokens into the model's fixed window. Four moves drive every decision: pick the layers (system, few-shot, history, retrieved, user), set the order (top-to-bottom, recency at the bottom), allocate the budget (trim history vs trim retrieval vs cram), and evict in the right order when it overflows (older chunks first, system prompt and last user message never). Models also forget the middle - the U-shaped lost-in-the-middle recall curve means critical info goes at the start or end, never buried. Same model, same query, different context engineering produces wildly different answers.
summary: Chapter-level summary of context engineering: layer selection, ordering, budget allocation, eviction, and avoiding the middle.
queries: what is context engineering in LLMs
how to design a prompt for fixed context window
prompt design tradeoffs for long context
what are the levers of context engineering
how does context engineering affect answers

text: Every model has a context window: a hard token budget the call has to live inside. A frontier model today is 128k tokens. Every prompt is a packing problem against that ceiling. The 128k window is broken into reserved zones: System Prompt (~4k, rules + persona, always present), Conversation History (~50k, prior turns, grows every turn), Retrieved Docs (~69k, RAG chunks, refilled per query), User Input (~1k, the new question, capped), Reserved For Output (~4k, headroom the model writes into). Anything past 128k gets dropped or summarized. Context engineering is the meta-skill of deciding what survives the cap on every single call.
summary: Introduces the 128k context window as a hard token budget split into five reserved zones that the prompt must fit inside.
queries: how big is the context window of a frontier LLM

how much room does conversation history take in context

text: The model reads top-to-bottom. Order is not cosmetic - it changes what the model attends to. The assembly stack is the same every call, with five layers in order: 1) System Prompt - persona, rules, tone, refusal policy, read first and anchors everything below. 2) Few-Shot Examples - 2 to 5 input + output pairs that pin the format, read before any real content. 3) Conversation History - prior turns of the thread, older turns nearest the top of the section. 4) Retrieved Chunks - RAG hits for this query, reranked closer-to-bottom = closer-to-query attention. 5) User Query - the thing to actually answer, sits at the very bottom for maximum recency. Recency = bottom. Whatever you want the model to weight most goes nearest the user query.
summary: Lays out the five-layer prompt assembly stack and explains why the user query sits at the bottom for recency.
queries: what order do prompt layers go in

text: 128k is a fixed pie. Every call is a tradeoff between more retrieval, more history, or more output. Three strategies for the same 128k budget: Cram Everything (System 4k + History raw 50k + Retrieved top-20 70k + User 1k + Output 3k) - at-risk, lost-in-middle kicks in. Trim History (System 4k + History summary 8k + Retrieved 80k + User 1k + Output 35k) - wins for fact-heavy tickets, keeps room for retrieval. Trim Retrieval (System 4k + History raw 50k + Retrieved top-3 8k + User 1k + Output 65k) - wins for long multi-turn convos, keeps room for output. Rule: summarize what you can compress, evict what you cannot, never starve the output. The trim and summarize moves are the two levers; which one wins depends on the ticket type.
summary: Compares three concrete ways to allocate a fixed 128k context budget across system, history, retrieval, user, and output.
queries: how to allocate context window tokens

text: Even with a 128k window, the model does not attend evenly. Recall drops in the middle and spikes at the start and end. This is the lost-in-the-middle effect, and it is the single biggest reason long prompts fail. Recall accuracy samples by position in the context: position 0 (Start) ~93%, 15% ~78%, 30% ~60%, 45% ~48%, 50% (Middle) ~45%, 55% ~48%, 70% ~62%, 85% ~82%, 100% (End) ~95%. The curve is U-shaped with peaks at both ends and a trough near the middle. Rule: put the most important info at the START or END of context, never in the middle. Buried in the middle is buried full stop. If a fact has to land, place it next to the user query or inside the system prompt - those are the two recall peaks.
summary: Describes the U-shaped lost-in-the-middle recall curve and the rule to place critical info at the start or end of context.

text: Inside the retrieved-chunks and history layers, ordering is its own choice. Two strategies cover the common cases. Relevance-First: use for factual queries and one-shot lookups. Rule - rerank retrieved chunks by similarity, top-3 sit closest to the user query. Example - ticket 'What's the refund window for plan Pro?' -> top-3 RAG hits next to the query. Recency-First: use for multi-turn conversations and long debugging threads. Rule - latest history at the bottom near the query for conversational coherence. Example - 6-turn back-and-forth -> last 2 user/assistant turns sit nearest the query. Factual lookup -> relevance-first. Long conversation -> recency-first. Hybrid -> rerank then recency-bias the last 2 turns. Same chunks, different order, different answer. Order is a free knob.
summary: Contrasts relevance-first ordering for factual lookups with recency-first ordering for multi-turn conversations.
queries: relevance first vs recency first ordering

text: When the packed prompt exceeds 128k, something has to leave. Eviction is not random - there is a ladder. Drop things whose loss costs the least, keep things whose loss breaks the call. Eviction ladder from cheapest loss to most critical: #1 Older Retrieved Chunks Beyond Top-3 (Evict First) - top-3 carry the answer almost always. #2 Mid-Conversation History Raw Turns (Summarize Then Evict) - compress middle 5+ turns into one summary line, keep newest 2 raw. #3 Few-Shot Examples Beyond The First Two (Evict Soon) - two examples pin the format, examples 3-5 are insurance. #4 Top-3 Retrieved Chunks (Rarely Evict) - these ARE the answer to this query. #5 Latest Assistant Response (Rarely Evict) - removing it loses the model's own context. #6 System Prompt + Last User Message (Never Evict) - the rules and the question itself; evicting either breaks the call. Summarize before you evict.
summary: Describes a six-tier eviction ladder for trimming over-budget prompts, ordered from cheapest loss to never-evict.
queries: how to trim a prompt that exceeds context window

Right box Result in green: Flows back to the model. Arrow from Model to Runtime labeled "Tool_use". Arrow from Runtime to Result labeled "Execute". Dashed loop-back arrow from Result back to Model labeled "Return: tool_result becomes the next input". Title: One tool call: Decide -> Execute -> Return. Shows the critical separation: model emits the tool_use block, runtime is the only place real code runs, result flows back as the next conversation input.

text: Triangular agent-loop diagram. Three circular nodes forming a triangle: Reason at the top in cyan (Which tool?), Act at the bottom-right in purple (Call tool), Observe at the bottom-left in green (Read result). Curved arrows form a clockwise cycle: Reason -> Act -> Observe -> Reason. Center label reads "Loop until done" in purple. Visualizes that an agent is just a single tool call (the atom) put in a loop until the model decides it has enough information to answer.

text: Chapter 13.7 introduces tool use as the bridge between a bare LLM and a working agent. A bare LLM only knows its training data; tools let it call real functions to fetch live data and trigger real-world side effects. A tool is a function with three parts the model reads every turn: name (identifier it emits), description (plain-English contract for WHEN to call), and parameters (typed arguments). Two roles stay strictly separated: model decides which tool and what arguments, runtime executes the real function. Model halts at tool_use, runtime validates and executes, then appends a tool_result that the model reads on the next turn. A single tool call is the atom; an agent is that atom in a Reason -> Act -> Observe loop that runs until done. Chapter establishes a canonical 8-tool support inventory (search_kb, lookup_customer, lookup_subscription, reset_password, change_email, process_refund, escalate_human, send_email), split into 3 read-only and 5 side-effectful, used throughout the rest of Act 2.
summary: Tools are functions the model decides to call and the runtime executes, looped via reason-act-observe to turn a chatty LLM into an agent.
queries: what is tool use in AI agents
how does an LLM become an agent
how do tools work in LLMs overview
tool use as bridge to real world

text: Pure LLM vs LLM with tools. Bare LLM only knows training data. Support agent needs live KB lookups, customer records, real refunds - text generation alone insufficient. Tools let model reach real world. Ticket T5: "What's the difference between Pro and Enterprise?" Pure LLM mode: relies on training data, plans may be outdated, may hallucinate. Flow: User asks T5 -> LLM guesses from memory -> returns possibly-stale answer. Answer: "Pro has more features than Enterprise, I think..." Verdict risky: facts wrong, no source. LLM + Tools mode: calls search_kb to fetch live comparison doc, answers from source. Flow: User asks T5 -> LLM calls search_kb('Pro vs Enterprise') -> KB returns current doc -> LLM answers from doc. Answer: "Pro: 10 seats, $49/mo, email support. Enterprise: unlimited seats, SSO, dedicated CSM." Verdict grounded: cites live docs, traceable to source. Same model, same prompt - only difference: one can call search_kb. That hook is difference between chatty box and working agent.
summary: Bare LLMs guess from stale training data; tools let them call live functions like search_kb so answers come from real sources.
queries: what's the difference between an LLM and an agent

text: A tool is a function the model can call. Exactly one thing: a function. You declare its signature. Model decides when to call it. Your runtime executes the function for real. Result flows back into conversation. Example signature: lookup_customer(email: string) -> Customer. Three-box flow: Model decides WHEN to call -> Runtime executes the real function -> Result flows back to model. Arrows labeled tool_use, Execute, Return: tool_result becomes the next input. Model = brain. Runtime = hands. Brain never touches the database directly. Critical separation: LLM never executes code itself - only emits a tool_use block. Your runtime does the actual call. This boundary is what makes tool use safe enough to ship.
summary: A tool is a function: the model decides when to call it, the runtime executes it, and the result returns to the model.

text: Name, Description, Parameters - three parts of every tool definition. Model reads all three at every turn and uses them to decide whether to call - and how. Part 1 Name: process_refund. The identifier the model emits when it decides to call this tool. Part 2 Description: "Refund a paid invoice. Refunds over $200 require human approval." Plain-English contract. This is what the model reads to decide WHEN to call. Part 3 Parameters: { invoice_id: string, reason: string }. The arguments the model must produce. Validated before runtime executes. Description is the most important field. Spend most editing time there. Model has never seen your codebase - description is its only manual. Vague description = model guesses; specific description = model rarely misfires.
summary: Every tool has three fields - name, description, parameters - and the description is what teaches the model when to call.

text: The model decides, the runtime executes. Two distinct roles. Model in charge of decisions - which tool, what arguments. Code in charge of side effects - reading DB, charging card, sending email. Never mix them. Step 1 User: sends message "Refund my last invoice, the charge was wrong." Step 2 Model Reads: compares message to every tool's description, looks for match. Step 3 Model Decides: thinks "User asked for refund - I should call process_refund." Or chooses NOT to call any tool. Step 4 Model Emits Tool_Use: generation halts. Response is tool_use block: { name: 'process_refund', input: {...} }. Step 5 Runtime Executes: your code sees block, validates inputs, calls real function, gets result. Step 6 Result Returns: runtime appends tool_result message and re-invokes model so it can continue. Model can also choose NOT to call any tool and just answer - valid outcome. Step 4: model halts at tool_use. It does not execute anything itself. Runtime is single chokepoint where every real-world side effect happens - exactly where you place auth, logging, rate limits.
summary: Walks through six steps: user asks, model reads tools, decides, emits a tool_use block, runtime executes, result returns - keeping decisions and side effects separated.
queries: where do side effects happen in agents

text: One call, or a loop of calls? Single tool call is the atom. Agent is what you get when you put that atom in a loop: reason, act, observe, then reason again with new evidence. Reason -> Act -> Observe -> Reason again. Reason: read messages so far, decide do I need a tool and which one? Act: emit a tool_use block, runtime calls function for real. Observe: read the tool_result, did it succeed, was info enough to answer? Triangular loop diagram: Reason top, Act bottom-right, Observe bottom-left, arrows clockwise, loop until done. Single tool call = atom. Agent loop = molecule. Formalized in 13.20. One ticket might be single search_kb call and done. Another might need lookup_customer, then lookup_subscription, then process_refund - three turns of loop before model finally answers.
summary: An agent is just a single tool call wrapped in a reason-act-observe loop that keeps cycling until the model decides it's done.
queries: difference between tool call and agent

text: The 8 tools our support agent has. Fixed inventory for rest of section. Every example uses these eight tools. search_kb(query) cyan - search customer support knowledge base. lookup_customer(email) blue - find customer by email address. lookup_subscription(customer_id) purple - get current plan and status for customer. reset_password(customer_id) indigo - send password-reset email to customer. change_email(customer_id, new_email) teal - update email on record. process_refund(invoice_id, reason) orange - refund paid invoice. Refunds over $200 require escalation. escalate_human(transcript, urgency) red - hand off conversation to human agent. send_email(template_id, vars) green - send templated email to customer. 8 tools total: 3 read-only (search, lookup, lookup), 5 side-effectful (reset, change, refund, escalate, send). Read-only tools cheap to retry; side-effectful tools must be guarded with auth, validation, approvals. process_refund is canary - any refund over $200 escalates instead of executing.
summary: Introduces the canonical 8-tool support-agent inventory split into 3 read-only and 5 side-effectful tools used throughout Act 2.

text: Chapter 13.8 teaches JSON Schema for tool definitions. A tool schema has four parts: name, description, input_schema (JSON-Schema object with properties), and required list. The model only ever sees the schema, never the implementation, so the schema is the contract. Mark only truly necessary fields required (e.g. invoice_id for process_refund, reason optional). Use enum to lock parameters to fixed values (urgency: low/medium/high) and format hints (format: email) to constrain shape. The description field is the single most important text - five rules: state what it does, state side effects, state when NOT to use, match user vocabulary, mention constraints/limits. Bad: "Refund function." Good: "Refund a paid invoice. Use only when customer confirmed cancellation. Refunds over $200 require human approval - call escalate_human instead." Reference shape: lookup_customer (read-only, safe to retry, email format).
summary: JSON Schema tool definition: name, description, input_schema, required - with enum, format, and rich descriptions as the model's contract.

text: Descriptions Are Read By The Model. The description field is plain language for the model. It is the most important text in your whole tool definition. Treat it like a manual entry the model will skim every turn. Five rules turn a bad description into a great one. RULE 1: State what the tool does in plain language - one sentence the model can match against the user's request. RULE 2: State the side effects - does it mutate state? Send email? Cost money? The model needs to know before calling. RULE 3: State when NOT to use the tool - negative examples prevent over-eager calls; "Do not use for refunds over $200" saves real dollars. RULE 4: Use the same vocabulary the user would use - if the user says cancel, do not name the tool terminate_subscription; match the word. RULE 5: Mention any constraints - rate limits, dollar thresholds, retry semantics, idempotency - put them all in the description. Description is the model's manual. Write it like you would write a runbook for a new hire. Spend ten times longer on the description than on the parameter names. A great description prevents wrong-tool calls, prevents the model from inventing arguments, and tells the model exactly when to call escalate_human instead of process_refund.
summary: Five rules for writing tool descriptions: state action, state side effects, state when NOT to use, match user vocabulary, mention constraints.

text: One Sentence Changes Everything. Same tool, same parameters. The only difference between a refund that goes wrong and one that goes right is the description string. BAD (Vague): "description": "Refund function." Why bad: No side effects mentioned. No dollar threshold. No when-not-to-use. The model will call it for anything that sounds refund-shaped. GOOD (Specific): "description": "Refund a paid invoice. Use only when the customer has confirmed cancellation. Refunds over $200 require human approval - call escalate_human instead for those." Why good: What it does. When to use. When NOT to use. The $200 threshold. The fallback tool. The model now has a real contract. Bad description: model refunds a $500 invoice. Good description: model calls escalate_human instead. The vague version costs you real dollars in mis-refunds. The specific version threads the $200 ceiling and the escalation fallback right into the model's prompt - so the model is choosing escalate_human before it ever attempts the refund.
summary: Concrete comparison: vague description leads model to over-refund; specific description with $200 cap and escalate_human fallback routes safely.

text: Six-row swim-lane diagram for the tool-call lifecycle showing the message kinds exchanged between User, Model, Runtime, and Tool actors. Row 1: User emits a user message (the request, e.g., "I can't log in - reset my password"). Row 2: Model emits a tool_use block (name + input). Row 3: Runtime emits a function call (the real invocation of the registered handler). Row 4: Tool emits a function return (the value or error from the side-effectful operation). Row 5: Runtime emits a tool_result block (wrapping the return for the model). Row 6: Model emits the final assistant message (the answer to the user). Each row pairs an actor on the left with the message kind on the right, and dashed down-arrows connect each step to the next, showing strict sequential flow.

text: Horizontal timeline diagram showing how tool calls interleave with a streaming assistant response. Time flows left to right across seven labeled phases: (1) Stream begins - assistant tokens start arriving. (2) Partial text streams - words appear one at a time. (3) Stream pauses - model decides to call a tool. (4) tool_use block emitted whole - arrives as a complete JSON object, not token-by-token. (5) Runtime executes - real function runs (200ms to 2s). (6) tool_result delivered - result appended, model re-invoked. (7) Stream resumes - new assistant tokens flow to build the final answer. Each phase is a colored segment along a single horizontal bar with numbered ticks underneath, emphasizing that text streams incrementally but tool blocks arrive atomically.
summary: Timeline diagram showing seven phases of a streaming response with an interleaved tool call: text streams incrementally, then pauses for an atomic tool_use, then runtime execution, tool_result, and stream resume.

text: Chapter 13.9 - Tool Call Lifecycle. One tool call passes through six actors (User, Model, Runtime, Tool, Runtime, Model) and is carried by two message kinds: tool_use (type, id, name, input) emitted whole by the model when it halts generation, and tool_result (type, tool_use_id, content, is_error) appended by the runtime after executing the real function. The runtime is the single chokepoint for all real-world side effects; the model never executes code itself. Ticket T1 (password reset) demonstrates the full loop: lookup_customer first to get customer_id c-9924, then reset_password using that id, then a final assistant message - 2 tool calls and 3 LLM turns. When streaming, assistant text arrives token-by-token but tool_use blocks arrive complete; the stream pauses while the runtime executes, then resumes. The latency budget for T1 totals 3.4s: 88% LLM calls (3.0s across three turns), 12% tool execution (0.4s). The model is the bottleneck - cutting LLM turns saves more than cutting tool latency.
summary: Tool calls flow through six actors via tool_use and tool_result messages, with the runtime as the side-effect chokepoint, streams pausing for whole tool blocks, and LLM turns dominating the 3.4s latency budget.
queries: summary of tool call lifecycle chapter

text: From request to final answer: one tool call passes through six actors. The user starts it, the model decides, the runtime executes, the tool returns, the runtime wraps the result, the model writes the answer. Every arrow is a message of a specific kind. The Tool-Call Swim-Lane has six rows: (1) User sends a user message like "I can't log in - reset my password." (2) Model reads context, picks a tool, emits a tool_use block with name and input. (3) Runtime validates the tool_use, looks up the real function, calls it with the input. (4) Tool runs the real side effect (DB, email, refund) and returns a value or an error. (5) Runtime wraps the return in a tool_result message and appends it to the conversation. (6) Model re-reads the conversation, sees the result, writes the final answer for the user. Six actors. Two messages (tool_use, tool_result) carry the call from model to runtime and back. The model never touches the tool directly - it only emits tool_use blocks. The runtime is the single boundary where every real-world effect (DB read, email send, refund) actually happens.
summary: The tool-call lifecycle passes through six actors via tool_use and tool_result messages, with the runtime as the single chokepoint for real-world side effects.

text: Tool-Use: model asks runtime to call. When the model decides to call a tool, generation halts and it emits a single tool_use block with four load-bearing fields. Example tool_use message: {"type": "tool_use", "id": "toolu_01abc...", "name": "reset_password", "input": {"customer_id": "c-9924"}}. Field roles: type is always the literal string "tool_use" - tells the runtime this block is a call request. id is unique per call - the matching tool_result must carry this same id back. name says which tool to call - must match one of the schema names you registered. input is the arguments - validated against the input_schema before the runtime executes. tool_use = type + id + name + input. That is the whole contract. The customer_id c-9924 inside input came from a previous tool_result in this same conversation - the model carries values forward between turns just like it carries words.
summary: A tool_use block has four fields (type, id, name, input) and is the model's request for the runtime to execute a named tool with given arguments.
queries: what fields does a tool_use block have
what is the id in tool_use for
how does the model request a tool call
how do tool_use and tool_result pair up
what does the model emit when calling a tool
JSON shape of a tool call request
is tool_use streamed token by token
what halts generation when a tool is called
how does the model pass arguments to a tool

text: Tool-Result: runtime returns the outcome. After the runtime executes the real function it wraps the return into a tool_result block and appends it to the conversation. The model then re-reads everything (user, prior assistant messages, tool_use, tool_result) and continues. Example tool_result message: {"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": "toolu_01abc...", "content": "Password reset email sent to alice@example.com.", "is_error": false}. Field roles: type is always "tool_result" - tells the model this block carries a return value. tool_use_id MUST match the id from the tool_use - this is how the model pairs request to response. content is the return value, usually a string or structured data - this is what the model reads next. is_error is a flag - true means the runtime failed, the model should recover instead of pretending it worked. tool_use_id pairs the response to the request. Same id in, same id out. If the tool failed (DB down, validation error, network blip), the runtime still produces a tool_result but flips is_error to true. The model reads the failure and adapts instead of assuming success.
summary: A tool_result block carries the runtime's return value back to the model, paired to the original tool_use by tool_use_id, with an is_error flag for failures.
queries: what fields does a tool_result block have

text: Trace: Ticket T1 password reset, end to end. The user says "I can't log in - reset my password." - plain English, no customer_id, just intent. Step 2 Model emits tool_use {name: lookup_customer, input: {email: "alice@example.com"}}; the email came from the conversation header, the model needs customer_id before it can reset. Step 3 Runtime returns tool_result {content: {customer_id: "c-9924", plan: "Pro"}} - DB hit. Step 4 Model emits second tool_use {name: reset_password, input: {customer_id: "c-9924"}}, now using c-9924 from step 3 - second turn of the loop. Step 5 Runtime returns tool_result {content: "Email sent."} - side effect happens here, the runtime is the single chokepoint. Step 6 Model writes final assistant message: "Done - check alice@example.com for the reset link." - no more tool_use, the model is satisfied. T1: 2 tool calls (lookup_customer + reset_password), 3 LLM turns, 1 final answer. The model never has c-9924 in step 2 - it only learns the id from step 3's tool_result. That is why the loop matters: each turn unlocks the next call by giving the model new evidence.
summary: Ticket T1 password reset trace shows 2 tool calls (lookup_customer then reset_password) and 3 LLM turns, with each tool_result giving the model the evidence needed for the next turn.

text: Tools while streaming. In production you usually stream assistant tokens to the UI for that typewriter effect. Tool calls fit cleanly inside a stream - but with one twist: text streams token by token; tool blocks arrive whole. Stream timeline phases: (1) Stream begins - assistant tokens start arriving one by one. (2) Partial text streams - "Let me look that up for you..." appears word by word. (3) Stream pauses - model decides to call a tool, token stream halts. (4) tool_use block emitted - arrives as one complete JSON object, NOT token-by-token. (5) Runtime executes - your code runs the real function, could take 200ms or 2s. (6) tool_result delivered - result message appended; model re-invoked to continue. (7) Stream resumes - new assistant tokens start flowing again, building the final answer. Tool blocks aren't streamed token-by-token; they arrive complete. Your UI shows partial text as it streams but holds the spinner during tool execution. When the tool_result lands and the stream resumes, the next batch of assistant tokens picks up where it left off and writes the answer using the result.
summary: Tool calls interleave with streaming assistant tokens: text streams token by token but tool_use blocks arrive whole, then runtime executes and the stream resumes.
queries: how do tool calls work with streaming
are tool_use blocks streamed token by token
stream pause when calling a tool
phases of a streamed tool call

text: Where the seconds go: T1 latency budget. Ticket T1 took 3.4 seconds end-to-end. The budget breakdown: LLM call 1 (reads user, emits lookup_customer) = 1200ms. lookup_customer tool execution = 200ms. LLM call 2 (reads result, emits reset_password) = 1100ms. reset_password tool execution = 200ms. LLM call 3 (reads result, emits final answer) = 700ms. Total = 3400ms = 3.4s. LLM calls = 3.0s (88%). Tool calls = 0.4s (12%). The model is the bottleneck. Every extra LLM call adds about 1 second. Speeding up T1 means cutting LLM turns, not tool latency. Parallel tool calls (next chapter) let you fire lookup_customer and lookup_subscription in one turn instead of two - shaving a whole LLM call from the budget.
summary: T1's 3.4-second latency is 88% LLM calls and 12% tool execution, so optimization targets cutting LLM turns rather than tool latency.

text: Chapter 13.10 covers parallel tool calls and tool_choice. The model can emit multiple tool_use blocks in a single assistant turn when calls are independent (don't read each other's output), letting the runtime fan them out concurrently to cut both tool latency and LLM turns. Dependent calls must serialize across turns. tool_choice has four modes: auto (default - model decides), required (must call something), none (must not call), and specific (force one named tool). Ticket T2 shows parallel lookups + serial mutations; ticket T4 shows 31% latency savings (6.8s -> 4.7s) from collapsing three independent lookups into one turn.
summary: Chapter overview: parallel tool calls speed up independent lookups by collapsing turns, while tool_choice controls whether and which tools the model can call.
queries: how to optimize agent latency with parallel tools
when to use parallel tool calls

text: Ticket T2: "Reset my password but my email also changed to new@example.com". Two independent lookups can run serial or parallel. Serial path: LLM call 1 (1200ms), lookup_customer (200ms), LLM call 2 (1200ms), lookup_subscription (200ms). Total 400ms tool work + 2 LLM turns. Parallel path: LLM call 1 (1200ms), then lookup_customer AND lookup_subscription concurrent (both 200ms). Total 200ms tool work + 1 LLM turn. Same two tool calls, but parallel cuts an entire LLM turn and halves the tool latency. Parallel only works when calls do not read each other's output.
summary: Compares serial vs parallel execution of two independent tool calls on ticket T2, showing parallel halves tool latency and saves an LLM turn.
queries: serial vs parallel tool calls latency

ticket T2 password reset email change example

text: Rule: independent calls go parallel, dependent calls go serial. Independent example: lookup_customer(email) and lookup_subscription(email) - neither reads the other's output, both take email and return a record. Verdict: parallel. Both tool_use blocks emitted in SAME assistant turn; runtime fans out, awaits both, returns both tool_results. Dependent example: lookup_customer(email) then process_refund(customer_id, ...) - process_refund needs customer_id from lookup_customer's result. Verdict: serial. Two assistant turns required: turn 1 = lookup, turn 2 = refund after tool_result appended. The rule: parallelize when calls don't read each other's outputs.
summary: Defines the parallel-vs-serial decision rule: independent calls parallelize in one turn, dependent calls require sequential turns.
queries: when can tool calls be parallel

text: tool_choice: "auto" is the default. Model decides whether to call a tool, which one, and how many. Scenarios under auto: (1) Ticket T5 "Pro vs Enterprise - what's different?" -> model picks search_kb(query="Pro vs Enterprise") since it's a knowledge question. (2) User: "Hey, good morning!" -> no tool, plain assistant reply, chitchat doesn't warrant a tool. (3) Ticket T2 "Reset my password and email also changed." -> lookup_customer AND lookup_subscription, two tool_use blocks in one turn - auto can pick more than one. Auto covers chitchat, single lookups, and parallel lookups from one config. Switch modes only when you need to control the model's hand.
summary: Explains tool_choice auto mode where the model decides whether and how many tools to call, with concrete chitchat, single, and multi-tool scenarios.

text: Four tool_choice modes. (1) auto - config: tool_choice: "auto". Model decides whether to call a tool, and which one(s). Use as default for most flows. (2) required - config: tool_choice: "required". Model MUST call at least one tool; plain-text replies blocked. Use when intent already routed (e.g., user clicked "Run a search"). (3) none - config: tool_choice: "none". Model MUST NOT call any tool; can only emit plain text. Use for final summarization, drafting from gathered context, or pure chitchat. (4) specific - config: tool_choice: { type: "tool", name: "search_kb" }. Force a specific tool; model can ONLY call this one named tool. Use for deterministic routing where your classifier already decided the API; model fills in arguments.
summary: Lists the four tool_choice modes (auto, required, none, specific) with config syntax and when to use each.
queries: tool_choice required vs none vs auto

text: Ticket T4: "Cancel my subscription, refund this month, and have someone call me." Three independent lookups (customer, subscription, refund policy) feed one mutation (process_refund). Serial (4 turns): LLM 1500ms, lookup_customer 200ms, LLM 1500ms, lookup_subscription 200ms, LLM 1500ms, lookup_refund_policy 200ms, LLM 1500ms, process_refund 200ms. Total = 6.8s, 4 LLM calls. Parallel (2 turns): LLM 1500ms, then lookup_customer + lookup_subscription + lookup_refund_policy concurrent (200ms), LLM 1500ms, process_refund 200ms, LLM 1300ms. Total = 4.7s, 3 LLM calls. Savings: 6.8s -> 4.7s = 31% faster, one fewer LLM call. Each LLM turn ~1.5s.
summary: Quantifies parallel savings on ticket T4: collapsing three lookups into one turn cuts total latency from 6.8s to 4.7s (31% faster).
queries: how much does parallel tool calling save

Step 4: Model turn 2 emits tool_use change_email(customer_id=c-9924, new_email=new@example.com) - must complete before reset_password so link goes to NEW email. Step 5: Runtime returns tool_result "Email updated." Step 6: Model turn 3 emits tool_use reset_password(customer_id=c-9924). Step 7: Runtime returns tool_result "Reset email sent." Step 8: Model turn 4: "Updated your email to new@example.com and sent a reset link there." First turn parallel; subsequent turns sequential because change_email must complete before reset_password.
