text: Validation Gate diagram: tool call flow with three stages. Top: Model box (cyan) emits tool_use block. Arrow down to middle: Runtime: Schema Validation box (green) - check types, required, enums, formats. From validation node two arrows branch. Left branch labeled Invalid leads to Return Malformed Error box (purple) - is_error: true, error_class: "malformed". Right branch labeled Valid leads to Execute Tool Function box (cyan) - HTTP call, DB write, etc. (expensive). Footer caption: Cheap validation saves expensive function calls. The diagram shows schema validation as the gate between the model's emitted tool_use and the real tool function, with invalid arguments returning a malformed error immediately and valid arguments proceeding to the expensive execution.
summary: SVG diagram illustrating the validation gate: tool_use arguments are schema-checked, then either returned as a malformed error or passed through to execute the real tool function.
queries: diagram of malformed error vs execute
tool_use to execute or error diagram

text: Chapter 13.11 Tool Errors, Retries, Validation. Production tool calls fail in four classes: transient (retry with exponential backoff), permanent (do not retry, surface), malformed (return schema error, let model fix arguments), business-rule (return policy reason, let model pick alternate tool or escalate). Errors are returned as structured data on the tool_result block with is_error: true and error_class field - never thrown as exceptions - so the model can route on them like any other field. Retry policy is per-class: transient gets 3 retries with 200ms/400ms/800ms backoff; permanent gets 0; malformed gets 1; business-rule gets 0. The runtime validates arguments against JSON schema before executing the tool, catching most malformed errors in microseconds. Mutations must accept an idempotency key derived from a stable input (invoice_id, request_id, UUID) so retries dedupe instead of double-spending. Ticket T4 trace shows model switching from process_refund to escalate_human in response to a business_rule error without any special-case code.
summary: Errors return as structured tool_result data classified into transient, permanent, malformed, or business-rule, and the retry policy, schema validation, and idempotency keys are all dictated by the class.
queries: summary of tool errors retries validation
how to handle tool failures in agents
errors as data in agent loops

text: Production tools fail. Retry strategy depends on WHY. Group every failure into four classes: Transient (rate limit HTTP 429, network timeout, upstream 503) - retry with exponential backoff, the call would probably succeed if tried again in a moment. Permanent (auth failed HTTP 401, customer not found, missing record) - surface to user, do not retry, the state not the network is the problem. Malformed (schema validation failed, missing required field, wrong type) - return error to model so it self-corrects with new arguments next turn. Business-rule (refund $350 > $200 auto-approve cap, blocked customer, outside policy window) - return structured rule-violated reason, model adapts or escalates. Each error carries error_class field: "transient", "permanent", "malformed", "business_rule".
summary: Tool failures sort into four classes - transient, permanent, malformed, business-rule - each demanding a different handling strategy.
queries: what are the four types of tool errors
how to classify tool call failures
what is a business rule error in agents

text: Runtime does not throw exception up the stack. It returns structured tool_result with is_error: true and error_class field. Model reads same fields it reads on success and adapts. Example tool_result message: {"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": "toolu_01xyz...", "content": "Refund denied: amount $350 exceeds $200 auto-approve cap.", "is_error": true, "error_class": "business_rule"}. is_error true means treat content as failure reason not answer. error_class tells model whether to wait/retry, fix arguments, escalate, or apologize. $350 over $200 cap = structured business_rule error, not stack trace. Because error is data, model routes on it - next assistant turn picks escalate_human on its own. You did not write "if business_rule then escalate" - model inferred from message.
summary: Tool errors return as structured tool_result blocks with is_error and error_class so the model can read and adapt without special exception code.
queries: how do tool errors return to the model
how does model handle tool failure

text: One retry policy across all errors is wrong. Retrying permanent auth failure burns three round-trips for nothing. Not retrying transient 503 fails a good call. Class drives retry budget, backoff schedule, who handles. Transient: retries 3, exponential backoff 200ms, 400ms, 800ms - brief downtime usually clears in under a second. Permanent: retries 0, surface immediately, no backoff - re-asking same impossible question wastes turns and tokens. Malformed: retries 1, return schema error to model, then escalate - model fixes own arguments on second try ~95% of time. Business-rule: retries 0, return policy reason, model picks alternate tool or escalates - retrying never flips a policy. Exponential backoff matters because thundering-herd retries amplify outages.
summary: Retry budget and backoff schedule must be matched to error class, not applied uniformly across all failure types.
queries: how many retries per error class
do not retry business rule violations

text: Most malformed errors never need to hit tool function. Runtime already has JSON schema from tool definition - it checks model's arguments against required fields, types, enums, formats in microseconds before opening HTTP socket or starting transaction. Validation gate flow: Step 1 Model emits tool_use (assistant turn includes tool_use block with input arguments). Step 2 Runtime validates against schema (check required fields, types, enums, formats - cheap and instant). Step 3 Branch on result: if invalid return malformed error immediately with is_error: true, error_class: "malformed"; if valid execute tool function (HTTP call, DB write - expensive). Cheap validation (microseconds) gates expensive function calls (hundreds of milliseconds). Schema-level validation gives model precise reason like "missing required field invoice_id" - much easier to fix than generic 400 from API. One retry usually does it.
summary: The runtime validates tool arguments against the JSON schema before executing, returning malformed errors instantly without paying for the real function call.
queries: why validate tool arguments before executing
cheap validation vs expensive function call

text: Reads are naturally safe to retry - they return same data. Mutations are different. process_refund without idempotency key can double-refund if runtime retries on flaky 503 that actually succeeded. Without Idempotency Key: signature process_refund(invoice_id, reason). Runtime retries on 503 but first call succeeded - customer gets refunded TWICE, $700 charged back instead of $350. Unsafe to retry. Never auto-retry a mutation without an idempotency contract. With Idempotency Key: signature process_refund(invoice_id, reason, idempotency_key). Key = hash(invoice_id), backend stores it, dedupes any retry. Second call returns SAME refund_id as first. Customer charged once. Safe to retry. Derive key from stable input (invoice_id, request_id, client UUID) so retries collide cleanly. For password resets use request_id; for new orders use client-generated UUID.
summary: Mutations need idempotency keys derived from stable inputs so that automatic retries dedupe instead of double-spending.
queries: how to safely retry a mutation
why retries are unsafe without idempotency
client UUID for new order requests

text: Ticket T4 end-to-end trace with business-rule error and recovery. User: "Cancel my subscription and refund my last invoice ($350)." Step 2 Model turn 1 emits tool_use lookup_customer(email). Step 3 Runtime returns tool_result customer_id: c-7741, is_error: false. Step 4 Model turn 2 emits tool_use process_refund(invoice_id: inv-882, reason: customer requested) - model attempts refund directly, $350 over $200 auto-approve cap but model does not know yet. Step 5 Runtime returns tool_result is_error: true, error_class: "business_rule", content: "Refund $350 exceeds $200 auto-approve cap." No retry - structured reason is signal model uses to adapt. Step 6 Model turn 3 adapts: emits tool_use escalate_human(customer_id: c-7741, reason: refund $350 over policy cap, urgency: medium). Step 7 Runtime returns tool_result "Escalation queued: ticket-9921 assigned to billing-team." Step 8 Model turn 4 final reply: "I've escalated your refund to a human agent who can approve the $350. You'll hear back within one business day." User never sees raw policy error - sees calm escalation message.
summary: A trace of ticket T4 shows the model receiving a business_rule error and autonomously switching from process_refund to escalate_human without any special-case routing code.
queries: full trace of business rule error recovery

text: Chapter 13.12 Why Protocols. Ad-hoc agent-to-tool wiring scales as M times N, so 5 agents and 6 tools already need 30 bespoke integrations. A shared protocol hub turns the math into M + N - 11 clean lines instead of 30 - and adding any new component costs only 1 connector. The three protocols to know are MCP (agent to tools, resources, prompts; Anthropic-led; production), A2A (agent to agent; Google-driven; newer), and HTTP + OpenAPI (legacy general-purpose transport). Adopt a protocol once you cross roughly 3 tools and 3 agents. Beyond reducing wiring, a protocol is a sandbox contract: it is the only legal channel between the trusted host (user data, model) and the untrusted third-party tool server, and it defines what may pass.
summary: Protocols solve agent integration sprawl by turning M times N wiring into M plus N, with MCP, A2A, and OpenAPI as the main options and a sandbox contract as the safety benefit.

text: Ad-hoc tool wiring does not scale. Every agent needs to call every tool, and without a shared protocol you write a custom connector per pair. With 5 agents and 6 tools that is 30 bespoke integrations. Each new tool forces every agent to learn it; each new agent forces every tool's API to be re-wrapped. The connector count grows as M times N - five times six is the small case and already looks like a tangle.
summary: Without a shared protocol, connecting M agents to N tools requires M times N hand-wired integrations that grow unmanageably.

text: A protocol is a shared contract that sits in the middle. Each agent speaks it once; each tool speaks it once. Same 5 agents, same 6 tools, but now 5 + 6 = 11 clean lines instead of 30. The hub-and-spoke shape turns the system additive instead of multiplicative. Add a 6th agent and you get 1 new connector. Add a 7th tool and you get 1 new connector. No agent rewrites a tool API ever again. The math turns from O(M times N) to O(M + N).
summary: A shared protocol hub reduces M times N connectors to M + N by giving every agent and every tool one common contract to speak.

text: Three protocols to know. MCP (Model Context Protocol): Anthropic-led open protocol; connects agent host to tools, resources, and prompts; production today; dominant pattern for production agent tooling. A2A (Agent To Agent): Google-driven open spec; connects agent to agent; lets a planner agent delegate to specialist agents over a shared message envelope; newer. HTTP + OpenAPI: the legacy stack the rest of the internet speaks; service to service; agents can call these but the host has to translate every endpoint into a tool definition by hand. MCP standardizes the agent-to-tool edge. A2A standardizes the agent-to-agent edge. OpenAPI is general-purpose HTTP transport.
summary: MCP standardizes agent-to-tool, A2A standardizes agent-to-agent, and HTTP plus OpenAPI is the legacy general-purpose stack.
queries: which protocol is production ready for agents
is OpenAPI used for agent tools
when to use MCP vs A2A

text: Decision grid for when protocols pay off. Few Tools / Few Agents (1-2 each): ad-hoc is fine, skip protocol. Many Tools / Few Agents: standardize on the tool side so new tools self-describe to the same agent. Few Tools / Many Agents: standardize on the agent side so every agent reads the same tool catalog. Many Tools / Many Agents: full protocol mesh - both sides must converge on the same wire format or every release introduces O(M times N) integration work. Decision rule: cross 3 + 3 (3 tools and 3 agents) and you want a protocol. The exact threshold depends on rate of change - if new tools land monthly you cross sooner.
summary: Use the 2x2 grid of tool count by agent count; the 3 tools plus 3 agents threshold is the typical production sign that a protocol is needed.

when is ad-hoc tool wiring still ok

at what scale do agents need a standard wire format
decision framework for adopting protocol layer

text: A protocol also enforces a trust boundary. Inside the host: user data, the model, and the mutations you are willing to authorize. Outside: tool server - third-party code you do not control. The protocol is the only channel that crosses the boundary. It dictates what may pass: the server lists capabilities, the host authorizes tool calls, results return through the same channel. Nothing else gets through. That sandbox boundary is what makes it safe to plug a third-party tool server into an agent that holds user data.
summary: The protocol acts as a sandbox contract - the only legal crossing between a trusted host with user data and an untrusted third-party tool server.

text: Chapter 13.13 covers MCP architecture: three roles (Host, Client, Server) where the host owns the model and trust boundary, clients are in-process adapters (one per server), and servers are separate processes exposing tools/resources/prompts. Typical topology is hub-shaped - one host with many clients each to its own server, merged into one model-visible catalog. Three transports share the same JSON-RPC wire format: stdio (local child process), HTTP (remote request/response), SSE (streaming over HTTP). Session lifecycle is fixed: launch client, connect, initialize handshake (exchange version and capabilities), list (discover tools/resources/prompts), then call. Discovery is plug-and-play: tools/list returns a catalog of {name, description, input_schema} entries the host gives the model as tool definitions, letting agents pick up new capabilities without host code changes.
summary: MCP architecture: three roles (host/client/server), hub topology, three transports (stdio/HTTP/SSE) over JSON-RPC, fixed session lifecycle, and plug-and-play discovery via tools/list.

text: MCP splits the agent stack into three roles. Host is the application the user opens (Claude Desktop, IDE, custom agent app); it owns the model, UI, and user data. Client is a connection living inside the host, one per server it wants to talk to; speaks the MCP wire protocol over a chosen transport. Server is a separate process or remote service that exposes tools, resources, and prompts. Three roles, one wire format. Host always owns the trust boundary; server always sits on the other side; client is the in-process adapter that connects them.
summary: MCP architecture has three roles: Host (user app), Client (in-process connection adapter), Server (external process exposing tools/resources/prompts).

text: Typical MCP topology is a hub: one host launches many clients, one per server it wants to talk to. Each client maintains its own connection. The host stitches all the listed tools into a single catalog the model can pick from. Example: 1 host hosting 3 clients, each wired to a different server (Postgres tool, Linear tool, Filesystem tool). The host is the hub - it adds or drops servers without the servers ever talking to each other. The Postgres server has no idea the Linear server is also connected, and the model sees one merged toolset from all three.
summary: Standard MCP topology is one host with many clients, each connected to one server; host merges all tools into a single catalog the model picks from.

text: MCP wire format is the same JSON-RPC regardless of how the bytes travel. Three transports in common use. Stdio: local only. Host launches the server as a child process and pipes JSON-RPC over stdin/stdout. Fastest, no network. Used by Claude Desktop and local MCP servers. HTTP: network. Standard request/response over HTTPS. Supports remote servers across the internet. Needs auth headers and TLS like any web API. SSE (Server-Sent Events): network plus streaming. One-way streaming channel from server to host on top of HTTP. Lets the server push intermediate updates without polling. Used when the server needs to push partial results, progress, or long-running notifications. Pick stdio for same-machine zero network surface, HTTP for hosted servers, SSE on top of HTTP when streaming progress matters.
summary: MCP supports three transports - stdio (local child process), HTTP (remote request/response), SSE (HTTP plus streaming) - all using the same JSON-RPC wire format.

text: MCP session follows a fixed lifecycle: connect, negotiate, discover, call. Five steps: (1) Host launches client - host creates a client connection per server. (2) Client connects to server - stdio child process or HTTP/SSE socket opens. (3) Initialize handshake - both sides exchange protocol version and capabilities. (4) List available tools/resources/prompts - server tells the host what it can do. (5) Call a tool, get a result - host invokes a named capability and receives the response. Lifecycle matters because host cannot call a tool blind. It must finish Initialize Handshake (so both sides agree on protocol version) then call list before it knows any tool names. Only after list returns does the model see a populated tool catalog.
summary: MCP session lifecycle has five steps: host launches client, client connects to server, initialize handshake, list capabilities, then call tools.

text: Discovery is what makes MCP plug-and-play. Host sends a tools/list request; server responds with the full catalog: each tool's name, human-readable description, and JSON schema for its input. Host hands this list to the model as the tool definitions for the next call. Example tools/list response: { "tools": [ { "name": "search_kb", "description": "Search the customer support knowledge base.", "input_schema": { "query": "string" } }, { "name": "lookup_customer", "description": "Look up customer by email.", "input_schema": { "email": "string" } } ] }. Host merges this list with other servers' catalogs and passes the combined set as the model's tool definitions on the next turn. That is how an agent built on MCP picks up new capabilities without code changes on the host.
summary: MCP tools/list response returns an array of tool entries with name, description, and input_schema; host merges across servers and passes them as model tool definitions.

text: Chapter 13.14 explains the three MCP primitives an MCP server exposes: Tools (callable actions the model invokes, with side effects), Resources (read-only data the host pulls in by URI), and Prompts (parameterized templates the user picks). Each primitive has a different access pattern - model picks tools, host pulls resources, user invokes prompts. Decision rule: Tool if side-effecting, Resource if read-only data, Prompt if reusable template.
summary: MCP servers expose three primitives - Tools (actions), Resources (data), Prompts (templates) - each with a distinct access pattern.
queries: tools vs resources vs prompts MCP
when to use tool vs resource MCP
action vs data vs template in MCP
what does an MCP server provide

text: An MCP server exposes three kinds of capabilities. Tools are things the model can do - callable actions like process_refund(invoice_id, reason). Resources are things the model can read - readable data addressed by URI like kb://articles/password-reset. Prompts are templates the host can offer - parameterized templates like summarize_ticket(ticket_id, max_words) that the user picks. Each solves a different need: Tool is action (model picks and calls), Resource is data (host pulls in and shows to model), Prompt is template (user picks, host fills slots). The split matters because each primitive has a different access pattern - the model decides when to call a tool, the host decides when to read a resource, the user picks which prompt to invoke.
summary: MCP servers expose Tools (actions), Resources (data), and Prompts (templates), each with a different access pattern - model, host, or user driven.
queries: what are the three MCP primitives
who decides when to call MCP tool
who decides when to read MCP resource
action vs data vs template MCP

text: A Tool is a named action with a typed input schema. It usually has side effects: mutates state, calls an external API, or spends money. The model picks one from the tool list and emits a tool call with the required arguments. Example: process_refund with inputSchema { invoice_id: string, reason: string }. Side effect: mutation - $-amount returned to the customer's card. The model chooses when to call. Tools always go through validation before the server actually runs the action. The inputSchema is how the host and the model know what arguments are required and what type each one must be.
summary: A Tool is a model-callable action with a typed input schema and side effects, like process_refund(invoice_id, reason) which refunds money.
queries: how does model call MCP tool

text: A Resource is read-only data the server exposes under a URI. The host fetches it on demand and feeds the content into the model's context, the same way you might attach a PDF in a chat. The model never calls a resource itself. Example URI: kb://articles/password-reset which resolves to the markdown body of the doc (read-only). The host decides when to read - e.g., before calling the model on a ticket. Resources are READ-ONLY by convention. The URI scheme is server-defined: it could be kb://, file://, github://, postgres://, or anything else. The host treats it as an opaque identifier and asks the server to resolve it when needed.
summary: A Resource is read-only data the server exposes under a server-defined URI like kb://articles/password-reset; the host fetches and injects it into the model's context.
queries: how does host fetch MCP resource
who reads MCP resource model or host
passing docs to model via MCP

text: A Prompt is a parameterized template the server publishes. The host surfaces these as slash-commands or quick actions for the user. When the user picks one, the host collects the arguments, fills the template, and sends the resulting message to the model. Example MCP Prompt shape: { name: "summarize_ticket", description: "Summarize a support ticket conversation.", arguments: [ { name: "ticket_id", required: true }, { name: "max_words", required: false } ] }. The arguments array lists each parameter with a required flag. ticket_id is required, max_words is optional. The host can either prompt the user for missing values or fill defaults before invoking.
summary: An MCP Prompt is a server-published parameterized template (e.g., summarize_ticket with ticket_id and max_words) that the host exposes as a slash-command for users.
queries: how does user invoke MCP prompt

text: When you design an MCP server, every capability falls into exactly one bucket. Decision rules: if you need to DO something (mutate state, call API) use a Tool. If you need to READ something (fact, doc, file) use a Resource. If you need a reusable prompt template the user can invoke use a Prompt. Decision rule summary: tool if side-effecting; resource if read-only data; prompt if template the user picks. Putting a read-only doc behind a tool wastes a model turn. Putting an action behind a resource hides it from the model entirely. Putting a one-shot question behind a prompt hides it from end users. The split is the contract.
summary: Decision rule for picking an MCP primitive: Tool for mutations/actions, Resource for read-only data, Prompt for user-invokable templates - mismatches waste turns or hide capabilities.

how to choose MCP capability type
tool vs resource vs prompt MCP design

text: An MCP server is mostly a sequence of declarations followed by a start. Five phases: (1) Define Identity (name, version), (2) Declare Tools (callable actions with schemas), (3) Declare Resources (URI patterns the host can read), (4) Declare Prompts (parameterized templates users can pick), (5) Start (begin listening on transport). The server itself is short; the handlers do the real work. Each declaration registers what the server offers; start opens the connection.

text: Tool registration example: Server.registerTool({ name: "search_kb", description: "Search the customer support knowledge base.", inputSchema: { query: "string" }, handler: async ({ query }) => { /* search logic */ } }). Registering a tool means giving it a name, a description the model will read, an input schema for arguments, and a handler that does the work on the server side. Name and description are what the model sees; inputSchema is the contract for the arguments. The handler runs server-side, NOT in the model's context. Everything but the handler is shipped to the host.
summary: Registering a search_kb tool needs a name, description, inputSchema, and a server-side handler.

text: Resource registration example: Server.registerResource({ uriPattern: "kb://articles/{id}", description: "Knowledge base article by ID.", handler: async ({ id }) => readMarkdown(id) }). Resources are addressed by URI patterns. The server registers a template like kb://articles/{id}; the host requests a concrete URI such as kb://articles/password-reset; the server matches the template, reads the markdown doc, and returns the body.
summary: Resources are registered with a URI pattern template and a handler that resolves concrete URIs.

text: Prompt registration example: Server.registerPrompt({ name: "summarize_ticket", description: "Summarize a support ticket.", arguments: [ { name: "ticket_id", required: true }, { name: "max_words", required: false } ], handler: async ({ ticket_id, max_words }) => buildPrompt(ticket_id, max_words) }). Registering a prompt means declaring a named template and the arguments it needs. The host collects argument values from the user, then asks the server to materialize the final prompt text. The server returns the resolved prompt string when the host invokes it with arguments.
summary: Prompts are registered with a name, arguments list, and a handler that builds the final prompt string.

text: Server lifecycle moves through four states: REGISTERED (definitions declared) then LISTENING (transport open) then ACTIVE (handling list and call requests) then CLOSED (shutdown). Transitions: connect() takes REGISTERED to LISTENING, initialize takes LISTENING to ACTIVE, shutdown() takes ACTIVE to CLOSED. Most of the lifetime is spent in ACTIVE, where the server handles list, call, read, and prompt-get requests from the host.
summary: Server lifecycle has four states (REGISTERED, LISTENING, ACTIVE, CLOSED) connected by connect, initialize, and shutdown transitions.

text: Test before connecting to a real LLM. Four strategies: (1) Test handler functions as plain unit tests - call them directly with sample inputs and assert the returned value. (2) Test list responses - assert all declared tools, resources, and prompts appear in the catalog. (3) Test transport connection - spin the server up over stdio or HTTP and assert the initialize handshake completes. (4) Use a mock host before connecting to a real LLM - drive list and call from a scripted host to catch contract bugs without paying for tokens. Production hygiene: test the server in isolation, then wire it to the host.
summary: Four testing strategies for MCP servers: unit-test handlers, assert list catalog, verify transport handshake, drive with a mock host.

text: Chapter 13.16 covers MCP security: an MCP server is third-party untrusted code, and the host must enforce a trust boundary. Five layers protect the user: (1) the host treats server code as untrusted by default, holding user data, the model, and mutations on its side; (2) the host launches the server in an isolated OS process sandbox with no shared memory, no filesystem access except granted directories, and no outbound network by default; (3) capability scope narrows what each host/agent can call via allowed-tools lists, resource URI prefix patterns like kb://articles/*, and a global read-only mode flag; (4) OAuth proves user consent for HTTP-transport servers via a four-step flow (user authorizes, host gets access token, token passes to server in each request, server checks scope) - stdio servers skip OAuth under local trust; (5) per-call consent prompts block side-effecting tools until the user approves, and an audit log records timestamp, tool, input, result, user, and decision for every call. Together these layers make it safe to plug third-party tool servers into agents holding sensitive user data.
summary: Five-layer MCP security model: untrusted server, OS sandbox, capability scope, OAuth, consent + audit.

text: Server code is untrusted by default. An MCP server is third-party code - it might be open source, vendor-supplied, or written by a teammate - but the host never assumes it is safe. The host holds the user data, the model, and every mutation worth caring about. The server holds the tool implementation. The boundary between them is what makes the architecture safe. A trust-boundary diagram shows an outer ring (Host - Trusted: User Data, Model, Allowed Mutations) surrounding an inner ring (Server - Untrusted: Third-Party Tool Code). Three protocol arrows cross the boundary: List (server -> host), Call (host -> server), and Result (server -> host). The MCP transport is the only legal crossing. The host owns user data and the model; the server runs third-party tool code; the protocol is the only thing the host has to police.
summary: Host treats every MCP server as untrusted third-party code; the protocol transport is the sole legal crossing of the trust boundary.

text: Sandbox: process isolation. The host launches the server in its own OS process. The host can spawn it, kill it, and shape what it sees, but it never shares memory with it. Whatever capability the server wants, it must ask for over the transport. Diagram: a Host Process box (Owns User Data + Model) at the top spawns - via a dashed Spawn arrow - a Server Process box (Third-Party Code) inside a dashed Sandbox container. Three sandbox rules: (1) Transport Only - the server communicates with the host only over the MCP transport, no back channel; (2) Filesystem Walled Off - no filesystem access except directories the host explicitly grants; (3) Network Off By Default - no outbound network unless the host opts in for this server. The sandbox is the OS-level fence: even if the tool code is buggy or hostile, it cannot reach beyond resources the host hands it.
summary: Host spawns server in an isolated OS process with no shared memory, no filesystem, and no network unless explicitly granted.

text: Capability scope per host. Even when a server declares ten tools and a hundred resources, the host can narrow what this particular agent is allowed to touch. Scope is a subset of what the server advertises, configured per host. Three scope dimensions: (1) ALLOWED TOOLS - a subset of declared tools the host permits; example: deny escalate_human for junior agents. (2) ALLOWED RESOURCES - prefix-matched URIs; example: allow kb://articles/* but not kb://internal/*. (3) ALLOWED MUTATION - a read-only mode flag that forces every tool call to be non-mutating regardless of declaration; example: read-only mode for evaluation runs. Capability scope is allow/deny lists at three levels: tool names, resource URI prefixes, and a global read-only flag.
summary: Host narrows server-declared capabilities via allowed-tools lists, resource URI prefix patterns, and a read-only mode flag.

text: OAuth: who granted what. When the server lives over HTTP, the host has to prove the user authorized it. OAuth is the standard answer: the user grants scopes once, the host exchanges that grant for an Access Token, and that token rides along on every server request. Four-step OAuth flow: (1) User Authorizes - user grants scopes; (2) Host Gets Access Token - host exchanges the grant; (3) Token Passes To Server - the access token rides along on each request; (4) Server Checks Scope - server validates the token before honoring the call. Pattern: Grant -> Token -> Request -> Check. The Access Token is the proof the server demands on every call. Stdio servers skip OAuth because they run under local trust; HTTP servers require it so the host can prove which user authorized the call.
summary: HTTP MCP servers use OAuth: user authorizes once, host gets an access token, token rides along on every request, server checks scope.

text: Consent + audit: user sees everything. Sandbox, scope, and OAuth handle the machine side. Consent and audit handle the human side. The host asks the user before any side-effecting call and writes every call to a log the user can review later. Consent prompts block the call until the user clicks Approve or Deny; example message: 'About to call process_refund / For invoice INV-9924 / Reason: "Customer Requested" / Allow? [Approve] [Deny]' - required for side-effecting tools. Audit log records every call with timestamp, tool, input, result, user, and the consent decision. Example rows: 14:02:11 lookup_customer {id:"C-104"} OK alice@co Auto; 14:02:34 process_refund {invoice:"INV-9924"} OK alice@co Approved; 14:03:02 send_email {to:"cust@x"} OK alice@co Approved; 14:05:50 delete_account {id:"C-104"} Blocked alice@co Denied. Sandbox keeps the server from reaching outside; scope keeps it from doing more than allowed; OAuth proves the user granted access; consent and audit keep the user in control of every effect.
summary: Per-call consent prompts block side-effecting tools until the user approves, and the audit log records timestamp, tool, input, result, user, and decision.

text: Chapter 13.17 introduces A2A (Agent-to-Agent), a protocol that complements MCP by letting one agent delegate an entire sub-problem to a peer agent that runs its own loop, model, memory, and tools. Agents publish an agent.json discovery card (name, description, skills, endpoint, auth) so peers can find them by skill. A delegation follows five steps: classify, lookup by skill, send request with full context, peer works, peer returns result. Because delegated tasks can take minutes or need approval, A2A streams intermediate updates back to the caller so progress is visible without polling. Decision rule: MCP Tool for a function call, MCP Resource for a doc, A2A when the work needs an entire other agent.
summary: A2A delegates whole sub-problems to peer agents via discovery cards, skill lookup, contextful requests, and streamed progress updates.
queries: What is A2A in one paragraph?

text: MCP and A2A solve different directions of agent communication. MCP handles agent-to-tool: an agent reaches out to a tool and gets a value back. A2A handles agent-to-agent: an agent hands an entire problem to a different agent and lets that agent run its own loop with its own model, memory, and tools. With MCP the agent stays in charge and just borrows a tool. With A2A the agent hands the wheel to a peer that runs its own loop to finish the job.
summary: Introduces A2A as agent-to-agent delegation, contrasted with MCP's agent-to-tool function calls.

text: Before one agent can delegate to another, it has to find a peer with the right skill. A2A solves discovery with a small JSON document each agent publishes at a known URL (agent.json - the agent's card). Example shape: { "name": "billing-specialist-v2", "description": "Handles billing-related support tickets including refunds, downgrades, and invoice disputes.", "skills": ["billing", "refund", "invoice"], "endpoint": "https://agents.example.com/billing", "auth": { "type": "oauth2" } }. Name identifies the agent. Description and skills are what other agents read to decide if this peer can help. Endpoint is the URL to call. Auth tells the caller how to prove they were granted access. Other agents discover this and decide whether to delegate.
summary: Agents publish an agent.json discovery card with name, description, skills, endpoint, and auth so peers can find them.

text: A delegated task is not a single function call. The receiving agent may need minutes, human approval, or several tool calls of its own. A2A solves this by letting the receiving agent stream intermediate updates back instead of going silent until the final result is ready. Example stream from billing agent: "Looked up customer...", "Processing refund...", "Refund > $200, need approval", "Final result returned". Streaming lets triage show the user progress in real time and react if an intermediate update needs human approval before the long task finishes. Triage stays informed without polling.
summary: A2A supports streaming intermediate updates from the delegated agent back to the caller so long tasks show progress and can pause for approval.

text: MCP and A2A live side by side, not in competition. The decision is about what you need from the other side: a function, a document, or an entire collaborator. Decision rules: Need to call a function? -> MCP Tool. Need to read a doc? -> MCP Resource. Need an entire other agent to handle a sub-problem? -> A2A Delegation. A2A is heavier than MCP. Use it when the delegated work needs the receiving agent's own loop, memory, and tools - not just a single function call.
summary: Decision framework: MCP Tool for function calls, MCP Resource for docs, A2A for delegating a whole sub-problem to another agent.

text: Chapter 13.18 contrasts two control-flow shapes for LLM systems. A workflow is a fixed DAG you wire in advance: the model is a smart cell, steps are known, output types predictable, cost and latency bounded, failures loud. An agent is an open loop where the model is the conductor, choosing the next tool each iteration: steps unknown in advance, count varies (2 calls or 20), cost and latency variable, failures soft. Most production systems are hybrid: a deterministic outer DAG (Classify -> Handle -> Reply -> Log) wraps a single agent step where creativity is needed. Decision rule: workflow when you can, agent when you must. The asymmetry of cost (one extra workflow node vs unbounded extra agent iterations) is why workflow is the default.
summary: Workflow is a fixed DAG with bounded cost; agent is an open loop with variable cost; production systems hybridize them with an agent step inside a deterministic skeleton.
queries: fixed DAG vs open loop summary
when to choose workflow vs agent

text: A workflow is a fixed DAG where you wire the steps in advance and the model follows the wires. An agent is an open loop where the model picks the next step every iteration. Same building blocks, very different control flow. Workflow side: fixed DAG (A -> B -> C -> D), the flow is the same every run. Agent side: a central Decide node with branching options (Tool 1, Tool 2, Tool 3, Tool 4) and a self-loop, where different runs take different paths. Choose the shape that matches how much variance the task actually has.
summary: Introduces workflow as a fixed DAG and agent as an open loop where the model picks the next move each iteration.

text: Workflow is the right shape when you already know what to do. The model is a smart cell inside a bigger pipeline you wrote, not the conductor. Pick this when the task is repeatable and the steps are knowable in advance. Pick workflow when: You can list the steps in advance; each step's output type is predictable; you need to guarantee certain steps execute; the cost / latency must be bounded. Example: when a ticket comes in: Classify -> Route -> Handle. Always those 3 steps. The model still calls tools and writes text. But you, not the model, decide which step runs in which order. The output of each step is predictable enough to wire to the next.
summary: Explains when to pick a workflow: steps known in advance, predictable outputs, bounded cost, and guaranteed step execution.
queries: when should I use a workflow
when to pick workflow over agent
what tasks are good for workflows

text: Agent is the right shape when you can't pre-draw the graph. The model is the conductor: it decides which tool to call, in what order, and when to stop. You pay for that flexibility with cost and unpredictability. Pick agent when: You don't know the steps in advance; the model needs to decide which tools to call and in what order; the task could take 2 calls or 20; you're willing to pay for flexibility. Example: research-style task like "summarize all customer issues from the past week." Agent decides which docs to read, which to skip, and when it has enough. One ticket might need 2 tool calls. The next might need 20. You can't draw that graph in advance because the shape of the task only becomes visible after the model starts digging.
summary: Explains when to pick an agent: unknown steps, model decides tool order, variable iteration count, willing to pay for flexibility.
queries: when should I use an agent
when to pick agent over workflow
what tasks need an agent loop
when graph cannot be drawn in advance

text: Most production systems are hybrid. Real systems are rarely pure. The common pattern is a workflow with one agent step inside. You keep the outer skeleton deterministic and let the model loose only inside the one node that actually needs creativity. Hybrid topology: outer DAG with four nodes - Classify (deterministic), Handle (agent step, drawn as a circle with a self-loop and a question mark), Reply (deterministic), Log (deterministic). The Classify step is deterministic. The Handle step is an agent. Reply and Log are deterministic again. This is the hybrid shape almost every production agent ships with. You get the safety and predictability of a workflow around the parts you can fully spec, and the freedom of an agent only where you actually need it.
summary: Shows the hybrid pattern: a deterministic outer DAG with one agent loop step inside, the default shape in production.
queries: what is a hybrid agent workflow
deterministic outer DAG agent inner node
how do real production agents look

text: Predictable cost vs variable cost. The biggest practical difference between workflow and agent is the shape of failure. Workflow failures are loud and bounded. Agent failures are quiet and unbounded - the agent keeps trying and the bill keeps climbing. Workflow (bounded and predictable): Cost = fixed per run. Latency = bounded by slowest step. Failure = hard crash, loud error. Agent (variable and soft-failing): Cost = variable, depends on iterations. Latency = variable, 2 calls or 20. Failure = soft-fail, gets stuck instead of crashing. Decision rule: Workflow when you can; agent when you must. The cost of an extra workflow node is one more tool call. The cost of an extra agent iteration is an unbounded number of tool calls. That asymmetry is why teams default to workflow and reach for an agent only on the steps that genuinely need it.
summary: Compares cost, latency, and failure modes of workflows vs agents and gives the decision rule: workflow when you can, agent when you must.
queries: hard crash vs soft fail agent
when must you use an agent

text: Workflows are built from three composable primitives: chaining wires step A's structured output into step B's input, routing classifies the input and dispatches to one of N branches via an LLM-based classifier, and parallelization fans out N concurrent workers and merges their results via an aggregator. Real production pipelines stack all three: chain into a classifier, route to a branch, parallelize heavy retrieval inside the branch, then chain the merged result into a final answer. The example support workflow shows each primitive at the level it fits: routing at the intent layer, chaining for the billing branch, parallelization for troubleshooting search_kb hypotheses.
summary: Chapter overview of the three workflow primitives chain, route, parallelize and how they stack in real pipelines.

text: Workflows are built from three composable primitives. Chaining wires step A's output into step B. Routing classifies the input and picks one branch. Parallelization fans out N workers and merges their results. Every production workflow stacks these three. Chain: A -> B -> C, sequential steps. Route: classifier picks one of N branches. Parallel: fan-out plus merge, concurrent workers. These are the lego bricks. Chaining is a sequence. Routing is a fork. Parallelization is a fan-out plus merge. Real workflows stack them in layers.
summary: Introduces the three composable workflow primitives chain, route, parallelize as the lego bricks of every production workflow.
queries: what are the three workflow primitives

text: Chaining: step A's output is step B's input. The structured output of one LLM call becomes the structured input of the next. The glue is JSON. Step A returns a typed object, your code reads it, and feeds the next step. Example chain Ticket -> Classifier -> Handler with JSON edge labels: {"text": "can't bill"} flows into Classifier which emits {"category": "billing"} flowing into Handler. Concrete shapes: Step A Output: {"category": "billing", "confidence": 0.92}. Step B Input: {"category": "billing", "ticket": "Can't bill my card."}. Because each step returns a typed object (the JSON schema from chapter 13.8), the next step can read fields like ticket.category by name. Chaining is just normal function composition with LLM calls as the functions.
summary: Chaining wires the structured JSON output of one LLM call into the input of the next, shown via a Ticket-Classifier-Handler billing example.
queries: step A output to step B input
reading fields by name across steps

text: Routing classifies the input and picks one branch. The classifier is itself an LLM call that returns a typed label. Your code then dispatches to the matching handler. Only the chosen branch runs. The intent classifier picks one of four branches: Billing, Product, Troubleshooting, or Chitchat. The classifier is itself an LLM call with few-shot examples (see section 13.3). One classification call replaces a hand-written if/else tree. The model labels the input, your code dispatches the matching branch handler. Adding a new branch is one new few-shot example plus one new handler.
summary: Routing uses an LLM classifier to label input and dispatch to one of N handler branches, replacing hand-coded if/else trees.
queries: ticket routing billing product troubleshooting chitchat
how to add a new routing branch

text: Parallelization runs N independent calls concurrently and aggregates the results. The total latency is the time of the slowest worker, not the sum. This is the cheapest way to cut wait time on independent sub-tasks. Fan-out to 3 workers, then aggregate: Worker 1 at 180ms, Worker 2 at 140ms, Worker 3 at 210ms, all running concurrently. Latency = max(worker time), not sum. If three retrieval calls each take 150-210ms, running them in series costs ~540ms. Running them concurrently costs ~210ms, the slowest one. The aggregator then merges all results into one structured object.
summary: Parallelization fans out N workers concurrently so total latency equals the slowest worker rather than the sum.

text: Real workflows stack primitives. Production workflows compose all three. A typical pipeline is: chain into a classifier, route to one of several branches, parallelize the heavy retrieval inside one branch, then chain the merged result into a final answer step. Composed topology: Ticket chains into Classifier, which routes to Billing / Troubleshoot / Chitchat. The Troubleshoot branch fans out to three parallel retrieval calls (search_kb, search_docs, search_tickets) that merge before chaining into the final Answer node. One pipeline, all three primitives stacked. Every primitive does one thing well. The real power comes from stacking them. You combine sequence, branch, and fan-out the same way you combine if, switch, and map in normal code.
summary: Production pipelines stack chain, route, and parallelize together like if-switch-map in normal code.
queries: chain plus route plus parallel pipeline
how do production workflows combine primitives

text: Support agent workflow written as primitive stack. Step 1 Ticket Arrives (chain): user message hits the inbound queue. Step 2 Classifier intent classification (route): routes to Billing / Troubleshooting / Escalation. Step 3 Billing Branch (chain): chain lookup_customer -> lookup_subscription -> respond. Step 4 Troubleshooting Branch (parallel): parallel search_kb for top-3 hypotheses then chain to response. Step 5 Escalation Branch (chain): escalate_human directly. Reading: step 2 routes, step 3 chains three tools, step 4 parallelizes search_kb, each branch picks the primitive that fits its shape. This is a workflow, not an agent. Every step is wired in advance. The only LLM decision-making is inside the classifier in step 2. The model is a smart cell inside a graph you fully control.
summary: Concrete support-ticket workflow with five labeled steps showing chain, route, and parallel primitives in their natural slots.

text: Chapter 13.20 The Agent Loop: an agent is a REASON / ACT / OBSERVE cycle that loops with growing history. Each iteration is a state machine with WAITING / REASONING / ACTING / OBSERVING primary states and DONE / FAILED / ESCALATED terminal states. After each OBSERVE a cheap termination check fires on three conditions - final answer (DONE), max iterations (ESCALATE), budget exceeded (ABORT). Cost per iteration is roughly 1 LLM call + 1 tool call, scaling linearly because history is replayed. Worked example: ticket T2 (password reset + email change) finishes in 4 iterations / 3 tool calls because the model adapts after each observation. Use a loop only when a later tool's input depends on an earlier tool's output - otherwise prefer a single call or fixed workflow.
summary: The agent loop is a REASON / ACT / OBSERVE cycle with state-machine semantics, per-iteration termination checks, linear cost growth, and is justified only when later tool inputs depend on earlier outputs.
queries: When to use agent loop vs single call

text: An agent is not a single call. It is a loop with exactly three beats: REASON (the model thinks), ACT (the runtime emits the tool_use), and OBSERVE (the runtime returns the tool_result). Then the model reasons again with the new observation glued to the history. Every agent system is some shape of this cycle. REASON reads the entire history so far; the model's next decision is always conditioned on every previous tool call and result, not just the original prompt.
summary: Agents run a three-beat REASON / ACT / OBSERVE cycle where each iteration conditions on the full prior history.

text: Zoom in on a single iteration and the loop becomes a state machine. Four primary states: WAITING (idle, awaiting input), REASONING (model call in flight), ACTING (tool call in flight), OBSERVING (got tool result). Transitions are triggered by User Input, LLM Response, Tool Result. From OBSERVING the loop returns to REASONING for the next iteration. Three terminal states end the run: DONE (final answer emitted, happy path), FAILED (unrecoverable error such as tool throw or schema break), ESCALATED (budget or max-iter cap hit, safety net).
summary: A single agent iteration is a state machine with WAITING / REASONING / ACTING / OBSERVING primary states and DONE / FAILED / ESCALATED terminal states.

text: Every iteration the runtime asks: should we keep looping? The check happens after OBSERVE and before the next REASON. Three terminate-yes branches: Final Answer Emitted? -> DONE (green). Max Iterations Exceeded? -> ESCALATE (amber). Budget Exceeded? -> ABORT (red). If none fire, the diamond returns No and the loop runs the next iteration. The termination check is cheap (no model call) and happens on every iteration. Without this guardrail an agent can spin forever on a tool that keeps almost-working. Each condition is formalized in chapter 13.23 (Loop Termination).
summary: After OBSERVE every iteration the runtime checks three terminate conditions - final answer, max iterations, budget exceeded - that respectively route to DONE, ESCALATE or ABORT.

How runtime decides to stop loop

text: One iteration is one full lap around the cycle. REASON: 1 LLM call (~$0.02 for a 5k-token reasoning context + 200-token response). ACT: 0 model cost (tool_use emitted in REASON's response), 1 tool execution cost (varies). OBSERVE: 0 model cost; result appended to history. TOTAL: roughly 1 LLM call + 1 tool call per iteration. Rule of thumb: cost per iteration = 1 LLM call + 1 tool call, scales linearly with iteration count. Reasoning context grows every iteration because the full history is replayed - by iteration 8 you might be paying for 40k tokens of context per call. This is why long agent runs get expensive fast.
summary: Each iteration costs about one LLM call plus one tool call, and total cost grows linearly because the full conversation history is replayed every REASON.
queries: How much does an agent iteration cost?
Cost of single agent loop iteration
Why are long agent runs expensive?
What is cost of REASON step?
Does ACT step cost extra model tokens?
Why agents bill so much over time?
How to budget per ticket agent?
Rule of thumb agent iteration cost

text: Trace of ticket T2: 'I forgot my password and my email also changed.' Two problems, order matters. Iter 1 - REASON: Need customer info before anything else. ACT: lookup_customer(email="ada@example.com"). OBSERVE: Got customer_id c-9924, Plan=Pro, Email Verified=False. Iter 2 - REASON: Email outdated, update first before reset lands in the wrong inbox. ACT: change_email(customer_id="c-9924", new_email="ada+new@example.com"). OBSERVE: Email updated, verification pending. Iter 3 - REASON: Now reset the password. ACT: reset_password(customer_id="c-9924"). OBSERVE: Reset email sent to ada+new@example.com. Iter 4 - REASON: Done. ACT: Emit final answer. No tool call. OBSERVE: TERMINATE. Loop exits. Outcome: 4 iterations, 3 tool calls, 1 final answer. The agent was never told 'update email before reset'; it worked that out in iteration 2 from iteration 1's observation.
summary: Worked four-iteration trace where the agent discovers via reasoning that email must be changed before triggering a password reset for customer c-9924.
queries: Ticket T2 password reset email change
Example of agent adapting to observation
Why does agent update email before reset?
Agent emits final answer no tool call
How agent learns from prior observation

text: Not every task needs a loop. A single tool call is cheaper, faster, and easier to debug. Single Call: one tool, one response, done. Example: 'What is the weather in Tokyo?' -> get_weather('Tokyo') -> return the result. No adaptation needed. Loop: multiple iterations, model adapts based on intermediate results. Example: ticket T2 where iter 1's observation changes what iter 2 decides to do. Decision rule: if the second tool's input depends on the first tool's output, you need a loop. Otherwise stay with a single call or a fixed workflow. That dependency is the whole reason the loop exists - the first tool's output reshapes what the model knows, and the model must re-reason before picking the second tool.
summary: Use a loop only when later tool inputs depend on earlier tool outputs; otherwise a cheaper single tool call or fixed workflow suffices.
queries: When do I need an agent loop?
Single tool call vs agent loop
Do I need agent for weather query?
Decision rule for using agent loop
When tool inputs depend on earlier output
Loop vs workflow vs single call
Cheapest way to call a tool

text: Chapter 13.21 ReAct Pattern: ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) forces the agent to emit three labeled blocks each iteration - Thought (the plan in plain language), Action (one tool call matching JSON schema), Observation (the runtime-written structured tool result). Same three-beat agent loop as 13.20, but reasoning becomes visible text instead of hidden state. Rule: one Action per iteration so the next Thought can react to the result. Worked trace for ticket T2 (password reset + email change) shows four cycles - lookup_customer, change_email, reset_password, final answer - with the loop terminating when the model writes an Answer instead of an Action. Cost: extra tokens and latency per Thought. Use ReAct when you need to audit reasoning, debug failures, harvest traces for fine-tuning a smaller model, or build user trust through visible reasoning. Use plain tool_use blocks otherwise.
summary: ReAct makes agent reasoning visible by emitting Thought/Action/Observation blocks each iteration, trading tokens for auditability, debug-ability, and training traces.

text: ReAct stands for Reasoning + Acting. Instead of hiding the model's reasoning inside its head and only emitting a tool call, ReAct asks the model to write the thought out loud, then the action, then the observation, in that order, every iteration. The loop is the same as the agent loop; what changes is that each block is now visible structured output. Three labeled blocks: Thought (what the model is thinking about the next step), Action (which tool to call and with which arguments), Observation (what the tool returned, fed back into the next thought). Key idea: Thought is INSIDE the model's response now, not hidden. Same three beats as the agent loop, but the Thought is no longer an internal step performed silently - it becomes a written block in the response, so evaluators, debuggers, and downstream tools can read it directly.
summary: ReAct = Reasoning + Acting: agent writes Thought, Action, Observation as visible structured output each iteration instead of hiding reasoning.
queries: how does ReAct differ from agent loop
what is Thought block in ReAct

text: The Thought block answers one question: why is the next action the right next action? It names the plan in plain language before any tool fires. A good Thought references the user's request, the state so far, and the order of remaining steps. Example Thought: 'The user asked to reset their password but mentioned the email changed. I should first lookup_customer with the OLD email, then change_email to the NEW one, then reset_password.' Why it matters: the Thought makes the plan visible for evals and debugging. When a reset later goes wrong, you can open the trace and read the model's plan in English. You learn whether the failure was bad reasoning (wrong plan) or bad execution (right plan, wrong tool argument). That split is impossible without a written Thought.
summary: The Thought block explains in plain language why the next action is correct, making the agent's plan auditable for evals and debugging.
queries: what goes in ReAct Thought block
separating bad reasoning from bad execution

text: After the Thought, the model emits exactly one Action. It is a tool name plus an argument object that matches the tool's JSON schema. No second tool, no batching - one call, one step. Example Action: lookup_customer({"email": "alice@example.com"}). Rule: one Action per iteration. Why only one? Because the next Thought must be free to react to whatever this Action returned. Batching two tool calls into one Action makes the model commit to a second step before it has seen the first result, which is exactly the trap the loop is supposed to avoid.
summary: Each ReAct iteration emits exactly one Action - a tool name plus a JSON-schema argument object - so the next Thought can react to its result.
queries: why not batch tool calls in ReAct
single tool call per ReAct cycle
why one action at a time
one step at a time agent

text: The Observation block holds the result the runtime received from the tool. The runtime writes this in - the model does not invent it. The Observation is appended to the conversation so the next Thought has the new ground truth to reason over. Example Observation: { customer_id: "c-9924", tier: "pro", primary_email: "alice@example.com" }. The model reads this and decides the next Thought. Notice the structure - keys like customer_id, tier, primary_email. The model is reading a JSON-shaped result, not a free-text paragraph. Keeping the Observation strictly structured is what lets the next Thought pull out specific fields like c-9924 instead of paraphrasing them.
summary: The Observation block is the structured tool result written in by the runtime, giving the next Thought ground-truth data to reason over.

text: Full ReAct trace for ticket T2 (password reset + email changed). Read top to bottom: Thought - Action - Observation, repeat. Four cycles, then the model decides the loop is done and writes the final answer instead of a fifth Action. Cycle 1 Thought: 'Customer wants reset, mentioned old email. Look them up first.' Action: lookup_customer({"email": "alice@example.com"}). Observation: { customer_id: "c-9924", tier: "pro", primary_email: "alice@example.com" }. Cycle 2 Thought: 'Email on record is outdated. Update it before the reset link goes to the wrong inbox.' Action: change_email({"customer_id": "c-9924", "new_email": "alice+new@example.com"}). Observation: { ok: true, primary_email: "alice+new@example.com", verification: "pending" }. Cycle 3 Thought: 'Email is fixed. Trigger the password reset now so the link lands at the new address.' Action: reset_password({"customer_id": "c-9924"}). Observation: { ok: true, reset_link_sent_to: "alice+new@example.com" }. Cycle 4 Thought: 'All steps done. Write the user-facing reply and stop the loop.' Answer: 'I updated your email to alice+new@example.com and sent a fresh reset link there. Click it within 30 minutes to set a new password.' Outcome: 4 cycles, 3 tool calls. Cycle 4 ends with an Answer instead of an Action, so the loop stops. The trace reads almost like a transcript of someone thinking aloud while doing the work - a human reviewer can audit every decision, and a downstream evaluator can score the Thoughts independently from whether the final answer was right.
summary: Full four-cycle ReAct trace for ticket T2 showing lookup_customer, change_email, reset_password, then a final answer that terminates the loop.
queries: password reset email changed ReAct walkthrough
ReAct lookup customer change email reset password

text: ReAct is not free. Each Thought costs tokens and slows the response. Modern tool-use APIs already produce structured tool_use blocks without forcing the reasoning to be written out. Plain tool-use: model emits tool_use blocks; reasoning stays in model's head; invisible to user/observability. ReAct: same loop, but model required to emit Thought + Action explicitly; reasoning written out as text. When to use ReAct: when you need to audit reasoning; when you need to debug failures; when you train a smaller model on the traces; when you need to build trust with the user. Once the trace is written down, it becomes a teaching signal. You can fine-tune a smaller model on big-model traces, you can show the Thoughts in a UI to make the agent feel transparent, and you can write evals that score the plan separately from the final answer.
summary: Use ReAct when you need auditing, debugging, training traces, or user trust; otherwise plain tool-use saves tokens and latency.
queries: when to use ReAct vs tool use
is ReAct worth the token cost

Caption: 'Model Picks Next Action Each Turn.' Contrasts with the plan-execute tree by emphasizing that no plan exists until step N+1 is chosen.

A bent dashed arrow runs from leaf 4 down to an appended fifth leaf 5. escalate_human (Reason: business_rule, Done) added by the executor to adapt to the error. text: Chapter 13.22 Plan-Execute + Reflection. Plan-Execute writes the full plan tree up front (root goal + named tool-call leaves) and a thin executor walks the leaves, passing results forward. Failed leaves trigger a local replan that appends new leaves instead of rebuilding. Worked through with ticket T4 (cancel + refund): four-leaf plan, refund fails on business_rule, executor adapts by appending an escalate_human leaf. Reflection layers a draft / score / revise cycle on top: critic grades the draft 0-10 with reasoning and fix_list, model revises if score < 7, capped revisions to prevent infinite loops. The chapter closes with a 2x2 decision matrix - task complexity vs auditability - mapping cells to Plain Tool-Use, ReAct, Plan-Execute, or Plan-Execute + Reflection. Pick the smallest loop shape the task forces.
summary: Chapter overview of Plan-Execute, executor adaptation, reflection cycle, and loop-pattern decision matrix.
queries: When to plan up front versus react
Plan tree root and leaves overview

text: ReAct chooses the next move iteration by iteration. Plan-Execute does the opposite: the model writes the full plan tree up front, then a simple executor walks the leaves. Same tools, same model, very different control shape. ReAct is reactive - the model picks the next action on the fly each turn with question-mark arrows between steps. Plan-Execute is deliberate - a root plan node fans out to leaf nodes and the whole plan tree is drawn before any leaf runs. Use ReAct when the next step truly depends on what just happened. Use Plan-Execute when you can name all the steps up front and just want them ordered, parallelized, or audited.
summary: Contrasts ReAct's step-by-step reactive choices with Plan-Execute's up-front plan tree.
queries: When to use plan first versus react
Agent that plans the whole task up front
Why use plan-execute instead of ReAct
Pick steps in advance agent pattern
Root plan node fanning out to leaves
When the next step depends on prior result

text: A plan is a tree. The root names the overall goal. Each leaf names a concrete tool call with the data it needs. For ticket T4 (a customer wants to cancel and get a refund) the plan has four leaves: 1. lookup_customer to get customer_id, 2. lookup_subscription to get invoice_id, 3. cancel the subscription, 4. process_refund (flagged with a business_rule error -> escalate note). The plan is structured output, not free text. The model emits four leaf objects, each with a tool name, an argument template, and a note about which earlier leaf supplies each argument. The fourth leaf flags a known business_rule snag the planner anticipates. Every leaf names a tool and the data it will need.
summary: Shows a concrete plan tree for ticket T4 cancel + refund with four named tool-call leaves.
queries: What does a plan tree look like
Example plan for cancel and refund ticket
Plan as structured output not free text
Each leaf names tool plus arguments
How agent plans cancel and refund
Plan tree drawn before any tool fires
What goes in a plan node

text: Once the plan is set, a thin executor walks the leaves in order. Each leaf calls its tool, captures the result, and feeds the result into the next leaf's arguments. For T4: leaf 1 returns customer_id c-9924 (Ok), leaf 2 returns invoice_id inv-7741 (Ok), leaf 3 returns status: cancelled (Ok), leaf 4 process_refund fails with business_rule Error. The executor catches the error and adapts by appending leaf 5: escalate_human with reason business_rule (Done). The executor did not throw the entire plan away. Leaves 1, 2, and 3 already succeeded - their results are kept. Only the failed leaf triggers a replan, and the replan appends one new leaf instead of rebuilding the tree from scratch. The model only re-enters the loop when something the plan did not anticipate happens.
summary: Executor walks plan leaves, keeps successes, and appends an escalate_human leaf on failure.
queries: How does plan executor walk leaves
What happens when a plan leaf fails
Re-enter model only on unanticipated event
Walk leaves in order pass results

text: Reflection sits on top of any loop. After the model writes a draft, the same model (or a separate critic call) grades it. If the grade is low, the model revises. Three-step cycle: 1. Model writes draft answer (first pass, free text or structured output, may be confidently wrong). 2. Model or critic scores the draft 0-10 with reasoning (covers accuracy, format, completeness, tone; reasoning spells out the misses). 3. If score < 7, model revises and re-evaluates (revision uses the critique as a diff list; re-score decides whether to stop or loop again). Safety valve: one revision cap typical, more triggers escalation. Critic prompt sketch: "Grade the draft 0-10 on accuracy, format, completeness. Return JSON: { score: int, reasoning: str, fix_list: [str] }. If score < 7, orchestrator runs the model again with the fix_list pasted into the prompt. Two failed revisions in a row escalate_human." The cost is one extra model call per draft, plus another full call if a revision is needed. Bound revisions so reflection cannot spiral into an infinite revise loop.
summary: Reflection adds a draft, score, revise cycle on top of any loop to catch confidently wrong answers.
queries: When to use reflection on top of loop
Critic JSON schema score reasoning fix_list

text: Four shapes, one decision matrix. Plot the task on two axes: how complex is the task, and how much do you need to audit the reasoning. The right loop falls out of the cell you land in. Simple task + low audit -> Plain Tool-Use (cheap single-call flow). Simple task + high audit -> ReAct (simple task, visible reasoning). Complex task + low audit -> Plan-Execute (complex plan, light audit). Complex task + high audit -> Plan-Execute + Reflection (complex plan, heavy audit, self-check). Decision shortcut: single tool, trusted output = Plain Tool-Use; few steps, need visible reasoning = ReAct; many steps, order known = Plan-Execute; high-stakes answer, must self-check = Plan-Execute + Reflection. Loops are not a ladder where bigger is always better. Each extra structure costs tokens, latency, and review surface. Pick the smallest shape that handles the task and graduate only when the task forces you to.
summary: Provides a 2x2 decision matrix over complexity and auditability mapping to four loop patterns.

Decision matrix tool use ReAct plan-execute reflection

Iterations 1-9 are yellow filled cells with normal stroke; iteration 10 is highlighted in red with bold stroke, a red STOP badge above it, and a bold X mark crossing the cell. Forward arrows connect each iteration cell to the next. Caption: 'Iteration Counter - Force-Terminate At Iter 10'. Visualizes the max-iters hard cap firing at iteration 10.

text: Diagram (sub 3): Horizontal budget bar from $0 to $0.50 with gradient fill - green from 0-30%, amber from 30-60%, red from 60-100%. Three vertical markers with circles at 30% ('Iter 1-2 consumed $0.15'), 60% ('Iter 3-4 consumed $0.30'), and 100% ('Budget exhausted - STOP at $0.50'). Caption: 'Budget Bar - Loop Terminates When Bar Hits 100%'. Visualizes cumulative cost consumption with markers showing where iterations land and where the cap fires.

text: Diagram (sub 5): Vertical fail-safe escalation flow with three stacked boxes connected by downward arrows. Box 1 (red): 'Loop Terminates - any non-success stop (max-iters / budget / halt)'. Box 2 (amber): 'Agent emits final message to user - I wasn't able to fully resolve this. A human agent will follow up.' Box 3 (cyan): 'Call escalate_human with partial transcript - hands off the full chain of reasoning so the human can resume.' Each box has a numbered circle badge (1, 2, 3). Caption: 'Fail-Safe Pattern - Triggered By ANY Non-Success Termination'.
summary: Fail-safe escalation flow showing terminate then user message then escalate_human with partial transcript handoff.

text: Chapter 13.23 covers loop termination for agent loops. Four stop conditions: Success (model emits final answer with end_turn instead of tool_use), Max-Iters (hard cap of 10-20 iterations), Budget (token/dollar cap, e.g., $0.50 per run), and Explicit Stop (tool returns halt:true or user cancels). Success detected when the model's response contains only text and no tool_use block. Max-iters protects against runaway loops. Budget caps in dollars are safer than iteration caps because one iteration can spend 10x another. Any non-success termination triggers the fail-safe escalation pattern: loop terminates, agent emits user-visible message, calls escalate_human with the full partial transcript. Production rule: no silent failures.
summary: Overview of 13.23 covering the four loop termination conditions and the fail-safe escalation pattern.
queries: how does an agent loop end
four ways an agent loop ends
what makes an agent stop looping
loop termination success max-iters budget halt
how to prevent runaway agent loops

text: Four ways a loop ends. An agent loop is a process that must stop. There are exactly four reasons it does. Three are non-success: the loop ran out of room, ran out of money, or was told to halt. The four stop conditions: Success - model emits final answer. Max-Iters - hit the cap (e.g., 10). Budget - token/cost exhausted. Explicit Stop - tool returns a 'halt' signal, or user cancels. One of these four conditions fires on every agent run. Every production agent must detect each one and respond differently.
summary: Introduces the four stop conditions that terminate any agent loop: success, max-iters, budget, and explicit stop.
queries: what are the four stop conditions
how many ways can an agent loop end
what reasons cause agent loop to terminate
happy path vs failure modes for agents
what does halt signal mean for agents
respond differently to each stop condition

text: Success: the model stops calling tools. The happy path is also the subtlest. The model never says 'I'm done' out loud. It just stops asking for tools. The runtime watches the most recent response and detects the absence of any tool_use block. Iteration N-1: model emits tool_use + text - still working, runtime runs the tool, feeds result back, loops again. Iteration N: model emits ONLY text (no tool_use). That's success - the model decided no more tools needed. Stop reason from the API is 'end_turn' instead of 'tool_use'. The final text is the answer to the user. This is the only stop condition that does not require an escalation message.
summary: How the runtime detects successful termination: the model emits text only with no tool_use block, stop_reason flips to end_turn.
queries: how to know agent is done

text: Hard cap: 10-20 iterations. The max-iter cap is the dumbest safety net but also the most important. A counter ticks up every loop pass. When it hits the cap, the runtime force-terminates the loop without asking the model. Production rule: 10-20 typical max-iter cap. Higher = pay-to-think; lower = miss answers. The cap protects against runaway loops where the model can't make progress and keeps re-trying the same tool. Without a cap a single buggy ticket can bill thousands of dollars in an hour.
summary: Max-iter hard cap of 10-20 iterations force-terminates runaway loops without consulting the model.
queries: how many iterations should agent loop allow
how to prevent infinite agent loop

text: Token / cost budget. Iteration count is a proxy for cost; the real number is dollars. A budget cap measures the total tokens (or dollars) spent across all iterations and stops the loop when the wallet is empty. Sample budget: $0.50 per agent run. Budget bar fills from $0 to $0.50 in stages: at 30% iter 1-2 consumed $0.15, at 60% iter 3-4 consumed $0.30, at 100% budget exhausted - STOP at $0.50. Budget is the SAFER cap than iterations - real cost can vary 10x per iteration. One iteration that triggers a thinking burst can spend 10k tokens; the next might spend 500. Iteration count doesn't see that. A dollar budget does.
summary: Token/dollar budget caps total spend across iterations and is safer than iteration count because per-iteration cost varies 10x.
queries: what is a token budget for agents
dollar budget cap for agent loop
why budget is safer than iteration cap
how to bound agent dollar spend
why iteration count is not enough

text:
Tools can say stop. Some tools are terminal by design. An escalate_human tool hands the conversation to a human and there is nothing left for the agent to do. The tool result includes a halt flag, and the runtime respects it. Tool result shape with halt signal:
{
  "tool": "escalate_human",
  "result": { "ticket_id": "T4", "human_assigned": true },
  "halt": true
}
The loop respects this signal and terminates cleanly. Similar pattern: a user cancellation injects a halt event. Explicit stops are the cleanest non-success case. The model doesn't get confused, the runtime doesn't guess, and there is a clear handoff record in the transcript.
summary: Terminal tools like escalate_human return halt:true in the result, and the runtime terminates the loop cleanly on that signal.
queries: how does a tool stop the agent loop

text: When the loop gives up, escalate. Any non-success termination (max-iters, budget, or explicit halt that wasn't a successful resolution) must trigger the same fail-safe pattern. Three steps: 1. Loop Terminates - any non-success stop (max-iters / budget / halt). 2. Agent emits final message to user - 'I wasn't able to fully resolve this. A human agent will follow up.' 3. Call escalate_human with partial transcript - hands off the full chain of reasoning so the human can resume. Production rule: NO SILENT FAILURES. Every non-success termination produces a user-visible message AND an escalate_human call with the full partial transcript. Silent failures are how agent bugs hide in production for weeks. The transcript is the load-bearing artifact: it carries the agent's reasoning, every tool call, every result, so the human picking up the ticket has full context.
summary: Fail-safe pattern for any non-success termination: terminate, message the user, escalate to human with the full partial transcript.
queries: what to do when agent loop fails

text: Memory Taxonomy - Short vs Long divides agent memory into short-term working memory (inside the context window, discarded at session end) and long-term persistent memory (in an external store, survives sessions). Long-term further splits into episodic (time-stamped events), semantic (stable facts as key-value or vectors), and procedural (recipes / cached workflows). Together they form a four-leaf tree - working, episodic, semantic, procedural - and a ticket like T2 shows each layer populated with concrete content. Each layer earns its place by solving a different problem with different lifespan and retrieval pattern.
summary: Chapter map of agent memory: short-term working plus long-term episodic, semantic, and procedural - four layers each solving a distinct problem.
queries: summary of short vs long term agent memory

all four memory layers explained briefly
