memory layer cheatsheet for LLM agents

text: Agent memory has two layers. Short-term (working) memory lives inside the active context window of the current conversation - the model reads and writes it every loop turn, and it is discarded when the session ends. Long-term (persistent) memory lives outside the context window in an external store, persists across sessions and conversations, and gets loaded back in on demand. Short = this conversation. Long = everything before.
summary: Agent memory splits into short-term working memory inside the context window and long-term persistent memory in an external store.
queries: what are the two layers of agent memory
short term vs long term memory in agents
working memory vs persistent memory LLM agent
context window memory vs external store

two types of memory for AI agents

text: Long-term memory is not one thing. It splits three ways by what kind of information it holds. Episodic memory stores past events with timestamps and content (example: past ticket #4521 - failed password reset escalated for MFA bug). Semantic memory stores facts as key-value or vectors (example: Alice = Pro tier, signed up 2024-08, prefers email). Procedural memory stores skills as recipes or cached workflows (example: refund > $200 -> escalate to human). Each sub-type answers a different question: events answer when, facts answer what, skills answer how.
summary: Long-term agent memory splits into episodic (events), semantic (facts), and procedural (skills) sub-types.

text: The full agent memory taxonomy is a tree with one root, two branches, four leaves. Root: Agent Memory. Branches: Short-Term and Long-Term. Short-Term has one leaf: Working (scratchpad), covered in chapter 13.25. Long-Term has three leaves: Episodic (events, 13.26), Semantic (facts, 13.27), Procedural (skills, 13.28). Summary and context-management close out the act at 13.29. The tree is the map for the rest of the memory chapters.
summary: Memory taxonomy tree maps root Agent Memory through short-term working and long-term episodic/semantic/procedural leaves.

text: Concrete memory snapshot for ticket T2 (Alice: password reset + email change). Working memory: customer alice@example.com / c-9924, issue = password reset + email changed, next = change_email then reset_password. Episodic memory: 2026-03-04 past ticket #4521 for failed password reset, escalated due to legacy MFA bug. Semantic memory: Alice = Pro tier, signed up 2024-08, prefers email contact, USD billing. Procedural memory: refund > $200 -> escalate_human; always confirm identity before change_email. text: Each memory layer solves a different problem with different retrieval patterns, update rules, and lifespans. Working memory holds the current task state across loop iterations. Episodic memory gives the agent recall of specific past events. Semantic memory provides stable customer facts without re-asking. Procedural memory caches learned routines so the agent does not re-derive them every time. That is why agents need four layers and not one - each holds a different shape of information.
summary: Each memory layer earns its place by solving a distinct problem: task state, event recall, stable facts, and cached routines.

text: Chapter 13.25 Working Memory - The Scratchpad. Working memory is the agent's in-context scratchpad, read on every REASON step and updated after every OBSERVE step. It is a structured object with five stable fields (customer_context, current_goal, completed_steps, next_step, constraints). It evolves iteration by iteration: ticket T2 fills customer_context, grows completed_steps, and finishes with next_step null. The scratchpad is discarded at task end, so important state must be promoted to long-term memory before the loop terminates. Compared to long-term memory: working memory lives in the prompt context window, is written by the model, holds a few hundred tokens, and is ephemeral; long-term memory lives in an external store, is written by promotion logic, holds millions of facts, and persists across sessions.
summary: Working memory is a structured in-context scratchpad with five fields, updated each loop turn, discarded at task end, and must be promoted before the loop ends to persist.

text: Working memory is the agent's in-context scratchpad. The agent reads it on every REASON step and updates it after every OBSERVE step. It lives inside the prompt context window itself, not in any external store. The loop around the note pad: 1. REASON: the model reads the scratchpad to decide the next tool. 2. ACT: the tool runs. 3. OBSERVE: the result comes back; the scratchpad is updated. 4. LOOP: back to step 1, this time with the updated note pad. The scratchpad is the agent's short-term reasoning trace. Lose it, and the agent has to re-derive what it was doing from scratch.
summary: Working memory is an in-context scratchpad the agent reads on REASON and updates on OBSERVE each loop turn.

text: The scratchpad is not free-form text. It is a structured object with stable field names so the model can read and write specific slots. Five fields anchor the running task. Working Memory shape: { "customer_context": "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro", "current_goal": "Reset password (email also changed)", "completed_steps": ["lookup_customer", "change_email"], "next_step": "reset_password", "constraints": ["refund cap $200"] }. customer_context answers who, current_goal answers what, completed_steps answers what so far, next_step answers what next, and constraints keep the agent within policy. The same five fields appear in chapter 13.42 observability traces.

text: Watch the scratchpad evolve across four loop turns of ticket T2 (Alice: password reset + email change). Each row is the state right after the iteration's OBSERVE step. Iteration 1: customer_context: "" (not yet looked up), completed_steps: [], next_step: "lookup_customer". Iteration 2: customer_context: "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro", completed_steps: ["lookup_customer"], next_step: "change_email". Iteration 3: customer_context: "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro", completed_steps: ["lookup_customer", "change_email"], next_step: "reset_password". Iteration 4: customer_context: "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro", completed_steps: ["lookup_customer", "change_email", "reset_password"], next_step: null (task complete). By iteration 4, next_step is null and the task is done. The scratchpad terminated cleanly, which is how loop termination (chapter 13.23) detects success.

text: When the loop terminates, the scratchpad is discarded. Anything worth keeping must be promoted to long-term memory BEFORE that discard. Otherwise it is gone. The timeline shows working memory stops: created at Task Start, updated through Iter N, then a Promote stop where useful state is copied to the Long-Term Store, then Task End where everything in the scratchpad is discarded. "Resolved by email reset link" is a working memory entry. After promotion to episodic memory, the next session can recall it. Skip the promote, and the next session asks Alice the same question from scratch.
summary: Working memory is discarded at task end so important state must be promoted to long-term memory before the loop terminates.

text: Side-by-side comparison of the in-context scratchpad and the external long-term store. Working memory lives in the prompt context window; long-term memory lives in an external store like a vector DB or Postgres. Working memory is written by the model on each loop turn; long-term memory is written by explicit promotion logic. Working memory capacity is a few hundred tokens; long-term memory holds millions of facts or events. Working memory lifespan: discarded when the task ends. Long-term lifespan: persistent across sessions. Working memory is fast, cheap, and ephemeral. Long-term memory is slower, more expensive, but persistent. Agents need both - and the bridge between them is the promote step.
summary: Working memory is fast, ephemeral, in-context, model-written; long-term memory is persistent, large, externally stored, written by promotion logic.

text: Episodic memory is the agent's store of time-stamped past events. Each episode is a structured record (id, customer_id, timestamp, summary, tools_used, outcome, 1024-float embedding) saved to a vector database. On each new session the host builds a recall query, runs ANN search against the vector DB, and injects the top-3 most relevant past episodes into the system prompt before the model reasons. A pruning policy bounds growth: drop clean-resolved events older than 12 months, keep rare escalations, summarize similar groups, and delete on compliance request. Episodic memory answers 'what happened, when?' - distinct from semantic memory (facts) and procedural memory (skills).
summary: Episodic memory stores time-stamped event records as vector embeddings, retrieves the top-3 relevant past events per session via ANN, and prunes by age, outcome, and compliance rules.

text: Episodic memory holds specific past events. Each event has a timestamp and a short description of what happened. Episodes answer 'what happened, when?'. They are NOT the customer's stable facts (that is semantic memory) and they are NOT how-to recipes (that is procedural memory).

text: Episodic memory lives in a vector database. Each event becomes an embedding; a search query becomes an embedding; an ANN index returns the most similar past events. Stage one: Event 'Failed Password Reset' -> Embed [1024 floats] -> Vector DB (Section 11.6 / 11.7). Stage two: Query 'What past issues?' -> Embed with same encoder -> ANN Search -> Top-K Events. Vector storage substrate covered in Section 11.6 (IVF) / 11.7 (HNSW). Here we use it as the layer below episodic memory: write once, search by similarity from then on.

text: When a new conversation starts, the agent retrieves relevant past episodes BEFORE reasoning about the current ticket. The host system fetches the top-3 events and injects them into the system prompt as context. Step 1: User Message Arrives - T2: 'I forgot my password and my email changed.' Step 2: Build Recall Query - Query = 'current ticket summary + customer_id c-9924'. Step 3: Retrieve Top-3 Episodes - ANN search returns 3 most similar past events. Step 4: Inject Into System Prompt - Episodes added to the prompt as context block. Step 5: Agent Reasons With Past Context - Past MFA-bug episode informs current approach. ChatGPT Memory works similarly: the host fetches relevant facts on each new conversation and injects them. The model does not 'remember' - the host system gives it the appearance of memory through retrieval-and-inject.
summary: On each new session the host retrieves top-3 past episodes by ANN search and injects them into the system prompt before the agent reasons.

text: Unbounded growth wrecks retrieval quality. Pruning policy decides what to keep and what to drop. Four rules cover most cases. Age > 12 months AND outcome = resolved cleanly -> Drop. Age > 12 months AND outcome = escalated -> Keep (rare patterns valuable). Aggregated similar events -> Summarize into one parent and drop children. Manual customer request -> Delete on demand (compliance). Compliance-driven deletion is non-negotiable. A customer can request their entire episodic log be wiped, and the agent must honor it within whatever window the regulation requires.
summary: Episodic memory must be pruned by age, outcome, aggregation, and compliance deletion to keep retrieval quality high.

text: Chapter 13.27 introduces semantic memory: the layer of an agent's long-term memory that stores stable facts about a customer or entity, separate from time-stamped episodes (episodic) and how-to recipes (procedural). Each customer has a structured profile card (customer_id, tier, signed_up, preferred_contact, primary_email, billing_currency, preferences) stored in a key-value store like Postgres for typed fields, plus a vector store for free-text long-tail constraints. The profile grows on confirmed observations (Day 1 has 4 fields, Day 30 adds preferred_contact, Day 90 adds nested preferences). The write rule: if the fact would still be true in 6 months, write it; otherwise leave it in working memory.
summary: Semantic memory holds stable customer facts in a structured profile (and optionally a vector store), grown via confirmed observations and gated by a 6-month truth test.

Semantic fact (stable state): Alice prefers email contact. Same person, different memory layer. Episodic answers 'what happened?', semantic answers 'what is true?'. Both are needed; they live in different stores with different update rules.

text:
Each customer has a profile card with stable fields, queried by exact key, updated on confirmed observations. Alice's profile card shape:
{
  "customer_id": "c-9924",
  "tier": "Pro",
  "signed_up": "2024-08",
  "preferred_contact": "email",
  "primary_email": "alice@example.com",
  "billing_currency": "USD",
  "preferences": { "skip_onboarding_emails": true }
}
customer_id is the join key. tier, preferred_contact, billing_currency are typed fields the agent can query directly. preferences is a nested bag for arbitrarily-shaped opt-ins.

text: Two storage substrates serve different fact shapes. Structured (key-value / Postgres): use when fields are typed, predictable, queried by exact match. Example: customer_id -> profile lookup. Vector (embedding + similarity): use when facts are free-text, queried by meaning. Example: 'User has a dairy allergy' embedded for retrieval. Most production agents use BOTH: structured for the spine, vector for the long-tail freetext. The spine (typed profile) is small and exact. The long-tail (free-form preferences and constraints) is large and fuzzy.
summary: Use key-value/Postgres for typed exact-match fields and a vector store for free-text similarity-matched facts; production agents combine both.

text: Semantic memory grows over time from confirmed observations. Day 1 (Signup): customer_id: c-9924, tier: Pro, signed_up: 2024-08, primary_email: alice@example.com. Day 30: all Day-1 fields plus preferred_contact: email, learned from 'Please email me at...'. Day 90: all Day-30 fields plus preferences.skip_onboarding_emails: true, learned from a user opt-out click. Profile fills only on CONFIRMED observations. A guess from one ambiguous message does not get written. A repeated preference observed across sessions does.
summary: Profile grows by confirmed observation: Day 1 has 4 base fields, Day 30 adds preferred_contact, Day 90 adds nested opt-out preferences.

text: Rule of thumb: if it would still be true in 6 months, write it to semantic memory. Otherwise leave it in the working scratchpad or skip it entirely. Write to semantic: stable preferences (always email), confirmed identity (Alice is account owner), constraints (dairy allergy). Do not write: transient mood ('frustrated today'), one-time requests ('send a copy this once'), guesses (uncertain inferences). 6-Months Test: would knowing this still help six months from now? If yes, write it. If no, it belongs in working memory or the chat history and should expire naturally.
summary: The 6-month test decides what becomes a semantic fact: stable preferences and constraints qualify, transient mood and one-time requests do not.

text: Chapter 13.28 covers procedural memory: the agent layer that stores HOW to do things (skills, recipes, cached workflows), not facts (semantic) or events (episodic). It contrasts a semantic fact ($200 refund cap) with a procedural recipe (when refund > $200, look up tier; Enterprise auto-approves up to $500, else escalate_human urgency=medium). Introduces a recipe library with 4 named workflows (Handle Refund Request, Resolve Password Issue, Escalate Billing Dispute, Resolve Sync Failure), each with a trigger and step count. Recipes are retrieved by embedding the new ticket, ANN-searching against trigger_embedding fields, surfacing top-1 (or no-match fallback to first-principles reasoning). Shows the recipe record shape: id, name, trigger_embedding (1024 floats), ordered steps array, success_rate (0.92 = 92% clean outcomes), uses count (187 runs). Closes with a Prompting vs Procedural Memory comparison table on 4 axes: where it lives (system prompt vs external store retrieved on demand), token cost (every conversation pays vs only when relevant), can learn (static vs updatable via success_rate), best for (universal rules like tone/format vs task-specific recipes).
summary: Chapter overview of procedural memory as cached how-to recipes retrieved by embedding similarity, with shape, analytics fields, and contrast against prompting.

text: Procedural memory encodes skills - HOW to do something - not facts about the world. Compare: a semantic fact says 'Refund cap is $200' (WHAT is true). A procedural skill says 'When a refund > $200 is requested, look up customer tier first. Enterprise customers self-approve up to $500. Otherwise call escalate_human with urgency=medium' (WHAT TO DO). The agent uses both: the fact says '$200 cap'; the procedure says 'if cap exceeded, escalate'. Facts live in semantic memory. How-to recipes live in procedural memory.
summary: Procedural memory stores how-to skills as opposed to semantic facts about state, with a refund example showing both layers cooperating.

text: The recipe library is a small set of named workflows the customer-support agent reuses. Each recipe has a trigger condition and an ordered step list. Four recipes: (1) Handle Refund Request - trigger: customer asks for refund, 3 steps. (2) Resolve Password Issue - trigger: login/password error, 4 steps. (3) Escalate Billing Dispute - trigger: disputed invoice or charge, 2 steps. (4) Resolve Sync Failure - trigger: data out-of-sync across devices, 5 steps. Each one used to be a prompt-engineering exercise; once it stabilized, it got moved into procedural memory.

text: Retrieve the recipe that matches the new ticket. A new ticket gets embedded; ANN search runs against the recipe library's trigger_embedding field; the top-1 match is surfaced (or 'no match' if similarity is below threshold). Five-stage pipeline: New Ticket (free text) -> Embed (1024 floats) -> ANN Search (vs library) -> Top-1 (or No Match) -> Use Recipe (scaffolding). The agent uses the retrieved recipe as a scaffold, then adapts to the specifics of the current ticket. If no recipe matches, the agent falls back to first-principles reasoning - and that run becomes a candidate to mine for a new recipe.
summary: Recipe retrieval pipeline embeds the new ticket and runs ANN search against trigger embeddings, surfacing top-1 as scaffolding or falling back to first-principles reasoning.

Trigger embedding for retrieval; steps for execution; success_rate and uses for analytics and self-improvement. success_rate = 0.92 means 92% of times this recipe ran, it produced a clean outcome. uses = 187 says the agent has run this recipe 187 times. text: Why not just prompt the recipe every time? Prompting and procedural memory both encode 'how to do X'. They differ across four axes. Where it lives: prompting is inside the system prompt; procedural is an external store retrieved on demand. Token cost: every conversation pays the prompt tokens; procedural is only retrieved when relevant. Can learn?: prompting is static and cannot update from outcomes; procedural is updatable - success_rate drives rewrite. Best for: prompting suits universal rules (tone, format); procedural wins for task-specific recipes. Prompting stays great for universal rules - tone, format, safety constraints. Procedural memory wins for task-specific recipes that change with real-world outcomes. Use both, deliberately.
summary: Four-axis comparison of prompting versus procedural memory covering location, cost, learnability, and best fit, with a use-both recommendation.

text: Rolling summary technique in three stages: Stage 1 conversation at 50% capacity (half-filled bar). Stage 2 oldest half summarized into a small summary block (purple slim block replacing the older half). Stage 3 capacity drops back to 25% (small summary plus reduced raw turns), so the conversation can continue. Arrows connect each stage; caption notes the cycle repeats every time the window hits 50% and the summary itself grows slowly as older summaries are re-compressed.
summary: Three-stage diagram showing rolling summary: 50% full, compress oldest half into a summary block, drop to 25% capacity.

text: Hierarchical summary tree: raw turn batches at the bottom layer become per-batch summaries at the middle layer, which combine into a single meta-summary at the top covering the whole conversation. Top has one Meta-Summary box. Mid has three Batch Summary boxes. Bottom has groups of small turn boxes (T1, T3, T5...) clustered under each batch summary. Lines connect leaves up to mid and mid up to the meta-summary.
summary: Hierarchical summary tree diagram with raw turn batches as leaves, per-batch summaries as mid-layer, and a single meta-summary at the top.

text: Four threshold bands across a context window utilization bar: at 25% do nothing (green band), at 50% compress half by summarizing oldest 25% (yellow band), at 75% aggressive summarize older half plus start hybrid retrieval (orange band), at 90% panic drop everything except summary plus last 5 turns plus top retrieved (red band). Each band labels the percent, action name, and action description.
summary: Four-band diagram of context utilization thresholds at 25%, 50%, 75%, and 90%, each tied to an escalating compression action.
queries: what happens at each context threshold

text: Chapter 13.29 covers summary memory and context window management. Long conversations exceed the context window (8k tokens fills by turn 30 at 250 tokens per turn), forcing compression. Rolling summary compresses the oldest half at 50% capacity. Hierarchical summarization builds a tree of batch summaries plus a meta-summary for week-long conversations. After compression, three retention strategies - recency, relevance, hybrid - decide which raw turns stay. Production triggers compression at four threshold bands: 25% nothing, 50% compress half, 75% aggressive, 90% panic. Final prompt assembly stacks system prompt, memory block, conversation summary, recent turns, retrieved turns, working memory, and the current user message.
summary: Chapter-level overview of context window management: rolling summary, hierarchical summary, recency/relevance/hybrid retention, threshold bands, and the final prompt assembly stack.
queries: summary memory and context window management overview
how do agents manage long conversations

text: Long conversations are not free. With an 8k token context window and ~250 tokens per turn, the window fills by turn 30. Past that, something has to give. The agent must compress old turns, drop them, or both. The rest of the chapter is about how to manage that ceiling.
summary: Context windows fill fast: 8k tokens at ~250 tokens/turn fills by turn 30, forcing compression or dropping.
queries: why doesn't a long conversation fit in the LLM
how many turns fit in 8k context window
what happens when context window fills up

text: The rolling-summary technique: when the window hits 50% capacity, summarize the oldest half into a 2-paragraph block. The summary replaces the raw turns. Capacity drops to 25%. Repeat every time the window hits 50%. The summary itself grows slowly as older summaries are re-compressed. The model itself usually generates the summary (a 2-paragraph LLM call per compression cycle). The summary lives in the conversation history at the spot where the raw turns used to be.
summary: Rolling summary compresses oldest half of conversation into a 2-paragraph block when context hits 50% capacity, dropping back to 25%.
queries: how rolling summaries work in LLM agents
two paragraph summary replace raw turns

text: For week-long conversations, flat rolling summary degrades. The hierarchical approach organizes raw turns into batches (leaves), per-batch summaries (mid), and a meta-summary of the whole conversation (top). Leaves are raw turn batches of about 10 turns each. Mid layer is per-batch summary. Top is the meta. Hierarchical wins for week-long conversations because the meta-summary stays small while you can still zoom into specific batches when needed.
summary: Hierarchical summarization builds a tree of raw turn batches, per-batch summaries, and a meta-summary on top for week-long conversations.
queries: how to handle week long chat history
why flat rolling summary fails long conversations

text: After summarization, what stays in the prompt? Three strategies, each with a different tradeoff between continuity and topical fit. Recency keeps the most-recent N turns raw and drops a summary of older turns - good for continuity-heavy conversations. Relevance keeps turns most similar to the current query and drops a summary of less-relevant turns - good for long-range topical recall. Hybrid keeps top-K recent plus top-K relevant and summarizes everything else - the best default in practice. Hybrid wins because most conversations have BOTH continuity needs (the user said 'this' earlier) AND topical recall needs (the user asked a similar thing 80 turns ago).
summary: Three retention strategies after summarization - recency, relevance, hybrid - with hybrid combining top-K recent plus top-K relevant turns as the production default.
queries: what turns to keep in context
most recent vs most relevant turns
how to choose which turns to keep

text: Production agents trigger compression at fixed thresholds, not on demand. Four bands cover the production rule: at 25% do nothing (plenty of room), at 50% compress half (summarize oldest 25%), at 75% aggressive summarize (summarize older half plus start hybrid retrieval), at 90% panic (drop everything except summary plus last 5 turns plus top retrieved). Threshold-based compression is production-friendly because the cost is predictable. On-demand compression can hide behind a single user turn that suddenly explodes the window.
summary: Four threshold bands at 25%/50%/75%/90% context utilization drive escalating compression - from nothing, to compress half, to aggressive summarize, to panic drop.
queries: what to do at 90 percent context full
last 5 turns plus summary panic mode

text: Memory plus context-engineering decide what the model sees on EVERY turn. The assembly order: 1. System Prompt (static + recipe from procedural memory). 2. Memory Block (retrieved episodes + profile card from semantic). 3. Conversation Summary (rolling / hierarchical summary of old turns). 4. Recent Turns (last N raw turns - recency). 5. Retrieved Turns (top-K relevant turns from older history - hybrid). 6. Working Memory (current scratchpad state). 7. Current User Message (what the user just said). Every layer comes from somewhere - memory chapters 13.24-13.28 supplied the sources; chapter 13.6's assembly stack joins them into a single prompt; this chapter closes the loop with summarization that keeps it all under the token budget.
summary: The final prompt stacks system prompt, memory block, summary, recent turns, retrieved turns, working memory, and the current user message in a fixed assembly order.
queries: what goes into the LLM prompt every turn
system prompt memory block summary order
how to build the agent prompt
where does procedural memory go in prompt
combining episodic semantic procedural in prompt
where does retrieved memory get injected

text: Diagram: single agent in the center labeled 'One Agent - 5 Jobs On One System Prompt', surrounded by five red role boxes labeled Retrieve, Classify, Refund, Troubleshoot, and Escalate. Visualizes the crowded role assignment that creates the performance ceiling for a single agent.

text: Diagram: a Dispatcher node at top with arrows down to three parallel agents - Agent A running lookup_customer at 1.2s, Agent B running lookup_subscription at 1.4s, Agent C running search_kb at 1.6s. Wall-clock time = max(1.2, 1.4, 1.6) = 1.6s instead of the 4.2s sum.
summary: Diagram shows dispatcher fanning out to three parallel worker agents whose wall-clock cost equals the slowest at 1.6s.

text: Diagram: two-layer planner-worker topology. Planner Agent at top decomposes the task and dispatches to Worker 1 (Lookup Customer) and Worker 2 (Process Refund) at the bottom layer. Workers each execute one sub-task and return results to the planner. Workers do not decompose; planner does not execute.
summary: Diagram shows a planner agent at the top dispatching sub-tasks to two worker agents that execute and return results.

text: Chapter 13.30 Why Multi-Agent? motivates moving from a single overloaded agent to multiple cooperating agents. Single agent loaded with many roles has a performance ceiling. Specialization gives each agent a tighter prompt and smaller tool set. Parallelism runs independent sub-tasks concurrently so wall-clock = max latency, not sum. Planner/worker separates task decomposition from execution. Multi-agent is wrong for single-step tasks, context-continuity tasks, latency-critical loops, and small dev teams. Decision rule: use multi-agent only if specialization, parallelism, or planner/worker fits the workload.
summary: Chapter 13.30 motivates multi-agent via specialization, parallelism, and planner/worker, and lists anti-patterns where one agent is the right answer.
queries: three reasons to use multi agent
when does multi agent make sense
core ideas of multi agent architecture
overview of why multi agent isn't always better

text: Why one agent sometimes isn't enough. A single agent loaded with all customer-support roles - retrieve, classify, refund, troubleshoot, escalate - has a crowded system prompt. Each role pulls the prompt in a different direction. Performance ceiling appears. Each new role added to the system prompt costs precision on every other role. The ceiling is real: at some point, splitting roles into separate agents beats stuffing them into one.
summary: One agent overloaded with many roles hits a performance ceiling because each added role degrades precision on the others.
queries: when does a single agent fail
performance ceiling of one LLM agent
what happens when one agent handles too many roles
drawbacks of stuffing many tasks into one agent
agent precision degradation with more jobs
five jobs one agent retrieve classify refund troubleshoot escalate

text: Specialization: one agent per role. Replace the crowded single agent with 3 focused agents. Each has a tighter system prompt, a smaller tool set, a clearer goal. Three specialists: Billing Specialist (tools: lookup_subscription, process_refund; handles refunds, invoices, tier changes), Troubleshooting Specialist (tools: search_kb, lookup_customer; diagnoses errors, outages, sync failures), Triage Router (tool: lookup_customer; classifies each ticket and routes to specialist). Each specialist has 1-2 tools, not the full 8-tool inventory. Smaller surface = fewer wrong-tool errors. The triage router picks which specialist a ticket goes to.
summary: Replacing one overloaded agent with specialized agents (billing, troubleshooting, triage router) each having a small tool set reduces wrong-tool errors.
queries: how to specialize agents by role
why smaller tool surface reduces errors
how to split a customer support agent

text: Run independent tasks at the same time. Some sub-tasks have no data dependencies. Run them on separate agents concurrently. The wall-clock cost is max(sub-task latency), not the sum. Example: Dispatcher fans out to Agent A (lookup_customer, 1.2s), Agent B (lookup_subscription, 1.4s), Agent C (search_kb, 1.6s). Sequential = 1.2 + 1.4 + 1.6 = 4.2s. Parallel = max(1.2, 1.4, 1.6) = 1.6s. Almost 3x faster. The win compounds as you add more independent sub-tasks.
summary: Independent sub-tasks run in parallel finish in max time not sum, cutting 4.2s sequential to 1.6s parallel.
queries: wall clock time vs sum of latencies
how much faster is parallel multi agent
when can sub tasks run concurrently

text: Planner vs Worker. Some agents are good at decomposing tasks; others are good at executing single steps. Separating these roles produces a 2-layer setup. Planner Agent at top decomposes the task. Worker 1 looks up customer, Worker 2 processes refund. The planner does NOT execute steps. The workers do NOT decompose. Each agent stays on its layer. This separation makes both layers more reliable than one mixed agent.
summary: Splitting planning from execution into two layers gives a planner that decomposes tasks and workers that execute single steps.
queries: agent that breaks tasks into subtasks
mixed agent vs separated layers reliability
lookup customer process refund worker example

text: Don't multi-agent a small problem. Multi-agent costs something: more LLM calls, more state to track, more places for failure. Four anti-patterns where one agent is the right answer: 1) Single-step tasks - just call the tool, adding a second agent doubles latency for zero gain. 2) Context continuity tasks - if the same context carries through every step, hand-off loses information. 3) Latency-critical loops - each agent adds 1-2 seconds of reasoning, a 100ms SLA loop cannot afford this. 4) Small dev teams - multi-agent failures (13.35) are hard to debug; a 2-person team will drown. Decision Rule: Multi-agent IF specialization OR parallelism OR planner/worker fits the workload. Otherwise single agent.
summary: Four anti-patterns (single-step, context continuity, latency-critical, small teams) say to skip multi-agent; otherwise use it only when specialization, parallelism, or planner/worker fits.
queries: when not to use multi agent
small dev team multi agent debugging
when one agent is the right answer

text: Hub and spoke topology diagram: an orchestrator agent at the top center has bidirectional arrows to three worker agents at the bottom (Worker 1, Worker 2, Worker 3), with no edges between workers. Each worker is labeled 'One Sub-Task'. Solid arrows go down from orchestrator to each worker; dashed return arrows go back up. Orchestrator labeled 'Plans + Aggregates'.
summary: Diagram showing hub-and-spoke orchestrator-worker topology with one orchestrator connected bidirectionally to three workers and no inter-worker edges.
queries: what does the orchestrator-worker topology look like
visualization of multi-agent hub and spoke
diagram of plans and aggregates orchestrator

text: Single worker card diagram: input sub-task description on the left ('Lookup Customer Tier for c-9924'), internal agent loop in the middle (Worker Agent running Reason -> Act -> Observe within its tool set only), and structured result returned to the orchestrator on the right ({ tier: 'Pro', usage: 'heavy' }). Arrows show data flowing left-to-right input -> worker -> structured output.
summary: Diagram of a single worker showing sub-task input, internal Reason-Act-Observe loop, and structured result output flowing left to right.

text: Chapter 13.31 covers orchestrator-worker, the most common production multi-agent shape. A central orchestrator plans, dispatches, and aggregates; workers each run one sub-task within their own tool set. Hub-and-spoke topology means workers only talk to the orchestrator, never to each other - making parallel dispatch safe. Orchestrator runs three phases (Plan, Dispatch, Aggregate) as separate LLM calls. A worker is itself an agent loop (Reason->Act->Observe) over a restricted tool set, returning a structured result. Worked example traces Ticket T3 across 5 steps with 3 parallel workers; total latency is max(worker) + plan + aggregate, not sum. Three aggregation patterns: concatenation (simple, verbose), voting (classification), synthesis (production default for NL answers).
summary: Chapter overview of orchestrator-worker: hub-and-spoke topology, Plan/Dispatch/Aggregate phases, worker structure, T3 trace, and three aggregation patterns.
queries: hub and spoke with planner and workers
how does parallel multi-agent latency work

text: One Planner, N Workers. The orchestrator-worker pattern is the most common production multi-agent shape. A central orchestrator plans and aggregates; workers each handle one sub-task. Hub and spoke topology - workers only talk to the orchestrator, never to each other. The hub-and-spoke shape means the orchestrator owns coordination cost; workers stay simple. No edges between workers means no peer-to-peer chatter, no shared-state corruption.
summary: Introduces orchestrator-worker as a hub-and-spoke multi-agent topology where one orchestrator plans and aggregates while N workers each handle one sub-task and never talk to each other.
queries: how does an orchestrator agent work
why workers don't talk to each other
what is the most common production multi-agent shape
who owns coordination in multi-agent systems

text: Plan, Dispatch, Aggregate. The orchestrator runs three phases per task. Each phase is a separate LLM call with a distinct system prompt - the same agent loops the model through three different mindsets. Plan: break the incoming task into 3-5 sub-tasks each workable in isolation. Dispatch: send each sub-task to its assigned worker agent. Aggregate: when workers return, merge results into a single coherent answer. The 3 phases give the orchestrator 3 separate minds: one to plan, one to dispatch, one to aggregate. Each is simpler than a single do-everything prompt.
summary: Describes the three orchestrator phases - Plan, Dispatch, Aggregate - each as a separate LLM call with its own system prompt.
queries: what phases does an orchestrator run
how does orchestrator break down tasks
separate LLM calls per orchestrator phase
how many sub-tasks should orchestrator make
why separate plan and aggregate phases

text: Execute One Sub-Task. Each worker takes one sub-task description, runs its own agent loop within its tool set, and returns a structured result. Workers do NOT talk to each other - only to the orchestrator. Example: sub-task input 'Lookup customer tier for c-9924' goes into Worker Agent which runs internal loop Reason -> Act -> Observe within its tool set only, then returns structured result { tier: 'Pro', usage: 'heavy' }. Workers don't talk to each other. If Worker A's result feeds Worker B's input, the orchestrator does that chaining. This is what makes parallel dispatch safe.
summary: Explains how a single worker executes one sub-task: receives input, runs an internal Reason-Act-Observe loop within its tool set, and returns a structured result to the orchestrator.
queries: how does a worker agent execute a sub-task
reason act observe loop in worker
can workers communicate with each other
internal agent loop within a worker
why workers only talk to orchestrator

text: Trace: Ticket T3 With Orchestrator-Worker. Ticket T3 = 'Dashboard slow + 500 errors'. The orchestrator plans 3 parallel sub-tasks, dispatches them to workers, then aggregates. Step 1 Orchestrator: receives T3, plans 3 sub-tasks: A) search_kb for 'dashboard slow'; B) search_kb for '500 errors'; C) lookup_customer for usage tier. Step 2 Worker A: returns top-3 KB articles on dashboard performance. Step 3 Worker B: returns top-3 KB articles on 500 errors. Step 4 Worker C: returns Alice's tier (Pro) + usage stats (heavy dashboard user). Step 5 Orchestrator: aggregates - composes single answer combining KB hits + customer context. Recommends dashboard optimization and escalates 500 errors to engineering. Workers A, B, C ran in parallel - steps 2-4 happened roughly at the same time. Total latency = max(worker time) + plan + aggregate, not sum.
summary: Walks through a concrete five-step trace where the orchestrator handles ticket T3 by dispatching three parallel workers (search_kb x2, lookup_customer) and then synthesizing a final answer.
queries: ticket T3 dashboard slow 500 errors trace
how does orchestrator handle parallel sub-tasks
max worker time vs sum of worker times

text: Three Ways To Aggregate. Aggregation - merging worker outputs into a single answer - has three common patterns, each with a sweet spot. Concatenation: stitch all worker outputs together in order. Simple but verbose. Best for quick prototypes; outputs already well-sectioned. Voting: workers each propose; orchestrator picks majority. Good for classification. Best for categorical outputs; hallucination reduction. Synthesis: orchestrator reads all worker outputs and writes a single coherent answer. Most common, most expensive. Best as production default for natural-language answers. Synthesis is the production default for natural-language answers. Voting wins when workers are doing classification. Concatenation is fine for structured outputs that need no rephrasing.
summary: Compares three aggregation patterns - concatenation, voting, synthesis - and when each is the right way to merge worker outputs into a final answer.

text: Three-level agent hierarchy diagram: top supervisor delegates to three middle-level supervisors which each delegate to two leaf specialists, with planar parent-child edges and no crossings. Top node labeled Supervisor in green. Middle row has Sub-Supervisor 1, Sub-Supervisor 2, Sub-Supervisor 3 in teal. Bottom row has six leaf nodes in cyan (two under each sub-supervisor). Caption: Top = Supervisor. Mid = Sub-Supervisors. Leaves = Specialists (Tools).
summary: Diagram showing a three-level agent tree with one root supervisor, three sub-supervisors, and six leaf specialists arranged without edge crossings.
queries: visual of supervisor sub-supervisor leaf tree

text: Chapter 13.32 covers Supervisor / Hierarchical multi-agent: a tree of agents where supervisors delegate to sub-supervisors which delegate to leaf specialists with narrow tool sets. Each supervisor runs five phases (receive, plan, route down, aggregate, return up). Pick hierarchical when the domain has nested sub-specialties; avoid going deeper than three levels. Customer support is the canonical fit with Billing, Troubleshooting, and Escalation sub-supervisors. Escalation up the tree is the safety net that prevents silent stalls and ensures dead-ends reach escalate_human.
summary: Hierarchical multi-agent organizes agents into a delegation tree with supervisor routing, three-level sweet spot, customer-support mapping, and upward escalation.
queries: agent tree levels and escalation summary

text: Hierarchical multi-agent is a tree. A supervisor delegates to sub-supervisors which delegate to leaf specialists. Each leaf has 1-2 narrow tools. Multiple LEVELS, not the single level of orchestrator-worker. Three levels is the practical sweet spot. Two levels is just orchestrator-worker renamed. Four levels usually signals over-engineering. Top = Supervisor. Mid = Sub-Supervisors. Leaves = Specialists (Tools).
summary: Hierarchical multi-agent organizes agents as a tree with supervisors delegating down to leaf specialists, with three levels as the sweet spot.
queries: how many levels should an agent hierarchy have
why three levels is best for agent tree
when is four levels too many in multi-agent
what does a leaf agent do in hierarchy
tree structure of agents with tools
what tools do leaf agents have
structure of a hierarchical AI agent system

text: At each level, a supervisor takes a sub-task, picks which child agent handles it (or breaks further and picks multiple children), waits for results, aggregates, and returns up to its own parent. Five phases per supervisor: 1) Receive - get a sub-task from the parent. 2) Plan - decide if single child handles or break into smaller sub-tasks. 3) Route Down - pick the child agent(s) best matched to the sub-task. 4) Aggregate - wait for children, combine their results. 5) Return Up - send the aggregated result to the parent supervisor. Every supervisor does the same 5 things at its level. Routing is the new responsibility compared to orchestrator-worker - a supervisor must know which child agent is right for which sub-task type.
summary: Each supervisor in the hierarchy runs the same five phases: receive, plan, route down, aggregate, return up.
queries: what does a supervisor agent do at each level

text: Choosing hierarchical over orchestrator-worker is a domain shape question. If your workflow has clear sub-areas with their own internal structure, the tree pays off. If it does not, flatten. Decision rules: Pick Orchestrator-Worker when workers are peer specialists at one level (example: 3 KB search workers run in parallel). Pick Hierarchical when domain has clear sub-domains (example: Billing has Refund / Invoice / Tier-Change as sub-areas). Avoid Hierarchical when tree depth > 3 levels (usually means over-engineering; flatten the design). Most production customer-support agents fit hierarchical because billing and troubleshooting are genuinely different sub-domains with different tools and policies.
summary: Pick hierarchical when domains have nested sub-specialties, orchestrator-worker when workers are peer specialists, and avoid hierarchy past three levels.

text: The customer-support agent fits hierarchical cleanly. Three sub-supervisors handle billing, troubleshooting, and escalation; each has its own specialists below. Root: Support Supervisor - all-tickets entry point, classifies and routes. Sub-Supervisor 1: Billing Supervisor - leaves are Refund Specialist, Invoice Specialist, Tier-Change Specialist. Sub-Supervisor 2: Troubleshooting Supervisor - leaves are Database Specialist, Network Specialist. Sub-Supervisor 3: Escalation Supervisor - leaf is Human Hand-Off (escalate_human). Each leaf specialist has 1-3 tools from the canonical 8-tool inventory: refund specialists use process_refund + lookup_subscription, network specialists use search_kb, escalation calls escalate_human.
summary: Concrete customer-support tree with a Support Supervisor root and Billing, Troubleshooting, and Escalation sub-supervisors each owning narrow leaf specialists.

text: A leaf specialist that cannot finish its task escalates up to its supervisor. The supervisor decides: retry with a different leaf, or escalate to ITS own parent. Eventually a top-level escalation reaches the Escalation Supervisor which calls escalate_human. Trace: Step 1 - Refund Specialist hits business_rule error on $250 refund and escalates up. Step 2 - Billing Supervisor considers Tier-Change leaf, rules it out, escalates up. Step 3 - Support Supervisor routes to Escalation Supervisor. Step 4 - Escalation Supervisor calls escalate_human with full trace. Escalation up the tree is the safety net. Without it, a leaf failure becomes a silent stall. With it, every dead-end eventually reaches escalate_human or a successful re-route.
summary: Escalation walks failures up the tree: leaf -> sub-supervisor -> root -> escalation supervisor -> escalate_human, ensuring no dead-end stalls.
queries: how does escalation work in agent hierarchy

what happens when leaf agent fails

text: Hand-off ring topology diagram with four agents arranged in a circle: Triage at top, Billing at right, Escalation at bottom, Troubleshooting at left. Each agent is connected by bidirectional arrows to its two neighbors, with one dashed diagonal cross-edge from Triage to Troubleshooting. The visual shows peer-to-peer transfer with no central parent — any agent can hand control to any other listed in its handoffs set. Contrast with hierarchical tree diagrams where edges only flow parent-to-child.
summary: Diagram of a four-agent hand-off ring (Triage, Billing, Escalation, Troubleshooting) connected as peers with bidirectional neighbor edges and a diagonal cross-edge, illustrating decentralized control transfer.
queries: what does hand-off ring look like
four peer agents in a circle

text: Chapter 13.33 introduces the Swarm hand-off pattern (in the OpenAI Agents SDK) as a decentralized alternative to orchestrator-worker. Each agent declares a handoffs list of peers it can transfer to. When agent A hands off to B, a hand-off package (full conversation history + working memory snapshot + hand-off reason) travels along so B resumes cleanly. A worked trace on ticket T4 ('cancel subscription + refund last invoice') shows triage -> billing -> escalation hand-offs ending in escalate_human. Hand-off topology is a ring/graph of peers, contrasted with the tree topology of supervisor-hierarchical setups: use ring when domains are equal peers, tree when domains nest.
summary: Overview of the Swarm hand-off pattern: agents declare handoffs lists, pass a context package on transfer, and form a ring/graph topology of peers rather than a centralized orchestrator or tree hierarchy.

text: The Swarm pattern (now in the OpenAI Agents SDK) replaces a central orchestrator with peer hand-offs. Instead of an orchestrator routing sub-tasks, an agent finishes its part and RETURNS the next agent to run. The runtime then switches control directly to that next agent. No central coordinator exists. Compared side by side: Orchestrator-Worker (delegation style) has the orchestrator pick who handles next, wait for the result, then pick again — central control. Hand-off / Swarm style has Agent A simply return 'next = agent B'; the loop switches control directly with no central coordinator. Hand-off is decentralized; orchestration is centralized. Both ship to production — the pick depends on whether you want a single 'brain' or peer-to-peer routing.
summary: Hand-off (Swarm) pattern lets one agent return the next agent to run, replacing centralized orchestration with decentralized peer-to-peer control transfer.

text:
In the Swarm pattern, each agent declares which other agents it can hand off to. The handoffs field is a list — similar to routes in a web framework. Shape:

Agent {
  name: "triage",
  instructions: "Classify intent. Hand off to billing for refund/invoice, troubleshooting for errors.",
  handoffs: [billing_agent, troubleshooting_agent]
}

The `name` and `instructions` are this agent's own identity. `handoffs` is the set of other agents this agent can transfer control to — basically a small DAG of allowed transitions.
summary: Each Swarm agent declares a handoffs list of other agents it is allowed to transfer control to, forming a small DAG of permitted transitions.
queries: how agents know who to hand off to

text: When agent A hands off to agent B, B does not start fresh. It receives a hand-off package with everything it needs to pick up cleanly. The package has three parts: (1) Full Conversation History — all turns since session start; (2) Working Memory Snapshot — current scratchpad state at hand-off time; (3) Hand-Off Reason — why the sender decided to hand off (e.g. 'classified as billing'). The receiving agent reads the entire package before its first reasoning step. No 're-litigation from scratch'. The conversation history plus working memory plus reason is the contract.
summary: A hand-off carries a package of full conversation history, working memory snapshot, and hand-off reason so the receiving agent can resume without restarting from scratch.
queries: what gets passed in a hand-off
how next agent picks up state

text: Trace for Ticket T4 = 'Cancel my subscription + refund last invoice.' Watch the hand-off ring move control across three agents. Step 1 — Triage Agent: receives T4. Step 2 — Triage Agent: classifies as billing. Returns hand-off to billing_agent. Loop switches. Step 3 — Billing Agent: receives full conversation history. Processes cancel. Step 4 — Billing Agent: refund > $200; business_rule error. Returns hand-off to escalation_agent. Step 5 — Escalation Agent: receives full trace. Calls escalate_human with cancel-and-refund context. Three agents, three hand-offs, one ticket. Each agent did its specialty. The hand-off package carried full context so the escalation_agent did not need to ask 'what happened before'.
summary: Concrete trace of ticket T4 showing triage handing off to billing, billing hitting a >$200 business rule and handing off to escalation, which calls escalate_human with full context.

text: Hand-off topology is a ring (or general graph) — any agent can transfer to any other, subject to the handoffs list. This differs from tree topology, which has fixed parent-child relationships. The diagram shows four peer agents (Triage, Billing, Escalation, Troubleshooting) arranged in a circle with bidirectional edges between neighbors and one diagonal cross-edge. Tree topology fits when domains are nested (billing has refund/invoice as sub-areas). Ring topology fits when domains are equal peers (triage, billing, troubleshooting, escalation are at the same level, no nesting).
summary: Hand-off uses ring or graph topology of peer agents, contrasted with hierarchical tree topology — pick ring when domains are equal peers and tree when domains are nested.
queries: when to use ring topology agents

text: Chapter 13.34 covers reflection-as-multi-agent. Critic pattern: a separate skeptical agent reviews the worker's draft, isolated from worker memory. Critic-revise loop: worker drafts, critic scores 0-10, revise if below threshold, capped by retry limit to avoid cost runaway. Debate pattern: three agents (Pro, Con, Judge) for contested decisions, triples LLM cost. Worked refund example shows the policy critic catching a >30-day rule and reducing a $180 refund to $120. Decision rule: critic for high-stakes mutations, debate for contested decisions, skip both for low-stakes lookups; trade off cost-of-error against extra LLM cost.
summary: Critic and debate are multi-agent reflection patterns that trade LLM cost for higher answer quality on high-stakes or contested decisions.

text: Critic is reflection-as-multi-agent. Instead of one agent self-critiquing (covered in 13.22), a separate critic agent reviews the worker's draft. Same idea as self-reflection but with more isolation - the critic does not share working memory with the worker. The worker agent produces a draft answer for the customer. The critic agent reads the draft plus the original question and scores or rewrites it. The critic has a different system prompt - it is told to be SKEPTICAL of the worker's draft. The separation makes it less likely the model just rubber-stamps its own earlier output.
summary: Critic pattern uses a separate skeptical agent (not the worker) to review the worker's draft, providing more isolation than self-reflection.

text: The critic-revise loop is bounded. Worker drafts; critic scores 0-10 with reasoning; if below threshold (e.g. 7), worker revises with critic's feedback; critic re-scores. Loop until score is high enough OR the retry cap is hit. Four stations: Worker Drafts -> Critic Scores 0-10 -> If < 7, Revise -> Critic Re-Scores -> back to Worker. The retry cap matters. Without it, a worker that can't please the critic loops forever - one of the cost-runaway failure modes covered in 13.35.
summary: Critic-revise runs as a bounded loop scoring 0-10 with a retry cap to prevent infinite revision and cost runaway.

text: Debate is a 3-agent variant. Agent Pro argues FOR a position; Agent Con argues AGAINST; Judge agent reads both arguments and the original question and picks one. Useful when the answer is contested or high-stakes enough to be worth two views. Debate triples the LLM bill versus single-agent. Use it sparingly - high-stakes contested decisions where you genuinely want two voices, not noise-reduction on a routine task.
summary: Debate uses three agents (Pro, Con, Judge) to argue both sides of a contested decision, tripling LLM cost.

text: Critic and debate are not free. Each extra agent doubles or triples the LLM bill and adds latency. Decision matrix: Use Critic for high-stakes mutations (refunds, escalations, contract changes), cost +1 LLM call per draft. Use Debate for contested decisions with multiple readings (policy edge cases, customer-vs-business tension), cost +3 LLM calls per round. Skip Both for simple low-stakes tasks (lookups, classifications, factual recall), no overhead. Rule of thumb: if a wrong answer is worth less than the extra LLM cost, skip the critic. If a wrong answer can cost $200 in refund overage, paying $0.01 for a critic call is the easiest trade you will ever make.
summary: Decision rule: use Critic for high-stakes mutations, Debate for contested decisions, skip both for low-stakes lookups based on cost-of-error vs LLM cost.

text: Chapter 13.35 covers four production failure modes of multi-agent systems: drift (agents disagree on the goal), infinite loop (hand-offs bounce forever), deadlock (agents wait on each other), and cost runaway (unbounded recursion blows spend). Each mode has a distinct signature, a production signal, an alert threshold, and a fix. Drift fix: shared plan summary every agent reads. Infinite loop fix: hand-off counter + chain history halting when same edge fires twice. Deadlock fix: timeouts on every inter-agent wait. Cost runaway fix: recursion depth cap, per-turn spend cap, circuit breaker. Alert thresholds: hand-off > 5 per turn; WAITING state > 30 seconds; spend rate > 3x baseline per turn; plan-summary divergence across agents. Monitoring all four is the price of shipping multi-agent safely.
summary: Four multi-agent failure modes - drift, infinite loop, deadlock, cost runaway - each with signal, alert threshold, and fix.

text: Drift = different agents in the same run interpret the user's intent differently. They each work hard toward their own version of the goal. Final answer is incoherent. Example on Ticket T4: Agent A reads it as 'Cancel Subscription Now'; Agent B reads it as 'Refund The Last Invoice'; Agent C reads it as 'Save The Customer (Anti-Churn)'. Three agents, three goals, one ticket. Fix drift with a shared plan summary output that every agent must read before its first reasoning step. If two agents disagree about the goal, the alert fires before any tools run.
summary: Drift is when agents in the same run interpret intent differently; fix with shared plan summary every agent reads.

text: Infinite loop = hand-off ring spins forever. Triage hands off to Billing; Billing hands back to Triage; Triage hands off to Billing again. No agent commits to handling the ticket. Symptom: hand-off rate per turn does not decrease (1, 2, 3, 4, 5...). Fix infinite loop with a hand-off counter per turn plus a hand-off chain history. If the same edge fires twice in a row, halt and escalate to human.
summary: Infinite loop is agents bouncing hand-offs forever; fix with hand-off counter and chain history that halts on repeat edges.

text: Deadlock = circular wait. Agent A is waiting on Agent B's result; Agent B is waiting on Agent A's result. Neither makes progress. Symptom: both agents in WAITING state simultaneously for more than 30 seconds with no state change. Fix deadlock with timeouts on every inter-agent wait. If A doesn't hear from B within N seconds, A surfaces 'timeout waiting on B' and the runtime breaks the cycle.
summary: Deadlock is two agents in circular wait on each other; fix with timeouts on every inter-agent wait.
queries: two agents waiting on each other
agent A waits B waits A

text: Cost runaway = a parent agent spawns sub-agents; each sub-agent spawns more; cost grows exponentially. By iteration 20, a normal $0.50 run is $50. Signal: spend rate climbing per turn without a corresponding answer-quality gain. Fix with a recursion depth cap, a per-turn spend cap, and a circuit breaker that halts when either is hit. If the agent decides to spawn 12 sub-agents, something is wrong with the planner prompt.
summary: Cost runaway is exponential spend from unbounded sub-agent spawning; fix with depth cap, spend cap, circuit breaker.
queries: what is cost runaway in multi-agent
planner prompt spawns too many sub-agents

text: Each failure mode has a production signal with a threshold that should page a human. Drift signal: planner outputs disagree on intent; threshold: detected by comparing plan summaries across agents. Infinite Loop signal: hand-off rate not decreasing; threshold: more than 5 hand-offs in a single turn. Deadlock signal: agents in WAITING state; threshold: more than 30 seconds with no state change. Cost Runaway signal: spend rate per turn climbing; threshold: more than 3x baseline cost per turn. Every multi-agent system in production must monitor all four. Skipping any one is a bet the corresponding failure mode never bites - and it always eventually does. Alert thresholds are the price of multi-agent.
summary: Production alert thresholds for each failure mode: plan disagreement, > 5 hand-offs/turn, > 30s WAITING, > 3x baseline spend.

text: Chapter 13.36 Agentic RAG: contrasts one-shot naive RAG (Query -> Retrieve -> Generate) with iterative agentic RAG that loops Search -> Judge -> Refine -> Search until the Judge says done or max iterations is reached. Judge is the critical component - too lenient produces naive RAG with extra steps, too strict produces cost runaway. Query rewriting progressively narrows broad questions by adding filters across iterations. Concrete trace of 'Customer-impact issues past 90 days' shows naive RAG cannot broaden a too-narrow search while agentic can. Costs 3-10x naive; use for research, multi-hop, find-all, or compare queries; stick with naive for factoid lookups. Frames agentic RAG as a multi-agent pattern closing Act 6.
summary: Chapter-level overview of agentic RAG as iterative search-judge-refine loop, with cost trade-offs and naive vs agentic decision rule.

text: Retrieve once vs retrieve in a loop. Naive RAG retrieves once and generates an answer. Agentic RAG puts retrieval inside an agent loop: search, judge the results, refine the query, search again. Iterative instead of one-shot. Mechanics covered in Section 12.29; here framed as a multi-agent pattern. Naive RAG is a pipeline (Query -> Retrieve -> Generate); agentic RAG is a loop (Query -> Retrieve -> Judge -> Done, with rewrite-back arrow if not enough). Section 12.2 covers naive pipeline; Section 12.29 covers loop mechanics; this closes Act 6 by framing it as multi-agent.
summary: Contrasts one-shot naive RAG pipeline with the iterative agentic RAG loop that wraps retrieval inside an agent.

text: Search, judge, refine, repeat. The agentic RAG loop has four stations: Search returns candidates; Judge asks 'do I have enough? what is missing?'; Refine rewrites the query with the gap in mind; Search again. Loop until Judge says 'done' or max iterations is hit. Each station is its own LLM call (or its own internal agent). The Judge is the most critical - if it always says 'I have enough', you have naive RAG with extra steps. If it never says 'enough', you have cost runaway.
summary: Breaks the agentic RAG loop into four stations - search, judge, refine, repeat - and highlights the Judge as the make-or-break component.

text: The agent rewrites its own query. Query rewriting is the smartest part of the loop. The Judge identifies what is missing; the Refine step turns that gap into a sharper next query. Three iterations on a real research question: Iteration 1 query 'Customer issues past 90 days' returns 5 generic articles, too broad. Iteration 2 query 'Customer-impact incidents past 90 days, severity > 2' returns better hits, 8 incidents. Iteration 3 query 'Customer-impact incidents past 90 days, severity > 2, status = resolved' returns best hits, 8 incidents all with resolution details. Each iteration narrows toward what the Judge wanted. By iteration 3, the query is so specific that top-K results are all relevant.
summary: Walks through three query rewrites that progressively narrow a broad research question into a precise filtered query.

text: Example: Customer-impact issues past 90 days. The research-style query the agent answers across the full loop. Step 1 Initial Query: 'Find all customer-impact issues in the past 90 days.' Step 2 Search Top-5: vector search returns 5 KB articles. Step 3 Judge: only 2 are customer-impact. Need broader retrieval with severity filter. Step 4 Rewrite + Search: query adds severity > 2 filter. New search returns 8 articles. Step 5 Final Aggregation: 8 incidents synthesized into a summary table with dates, severities, resolutions. The final aggregation is a structured artifact: 8 incidents with dates, severity ratings, and resolution summaries. Naive RAG could not have produced this - no way to broaden a search when the first attempt is too narrow.
summary: Five-step trace shows how agentic RAG answers a research question that naive RAG cannot, ending in a structured incident summary table.

text: When to iterate retrieval. Agentic RAG costs 3-10x more than naive RAG. Use it when answer quality matters more than latency. Otherwise stick with naive pipeline (Section 12.2). Decision: Naive RAG when direct factoid lookups, single-doc questions, example 'What is the refund policy?' - cost is 1 retrieval, 1 generation, cheap. Agentic RAG when research questions, multi-hop, find-all or compare queries, example 'Find all customer-impact issues past 90 days' - cost is 3-10x naive cost, slow. Naive RAG pipeline covered in Section 12.2; agentic RAG mechanics in Section 12.29; here framed as multi-agent pattern closing the multi-agent chapters.
summary: Decision rule for picking naive vs agentic RAG based on query type, latency tolerance, and 3-10x cost multiplier.

text: Chapter 13.37 (Why Eval Agents Differently) explains why agents need a different eval approach than single-shot LLMs. Three structural reasons make agents harder: non-determinism (same ticket → different traces), multi-step (8 tool calls = 8 failure points), and silent failure (no crash, wrong action). Real production incidents include $50K unauthorized refunds, hallucinated policy quoted for 3 weeks, drift to chitchat, and cross-tenant data leaks. Offline eval (frozen golden set, before every deploy) and online eval (1-5% live traffic sampling, continuous) are complementary; both required. Some failure modes need human review: tone failures, hidden hallucinations, cross-session drift (20-50 traces/week/agent). Full pipeline has 5 stages: eval set (13.41), per-trace step grading (13.40), end-to-end LLM-as-Judge (13.39), online sampling + drift signal (13.41), alerting + rollback.
summary: Overview of why agents need specialized evaluation: structural difficulty, production failure stories, offline vs online eval, human review need, and the five-stage pipeline.

text: Three Reasons Agents Are Harder To Eval. A pure LLM eval grades input vs output - one shot, one score. Agents differ on three structural axes that make naive eval miss most production failures. 1) Non-Determinism: same ticket can take different traces on different runs; you cannot compare outputs directly. 2) Multi-Step: eight tool calls in a row means eight places to fail; the final answer hides where the trace went wrong. 3) Silent Failure: the agent does not crash. It calls the wrong tool, sends the wrong email, or misses an escalation without raising an error. Every Section 13 production failure mode (13.35) maps to at least one of these axes. Eval design must address all three or production incidents slip through.
summary: Three structural reasons agents are harder to eval than single-shot LLMs: non-determinism, multi-step traces, and silent failure modes.

text: What Breaks When You Don't Eval. Real production incidents from teams that shipped agents without an eval pipeline. Every one of these went unnoticed for days or weeks. (1) Unauthorized Refunds: a refund agent issued $50K in unauthorized refunds over a weekend before anyone noticed. (2) Hallucinated Policy: a support agent confidently quoted a non-existent refund policy for 3 weeks of production traffic. (3) Drift To Chitchat: a multi-agent system drifted from billing questions to open-ended chitchat over 2 days. (4) Cross-Tenant Data Leak: a tool security regression let one customer's profile leak into another's active session. Each incident shows the same shape: silent failure + production traffic + late detection. Eval is the only tool that converts "late detection" into "same-day".
summary: Four real production incidents (refunds, hallucinated policy, drift, tenant leak) showing what slips through when agents ship without eval.
queries: when did teams notice agent bug
how long do agent bugs go unnoticed

text: Offline (Before Ship) vs Online (After Ship). Two complementary loops. Offline grades a frozen golden set before every deploy. Online samples real production traffic continuously. Production agents need BOTH; either alone leaves a gap. Comparison: Input - Offline uses frozen golden eval set; Online uses sampled live traffic. Cadence - Offline before every deploy; Online continuous, 1-5% sampling. Cost - Offline cheap per run (small set); Online expensive at scale. Catches - Offline finds regressions on known cases; Online finds drift, new failure modes, real distribution. Offline tells you if known cases regressed. Online tells you if the actual production distribution shifted. Skip one and you ship blind on that dimension.
summary: Offline eval grades frozen golden sets before deploy; online eval samples 1-5% of live traffic continuously; production needs both.
queries: cost comparison offline vs online eval
why need both offline and online eval

text: Some Failure Modes Need Humans. Automated judges are cheap but blind on three categories. Plan budget for a small but permanent human review loop on every production agent. (1) Tone Failures: rude, robotic, condescending responses. Need human spot-check. (2) Hidden Hallucinations: invented policy the judge cannot detect because the judge does not know the policy either. Needs a RAG-grounded judge + human. (3) Cross-Session Drift: long-term personality or style drift across many sessions. Needs a human reviewer looking at the whole population. Rule of thumb: 20-50 human-reviewed traces per week per agent. Cheap relative to a single production incident, and the only signal the judge cannot produce.
summary: Three failure modes that automated judges miss and require human review: tone, hidden hallucinations, cross-session drift; budget 20-50 traces/week/agent.

text: What A Full Pipeline Looks Like. Five stages, each handled by a later chapter in this act. Stage 1 builds the eval set. Stages 2-3 grade traces and answers. Stage 4 watches production. Stage 5 acts on the signal. Stage 1: Eval Set (Golden + Adversarial + Regression Cases) - chapter 13.41. Stage 2: Per-Trace Step Grading (score each tool call) - chapter 13.40. Stage 3: End-To-End Judge (score final answer with LLM-as-Judge) - chapter 13.39. Stage 4: Online Sampling (grade 1-5% of production traffic + drift signal) - chapter 13.41. Stage 5: Alerting + Rollback (page engineer when quality drops) - Ops. The pipeline costs more than a single judge call, but it is the only structure that catches the three failure modes from sub=0 before they reach a customer.
summary: Five-stage agent eval pipeline: eval set, per-trace step grading, end-to-end judge, online sampling with drift signal, and alerting plus rollback.

Red dashed marker at right shows $0.50 target. Caption warns runaway iteration risk: each extra loop adds $0.20 in output tokens.

text: Chapter 13.38 covers the four eval dimensions for production agents: Correctness, Latency, Cost, and Safety. Each is shown with a target, weight, and visual. Correctness target > 90% resolution (weight 0.5). Latency target P95 < 8s (weight 0.2) shown via histogram with P50 = 3.2s, P95 = 7.5s, P99 = 14s. Cost target < $0.50 per ticket (weight 0.2) broken into input tokens $0.06 + output tokens $0.20 + tool calls $0.12 = $0.38. Safety target > 99% refusal (weight 0.1) with four sub-metrics: refusal rate, false refusal rate, escalation rate, prompt-injection detection. A composite trace score formula combines all four: trace_score = 0.5*correctness + 0.2*latency_score + 0.2*cost_score + 0.1*safety_score, target > 0.85. Example computation: 0.5*0.92 + 0.2*0.78 + 0.2*0.84 + 0.1*0.97 = 0.885 (passes).
summary: Production agents graded on four axes (correctness, latency, cost, safety) combined into one weighted composite score.

A trace scoring 1.0 on correctness but blowing the latency budget or leaking a refund is still a failure. Treat all four as first-class. Four axis cards: Correctness target > 90% Resolution, weight 0.5. Latency target P95 < 8s, weight 0.2. Cost target < $0.50 / Ticket, weight 0.2. Safety target > 99% Refusal, weight 0.1.

text: Correctness is binary at task level. Definition: Task Completed = User Goal Achieved AND No Incorrect Side Effects. Metric: Resolution Rate = Correct Traces / Total Traces. Target > 90%. Example T2 (Reset Password + Update Email): agent called reset_password and update_email, both effects persisted, user confirmation email sent. Result: CORRECT. Counter-example T4 (refund + cancel): correctness fails if agent issued refund but skipped cancel, even if user got a polite response.
summary: Correctness is binary: user goal achieved with no incorrect side effects, measured by resolution rate above 90%.

text: Latency is wall-clock time from user message to final answer. Average alone hides the bad tail. Track percentiles: P50, P95, P99. The tail is where users churn. Histogram shows: P50 = 3.2s, P95 = 7.5s (just inside 8s target), P99 = 14s long tail. Latency budget for a support ticket: 1.5s LLM call x 3 iterations + 200ms tool call x 6 = 5.7s. Remainder (under 8s P95) absorbs network jitter and retries.
summary: Latency measured as P50/P95/P99 wall-clock time; budget breakdown for a support trace fits under the 8s P95 target.

text: Cost is dollar amount per trace. Input tokens cheap, output tokens cost ~5x more, tool calls add their own infrastructure cost. Runaway iterations are the number-one source of cost overshoots. Cost breakdown bar for typical support trace: Input Tokens $0.06 + Output Tokens $0.20 + Tool Calls $0.12 = $0.38 / Ticket, against $0.50 target. Each extra loop adds $0.20 in output tokens. Cap max iterations (10-20 per loop termination policy from 13.23) and you cap worst-case cost. Without the cap, a 100-step runaway can hit $5 on a single trace.
summary: Per-trace cost broken into input tokens, output tokens, and tool calls; runaway iterations the biggest overshoot risk.

text: Safety has four sub-metrics, each with target and customer-support example. Refusal Rate (Prohibited Actions) target > 99%: Agent refuses to issue refunds above policy limit without manager approval. False Refusal Rate target < 1%: Agent should not reject legitimate requests by mistake. Escalation Rate (Refund > $200) target 100%: Every large refund must route to human reviewer. Prompt-Injection Detection target > 95%: Agent catches user inputs trying to override the system prompt. Safety scores need lowest tolerance: 99% refusal rate still permits 1 in 100 prohibited actions. For sensitive verticals push to 99.9%+.
summary: Safety decomposed into refusal rate, false refusal rate, escalation rate, and prompt-injection detection with strict targets.

text: One number for the dashboard. Stakeholders want a single trace score. Weight the four axes; correctness dominates for support agents but weights tunable per use case. Trigger alert when composite drops below target line. Composite Trace Score Formula: trace_score = 0.5 * correctness + 0.2 * latency_score + 0.2 * cost_score + 0.1 * safety_score. Each component normalized 0-1. Target composite > 0.85. Example using sub=0 sample run: 0.5 * 0.92 + 0.2 * 0.78 + 0.2 * 0.84 + 0.1 * 0.97 = 0.885 > 0.85 (Passes).
summary: Composite trace score combines four normalized axes with tunable weights; example sample passes at 0.885 > 0.85 target.

text: Chapter 13.39 covers LLM-as-Judge for grading agent outputs. It compares pairwise versus scalar scoring (scalar wins as the production default), specifies a customer-support rubric totaling 10 points (correctness 0-3, completeness 0-2, tone 0-2, citations 0-2, escalation 0-1), enumerates three judge biases (length bias, position bias, self-preference) with mitigations (length-normalize, randomize position, diversify judge model family), explains calibration against human annotators with a target Pearson correlation greater than 0.7 and a worked example at r = 0.78, presents a canonical judge prompt that forces JSON output with field names correctness/completeness/tone/citations/escalation/total/reasoning, and connects to Section 12.32 RAG faithfulness and answer-relevance judging by showing mechanics are identical (rubric + JSON + calibration) while the agent scope adds task completion, trace correctness, and multi-step side-effecting tool grading.

text: LLM-as-Judge means using one LLM to grade another LLM's output. Two scoring styles dominate production: pairwise and scalar. Pairwise: judge sees Answer A and Answer B side by side and picks which is better; best for ranking two models or two prompt variants head-to-head; weakness is it cannot express absolute quality and reports only relative wins. Scalar: judge scores one answer from 0 to 10 with a written justification; best for absolute quality tracking, dashboards, and regression alerts; weakness is more variance across runs, needs a rubric to reduce drift. Pairwise is great for model selection sprints. Scalar is the production default for ongoing trace quality dashboards.
summary: Two judge scoring styles: pairwise for head-to-head ranking, scalar for absolute dashboards (scalar is production default).

text: Tell the judge what to score on. Without a rubric, scalar scores drift week over week; with a rubric the judge has anchors. Use five criteria for customer support with a total budget of 10 points. Customer-Support Judge Rubric: Correctness 0-3 (answer matches the policy / KB; 3 = exact, 0 = contradicts policy); Completeness 0-2 (answer addresses every part of the user question); Tone 0-2 (friendly + professional, penalize condescension or robot-speak); Citations 0-2 (policy references included when applicable); Escalation 0-1 (correctly escalated when outside scope - refund > $200, account lock). Explicit ranges + per-criterion definitions cut judge variance by roughly half compared to 'score this 0-10' without anchors. The rubric is the most valuable artifact in the eval pipeline.
summary: Five-criterion 10-point customer-support judge rubric: correctness, completeness, tone, citations, escalation.

text: What the judge gets wrong. LLM judges have three known biases; every production setup should mitigate all three before trusting the dashboard. Length Bias: longer answers get higher scores even when content is identical; mitigation is length-normalize scores or truncate to a standard budget before judging. Position Bias: in pairwise the FIRST answer is favored 5-10% of the time regardless of quality; mitigation is randomize position per run and average across both orderings. Self-Preference: a judge using model X rates model X's outputs higher than other models; mitigation is use a judge from a different model family and diversify judge mix periodically. Three biases, three mitigations: length normalization, position randomization, judge diversification. None are optional in production.
summary: Three LLM judge biases (length, position, self-preference) and their mitigations.
